title: SLO-driven fidelity onboarding checklist and playbook
preview:
Overview

This document is a pragmatic playbook for onboarding customers onto SLO-driven fidelity tiers. It describes the mapping between customer SLOs (latency, availability, correctness), recommended fidelity tiers, required telemetry, operational checks, and a step-by-step checklist for commercial and technical leads. Use this when contracting customers for dedicated or private deployments and when migrating high-usage tenants from hosted to dedicated environments.

Audience

- Product managers and solutions engineering
- Customer success and account teams
- Platform and runtime engineers (serving, routing, telemetry)

Goals

1. Provide a repeatable alignment process that converts customer SLAs into recommended fidelity packages.
2. Ensure operational readiness (telemetry, runbooks, rollback) before committing to a fidelity onboarding date.
3. Minimize regressions in perceived quality while keeping unit cost under the negotiated budget.

Principles

- SLOs lead decisioning: fidelity (quantization, model variant, batching) should be chosen to meet the agreed SLO window, not vice versa.
- Measure before change: collect 7 days of representative telemetry prior to finalizing package.
- Guardrails: automated quality checks (sample prompts) must pass before enabling lower-cost fidelity modes.

SLO -> Fidelity mapping (reference matrix)

| Customer SLO Profile | Latency target (P95) | Availability target | Recommended Fidelity Tier | Typical Runtime Configs | Cost impact estimate |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---:|
| Ultra-low latency | <= 80ms | 99.99% | High-fidelity stream (A) | no quantization, 8-bit cache warm, aggressive prefetch | +40% unit cost |
| Low-latency transactional | <= 200ms | 99.9% | Balanced fidelity (B) | int8 quant, prefix caching, small batching | +10% unit cost |
| Throughput-first | <= 500ms | 99.5% | Cost-optimized (C) | int8+gptq, larger batching, cold-kv eviction | -35% unit cost |
| Cost-minimum | > 500ms acceptable | 99.0% | Minimal fidelity (D) | heavy quant, lower-context-length, opportunistic caching | -60% unit cost |

Notes: numbers are starting guidelines; individual customer workloads may shift mapping. The product team maintains the fidelity definitions in the model catalog (redwood-console: /catalog/fidelity-defs).

Telemetry and validation signals required

Collect the following at 1m aggregation for a 7–14 day window before and after rollout: 

- redwood-serving: request P50/P95/P99 latency, tokens generated, request size
- redwood-routing: route fallback count, capacity-mismatch events
- console-telemetry: end-to-end success rate, sample response diffs (automated eval capture)
- optimize-advisor: estimated cost per 1k tokens and suggested batch sizes

Quality validation: run a prompt-set (100–500 canonical prompts covering critical intents) through both baseline and candidate fidelity. Required thresholds:

- Semantic delta (automated embedding-based): cosine difference <= 0.06 average
- Task accuracy (when labeled golden outputs exist): delta <= 3% absolute drop
- Regression exceptions: zero critical hallucination rate increases for safety prompts

Operational checklist (numbered)

1. Legal / Commercial alignment
   - Confirm target SLOs, billing model (usage vs reserved), and trial period.
   - Document commitments in CD/contract appendix: include fallback and rollback terms.

2. Pre-rollout telemetry collection (7–14 days)
   - Enable sampling via redwood-routing debug flags: curl -X POST https://console.internal/api/telemetry/sampling -d '{"tenant":"TENANT_ID","rate":0.05}'
   - Ensure optimize-advisor has run the cost scan and attached recommended config.

3. Baseline quality sweep
   - Run canonical prompt-set through current production variant and store golden outputs.
   - Run the same set through candidate fidelity variants using the eval harness (applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness).
   - Produce delta report and sign-off by owner (Product + SRE).

4. Staging rollout and smoke tests
   - Deploy candidate fidelity to a staging pool (redwood-serving/stage-pool).
   - Run 10% of production traffic (traffic-shadowing) for 48 hours.
   - Monitor: P95 latency, error rate, fallback events, and sample diff.

5. Canary and phased ramp (if staging passes)
   - Canary: 1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 50% (6–12 hour steps) with automated gates:
     - Gate A: P95 latency within agreed window and error increase < 0.02%
     - Gate B: Semantic delta and labeled accuracy within thresholds
   - Use routing policy: policy=route-by-slo, fallback=previous-variant for N failures.

6. Full transition and billing switchover
   - Coordinate with billing: change tenant plan effective time, toggle metering flags.
   - Post-rollout: retain double telemetry for 72 hours (baseline + new).

7. Rollback plan
   - Immediate rollback if any critical gate fails for >30 minutes.
   - Execute: traffic reassign via redwood-routing: POST /v1/routing/assign {"tenant":"TENANT_ID","variant":"baseline"} and notify stakeholders.

Required configs and templates (table)

| Template name | Location | Purpose | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| fidelity-profile.json | /catalog/fidelity-defs | Defines quant, batch, cache settings | eng-serving-runtime |
| onboarding-checklist.md | /playbooks/onboarding | Customer-facing checklist | customer-success |
| sampling-config | redwood-routing flags | Telemetry sampling for pre-rollout | eng-platform |

Example fidelity-profile.json (excerpt)

```
{
  "name": "balanced-B",
  "quantization": "int8",
  "max_batch_tokens": 2048,
  "kv_cache_policy": "warm-eligible",
  "fallback_variant": "high-fidelity-A"
}
```

Ownership and contacts

- Product owner: Priya Natarajan (product) — overall decision authority
- Runtime owner: Eng-serving-runtime oncall channel (#eng-runtime)
- SRE: contact SRE rotations via oncall-and-incident-response/rota
- Customer success: Account lead and Solutions Engineer (entry in customer record)

Rollout timeline example (4 week plan)

Week 0: Contract sign, sample telemetry enabled
Week 1: Telemetry collection and baseline sweep
Week 2: Staging rollout and smoke tests
Week 3: Canary and phased ramp to 50%
Week 4: Complete transition, billing switchover, 14-day monitoring window

Success metrics and postmortem triggers

Track these for 30 days post-transition:
- SLA compliance: target > 99% of 1-minute windows meeting SLO
- Cost delta: within +/-10% of pre-agreed estimate; product must approve >10% variance
- Quality regressions: any labeled intent accuracy drop >3% triggers a triage
- Customer reported incidents: >1 Sev2 within 7 days triggers formal review

Common edge cases and mitigations

- Burst traffic causing cache thrash: enable conservative kv_cache_policy and increase warm-eligible thresholds.
- Model variant availability shortage: use policy route-by-cost to redirect to alternate region or lower-fidelity variant with explicit consent.
- Regulated workloads: use private deployment checklist (eng-private-deployments/customer-requirements-library) with encryption key rotation step.

Appendix: Sample sign-off rubric

- Product sign-off: quality delta within thresholds, commercial terms documented
- SRE sign-off: staging smoke tests passed, runbooks updated, oncall briefed
- CS sign-off: customer acceptance test plan executed and customer approves sample outputs

Related pages

- /product-docs/requirements/prd-fidelity-decision-service-and-sdk-2028-04-11
- /eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/fidelity-benchmarks-2029-05-10

FAQ

Q: What if customer requests a lower cost than mapping suggests?
A: Offer a staged fidelity reduction with short-term trial and stronger telemetry + rollback windows. Require explicit customer acknowledgment of potential minor regressions.

Q: How do we bill during a canary?
A: Billing remains on baseline until commercial switchover; usage during canary is meta-tagged for reconciliation.

Document maintenance

- Owner: product (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: quarterly or after any fidelity product release.

End of document

title: Conversational Journey Maps and Price Sensitivity Tests for Technical Buyers
preview:
Overview

This page is a consolidated sales enablement handbook for GTM teams working with technical buyers evaluating Redwood Inference for conversational products. It couples user journey maps (developer and product-owner perspectives) with empirical price-sensitivity test templates and an evidence-backed competitive brief. The goal is to equip Sales Engineers, Solutions Architects, and AEs with repeatable conversation scripts, testable pricing scenarios, and clear security FAQ answers for prospect diligence.

Use cases in scope
- Real-time chatbots (sub-200ms token latency targets)
- Multi-turn assistants with long context (up to 64k tokens)
- High-throughput embedding and reranking pipelines
- Edge / hybrid deployments for regulated customers

Quick reference: what to carry to a technical call
1) Two-slide technical narrative (latency/cost tradeoffs + fallback story)
2) One-page pricing sensitivity table with 3 scenarios (pilot / scale / enterprise commit)
3) Live demo checklist (API keys, sample prompts, sample dataset, model variant to demo)
4) Security evidence bundle pointer (redwood-kms-evidence.pdf)

Section 1 — Conversational journey maps
Purpose: map buyer personas to technical triggers, evaluation criteria, and Redwood talking points. For each persona, we present a short narrative, key metrics they care about, and suggested demo prompts.

Persona A: Platform Engineer (internal platform team)
- Evaluation triggers: standardize inference across teams, predictable unit economics, RBAC and compliance integration.
- Key metrics: tokens/sec per GPU, p95 latency, per-token cost, failure modes.
- Talking points: Dedicated pools, autoscale policies, per-route token accounting in Redwood Console, KMS integrations.
- Demo prompts: multi-tenant routing example + trace showing per-route latency breakdown.

Persona B: Product Lead (customer-facing feature owner)
- Evaluation triggers: response quality, consistent latency, rollback capability during quality regressions.
- Key metrics: quality eval pass rate on golden prompts, 99th percentile total time-to-first-token, canary rollout success rate.
- Talking points: Redwood Optimize for recommended quantization & batching, automatic rollback policies, prompt-set linked quality alerts.
- Demo prompts: A/B response comparison to show model variant choice impact and Redwood’s rollout controls.

Persona C: Security & Compliance Officer
- Evaluation triggers: data residency, auditability, key controls.
- Key metrics: encryption at rest/in transit, audit log retention, isolation topology (VPC + Private).
- Talking points: Private deployments (VPC/on-prem), HSM/KMS integrations, per-request audit IDs, SOC2 and ISO evidence pointer.
- Demo prompts: audit log drilldown + show request header obfuscation and PII scrubbing options.

Section 2 — Price sensitivity test templates (method + scenarios)

Overview: We recommend running three price-sensitivity experiments during a pilot to calibrate required capacity and convert to a committed plan. The templates below are designed for customer pilots that will later become Dedicated or Private deployments.

Experiment setup checklist:
- Duration: 7–14 days per experiment
- Dataset: 5k representative user interactions (include extremes: long context, short prompts, multimodal if applicable)
- Metrics to capture: avg tokens/request, p50/p95 latency, cost per 1k tokens (hosted), end-to-end throughput
- Instrumentation: enable token-level billing metadata, route-level latency traces, and KV cache hit rate in Redwood Console

Pricing scenario matrix (table)
| Scenario | Description | Key thresholds | Recommended Redwood ask | Success criteria |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| Trial-Pilot | Low-volume proof (<= 5M tokens/month) | p95 < 500ms, cost < $0.02/1k tokens (hosted) | Free pilot credits + 2-week Dedicated trial | Evidence of consistent p95 and expected unit cost |
| Growth | Steady product usage (5M - 50M tokens/month) | p95 < 250ms, cost < $0.01/1k tokens | 6–12 month reserved capacity (Dedicated) with autoscale buffer | Unit economics within 15% of pilot projection |
| Enterprise-Commit | High volume + compliance (50M+ tokens/month or Private) | p95 < 150ms, contractual SLA 99.9% | Annual Reserved + Private deployment option and premium SLAs | Contract value aligns with expected TCO and cost-savings vs self-hosting |

Notes on negotiation levers
- Batching and caching profiles: quantify expected token reduction due to prefix caching (report cache hit rate).
- Model variants: provide cost deltas for top-performing 2–3 open models and a Redwood-optimized quantized variant.
- Commitment term: show 12 vs 36 month unit price curves and step-down discounts.

Section 3 — Proof points and compact case studies

Case study: FastReply (mid-market, conversational support)
- Problem: inconsistent SLA with prior LLM vendor, high per-session cost during peak spikes.
- What we did: migrated to Redwood Dedicated, enabled prefix caching for common support threads, and used Redwood Optimize recommendations to move to a 4-bit quantized variant for non-latency-critical routes.
- Result: 48% reduction in per-session cost, p95 latency improved from 820ms to 190ms, and successful canary rollout across 12k daily sessions.
- Materials: sample benchmarking report (redwood-fastreply-bench-2028.pdf).

Case study: FinAssist (regulated financial assistant)
- Problem: requirement for audit logs, KMS integration, and on-premise model hosting.
- What we did: deployed Redwood Private in customer VPC, integrated customer KMS with HSM-backed keys, and configured per-request audit IDs sent to the customer SIEM.
- Result: passed internal security review, met data residency requirements, and enabled product launch in an EU market.

Section 4 — Competitive brief (concise)

This section summarizes how to position Redwood against the three common counteroffers during technical evaluation. Keep this on a single page for deal rooms.

Competitor A: Large cloud provider managed endpoints
- Strengths: deep cloud integrations, global regions, brand trust.
- Redwood counter: lower unit cost for inference at scale via runtime batching + prefix/KV cache and transparent model-performance profiles; unified API across hosted/dedicated/private reduces migration friction.

Competitor B: LLM API pure-play (simple dev UX)
- Strengths: easy onboarding, strong docs, low initial friction.
- Redwood counter: comparable developer UX plus explicit production controls (SLOs, per-route cost tracking, rollout policies) and deployed options for regulated workloads.

Competitor C: Self-hosted inference frameworks
- Strengths: full control of stack and cost if large engineering org.
- Redwood counter: fewer ops overheads, predictable SLAs, Smart Routing to preserve uptime during capacity constraints, and cost-advantage analyses showing faster time-to-value.

Section 5 — Sales engineer conversation scripts and objection handling

Intro: below are short scripts to use live in a call. Keep them tight and metric-backed.

Script: latency concern
- Buyer: "We need <200ms p95 for chat."
- SE: "Understood. For comparable deployments we've delivered p95 <= 180ms using Dedicated pools with optimized kernels and continuous batching. On the pilot we instrument trace data and share a p95 baseline within 72 hours so you can validate. Would you prefer we size the Dedicated trial for peak concurrency or average load?"

Script: pricing pushback ("Your hosted price is higher than competitor")
- SE: "We often see higher apparent hosted cost if comparing raw per-token numbers. Two key differences: (1) our reported per-token cost accounts for throughput-optimizing features like KV cache and batch scheduling which reduce effective downstream cost; (2) we include guaranteed performance and rollback tooling that lower product risk. For fairness, let us run one of our sensitivity tests against your dataset and return a visible cost projection in <7 business days."

Section 6 — Security FAQ (concise answers for prospect diligence)

Q: Can customer keys be used for encryption at rest?
A: Yes. Redwood supports customer-managed keys via KMS (AWS/GCP/Azure) and HSM-backed key rotation. We provide a template integration runbook for Private deployments.

Q: How are audit logs provided?
A: Request-level audit logs with request IDs, model variant, and route metadata can be streamed to customer SIEM or stored in our encrypted audit store. Retention policies are configurable per customer.

Q: Is data used to train models?
A: No. For enterprise and Dedicated customers, data is not used to train public models. We maintain strict data-use contracts and provide an evidence package in the security bundle.

Q: What certifications do you hold?
A: Redwood maintains SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001. Private/Enterprise customers can request our compliance package via the Contracting team.

Section 7 — Live-demo checklist and commands

Pre-call (15 minutes)
- Verify API key scoped to demo org and route-level rate limits
- Upload small sample dataset (<= 200 prompts) for on-the-fly tests
- Configure a canary route and rollback policy in Redwood Console

Minimal curl to run a sample chat (replace API_KEY and MODEL_VAR):

curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer API_KEY" 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
  -d '{"model":"MODEL_VAR","prompt":"Summarize recent user messages","max_tokens":128}'

Post-demo deliverables
- Baseline telemetry report (p50/p95 latency, token counts, cache hit rate)
- Cost projection workbook (three scenarios from Section 2)
- Security evidence package link and next-step recommended architecture

Appendix: conversion checklist for pilots to committed deals
1) Capture baseline telemetry for pilot across peak windows
2) Validate quality regressions with Redwood Optimize prompt set testing
3) Agree on a clear success metric (SLA or cost-savings target)
4) Propose a 12-month reserved capacity term with a 10% holdback for first-month refunds if SLO missed

If you have questions or need a bespoke slide pack for an executive briefing, contact: go-to-market@redwood.ai or add a request to the 'SE resources' channel in Slack.

- Result: passed internal security review, met data residency requirements, and enabled product launch in an EU market.

Section 4 — Competitive brief (concise)

This section summarizes how to position Redwood against the three common counteroffers during technical evaluation. Keep this on a single page for deal rooms.

Competitor A: Large cloud provider managed endpoints
- Strengths: deep cloud integrations, global regions, brand trust.
- Redwood counter: lower unit cost for inference at scale via runtime batching + prefix/KV cache and transparent model-performance profiles; unified API across hosted/dedicated/private reduces migration friction.

Competitor B: LLM API pure-play (simple dev UX)
- Strengths: easy onboarding, strong docs, low initial friction.
- Redwood counter: comparable developer UX plus explicit production controls (SLOs, per-route cost tracking, rollout policies) and deployed options for regulated workloads.

Competitor C: Self-hosted inference frameworks
- Strengths: full control of stack and cost if large engineering org.
- Redwood counter: fewer ops overheads, predictable SLAs, Smart Routing to preserve uptime during capacity constraints, and cost-advantage analyses showing faster time-to-value.

Section 5 — Sales engineer conversation scripts and objection handling

Intro: below are short scripts to use live in a call. Keep them tight and metric-backed.

Script: latency concern
- Buyer: "We need <200ms p95 for chat."
- SE: "Understood. For comparable deployments we've delivered p95 <= 180ms using Dedicated pools with optimized kernels and continuous batching. On the pilot we instrument trace data and share a p95 baseline within 72 hours so you can validate. Would you prefer we size the Dedicated trial for peak concurrency or average load?"

Script: pricing pushback ("Your hosted price is higher than competitor")
- SE: "We often see higher apparent hosted cost if comparing raw per-token numbers. Two key differences: (1) our reported per-token cost accounts for throughput-optimizing features like KV cache and batch scheduling which reduce effective downstream cost; (2) we include guaranteed performance and rollback tooling that lower product risk. For fairness, let us run one of our sensitivity tests against your dataset and return a visible cost projection in <7 business days."

Section 6 — Security FAQ (concise answers for prospect diligence)

Q: Can customer keys be used for encryption at rest?
A: Yes. Redwood supports customer-managed keys via KMS (AWS/GCP/Azure) and HSM-backed key rotation. We provide a template integration runbook for Private deployments.

Q: How are audit logs provided?
A: Request-level audit logs with request IDs, model variant, and route metadata can be streamed to customer SIEM or stored in our encrypted audit store. Retention policies are configurable per customer.

Q: Is data used to train models?
A: No. For enterprise and Dedicated customers, data is not used to train public models. We maintain strict data-use contracts and provide an evidence package in the security bundle.

Q: What certifications do you hold?
A: Redwood maintains SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001. Private/Enterprise customers can request our compliance package via the Contracting team.

Section 7 — Live-demo checklist and commands

Pre-call (15 minutes)
- Verify API key scoped to demo org and route-level rate limits
- Upload small sample dataset (<= 200 prompts) for on-the-fly tests
- Configure a canary route and rollback policy in Redwood Console

Minimal curl to run a sample chat (replace API_KEY and MODEL_VAR):

curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer API_KEY" 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
  -d '{"model":"MODEL_VAR","prompt":"Summarize recent user messages","max_tokens":128}'

Post-demo deliverables
- Baseline telemetry report (p50/p95 latency, token counts, cache hit rate)
- Cost projection workbook (three scenarios from Section 2)
- Security evidence package link and next-step recommended architecture

Appendix: conversion checklist for pilots to committed deals
1) Capture baseline telemetry for pilot across peak windows
2) Validate quality regressions with Redwood Optimize prompt set testing
3) Agree on a clear success metric (SLA or cost-savings target)
4) Propose a 12-month reserved capacity term with a 10% holdback for first-month refunds if SLO missed

If you have questions or need a bespoke slide pack for an executive briefing, contact: go-to-market@redwood.ai or add a request to the 'SE resources' channel in Slack.

title: Rapid Authorization Containment and Evidence Orchestration Playbook
preview:
Overview:

Purpose: This playbook prescribes a fast, auditable, cross-functional process for containing authorization-related incidents (privilege escalation, token misuse, delegated-authorization failures), collecting compliance-grade evidence, and orchestrating multi-team handoffs while respecting data residency and retention constraints. It is intended for SREs, SecOps, on-call engineers, product security, and legal.

Scope: RBAC and auth incidents impacting production inference endpoints, control-plane configuration (roles, attribute stores, delegation), audit log integrity, or any event that may require regulatory evidence packaging (SOC2 / ISO / regional data residency). Excludes non-authorization incidents (use standard incident process).

Quick summary of goals:
- Contain active authorization exposure within 15 minutes for critical incidents.
- Secure and snapshot forensic evidence within 60 minutes of detection.
- Produce a packaged evidence bundle suitable for auditors and legal within 48 hours.

Ownership and roles:
1) Incident Lead (IL) — primary decision maker for containment and communications. Typical: SecOps on-call.
2) Forensic Engineer (FE) — executes log snapshots, chain-of-custody packaging, and ensures immutability. Typical: SRE/Platform engineer.
3) Auth Owner (AO) — owner of the affected auth system (RBAC, attribute service, token issuer).
4) Legal & Compliance Liaison (LCL) — evaluates cross-border evidence transfer and legal hold requirements.
5) Oncall Product Engineer (PE) — fixes product-side misconfigurations or rollbacks.

Escalation matrix (initial):
- P0: All-hands. IL pages Security Pager (SLA 15 min). Notify CTO/CDO.
- P1: SecOps & FE within 30 min. Notify Product and SRE leads.
- P2: Normal on-call review, follow standard SLA.

Detection and classification:
- Signals: entitlement-denied anomalies, sudden expansion of role bindings, long-lived token issuance spikes, delegation grant API errors, unusual authz decision latencies.
- Primary detectors: authz-decision-rate-monitor (alert if delta > 200% baseline), entitlement-delta-feed, token-issuer-threshold alarms, audit ingestion gaps.
- Triage categories: accidental misconfig (configuration drift), service compromise (exposed keys/service account), delegated-authorization exploit, audit-log tampering.

Immediate containment steps (IL / AO / FE) — target 15 minutes:
1) Isolate: if possible, toggle auth-policy read-only or switch to safe-mode routing (policy: global-fallback-deny). Example: 
   - CLI: redwoodctl auth policy set --mode read-only --scope production
2) Revoke tokens: invalidate suspicious tokens or rotate the token issuer signing key (if rotation safe given client constraints). Use short-lived revoke lists for federated tokens.
   - Example command: redwoodctl auth tokens revoke --selector "issuer:external,age<24h"
3) Rollback recent role bindings: list role-binding changes in last 30 minutes and revert approved commits.
   - Query example: SELECT * FROM entitlement_audit WHERE timestamp > now() - 30m AND action IN ('bind','grant')
4) Apply limiting firewall rules or rate limits for the affected service(s) to prevent broad exploitation.

Evidence collection checklist (FE) — target snapshot in 60 minutes:
1) Preserve audit streams: ensure no further ingestion overwrites the current partition. Mark current offsets and copy to immutable storage (S3 bucket: s3://redwood-forensics/immutable/YYYYMMDD/incident-id).
   - CLI example: forensicsctl snapshot-audit --stream authz-stream --to s3://redwood-forensics/immutable/2028-06-01/INC-12345 --note "initial snapshot"
2) Export entitlement change logs (RBAC bindings) with full context (actor, actor_metadata, change_request_id, before/after).
3) Capture system state: configs, running policy versions, signed kernel/agent versions, and relevant tracer IDs.
4) Collect ephemeral artifacts: process dumps only when authorized by LCL. Document chain-of-custody (who requested, who executed).
5) Record preservation metadata: timestamp (UTC), preserver identity, hashing algorithm (sha256), storage location, retention SLA label.

Chain-of-custody and evidence packaging:
- All collected artifacts must be SHA-256 hashed and stored in an immutable object store. The FE must record a manifest that includes: artifact name, SHA-256, timestamp, collector, and storage URI.
- Use evidence-bundler tool to produce a signed bundle: evidence-bundler assemble --manifest <manifest.json> --sign-with /keys/forensics-signing-key.pem --output INC-12345-bundle.tar.gz
- Attach all relevant audit partitions, entitlement CSVs, config diffs, and a narrative timeline (see Timeline section).

Data residency and cross-border considerations:
- Before exporting audit data across regions, consult LCL. By default, evidence stays in the originating residency region for 72 hours. Any transfer beyond that requires a documented justification and an encryption-at-rest policy using customer KMS where applicable.
- Retention mapping (example table):
  Resource Type | Default Retention | Evidence Access SLA | Residency constraint
  --------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------
  Auth audit logs | 365 days | 60 min snapshot | stored in originating region unless approved
  Entitlement diffs | 540 days | 24 hrs | mirrored for DR but restricted for PII
  Token issuance records | 90 days | 60 min | redact PII before cross-border transfer

Secure SDLC tie-ins (pre-incident hardening):
- All role-binding changes must flow through Git-backed policy repos (policy-as-code) with signed commits and CI checks including smoke tests and entitlement simulation.
- Release gates: any auth policy change targeting production must have canary and automated policy-simulated-test pass rate >= 99.5%.
- Mandatory code-signing for any binary that can modify auth tables.

Investigative timeline and documentation (IL / FE):
1) Create incident timeline doc entry (confluence path: /incidents/INC-12345) with the following sections: detection, initial containment, evidence snapshots, changes made, communications, and next steps.
2) Document all commands run, files exported, and identities of actors. Use the template below for the narrative section.

Template: Narrative timeline entry
- Detected at (UTC): 2028-06-01T10:32:00Z
- Detected by: authz-decision-rate-monitor rule #53
- Initial severity: P0 -> escalated to P0 at 10:40Z
- Containment actions: CLI commands issued (list), tokens revoked, snapshot URIs
- Evidence bundle URI: s3://redwood-forensics/immutable/2028-06-01/INC-12345/INC-12345-bundle.tar.gz

Regulatory packaging and evidence handoff (LCL / Legal):
- Within 48 hours prepare an evidence report including: incident narrative, signed evidence bundle, statement of residency (where each artifact is stored), redaction notes for PII, and certification of chain-of-custody.
- For GDPR or regional data controls, include a data-flow diagram and export justification.
- Legal may request additional retention hold; FE should create a retention-hold tag on the evidence object.

Risk and exceptions governance:
- If containment actions (e.g., rotating root signing key) will materially impact customer availability, IL must open an exception request using the Authorization-Exception form and route to the Authorization Board. Include risk justification, rollback plan, and timeframe.
- All exceptions must be time-limited (max 7 days for emergency escalations without Board approval).

Post-incident actions and lessons learned:
- Within 7 days: IL to coordinate a 90-minute post-incident review (PIR) with SRE, Auth Owner, Product, and Legal. Publish PIR findings and action items into the incident record.
- Within 30 days: complete remediation tickets (policy-as-code fixes, CI test additions, telemetry improvements).
- Update policy-as-code repos and create regression tests in eval-harness to detect similar failures.

Operational tooling and recipes (appendix):
- Useful CLI snippets:
  - List recent entitlement binds: redwoodctl db query --sql "SELECT * FROM entitlement_audit WHERE timestamp > now() - interval '1 hour' ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT 200"
  - Snapshot audit stream: forensicsctl snapshot-audit --stream authz-stream --partition current --to s3://...
- Evidence bundler usage: evidence-bundler assemble --manifest manifest.csv --sign-with /keys/forensics-signing-key.pem --output <bundle>.tar.gz

Metrics and SLOs to monitor post-deployment:
- Mean Time to Contain (MTTC) for auth incidents: target <= 15 minutes (P0).
- Mean Time to Evidence Snapshot: target <= 60 minutes.
- Evidence bundle completeness: percent of incidents with completed bundle within 48 hours: target >= 95%.

Related references and links:
- Policy-as-code repo: git@internal:redwood/policy-repo.git
- Audit schema spec: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-schema-versioning-and-evidence-tagging-2026
- RBAC change retention policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/entitlement-provenance-and-retention-framework-2026

Revision history:
- 2028-03-15: Drafted by Priya Natarajan
- 2028-04-02: Reviewed by Ethan Cole and Maria Alvarez, added cross-region residency notes
- 2028-06-01: Published (updated evidence bundler commands, clarified exception process)

End of playbook. For questions or simulation requests (chaos-testing of authorization controls) contact security-oncall@redwood.example.internal or Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.example.internal).

title: Commercial Dossier for Technical Partners — Pricing, Messaging, Case Studies, & Security FAQ
preview:
Summary
-------
This dossier is a single-page commercial reference for technical partners (system integrators, ISVs, and channel partners) who co-sell or embed Redwood Inference. It bundles: concise positioning and messaging tailored to technical buyers, a short competitive brief, partner-facing pricing scenarios, a turn-key pilot and onboarding checklist, an outline for a partner-facing pitch deck, two anonymized case narratives, and a curated security FAQ that frequently appears during partner diligence. Use this during joint sales calls, partner enablement sessions, and pilot scoping.

Quick takeaways
- Core value proposition for partners: deliver predictable, production-ready LLM inference to customers while minimizing ops burden and preserving cost controls.
- Primary partner win conditions: predictable latency, controllable unit economics, auditability for regulated data, and simple integration paths (API + SDKs + DevOps automations).
- Pilot success metric (joint): demonstrate a production integration that meets customer SLOs, shows >=25% cost improvement vs current serving approach (or clear path to), and obtains one logged business outcome (e.g., time saved per user, reduction in human review).

Positioning & Messaging (technical buyer focus)
------------------------------------------------
Suggested short pitches (30–60s):
- For Platform Engineers: "Redwood gives you a single inference runtime that you can run as a hosted API, reserved Dedicated pools, or a private VPC. You get consistent latency, predictable throughput, and the operational tooling to measure and control token costs—so you can ship LLM features without owning fleet complexity."
- For ML/Model Ops: "Redwood’s serving runtime manages batching, KV cache, and quantization profiles with transparent performance tradeoffs. You can pin model versions, run canaries, and detect quality regressions before rollout."
- For Security/Compliance stakeholders: "We provide tenant isolation on Dedicated, key management (KMS/HSM) integrations for Private deployments, and audit logs that map tokens and requests to customer IDs for compliance evidence."

Message bank (talking points to reuse in calls)
- Predictability: Transparent model performance profiles and per-token cost drivers.
- Portability: Same API and rollout semantics across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.
- Cost controls: Intelligent batching, prefix/KV caching, and quantization recommendations.
- Reliability: Automated routing and fallback to compatible variants to keep apps online.
- Observability: Per-model dashboards, token-level traces, and rollback hooks.

Competitive brief (concise)
-------------------------
Competitor archetypes to prepare for: managed cloud endpoint vendors, vertical LLM API providers, and in-house self-hosting.
- Managed cloud endpoints (large cloud provider): strengths: deep cloud integrations, software ecosystem; weaknesses vs Redwood: opaque inference economics, limited model catalog choices, and less prescriptive performance optimizations for LLM-specific runtimes.
- Vertical LLM API providers: strengths: domain-specific models, out-of-the-box prompts; weaknesses: limited deployment flexibility, poor observability for model regressions, harder to host on private infra.
- Self-host / Build-your-own: strengths: max control, potentially lower long-run cost at scale; weaknesses: high engineering overhead, slow iteration, and risk of regressions without evaluation harnesses.

Competitive rebuttal snippets
- If they say "we can self-host cheaper": "At small-to-medium scale we routinely see hidden ops and tuning costs; our runtime + tooling reduces total cost of ownership by reducing human tuning cycles and automating batching, caching, and quantization recommendations."
- If they ask about vendor lock-in: "We expose standard model artifacts and encourage pinned model versions. Customers can export model configs and workloads—our value is in serving economics and operational maturity, not opaque formats."

Pricing guidance for partner conversations
-----------------------------------------
Use these scenarios as conversation anchors; final pricing is customer-specific and should be approved by commercial. The numbers below are illustrative and reflect typical configurations used in partner pilots.

Pricing scenarios (illustrative)
| Scenario | Deployment | Typical monthly commit | Representative unit economics | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Small integration pilot | Hosted API | $2k–$10k | ~$3.50 per 1M input tokens; $7 per 1M output tokens | Short-term proof of concept, no infra changes |
| High-throughput SaaS feature | Dedicated (reserved) | $20k–$100k | Effective unit cost may fall by 3x vs hosted after autoscale and batching | Reserved GPU pools, long-running commit |
| Regulated enterprise | Private (VPC/On-prem) | $50k+ platform + support | Platform fee + infra; usage metered internally | Includes KMS integration and audit retention |

Up-sell levers to highlight to partners
- Dedicated capacity for predictable per-request latency and predictable unit economics.
- Observe-addons: extended retention for logs/traces, advanced evals retention, and custom quantization/benchmarking engagements.
- Professional services: model onboarding, prompt engineering, and reliability tuning.

Pilot & Integration Checklist (partner-facing, 8 steps)
-----------------------------------------------------
1) Pre-call: capture customer goals, throughput expectations (RPS), 95th pct latency SLO, and regulatory constraints (data residency, log retention).
2) Architecture sketch: agree on Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. Capture VPC peering, egress rules, and KMS needs.
3) Data/Prompt selection: partner and customer pick a canonical prompt set and 50–200 evaluation examples.
4) Instrumentation plan: turn on per-route tracing, token cost dashboards, and evaluation hooks.
5) Pilot contract: commit level, timebox (30–90 days), success metrics (latency, cost per 1k tokens, business outcome).
6) Onboarding: API keys, SDK integration (node/python), sample ingesters, and CI pipeline hook for rollout.
7) Run pilot: daily synthesis, weekly checkpoint with quant metrics, one mid-pilot rollback test.
8) Graduation: conduct handoff, agree SLA tier, and document routing/failover policies.

Suggested integration scripts and CLI snippets
- Quick SDK call (Node.js):
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.RW_API_KEY });
  const r = await client.generate({ model: 'rwd-small-1', prompt: 'Summarize customer ticket', maxTokens: 150 });
  // capture r.latency_ms and r.token_usage for pilot dashboard

Pitch deck outline (partner-facing)
----------------------------------
1) Title / joint logo slide (1)
2) Problem statement / customer pain (1)
3) Joint solution overview (technical architecture diagram) (1)
4) Live demo / telemetry screenshots (2)
5) Economics snapshots (illustrative pricing scenarios + cost savings) (1)
6) Security & compliance (KMS, audit, data residency) (1)
7) Pilot scope & success metrics (1)
8) Commercial terms & next steps (1)
Notes: prepare a single-slide appendix with model performance profiles (latency by seq length, throughput by GPU type) that can be shared with SRE teams.

Two short anonymized partner case narratives (use in sales narratives)
---------------------------------------------------------------
Case A — Enterprise knowledge base search (integrator + Redwood)
- Customer: Large financial services firm (anonymized).
- Challenge: Customer's in-house model endpoints had inconsistent latency under peak loads and opaque cost per query.
- Approach: Partner integrated Redwood Dedicated with autoscaling pools, enabled prefix caching for common queries, and pinned model version for canary testing.
- Results: 45% reduction in average token cost, 2x improvement in 95th percentile latency, and production rollout across two business units in 8 weeks.

Case B — Regulated healthcare triage assistant (Redwood + Partner)
- Customer: Regional health system required private deployment and HSM-backed keys.
- Challenge: Audit trails and patient-data isolation; model updates needed staged rollouts.
- Approach: Private deployment inside customer VPC, KMS-integrated keys, evaluation harness to detect QA regressions, and canary rollouts for model updates.
- Results: Met regulatory audit requirements, reduced manual triage by 28%, and achieved a signed 12-month Dedicated commitment.

Security FAQ (partner diligence edition)
--------------------------------------
Q: Where are customer inputs and outputs stored?
A: By default, Hosted API stores short-term operational logs and traces for 7 days. For Dedicated and Private, storage policies are controlled by the customer; we offer configurable retention windows and can disable retention during pilot if required.

Q: What encryption options do you support for keys?
A: We support cloud-provider KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS), and HSM-backed solutions for Private deployments. Keys can be customer-managed; Redwood supports envelope encryption for transport and at-rest storage where applicable.

Q: How do you provide audit evidence for compliance reviews?
A: We provide request-level audit logs containing timestamps, route/model, token counts, request IDs, and hashed user identifiers. For Private deployments we can ship logs to customer SIEMs or configure S3/GCS exports.

Q: How do you handle model quality regressions?
A: We tie model rollouts to evaluation suites and alert on data drift or regressed prompt sets. Rollouts default to canary (5–20%) then staged ramps, and we provide automated rollback hooks if regression metrics exceed thresholds.

Q: What are SLA/operational targets partners can promise customers?
A: Standard SLAs by deployment type are: Hosted — uptime 99.9% platform-wide, Dedicated — customizable SLA (typically 99.95% with committed capacity), Private — governed by deployment contract. Typical latency targets are negotiated by workload; example target for interactive text generation is 95th pct <200 ms for small responses on Dedicated.

Q: How is customer data isolated in multi-tenant environments?
A: Tenant isolation occurs at three layers: logical request routing and per-tenant cache namespace, workload isolation in Dedicated pools, and network isolation via VPC for Private. We also provide RBAC for Console and API key scopes.

Operational & escalation play (partner responsibilities)
-----------------------------------------------------
- Redwood responsibilities: runtime availability, maintenance windows scheduling, security patching for managed control plane, and providing production telemetry.
- Partner responsibilities: integration, onboarding scripts, customer success during pilot, and first-line support to the customer.
- Escalation path: 1) Partner SRE -> Redwood Partner SE (email/escalation) -> Redwood SRE on-call -> Engineering pager. Include joint runbook link in the partner contract.

Appendix: Suggested pilot SLA template (excerpt)
------------------------------------------------
- Pilot window: 60 days from onboarding completion.
- Availability target during pilot: 99.5% for Hosted pilots; Dedicated pilots inherit reserved capacity uptime.
- Success criteria: 95th percentile latency within 2x production goal, token unit economics within agreed variance, and at least one validated business outcome.

Appendix: Owner matrix
- Redwood lead: Partner Solutions Engineer (owner: product enablement)
- Partner lead: Partner success manager (owner: partner org)
- Commercial owner: Channel Sales Manager

How to use this dossier in the field
-----------------------------------
- Pre-call: load the Pitch deck outline and tailor the architecture slide.
- Kickoff: agree success metrics and instrument the evaluation harness.
- Weekly syncs: share token usage and latency charts; call out anomalies earlier than technical buyers expect.

Contact and next steps
----------------------
- Redwood Partner SEs: partner-se@redwood.ai (request enablement lab access)
- For commercial approval: channel-sales@redwood.ai
- To request a dedicated pilot configuration: pilot-config@redwood.ai

Related internal references
- See our internal "Platform adoption roadmap" for partner motion triggers and discount thresholds.
- See eng-private-deployments deployment guides for VPC and on-prem packaging details.

Document history
----------------
- Created by Priya Natarajan (go-to-market) 2027-03-02.
- Reviewed by Liam O'Connor and Maya Chen 2027-04-15.
- Updated with additional pricing scenarios and SLA excerpt 2027-06-12.
text:
Summary
-------
This dossier is a single-page commercial reference for technical partners (system integrators, ISVs, and channel partners) who co-sell or embed Redwood Inference. It bundles: concise positioning and messaging tailored to technical buyers, a short competitive brief, partner-facing pricing scenarios, a turn-key pilot and onboarding checklist, an outline for a partner-facing pitch deck, two anonymized case narratives, and a curated security FAQ that frequently appears during partner diligence. Use this during joint sales calls, partner enablement sessions, and pilot scoping.

Quick takeaways
- Core value proposition for partners: deliver predictable, production-ready LLM inference to customers while minimizing ops burden and preserving cost controls.
- Primary partner win conditions: predictable latency, controllable unit economics, auditability for regulated data, and simple integration paths (API + SDKs + DevOps automations).
- Pilot success metric (joint): demonstrate a production integration that meets customer SLOs, shows >=25% cost improvement vs current serving approach (or clear path to), and obtains one logged business outcome (e.g., time saved per user, reduction in human review).

Positioning & Messaging (technical buyer focus)
------------------------------------------------
Suggested short pitches (30–60s):
- For Platform Engineers: "Redwood gives you a single inference runtime that you can run as a hosted API, reserved Dedicated pools, or a private VPC. You get consistent latency, predictable throughput, and the operational tooling to measure and control token costs—so you can ship LLM features without owning fleet complexity."
- For ML/Model Ops: "Redwood’s serving runtime manages batching, KV cache, and quantization profiles with transparent performance tradeoffs. You can pin model versions, run canaries, and detect quality regressions before rollout."
- For Security/Compliance stakeholders: "We provide tenant isolation on Dedicated, key management (KMS/HSM) integrations for Private deployments, and audit logs that map tokens and requests to customer IDs for compliance evidence."

Message bank (talking points to reuse in calls)
- Predictability: Transparent model performance profiles and per-token cost drivers.
- Portability: Same API and rollout semantics across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.
- Cost controls: Intelligent batching, prefix/KV caching, and quantization recommendations.
- Reliability: Automated routing and fallback to compatible variants to keep apps online.
- Observability: Per-model dashboards, token-level traces, and rollback hooks.

Competitive brief (concise)
-------------------------
Competitor archetypes to prepare for: managed cloud endpoint vendors, vertical LLM API providers, and in-house self-hosting.
- Managed cloud endpoints (large cloud provider): strengths: deep cloud integrations, software ecosystem; weaknesses vs Redwood: opaque inference economics, limited model catalog choices, and less prescriptive performance optimizations for LLM-specific runtimes.
- Vertical LLM API providers: strengths: domain-specific models, out-of-the-box prompts; weaknesses: limited deployment flexibility, poor observability for model regressions, harder to host on private infra.
- Self-host / Build-your-own: strengths: max control, potentially lower long-run cost at scale; weaknesses: high engineering overhead, slow iteration, and risk of regressions without evaluation harnesses.

Competitive rebuttal snippets
- If they say "we can self-host cheaper": "At small-to-medium scale we routinely see hidden ops and tuning costs; our runtime + tooling reduces total cost of ownership by reducing human tuning cycles and automating batching, caching, and quantization recommendations."
- If they ask about vendor lock-in: "We expose standard model artifacts and encourage pinned model versions. Customers can export model configs and workloads—our value is in serving economics and operational maturity, not opaque formats."

Pricing guidance for partner conversations
-----------------------------------------
Use these scenarios as conversation anchors; final pricing is customer-specific and should be approved by commercial. The numbers below are illustrative and reflect typical configurations used in partner pilots.

Pricing scenarios (illustrative)
| Scenario | Deployment | Typical monthly commit | Representative unit economics | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Small integration pilot | Hosted API | $2k–$10k | ~$3.50 per 1M input tokens; $7 per 1M output tokens | Short-term proof of concept, no infra changes |
| High-throughput SaaS feature | Dedicated (reserved) | $20k–$100k | Effective unit cost may fall by 3x vs hosted after autoscale and batching | Reserved GPU pools, long-running commit |
| Regulated enterprise | Private (VPC/On-prem) | $50k+ platform + support | Platform fee + infra; usage metered internally | Includes KMS integration and audit retention |

Up-sell levers to highlight to partners
- Dedicated capacity for predictable per-request latency and predictable unit economics.
- Observe-addons: extended retention for logs/traces, advanced evals retention, and custom quantization/benchmarking engagements.
- Professional services: model onboarding, prompt engineering, and reliability tuning.

Pilot & Integration Checklist (partner-facing, 8 steps)
-----------------------------------------------------
1) Pre-call: capture customer goals, throughput expectations (RPS), 95th pct latency SLO, and regulatory constraints (data residency, log retention).
2) Architecture sketch: agree on Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. Capture VPC peering, egress rules, and KMS needs.
3) Data/Prompt selection: partner and customer pick a canonical prompt set and 50–200 evaluation examples.
4) Instrumentation plan: turn on per-route tracing, token cost dashboards, and evaluation hooks.
5) Pilot contract: commit level, timebox (30–90 days), success metrics (latency, cost per 1k tokens, business outcome).
6) Onboarding: API keys, SDK integration (node/python), sample ingesters, and CI pipeline hook for rollout.
7) Run pilot: daily synthesis, weekly checkpoint with quant metrics, one mid-pilot rollback test.
8) Graduation: conduct handoff, agree SLA tier, and document routing/failover policies.

Suggested integration scripts and CLI snippets
- Quick SDK call (Node.js):
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.RW_API_KEY });
  const r = await client.generate({ model: 'rwd-small-1', prompt: 'Summarize customer ticket', maxTokens: 150 });
  // capture r.latency_ms and r.token_usage for pilot dashboard

Pitch deck outline (partner-facing)
----------------------------------
1) Title / joint logo slide (1)
2) Problem statement / customer pain (1)
3) Joint solution overview (technical architecture diagram) (1)
4) Live demo / telemetry screenshots (2)
5) Economics snapshots (illustrative pricing scenarios + cost savings) (1)
6) Security & compliance (KMS, audit, data residency) (1)
7) Pilot scope & success metrics (1)
8) Commercial terms & next steps (1)
Notes: prepare a single-slide appendix with model performance profiles (latency by seq length, throughput by GPU type) that can be shared with SRE teams.

Two short anonymized partner case narratives (use in sales narratives)
---------------------------------------------------------------
Case A — Enterprise knowledge base search (integrator + Redwood)
- Customer: Large financial services firm (anonymized).
- Challenge: Customer's in-house model endpoints had inconsistent latency under peak loads and opaque cost per query.
- Approach: Partner integrated Redwood Dedicated with autoscaling pools, enabled prefix caching for common queries, and pinned model version for canary testing.
- Results: 45% reduction in average token cost, 2x improvement in 95th percentile latency, and production rollout across two business units in 8 weeks.

Case B — Regulated healthcare triage assistant (Redwood + Partner)
- Customer: Regional health system required private deployment and HSM-backed keys.
- Challenge: Audit trails and patient-data isolation; model updates needed staged rollouts.
- Approach: Private deployment inside customer VPC, KMS-integrated keys, evaluation harness to detect QA regressions, and canary rollouts for model updates.
- Results: Met regulatory audit requirements, reduced manual triage by 28%, and achieved a signed 12-month Dedicated commitment.

Security FAQ (partner diligence edition)
--------------------------------------
Q: Where are customer inputs and outputs stored?
A: By default, Hosted API stores short-term operational logs and traces for 7 days. For Dedicated and Private, storage policies are controlled by the customer; we offer configurable retention windows and can disable retention during pilot if required.

Q: What encryption options do you support for keys?
A: We support cloud-provider KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS), and HSM-backed solutions for Private deployments. Keys can be customer-managed; Redwood supports envelope encryption for transport and at-rest storage where applicable.

Typical latency targets are negotiated by workload; example target for interactive text generation is 95th pct <200 ms for small responses on Dedicated.

Q: How is customer data isolated in multi-tenant environments?
A: Tenant isolation occurs at three layers: logical request routing and per-tenant cache namespace, workload isolation in Dedicated pools, and network isolation via VPC for Private. We also provide RBAC for Console and API key scopes.

Operational & escalation play (partner responsibilities)
-----------------------------------------------------
- Redwood responsibilities: runtime availability, maintenance windows scheduling, security patching for managed control plane, and providing production telemetry.
- Partner responsibilities: integration, onboarding scripts, customer success during pilot, and first-line support to the customer.
- Escalation path: 1) Partner SRE -> Redwood Partner SE (email/escalation) -> Redwood SRE on-call -> Engineering pager. Include joint runbook link in the partner contract.

- Availability target during pilot: 99.5% for Hosted pilots; Dedicated pilots inherit reserved capacity uptime.
- Success criteria: 95th percentile latency within 2x production goal, token unit economics within agreed variance, and at least one validated business outcome.

- Partner lead: Partner success manager (owner: partner org)
- Commercial owner: Channel Sales Manager

-----------------------------------

title: Satellite Team Tool Access and Mentor Pairing Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose
This playbook defines the process, roles, SLAs, and artifacts required to onboard and support satellite teams, project rotations, and short-term contractors who require scoped access to Redwood systems. It pairs an access lifecycle with a mentor pairing program to accelerate impact while maintaining security and cost controls.

Scope
- Applies to any transient or temporary team event lasting between 2 days and 12 months: rotations, shadowing, pilot squads, contractor engagements, and hackweeks.
- Not intended for full-time hire onboarding (see new-hire orientation).
- Covers tool access (Okta, Redwood Console, GCP projects, Vault, Jira, GitHub org), mentor pairing, and deprovisioning.

Goals
- Reduce time-to-first-meaningful-commit for satellite contributors to under 2 business days for standard access and 4 hours for urgent business-critical cases.
- Ensure least-privilege with automated expiry and quarterly auditability.
- Provide explicit mentor ownership to reduce support load on platform teams.

Ownership and Roles
- Requestor: Team lead sponsoring the satellite access (manager or product owner). Responsible for initial justification and tagging in requests.
- Sponsor (Engineering or Product): Approves scope and duration.
- People Ops (Access Admin): Coordinates approvals, issues Okta group invites, and schedules mentor pairing if requested. SLA: 24 business hours for standard requests.
- Security (Reviewer): Performs risk review for requests with sensitive scope (see Risk matrix below). SLA: 48 business hours.
- Mentor: Senior engineer from host team assigned for 4–8 weeks. Responsible for onboarding, API walkthroughs, and weekly syncs.
- Platform Concierge (eng-platform): Executes provisioning where automation is not available and maintains the access templates.

Access Categories and Typical Entitlements
1) Read-only observer (no write access): Slack channels, Redwood Console read dashboards, project docs.
2) Scoped contributor (code + deployment but restricted): GitHub repo write, non-prod GCP project, test service tokens.
3) Production observer: Read-only access to production dashboards and logs; no deploy. Requires extra approval.
4) Production operator (limited): Deploy privileges for a single microservice / model-serving pool. Requires sponsor and security justification.

Risk Matrix (high level)
- Low risk: access limited to internal docs, non-prod GCP, staging Redwood Console. No Security review.
- Medium risk: write access to code repos, test infra, non-prod secrets. Requires security notification.
- High risk: production secrets, deploy rights, customer data. Requires security review and sponsor sign-off.

Requesting Access: Step-by-step
1. Create a Satellite Access Request in the Access Portal (link: /tools/access-portal). Use the template: 
   - Title: [Team] Satellite Access – <Short description>
   - Duration: select end date (maximum 12 months)
   - Scope: choose one or more presets: observer, scoped-contributor, prod-observer, prod-operator
   - Sponsor: add sponsor email
2. Sponsor approves the request in the portal (one click).
3. People Ops validates role mapping and assigns Okta groups within 24 business hours for standard requests. For urgent requests mark "fast-lane" and ping @access-concierge on Slack. SLA: 4 hours for approved fast-lane requests during business hours.
4. Platform Concierge provisions named resources using the appropriate Access Template (see templates table below).
5. Mentor pairing: Requestor selects mentor pairing option in the portal (recommended). People Ops assigns a mentor within 48 hours from the host-team mentor pool.

Onboarding Checklist (Mentor + Mentee) — complete within first 5 business days
1. Kickoff video call (30–45m): introductions, objectives, sprint goals, success metrics.
2. Walkthrough of the primary repo and CI/CD flow.
3. Provide developer sandbox links and test dataset pointers.
4. Demo Redwood Console route for the service(s) in scope.
5. Agree on communication channels and on-call handoffs (if any).
6. Set 1:1 cadence: weekly for first month, then biweekly.

Access Templates (table)

Name | Description | Example Entitlements | Default Duration | Responsible
--- | --- | --- | --- | ---
observer | Read-only access for short engagements | Slack read, Console dashboards (read), Docs | 30 days | People Ops
scoped-contributor | Code + non-prod infra access | GitHub write, non-prod GCP project, test Vault tokens | 90 days | Platform Concierge
prod-observer | Read-only prod monitoring | Grafana read, Sentry read, prod logs view | 30 days | People Ops + Security
prod-operator | Limited deploy rights to 1 service | Deploy token restricted to service X, audit logging enabled | 30 days | Sponsor + Security + Platform Concierge

(When copying into Access Portal, choose the template and apply additional constraints.)

Mentor Pairing Program Details
- Purpose: Rapid context transfer, guardrails education, and human escalation path.
- Commitment: Mentor expected to spend 4–6 hours/week for first 4 weeks, then 2 hours/week for the following 4 weeks. Expectation set in mentor roster.
- Mentor compensation: 2 hours/week of mentoring flagged as "time goodwill" and can be logged to team capacity planning.
- Metrics: mentee time-to-first-PR, number of support tickets opened to infra teams, and mentor satisfaction survey after 6 weeks.

Escalation and Support
- First-line: Mentor and sponsor. For access failures, ping #access-ops (Slack) with request ID.
- Second-line: Platform Concierge (oncall rotation listed on the internal roster).
- Security incidents: Immediately email sec-incident@redwood.ai and create incident ticket referencing the request ID.

Automated Deprovisioning and Renewal
- All satellite access is issued with an expiration date. Access Portal will notify requestor and sponsor 7 days before expiry.
- Auto-renewal: requestor can submit renewal up to 14 days before expiry. Renewals require sponsor re-approval. Max cumulative duration is 12 months.
- Early deprovisioning: Sponsor or People Ops can close a request; provisioning automation will revoke tokens and remove Okta group membership within 1 hour of approval.

Audit and Compliance Requirements
- Quarterly audit: People Ops will run an access report for all active satellite grants and send to Security for review.
- Retention: access request records are retained for 3 years.
- Proof of least-privilege: for any prod or high-risk grant, the sponsor must provide a short justification and acceptance of additional monitoring.

Example CLI snippets and YAML for scoped-contributor request (paste into Access Portal advanced field)

---yaml
requester: jane.doe@redwood.ai
team: Data Science – Project Nimbus
scope: scoped-contributor
repos:
  - github.com/redwood/example-repo
nonprod_project: gcp-nimbus-dev
duration_days: 60
mentor_requested: true
---

Quick Troubleshooting (common issues)
- Missing GitHub repo permissions: confirm org membership; if the repo is private, ensure the repo slug is correct.
- GCP project not visible: verify billing account mapping and requestor's email domain.
- Vault tokens failing: rotate token via the Access Portal and confirm environment variable names.

KPIs and Success Criteria
- Primary KPIs: time-to-first-PR, percentage of requests fulfilled within SLA, mentor program NPS.
- Target: 90% of standard requests fulfilled within 24 business hours; mentor NPS >= 8/10.

Offboarding and Lessons Learned
- Offboarding checklist (automated): revoke Okta groups, disable deploy tokens, remove from Slack-private channels, archive any ephemeral GCP projects, and and mark request closed.
- Post-mortem: for engagements > 3 months, mentor must submit a short 1-page summary with wins, blockers, and suggested improvements. This gets added to the Access Portal record.

Appendix: Useful links and templates
- Access Portal: /tools/access-portal
- Mentor roster and sign-up: /people/mentor-roster
- Access templates (raw): /eng-platform/templates/access-templates.yaml
- Emergency contact: security oncall: +1-555-0123 (internal only)

Frequently Asked Questions
- Q: Can a contractor be given permanent access if the engagement extends beyond 12 months?
  A: No — convert to full-time hire or re-issue a new request with full sponsorship and review.
- Q: Who pays for additional GCP costs created by a satellite project?
  A: The sponsoring team covers costs. Chargeback tags are required at provisioning time.

Change Log
- 2027-03-15: Playbook created (Priya Natarajan).
- 2027-05-10: Added fast-lane SLA and mentor compensation guidance (reviewers: Marcus Lee).
- 2027-06-02: Tightened renewal rules and clarified audit cadence (Security input).

- 2027-03-15: Playbook created (Priya Natarajan).
- 2027-05-10: Added fast-lane SLA and mentor compensation guidance (reviewers: Marcus Lee).
- 2027-06-02: Tightened renewal rules and clarified audit cadence (Security input).

title: Enterprise fidelity surcharge and grace period policy
preview:
Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference's enterprise fidelity surcharge policy and associated grace period rules. Its goal is to provide a predictable commercial mechanism for covering incremental cost when customers request fidelity guarantees (higher-cost model variants, pinned quantization levels, or reserved warm capacity) while giving a clear transition window for customers migrating across fidelity bands.

Summary (one paragraph)
Enterprise customers may opt into fidelity guarantees for mission-critical routes. When a customer selects a fidelity guarantee that increases runtime cost materially (as defined in the Surcharge Thresholds table), a fidelity surcharge may be applied to their invoice. Customers receive a configurable grace period during onboarding or migration where surcharges are delayed, partly discounted, or phased in to reduce churn and provide time to optimize usage.

Scope
- Applies to: Enterprise and Dedicated customers with contract value >= $50k ARR or explicit SLO add-on contracts.
- Excludes: Self-serve hosted customers on public free/trial tiers unless explicitly included in a signed addendum.
- Applies to: per-route fidelity guarantees, reserved warm pool attachments, and quantization lock requests.

Definitions
1. Fidelity guarantee: any contractual commitment to maintain a specific model variant, quantization level, KV cache hit rate, or reserved capacity that increases serving cost relative to a baseline.
2. Baseline consumption: the customer’s prior month weighted token-average cost using default (non-guaranteed) variants.
3. Surchargeable delta: the portion of incremental unit cost above the Surcharge Threshold that is eligible for surcharge.

Policy details
- Surcharge Thresholds (decision rule): If a fidelity change increases estimated unit cost by >= 12% relative to the baseline, the difference above 12% becomes potential surchargeable delta.
- Surcharge rate: default 75% of surchargeable delta for the first 12 months of a contract; negotiable in enterprise agreements.
- Minimum invoice impact: surcharge will not be applied if projected monthly surcharge < $500 without explicit customer opt-in.

Grace period model (three modes)
1. Ramp-in (default): a linear phase from 0% to full surcharge over the chosen grace window (30/60/90 days). Example: 90-day window applies 33% of the surcharge in month 1, 66% in month 2, 100% in month 3.
2. Discounted delay: customer pays 0% for the grace window, then full surcharge on day (grace_end + 1). Reserved for strategic customers.
3. Hybrid (customer-engineered): negotiated mix of partial immediate charge + shorter ramp.

SLA and time-of-effect rules
- Effective date: fidelity surcharge is effective on the date the fidelity guarantee transitions to an enforced state (e.g., model variant pinned on production).
- Retroactivity: no retroactive surcharges for changes that occurred prior to executed contract amendment.
- Auto-revert: if the customer rolls back fidelity within the grace window, any invoiced surcharge during that window will be credited in the next invoice.

Pricing examples (illustrative)
| Scenario | Baseline unit cost | Guaranteed unit cost | Delta (%) | Surchargeable Delta | Applied surcharge (monthly) | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| A: small change | $0.0008 | $0.0009 | 12.5% | 0.5% unit | $0 (below minimum) | No surcharge applied by min-invoice rule |
| B: moderate change | $0.0008 | $0.0010 | 25% | 13% unit | $1,170 | 75% of 13% * consumed tokens (example 12M tokens) |
| C: reserved warm pool | $0.0012 | $0.0019 | 58% | 46% unit | $8,280 | Large enterprise reserved capacity example |

Operational playbook (ops + sales + billing)
1. Sales signals: During contract negotiation, AE flags required fidelity guarantees in deal desk form and chooses default grace window (30/60/90).
2. Solutioning: SE produces a technical fidelity impact estimate (template below) and attaches to the quote.
3. Legal/Finance: include fidelity surcharge clause in contract appendix B with agreed surcharge rate and grace mode.
4. Provisioning: On activation, product-platform tags the customer routes with metadata {fidelity: guaranteed, surcharge_rate: X, grace_window_days: Y}.
5. Billing: The billing pipeline evaluates the tagged routes each billing cycle, computes estimated surchargeable delta, applies ramp logic, and emits provisional invoice lines with the suffix: "Fidelity Surcharge (provisional)".
6. Communication: Automated email to billing contact on first provisional surcharge and final invoice.
7. Dispute handling: If customer disputes within 21 days, mark invoice line as disputed and halt collection while dispute is triaged.

Technical fidelity impact estimate (SE template)
- Inputs required from SE: route(s), baseline model variant, proposed guarantee (model/quantization/warm pool), expected hourly qps, expected session/profile length, measured token distribution if available.
- Output fields: estimated % unit cost increase, recommended grace_window (30/60/90), estimated monthly surcharge at given consumption bands (Low/Medium/High).

Example SE estimate (YAML snippet)
---
route: /v1/chat/interactive
baseline_variant: rwd-lite-1
proposed_variant: rwd-pico-4q
qps_estimate: 12
sessions_per_minute: 4
avg_tokens_per_session: 260
estimated_unit_cost_baseline: 0.00085
estimated_unit_cost_guaranteed: 0.00140
estimated_delta_percent: 64.7
recommended_grace_window_days: 60
monthly_surcharge_low: $1,200
monthly_surcharge_medium: $5,600
monthly_surcharge_high: $14,500
---

Billing integration rules (implementation notes)
- Billing system will receive a dedicated metadata feed from the platform tagging per-route fidelity attributes. Fields required: customer_id, route_id, fidelity_tag, effective_date, grace_window_days, surcharge_rate.
- On each billing cycle compute: adjusted_unit_cost = baseline_unit_cost + max(0, (guaranteed_unit_cost - baseline_unit_cost) - threshold_delta). surcharge_line = adjusted_unit_cost * surcharge_rate * consumed_tokens * ramp_factor.
- Ramp factor = linear interpolation based on days since effective_date / grace_window_days for Ramp-in mode.

Customer communications (templates)
- First provisional: Subject: "Provisional fidelity surcharge applied to your account" Body: short explanation, expected monthly impact, link to console cost insights and how to opt-out.
- Final invoice: Subject: "Final invoice: fidelity surcharge details" Body: line-itemized surcharge table, link to dispute form.

Monitoring and KPIs
- Adoption rate: % of enterprise customers with fidelity guarantees (goal: 12% by EOY).
- Average surcharge per customer and median monthly surcharge.
- Dispute rate: % of surcharge invoices disputed (goal: <3%).
- Churn delta: monthly churn rate for customers with new fidelity surcharges vs matched controls.
- Time-to-optimization: median days from surcharge start to measured 10% reduction in surcharge via batching/quantization changes.

Metrics feed and dashboards
- Events: fidelity_guarantee.added, fidelity_guarantee.activated, fidelity_guarantee.reverted, fidelity_surcharge.invoiced, fidelity_surcharge.credited.
- Dashboards: Pricing -> Fidelity Surcharge Summary (per-customer, monthly), Billing -> Dispute Tracker, Product -> Time-to-optimization heatmap.

Customer experience and mitigations
- Provide a one-click downgrade path in Console to revert fidelity guarantees (with confirm and billing preview).
- Suggest optimization playbook automatically (top 3 levers: batch window, quantization profile, prefix cache tuning).
- Offer a one-time credit equal to 25% of first-month surcharge for customers who complete a joint optimization workshop with Solutions Engineering within the grace window.

Rollout plan (90 days)
1. Week 0–2: Finalize legal clause and billing metadata contract. Run deal desk training.
2. Week 3–6: Implement billing metadata API and ramp-factor computation in billing pipeline (staging).
3. Week 7–9: QA + finance signoff, dry-run invoices for a sample cohort.
4. Week 10–12: GA to new enterprise deals, automated customer communications enabled.

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: High dispute volume increases A/R workload. Mitigation: pre-billing preview emails, clear console cost previews, and AE/CS preparedness training.
- Risk: Surprise cost for customers. Mitigation: require explicit customer acceptance in contract—no surprise billing for default self-serve customers.
- Risk: Engineering backlog for metadata feed. Mitigation: scoped MVP: route-level tags only, expand to variant-level in Q2.

Ownership and contacts
- Product owner: Priya Natarajan (product).
- Billing engineering owner: Aisha Patel (finance-legal & eng).
- Solutions engineering contact: Connor Li (go-to-market).
- Legal reviewer: Maria Svensson (finance-legal).

Related docs and appendices
- Appendix A: Contract clause language (copy/paste ready)
- Appendix B: SE estimation spreadsheet template (CSV)
- Appendix C: Billing schema (proto) and sample messages

Appendix A (contract language snippet)
"Customers electing fidelity guarantees that result in an incremental unit price above Redwood Inference's Surcharge Threshold may be subject to a fidelity surcharge as described in Appendix B. Surjective application of such surcharge will be subject to the agreed grace window and dispute rights set forth in the SOW."

Next steps
- Gather legal feedback by 2027-03-31.
- Complete billing API implementation by 2027-04-15 (staging).
- Run pilot with 3 enterprise customers during April 2027.

Document change log
- 2027-03-20: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2027-03-28: Added ramp model and technical SE template after review with Solutions Engineering.
- 2027-04-01: Incorporated billing pipeline notes and reviewer feedback.
