title: SLA-aware Admission Controller and Backpressure Playbook
preview:
Summary:

This playbook defines the SLA-aware Admission Controller and Backpressure strategy for Redwood's serving runtime. It describes the design, configuration, operational runbook, and recommended testing/benchmarking steps to ensure tenant-level SLOs are respected under mixed workloads and overload scenarios. The playbook targets runtime-admission (service), kernel-scheduler (service), and dispatcher components and is intended for on-call, runtime engineers, and SREs.

Goals and scope:

- Ensure per-tenant and per-route latency SLOs are prioritized when system capacity is constrained.
- Provide deterministic admission behavior that is auditable and testable.
- Define backpressure signals exposed to clients and internal services.
- Offer a practical operational runbook for detecting, mitigating, and rolling back admission policy changes.

Out of scope:

- Model-level quality regressions (handled by Optimize/evals).
- Long-term capacity provisioning (handled by capacity-planning team).

Background and motivation:

As traffic patterns diversify and multi-tenant traffic mixes (short-chat, long-generation, embeddings), the runtime must protect defined SLOs (p95/p99 for latency) for high-priority tenants. Simple FIFO queues allow noisy tenants to consume disproportionate kernel slots and drive SLO violations. An admission controller that incorporates SLA buckets, dynamic scoring, and token budgets prevents this while enabling graceful degradation for best-effort traffic.

Design overview (high level):

1) Components
- runtime-admission: front-line HTTP/gRPC gateway that accepts requests, validates routing, and evaluates admission score.
- kernel-scheduler: allocates kernel slots and issues low-level backpressure when GPU resources are saturated.
- dispatcher: routes admitted requests to worker shards and observes queueing metrics.

2) Signals and inputs to admission decision
- Tenant SLA tier (platinum/gold/silver/best-effort).
- Real-time queue depth and queue wait time (per-tenant).
- Recent kernel eviction/timeout rates and per-shard utilization.
- Token budget consumption (per-tenant per-window).
- Experiment/canary overrides (for rollout testing).

3) Outcome actions
- Admit immediately and assign priority metadata.
- Defer (hold in bounded waiting queue with estimated wait).
- Reject with structured payload (429 with retry-after and fallback model suggestion).
- Route to best-effort fallback pool (quantized/low-cost variant).

Admission algorithm (practical rules):

- Each tenant has a configured SLA bucket: {tier, p95_ms_target, burst_tokens_per_minute, steady_tokens_per_minute}. Example tiers: platinum (p95 120ms), gold (p95 200ms), silver (p95 500ms), best-effort (no p95 SLO).

- Score calculation (simplified):
  score = (tier_weight / (1 + queue_wait_ratio)) * available_token_ratio - penalty(kernel_eviction_rate)
  where values are normalized to [0,1]. The admission threshold is configurable per-service.

- Token budgets are refilled on a leaky-bucket; requests consume budget proportional to estimated token cost (sequence length * model_cost_factor).

Backpressure signals and API:

- kernel-scheduler exposes gRPC stream /v1/backpressure with messages: {shard_id, congestion_level, suggested_drain_ms, affected_models}.
- runtime-admission subscribes and translates congestion_level to per-tenant queue caps and admission thresholds.
- HTTP response header additions for observability and client behavior: X-Redwood-Admission: admitted|deferred|rejected, X-Redwood-Estimated-Wait: <ms>, X-Redwood-Policy-Id: <id>.

SLOs and thresholds (examples):

- Default per-tenant p95 targets (example): platinum 120ms, gold 200ms, silver 500ms.
- Maximum admission queue depth per shard: 512 requests.
- Defer-to-reject pivot: if average estimated wait > 2 * p95_target for tier AND kernel_eviction_rate > 0.05, switch to reject for best-effort.

Configuration examples (YAML):

# admission-policy.yaml
policy:
  name: sla-aware-default
  tiers:
    - name: platinum
      p95_ms: 120
      tier_weight: 1.0
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 60
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 20
    - name: gold
      p95_ms: 200
      tier_weight: 0.7
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 40
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 12
    - name: silver
      p95_ms: 500
      tier_weight: 0.4
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 20
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 6
    - name: best-effort
      p95_ms: 0
      tier_weight: 0.1
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 10
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 2

# kernel backpressure thresholds
kernel:
  congestion_levels:
    - level: 0
      util_pct: 0-70
    - level: 1
      util_pct: 71-85
    - level: 2
      util_pct: 86-95
    - level: 3
      util_pct: 96-100
  level_actions:
    0: no_action
    1: tighten_admission_by: 10%
    2: tighten_admission_by: 25%
    3: reject_non_critical: true

Operational runbook (oncall) - overload detection:

1) Detect: Alerts to pager when aggregated kernel_utilization > 92% for > 60s OR p95 per-tenant latency > configured p95_target for two consecutive 30s windows.
2) Triage: Use dashboards: Runtime -> Admission -> Per-tenant queue depth, Kernel -> shard_utilization and eviction_rate, Dispatcher -> per-route latency heatmap.
3) Short-term mitigation (fast, <5m):
   a) Increase admission strictness: curl -XPOST http://runtime-admission/internal/policy/tighten -d '{"factor":0.25}'
   b) Apply emergency routing to fallback pool: kubectl patch svc dispatcher -p '{"spec": {"selector": {"pool":"fallback"}}}' (use only for best-effort heavy traffic).
   c) If single tenant causing noise, temporarily throttle via: POST /internal/tenants/{id}/throttle with {"rate": "5 rps"}.
4) Medium-term mitigation (5-30m):
   a) Temporarily increase worker replicas for fallback pool if capacity available.
   b) Engage capacity-planning if average utilization remains > 85% for > 30m.
5) Communication: Post status on #runtime-ops with subject [Admission Overload] and include quick metrics snapshot: top 5 tenants by queue depth, kernel_eviction_rate, affected models.

Runbook: admission policy rollback and canary steps:

- Canary rollout matrix: 1% traffic for 15m, observe p95/p99 and kernel_eviction_rate. If no regression, progress to 10% for 30m, then 50% for 60m, then full.
- Rollback command: POST /internal/policy/activate with previous_policy_id or kubectl apply -f admission-policy-previous.yaml.
- Safety gates automated: If p95 for any platinum tenant degrades by >20% vs baseline or kernel_eviction_rate > 3%, automatically rollback to last stable policy.

Diagnostics and telemetry to collect (minimum):

- Per-request: tenant_id, route_id, estimated_token_cost, admission_outcome, estimated_wait_ms, kernel_shard_id.
- Aggregates: per-tenant queue depth, average wait, admitted rate, rejected rate, kernel_eviction_rate per shard.
- Traces: end-to-end trace from runtime-admission to kernel-scheduler with 5% sampling for normal, 50% for overload.

Testing and benchmarking plan:

- Synthetic scenarios in the perf harness: mix of short (<=64 tokens) chat, medium (256 tokens) generation, long (>=1024 tokens) generations, and embedding streams.
- Run 6h soak tests with 70/20/10 mix (short/medium/long) at 1.5x expected peak to validate admission fairness and SLO preservation.
- Validate canary using shadow traffic: compare admitted outcomes and latency deltas against baseline.

Example troubleshooting checklist (quick):

- If many deferred requests but low admitted latency: inspect token budgets and refill rates.
- If kernel_eviction_rate high: check large-sequence tenants and apply temporary model-variant routing.
- If single shard hot: verify shard affinity and consider rebalance (see runtime-shard-placement playbook).

Ownership and escalation:

- Primary owner: runtime-scheduling team (oncall: runtime-sched-rotations).
- Secondary: eng-sre.
- For capacity issues escalate to capacity-planning lead.

Compatibility notes / Release considerations:

- Admission changes are backward compatible for clients; runtime will continue to return structured 429 responses.
- When introducing new header fields (X-Redwood-Estimated-Wait), coordinate with SDK team and document in SDK changelog.

Related artifacts and links:
- Perf harness job: perf/sla-admission-harness (internal CI)
- Telemetry dashboard: /dashboards/runtime-admission-sla
- Kernel backpressure gRPC proto: proto/runtime/backpressure.proto

Appendix: sample 429 response payload (JSON)

{
  \"error\": \"capacity_exceeded\",
  \"reason\": \"platinum-sla-protect\",
  \"retry_after_seconds\": 8,
  \"suggested_fallback\": \"gpt4-small-quantized\"
}
Notes and future work:

- Research item: incorporate model-level estimated compute (measured in flops or kernel cycles) into token budget calculation.
- Explore reinforcement learning approach for dynamic admission thresholds.

Document history:
- 2027-02-14 created by Priya Natarajan
- 2027-03-01 updated: clarified backpressure API and added automatic safety rollback gate.

- Token budget consumption (per-tenant per-window).
- Experiment/canary overrides (for rollout testing).

3) Outcome actions
- Admit immediately and assign priority metadata.
- Defer (hold in bounded waiting queue with estimated wait).
- Reject with structured payload (429 with retry-after and fallback model suggestion).
- Route to best-effort fallback pool (quantized/low-cost variant).

Admission algorithm (practical rules):

- Each tenant has a configured SLA bucket: {tier, p95_ms_target, burst_tokens_per_minute, steady_tokens_per_minute}. Example tiers: platinum (p95 120ms), gold (p95 200ms), silver (p95 500ms), best-effort (no p95 SLO).

- Score calculation (simplified):
  score = (tier_weight / (1 + queue_wait_ratio)) * available_token_ratio - penalty(kernel_eviction_rate)
  where values are normalized to [0,1]. The admission threshold is configurable per-service.

- Token budgets are refilled on a leaky-bucket; requests consume budget proportional to estimated token cost (sequence length * model_cost_factor).

Backpressure signals and API:

- kernel-scheduler exposes gRPC stream /v1/backpressure with messages: {shard_id, congestion_level, suggested_drain_ms, affected_models}.
- runtime-admission subscribes and translates congestion_level to per-tenant queue caps and admission thresholds.
- HTTP response header additions for observability and client behavior: X-Redwood-Admission: admitted|deferred|rejected, X-Redwood-Estimated-Wait: <ms>, X-Redwood-Policy-Id: <id>.

SLOs and thresholds (examples):

- Default per-tenant p95 targets (example): platinum 120ms, gold 200ms, silver 500ms.
- Maximum admission queue depth per shard: 512 requests.
- Defer-to-reject pivot: if average estimated wait > 2 * p95_target for tier AND kernel_eviction_rate > 0.05, switch to reject for best-effort.

Configuration examples (YAML):

# admission-policy.yaml
policy:
  name: sla-aware-default
  tiers:
    - name: platinum
      p95_ms: 120
      tier_weight: 1.0
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 60
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 20
    - name: gold
      p95_ms: 200
      tier_weight: 0.7
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 40
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 12
    - name: silver
      p95_ms: 500
      tier_weight: 0.4
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 20
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 6
    - name: best-effort
      p95_ms: 0
      tier_weight: 0.1
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 10
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 2

# kernel backpressure thresholds
kernel:
  congestion_levels:
    - level: 0
      util_pct: 0-70
    - level: 1
      util_pct: 71-85
    - level: 2
      util_pct: 86-95
    - level: 3
      util_pct: 96-100
  level_actions:
    0: no_action
    1: tighten_admission_by: 10%
    2: tighten_admission_by: 25%
    3: reject_non_critical: true

Operational runbook (oncall) - overload detection:

1) Detect: Alerts to pager when aggregated kernel_utilization > 92% for > 60s OR p95 per-tenant latency > configured p95_target for two consecutive 30s windows.
2) Triage: Use dashboards: Runtime -> Admission -> Per-tenant queue depth, Kernel -> shard_utilization and eviction_rate, Dispatcher -> per-route latency heatmap.
3) Short-term mitigation (fast, <5m):
   a) Increase admission strictness: curl -XPOST http://runtime-admission/internal/policy/tighten -d '{"factor":0.25}'
   b) Apply emergency routing to fallback pool: kubectl patch svc dispatcher -p '{"spec": {"selector": {"pool":"fallback"}}}' (use only for best-effort heavy traffic).
   c) If single tenant causing noise, temporarily throttle via: POST /internal/tenants/{id}/throttle with {"rate": "5 rps"}.
4) Medium-term mitigation (5-30m):
   a) Temporarily increase worker replicas for fallback pool if capacity available.
   b) Engage capacity-planning if average utilization remains > 85% for > 30m.
5) Communication: Post status on #runtime-ops with subject [Admission Overload] and include quick metrics snapshot: top 5 tenants by queue depth, kernel_eviction_rate, affected models.

Runbook: admission policy rollback and canary steps:

- Canary rollout matrix: 1% traffic for 15m, observe p95/p99 and kernel_eviction_rate. If no regression, progress to 10% for 30m, then 50% for 60m, then full.
- Rollback command: POST /internal/policy/activate with previous_policy_id or kubectl apply -f admission-policy-previous.yaml.
- Safety gates automated: If p95 for any platinum tenant degrades by >20% vs baseline or kernel_eviction_rate > 3%, automatically rollback to last stable policy.

Diagnostics and telemetry to collect (minimum):

- Per-request: tenant_id, route_id, estimated_token_cost, admission_outcome, estimated_wait_ms, kernel_shard_id.
- Aggregates: per-tenant queue depth, average wait, admitted rate, rejected rate, kernel_eviction_rate per shard.
- Traces: end-to-end trace from runtime-admission to kernel-scheduler with 5% sampling for normal, 50% for overload.

Testing and benchmarking plan:

- Synthetic scenarios in the perf harness: mix of short (<=64 tokens) chat, medium (256 tokens) generation, long (>=1024 tokens) generations, and embedding streams.
- Run 6h soak tests with 70/20/10 mix (short/medium/long) at 1.5x expected peak to validate admission fairness and SLO preservation.
- Validate canary using shadow traffic: compare admitted outcomes and latency deltas against baseline.

Example troubleshooting checklist (quick):

- If many deferred requests but low admitted latency: inspect token budgets and refill rates.
- If kernel_eviction_rate high: check large-sequence tenants and apply temporary model-variant routing.
- If single shard hot: verify shard affinity and consider rebalance (see runtime-shard-placement playbook).

Ownership and escalation:

- Primary owner: runtime-scheduling team (oncall: runtime-sched-rotations).
- Secondary: eng-sre.
- For capacity issues escalate to capacity-planning lead.

Compatibility notes / Release considerations:

- Admission changes are backward compatible for clients; runtime will continue to return structured 429 responses.
- When introducing new header fields (X-Redwood-Estimated-Wait), coordinate with SDK team and document in SDK changelog.

Related artifacts and links:
- Perf harness job: perf/sla-admission-harness (internal CI)
- Telemetry dashboard: /dashboards/runtime-admission-sla
- Kernel backpressure gRPC proto: proto/runtime/backpressure.proto

Appendix: sample 429 response payload (JSON)

{
  \"error\": \"capacity_exceeded\",
  \"reason\": \"platinum-sla-protect\",
  \"retry_after_seconds\": 8,
  \"suggested_fallback\": \"gpt4-small-quantized\"
}
Notes and future work:

- Research item: incorporate model-level estimated compute (measured in flops or kernel cycles) into token budget calculation.
- Explore reinforcement learning approach for dynamic admission thresholds.

Document history:
- 2027-02-14 created by Priya Natarajan
- 2027-03-01 updated: clarified backpressure API and added automatic safety rollback gate.

title: Typed ID and Opaque Identifier Patterns for Inference APIs
preview:
Overview:
This document specifies recommended patterns for representing identifiers (IDs) in Redwood Inference services and SDKs. The goal is to increase type safety, reduce accidental cross-tenant or cross-resource ID mixing, make telemetry more explicit, and enable safer migrations between identifier formats. These recommendations apply to public-facing inference APIs, internal service-to-service RPCs, and developer SDKs.

Why this matters:
- Simple untyped strings lead to accidental misuse (e.g., passing a model_id where a deployment_id is required).
- Observable signals and logs lose structure when different ID classes are conflated.
- Security and auditability improve when IDs are clearly typed and opaque where appropriate.
- Backwards-incompatible ID shape changes (e.g., adding versioning prefixes) are safer when wrapped by a typed adapter.

Scope:
- Applies to all new APIs and SDK surfaces (HTTP/REST, streaming, gRPC, and SDK clients).
- Does not mandate retrofitting every existing endpoint; see Migration section for gradual rollout guidance.
- Focuses on identifier representation, validation, logging, and transformation patterns.

Terminology:
- ID class: logical category of identifier (model_id, deployment_id, tenant_id, prompt_set_id, eval_id, request_id).
- Opaque ID: a stable, non-semantic string produced by the system (e.g., rwd-mdl_3f2d1a).
- Typed ID wrapper: a language-level construct (class/type/struct) that encapsulates an opaque ID and provides validation/serialization logic.

Principles:
1) Use explicit typed wrappers in SDKs and strongly-typed services:
   - Language clients (TypeScript, Go, Rust) must expose typed ID types for commonly used ID classes.
   - Example: ModelID, DeploymentID, TenantID.

2) Keep wire format stable and simple:
   - On the wire use a human-readable but opaque string (no JSON objects for IDs).
   - Wire examples: "rwd-tn_9b2c", "rwd-mdl_1a8f-v2".

3) Provide deterministic parsing and strict validation at service boundaries: 
   - Validate ID prefix and checksum where used; reject unknown formats with 4xx and a clear error code (INVALID_ID_FORMAT).

4) Avoid leaking internal semantics:
   - Do not encode internal regions, zones, or hardware-level affinity in user-visible IDs. Use separate metadata if needed.

5) Telemetry and logs should annotate ID class and sensitivity:
   - Use structured fields: id.class=model id.value=(redacted)|id.hash=sha256_prefix.
   - Do not store full PII or long-lived secrets alongside IDs.

Pattern recommendations (by surface):
A. Public REST/gRPC APIs (server-side):
- Accept IDs as strings in the HTTP/gRPC schema but perform server-side validation into typed domain objects immediately on request ingress.
- Return 400 with structured error when validation fails.
- Example error payload: 
  {
    "error_code": "INVALID_ID_FORMAT",
    "id_class": "deployment_id",
    "received": "zzz-123"
  }

B. SDKs (TypeScript/Go/Python):
- Expose typed constructors and factory helpers that validate and hold the opaque string.
- Implement toString()/Serialize methods that yield the wire format.

TypeScript example:
```ts
// packages/sdk/src/types/ids.ts
export class ModelID {
  private constructor(private readonly id: string) {}

  static from(id: string): ModelID {
    if (!/^rwd-mdl_[0-9a-f]{6}(?:-v[0-9]+)?$/.test(id)) {
      throw new Error('Invalid ModelID format');
    }
    return new ModelID(id);
  }

  toString(): string { return this.id; }
}
```

Go example:
```go
// sdk/ids.go
type DeploymentID string

func NewDeploymentID(s string) (DeploymentID, error) {
    if !deploymentIDRegex.MatchString(s) {
        return \"\", fmt.Errorf(\"invalid deployment id\")
    }
    return DeploymentID(s), nil
}
func (d DeploymentID) String() string { return string(d) }
```

Python example (best-effort for dynamic languages):
```py
# sdk/ids.py
import re

class TenantID(str):
    _re = re.compile(r"^rwd-tn_[0-9a-z]{6}$")
    @classmethod
    def validate(cls, s: str):
        if not cls._re.match(s):
            raise ValueError("invalid tenant id")
        return cls(s)

# Usage: tid = TenantID.validate("rwd-tn_9b2cde")
```

C. Service-to-service RPCs and internal models:
- Prefer strongly-typed protobuf messages that use string fields but map to typed domain objects in service handlers.
- Avoid introducing complex, nested ID objects in the public proto surface. Keep mapping logic in the server layer to avoid coupling clients.

D. Request-level ephemeral IDs (trace/request ids):
- Keep request IDs as separate ephemeral ids (rwd-req_xxx). Do not overload user IDs. Use them for tracing and correlate with user/tenant IDs in service logs.

ID Versioning and Migration:
- Add a version marker in the ID string when the format meaningfully changes (suffix or prefix), e.g., rwd-mdl_1a8f-v2.
- Services should accept older version formats for at least 6 months and log deprecation telemetry.
- Migration plan for a new ID format:
  1) Design new format and add server-side acceptance logic behind a feature flag.
  2) Update internal SDK factories to emit the new format for newly created resources.
  3) Enable gradual producer rollout (canary, 10%, 50%, 100%).
  4) After 6 months, emit deprecation warnings when old-format IDs are used; after 12 months, consider rejecting old formats (requires product/GO approval).

Logging and Observability guidance:
- Do not log full plain IDs in high-cardinality metrics. Use redaction and hashing: store id.hash=sha256(id)[:8] for dashboards and metrics.
- When debugging incidents, run a gated tool that can expand hashes to actual IDs using secure audit logs (access-controlled).
- Label telemetry with id.class to support slicing in dashboards (e.g., model vs deployment).

Security and privacy considerations:
- Consider marking certain ID classes as sensitive (customer-managed keys, secret-annotated resources) and redact them in unrestricted logs.
- For customer-hosted private deployments, the private control plane may use different ID namespaces; always normalize when ingesting into the public telemetry pipeline.

Linting and tests:
- Add static lint rules in SDK repos to forbid raw-string usage where a typed ID exists. Example ESLint rule: no-raw-modelid.
- Unit tests: each ID class should include negative and positive tests for accepted formats, serialization round-trip, and invalid-input errors.
- Integration tests: service e2e tests must verify request validation and error payloads for malformed IDs.

Migration checklist (recommended for service owners):
1) Inventory endpoints that accept or return each ID class.
2) Add typed adapters at ingress/egress points.
3) Update SDKs with typed constructors and mark old factory helpers as deprecated.
4) Add telemetry for id.format and id.version.
5) Run canary rollouts and monitor error rates related to INVALID_ID_FORMAT.
6) Communicate SDK changes in the public changelog and migration guide.

Operational ownership and rollout owners:
- Ownership: infra-api-platform (primary), runtime-team (secondary) for runtime-related ID affinity.
- SDK changes: devexp-sdk-team (primary).
- Security review: security-compliance (required for any ID format that exposes internal metadata).

Related patterns and references:
- See: \"request-provenance-and-enrichment-standard\" for telemetry correlation patterns.
- See: \"model-artifact-provenance-and-signing-standard\" for artifact naming and provenance (IDs separate from artifact blobs).

Examples and FAQs:
Q: Why not use UUIDs everywhere?
A: UUIDs are acceptable for randomness and uniqueness, but human-readable opaque prefixes (rwd-mdl_) help debugging and telemetry classification without leaking internal routing or region info.

Q: Can IDs contain forward slashes or JSON meta?
A: No. Keep IDs URL-safe and ASCII. Avoid JSON or delimiter-packed payloads that complicate parsing.

Q: How should web consoles display IDs?
A: Display the full ID in restricted UI elements (two-step reveal) and display a short hash in lists to reduce exposure.

Rollout timeline recommendation:
- Month 0–1: Publish this standard and add linting rules to SDK repos.
- Month 1–3: Implement typed adapters at service ingress points and ship SDK factories.
- Month 3–6: Gradual rollout of typed IDs in new resource creation flows; monitor INVALID_ID_FORMAT and client error rates.
- Month 6+: Deprecation notices for old factories; begin planning stricter enforcement if adoption is high.

Appendix: Quick ID format table:
| ID class | Example wire form | Recommended wrapper | Notes |
|----------|------------------:|---------------------|------|
| tenant_id | rwd-tn_9b2cde | TenantID (str validator) | Short-lived in some internal systems |
| model_id | rwd-mdl_1a8f | ModelID | May include -vN suffix for shape version |
| deployment_id | rwd-dpl_77a4 | DeploymentID | Keep distinct from model_id |
| request_id | rwd-req_5e9a | RequestID (ephemeral) | Used for tracing only |

Contact and feedback:
- Primary author: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.ai)
- Office hours: weekly API guild meeting (Wednesdays 10:00 PT)
- For questions: file a ticket in #api-standards or open a short RFC in the architecture repo.

title: Private deploy networking bridge and fallback patterns
preview:
Overview

This document describes recommended networking bridge and fallback patterns for Redwood Private (VPC and on‑prem) deployments. It focuses on field-safe connectivity designs that preserve control‑plane reachability, allow safe model hot‑swaps, and provide deterministic failure modes when network paths are constrained (air‑gapped customers, intermittent interconnects, or restricted egress environments).

Audience

- Deployment engineers executing customer installs
- Field operations and SRE teams responsible for day‑2 networking
- Solutions engineering and customer success for pre‑sales readiness checks

Scope and goals

- Provide a small set of vetted bridge and fallback patterns that balance security, observability, and reliability.
- Document rollout and rollback procedures for network modifications without requiring full service restarts.
- List concrete customer requirements and acceptance criteria for connectivity.
- Include example configs, iptables/systemd snippets, and monitoring queries.

Patterns summary (high level)

1) Local bridge + control-plane proxy (recommended for constrained egress)
- A lightweight sidecar proxy (redwood-bridge-proxy) runs in customer VPC/on‑prem to relay control plane and telemetry over a single allowed outbound connection.
- Strongly preferred when customers allow only outbound TLS over port 443.
- Supports transparent retry and multiplexing of control messages.

2) Mutual TLS (mTLS) site-to-site tunnel with ephemeral keys
- Strongly recommended for customers who allow inbound VPN-like tunnels from a fixed IP range.
- Use ephemeral certificates rotated by the operator every 24–72 hours.
- Falls back to pattern 1 when site-to-site connectivity flaps.

3) Queued store-and-forward bridge for air-gapped segments
- Useful for batch workloads where strict real-time control is optional.
- Control plane messages are queued to an operator-managed handoff node; large model artifacts are staged via signed bundles on removable media or a dedicated transfer appliance.

4) Sidecar proxy with local KV-cache mirroring for model hot-swap safety
- Maintains a local LRU prefix/KV cache copy to support short-term inference traffic during transient control-plane outages.
- Cache TTLs and size are operator-configurable and critical to avoid stale state.

Architecture (text diagram)

Customer VPC / On‑Prem Network
  +-----------------------------------------------------------+
  | Tenant nodes (inference)  -- redwood-sidecar -- redwood-bridge-proxy |
  |    |
  |    +--> Local KV cache (redis-lite)
  |
  |  (WAN / Interconnect) <--- mTLS tunnel or TLS egress to Redwood control plane
  +-----------------------------------------------------------+

SLOs, thresholds and failure modes

Recommended SLOs
- Control plane heartbeat: 99.9% of 10s heartbeats succeed per 30d window.
- Model management commands (deploy/undeploy): median completion < 45s when control plane reachable.
- Telemetry upload: 95% of agent metrics delivered within 5 minutes.

Key thresholds and operator actions
- If heartbeat drops below 99.0% for 10 minutes: open P0 to field ops and trigger local cache scale-up.
- If control plane unavailable for > 30 minutes and queued job backlog exceeds 5k requests: enable degraded mode (reject new long-running requests and surface a 503 to upstream).

Pre‑installation customer requirements matrix

| Requirement | Minimum | Preferred | Why it matters |
|------------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Allowlist IPs | outbound to 52.14.32.0/24 | outbound to dedicated operator pool | control plane connectivity |
| Ports | 443 outbound TLS | 443 outbound, 7843 for mTLS | single-port simplicity vs tunnel performance |
| MTU | 1400 | 9000 supported | large model artifact transfer reliability |
| Internal DNS | resolvable hostnames for control-plane endpoints | private DNS forwarding | certificate SAN and SNI consistency |
| KMS integration | local keywrap supported | HSM/KMS with rotation | customer encryption-at-rest requirements |
| Latency to operator endpoints | < 150ms | < 50ms | model deploy and healthchecks responsiveness |
| Egress policy | allow outbound to operator endpoints | allow mutual tunnels | feature enablement (telemetry, live rollouts) |

Configuration table (examples)

| Component | Example setting | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| redwood-bridge-proxy | --egress-host=control.redwood.ai:443 --cert-rotate=72h | single process for egress with auto rotation |
| sidecar-cache | max-entries=100000 ttl=2m | protect memory while providing prefix cache for transient disconnects |
| healthcheck | /healthz interval=10s timeout=3s | quick failure detection and automatic local fallback |

Note: above table rendered here as monospace YAML example below.

Example redwood-bridge-proxy systemd unit (recommended)

[Unit]
Description=Redwood Bridge Proxy
After=network-online.target

[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/redwood-bridge-proxy --egress-host=control.redwood.ai:443 --log-level=info --cert-rotate=72h
Restart=on-failure
LimitNOFILE=65536

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

iptables minimal outbound allowlist example

# allow outbound HTTPS to operator pool
iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -d 52.14.32.0/24 --dport 443 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
# allow local loopback
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT
# drop everything else by default (customer may use more sophisticated rules)
iptables -A OUTPUT -j DROP

Model artifact transfer checklist (airgap-friendly)

- Ensure artifact bundles are signed with operator key and timestamped.
- Validate SHA256 checksum on receiving appliance.
- Verify compat matrix: model version -> runtime kernel build (see reference-architectures page).
- If MTU < 1400, enable chunked transfer and resume support.

Deployment steps: safe networking change (step-by-step)

1) Preflight (field engineer)
  a) Run connectivity-check.sh against operator endpoints (script below).
  b) Confirm DNS resolution and TLS validation with openssl s_client.
  c) Validate firewall rules with tcpdump and simulated egress.
2) Enable bridge proxy in monitor-only mode
  a) Deploy sidecar with --dry-run to observe connection attempts.
  b) Confirm proxy establishes TLS to control plane but does not accept control commands.
3) Switch control plane routing to bridge (gradual)
  a) Promote bridge proxy to active for 10% of traffic via traffic selector.
  b) Monitor heartbeats and control command latency for 15 minutes.
4) Full cutover
  a) Promote bridge for 100% traffic.
  b) Keep original path for 30 minutes as cold fallback.
5) Post validation
  a) Run synthetic inference tests (100 small requests, record latency and error rate).
  b) Verify telemetry ingestion and disk watermark for local cache.

Rollback procedure (networking changes)

1) If errors exceed thresholds (>=5% failure rate or control commands > 3x median latency) for 10 minutes, perform immediate rollback:
  a) Revert routing at traffic selector to previous path.
  b) If routing cannot be reverted, enable degraded mode and surface clear customer-facing error.
2) Postmortem steps
  a) Collect tcpdump and proxy logs from affected nodes.
  b) Escalate to networking SME and open internal incident.

Upgrade procedure (bridge proxy)

- Rolling upgrade (preferred):
  1) Mark one rack/node group for upgrade.
  2) Deploy new proxy image with canary config (monitor-only).
  3) Promote to active for 5% traffic for 10m.
  4) If metrics OK, proceed in waves (25%, 50%, 100%) with 15m observation windows.
- If proxy upgrade requires config change that is not backward compatible, perform co‑existence test and schedule maintenance window.

Day‑2 operations: monitoring, alerts, and runbook links

Essential metrics to export
- bridge_proxy.connection_success_rate (1m/5m/30m)
- bridge_proxy.cert_age_seconds
- sidecar_cache.hit_rate and eviction_rate
- controlplane.command_latency_p50/p95/p99
- telemetry.uplink_backlog_count

Suggested alerts (examples)
- P0: bridge_proxy.connection_success_rate < 95% for 5m -> page oncall SRE and field ops
- P1: controlplane.command_latency_p95 > 120s for 15m -> notify platform eng
- P2: sidecar_cache.eviction_rate > 0.5 for 10m -> capacity increase recommended

Runbook excerpts (quick actions)
- Recover proxy TLS: restart redwood-bridge-proxy service, check /var/log/redwood/bridge.log, rotate cert if expired.
- Rehydrate local cache: scale sidecar-cache up by 2x for 30 minutes and monitor eviction_rate.

Instrumentation examples (PromQL)
- bridge connection success rate (1m): sum(rate(bridge_proxy_success_total[1m])) / sum(rate(bridge_proxy_attempt_total[1m]))
- control command p95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(control_command_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le))

Security and compliance notes

- All operator certificates must be logged and auditable; TF plan and inventory should be stored in customer-provided S3 or equivalent with ACLs.
- On air-gapped installs, use signed artifact manifests and enforce signature verification during consumption.
- KMS-integrated deployments must demonstrate key-rotation test prior to GA signoff.

Ownership and escalation

- Deployment owner: eng-private-deployments field team (Primary: Marco Alvarez).
- Networking SME: Diego Ramos.
- Oncall SRE: see /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotations for current duty.
- Customer success escalation: contact mapped CSM for contract-specific SLAs before any service-affecting change.

Appendices

A) connectivity-check.sh (example)
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
ENDPOINT=control.redwood.ai:443
echo "Resolving $ENDPOINT"
host=${ENDPOINT%:*}
port=${ENDPOINT#*:}
if ! getent hosts $host >/dev/null; then
  echo "DNS failure for $host"; exit 2
fi
openssl s_client -connect ${host}:${port} -servername ${host} -brief < /dev/null 2>&1 | sed -n '1,20p'
exit 0

B) Example minimal sidecar config (YAML)
component: sidecar-cache
settings:
  max_entries: 100000
  ttl_seconds: 120
  disk_persist: true
  persist_path: /var/lib/redwood/cache

References and related pages
- Edge gateway and airgapped bridge architecture (reference page)
- Private deploy onboarding and failover runbook
- Onprem packaging: container signing and verification playbook

Document maintenance
- Owners should review this page quarterly or whenever operator egress policy changes.
- Major updates must be coordinated with security and customer success (confidentiality: restricted).
text:
Overview

This document describes recommended networking bridge and fallback patterns for Redwood Private (VPC and on‑prem) deployments. It focuses on field-safe connectivity designs that preserve control‑plane reachability, allow safe model hot‑swaps, and provide deterministic failure modes when network paths are constrained (air‑gapped customers, intermittent interconnects, or restricted egress environments).

Audience

- Deployment engineers executing customer installs
- Field operations and SRE teams responsible for day‑2 networking
- Solutions engineering and customer success for pre‑sales readiness checks

Scope and goals

- Provide a small set of vetted bridge and fallback patterns that balance security, observability, and reliability.
- Document rollout and rollback procedures for network modifications without requiring full service restarts.
- List concrete customer requirements and acceptance criteria for connectivity.
- Include example configs, iptables/systemd snippets, and monitoring queries.

Patterns summary (high level)

1) Local bridge + control-plane proxy (recommended for constrained egress)
- A lightweight sidecar proxy (redwood-bridge-proxy) runs in customer VPC/on‑prem to relay control plane and telemetry over a single allowed outbound connection.
- Strongly preferred when customers allow only outbound TLS over port 443.
- Supports transparent retry and multiplexing of control messages.

2) Mutual TLS (mTLS) site-to-site tunnel with ephemeral keys
- Strongly recommended for customers who allow inbound VPN-like tunnels from a fixed IP range.
- Use ephemeral certificates rotated by the operator every 24–72 hours.
- Falls back to pattern 1 when site-to-site connectivity flaps.

3) Queued store-and-forward bridge for air-gapped segments
- Useful for batch workloads where strict real-time control is optional.
- Control plane messages are queued to an operator-managed handoff node; large model artifacts are staged via signed bundles on removable media or a dedicated transfer appliance.

4) Sidecar proxy with local KV-cache mirroring for model hot-swap safety
- Maintains a local LRU prefix/KV cache copy to support short-term inference traffic during transient control-plane outages.
- Cache TTLs and size are operator-configurable and critical to avoid stale state.

Architecture (text diagram)

Customer VPC / On‑Prem Network
  +-----------------------------------------------------------+
  | Tenant nodes (inference)  -- redwood-sidecar -- redwood-bridge-proxy |
  |    |
  |    +--> Local KV cache (redis-lite)
  |
  |  (WAN / Interconnect) <--- mTLS tunnel or TLS egress to Redwood control plane
  +-----------------------------------------------------------+

SLOs, thresholds and failure modes

Recommended SLOs
- Control plane heartbeat: 99.9% of 10s heartbeats succeed per 30d window.
- Model management commands (deploy/undeploy): median completion < 45s when control plane reachable.
- Telemetry upload: 95% of agent metrics delivered within 5 minutes.

Key thresholds and operator actions
- If heartbeat drops below 99.0% for 10 minutes: open P0 to field ops and trigger local cache scale-up.
- If control plane unavailable for > 30 minutes and queued job backlog exceeds 5k requests: enable degraded mode (reject new long-running requests and surface a 503 to upstream).

Pre‑installation customer requirements matrix

| Requirement | Minimum | Preferred | Why it matters |
|------------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Allowlist IPs | outbound to 52.14.32.0/24 | outbound to dedicated operator pool | control plane connectivity |
| Ports | 443 outbound TLS | 443 outbound, 7843 for mTLS | single-port simplicity vs tunnel performance |
| MTU | 1400 | 9000 supported | large model artifact transfer reliability |
| Internal DNS | resolvable hostnames for control-plane endpoints | private DNS forwarding | certificate SAN and SNI consistency |
| KMS integration | local keywrap supported | HSM/KMS with rotation | customer encryption-at-rest requirements |
| Latency to operator endpoints | < 150ms | < 50ms | model deploy and healthchecks responsiveness |
| Egress policy | allow outbound to operator endpoints | allow mutual tunnels | feature enablement (telemetry, live rollouts) |

Configuration table (examples)

| Component | Example setting | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| redwood-bridge-proxy | --egress-host=control.redwood.ai:443 --cert-rotate=72h | single process for egress with auto rotation |
| sidecar-cache | max-entries=100000 ttl=2m | protect memory while providing prefix cache for transient disconnects |
| healthcheck | /healthz interval=10s timeout=3s | quick failure detection and automatic local fallback |

Note: above table rendered here as monospace YAML example below.

Example redwood-bridge-proxy systemd unit (recommended)

[Unit]
Description=Redwood Bridge Proxy
After=network-online.target

[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/redwood-bridge-proxy --egress-host=control.redwood.ai:443 --log-level=info --cert-rotate=72h
Restart=on-failure
LimitNOFILE=65536

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

iptables minimal outbound allowlist example

# allow outbound HTTPS to operator pool
iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -d 52.14.32.0/24 --dport 443 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
# allow local loopback
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT
# drop everything else by default (customer may use more sophisticated rules)
iptables -A OUTPUT -j DROP

Model artifact transfer checklist (airgap-friendly)

- Ensure artifact bundles are signed with operator key and timestamped.
- Validate SHA256 checksum on receiving appliance.
- Verify compat matrix: model version -> runtime kernel build (see reference-architectures page).
- If MTU < 1400, enable chunked transfer and resume support.

1) Preflight (field engineer)

2) Enable bridge proxy in monitor-only mode

  b) Keep original path for 30 minutes as cold fallback.
5) Post validation
  a) Run synthetic inference tests (100 small requests, record latency and error rate).
  b) Verify telemetry ingestion and disk watermark for local cache.

Rollback procedure (networking changes)

1) If errors exceed thresholds (>=5% failure rate or control commands > 3x median latency) for 10 minutes, perform immediate rollback:
  a) Revert routing at traffic selector to previous path.
  b) If routing cannot be reverted, enable degraded mode and surface clear customer-facing error.
2) Postmortem steps
  a) Collect tcpdump and proxy logs from affected nodes.
  b) Escalate to networking SME and open internal incident.

Upgrade procedure (bridge proxy)

- Rolling upgrade (preferred):
  1) Mark one rack/node group for upgrade.
  2) Deploy new proxy image with canary config (monitor-only).
  3) Promote to active for 5% traffic for 10m.
  4) If metrics OK, proceed in waves (25%, 50%, 100%) with 15m observation windows.
- If proxy upgrade requires config change that is not backward compatible, perform co‑existence test and schedule maintenance window.

- KMS-integrated deployments must demonstrate key-rotation test prior to GA signoff.

Ownership and escalation

- Oncall SRE: see /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotations for current duty.

title: Real-time SLO Reallocation and Hot-Path Capacity Procedure
preview:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes an operational pattern and step-by-step procedure for re-allocating SLO budget and rapidly provisioning or shifting capacity to hot execution paths ("hot-path" services) during acute traffic events that threaten customer-facing SLOs. It combines manual escalation, deterministic automation gates, and verification checks so that SREs and paged responders can act quickly with low risk.

Scope and motivation
--------------------
- Applies to Redwood services that touch real-time inference and request-routing: inference-gateway, request-router, kv-cache, continuous-batcher, model-worker, and capacity-allocator.
- Use when an SLO is projecting to miss within the current burn window due to sudden load, model regression, or regional capacity loss.
- Goal: restore SLO headroom to a safe state within 10–30 minutes for high-severity incidents while preserving user isolation and avoiding cascading failures.

Key concepts and definitions
----------------------------
- Hot-path: service components in the per-request low-latency stack (inference-gateway, kv-cache, continuous-batcher).
- SLOs considered: latency_p95, latency_p99, availability (percent success rate), and embedding-throughput.
- Error budget: rolling 30-day budget unless otherwise stated.
- Burn rate thresholds: Normal (<3x), Elevated (3–8x), Emergency (>8x). Automation gates and actions differ by band.

Primary owners and on-call responsibilities
-------------------------------------------
- SLO steward: service owner (example: inference-gateway -> @inference-platform).
- On-call: eng-sre pagers for the service and platform-sre for capacity/cluster operations.
- Automation owner: runtime-ops (capacity-allocator, scheduler).
- Escalation: follow the team’s on-call escalation matrix; primary SRE must notify product-resp only for customer-facing SLA risk.

Observability and signals to monitor
-----------------------------------
- Prometheus alerts (examples):
  - slo_burn_rate_30d > 3 for service="inference-gateway" (Severity: P2)
  - slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes < 60 (Severity: P1)
  - avg_p95_latency{service="inference-gateway",region="*"} > 200ms (Severity: P1)
  - available_gpu_units < 10% (Severity: P1)

- Helpful PromQL (examples):
  - SLO burn rate (30d):
    sum(increase(slo_errors_total[30d])) / sum(increase(slo_requests_total[30d])) / (target_error_fraction)
  - p95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, service))

Decision matrix (summary)
-------------------------
| Signal | Burn rate band | Immediate action | Automatable? | Owner | Time objective |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---:|
| SLO projection < 60m | Emergency (>8x) | Trigger emergency capacity workflow: force-burst reservations, cross-region shift, traffic shed lower-priority tenants | Semi-automated (requires human confirm) | on-call + platform-sre | 10–30m |
| p95 latency increase > 2x | Elevated (3–8x) | Increase batch concurrency for low-impact models; enable KV aggressive cache promotion | Automatable with canary | service owner | 5–15m |
| GPU scarcity (<10%) | Any | Initiate rapid GPU reclamation & evict non-critical workloads | Automated with guardrails | capacity-allocator | 5–20m |

(See full table in "Actions and thresholds" section below.)

Safe automation gates
---------------------
All automated actions must satisfy the following preconditions before execution:
1) Observability confirm: two independent signals (metrics + tracing slowdown) show the same impact.
2) Capacity sanity: min_headroom >= 5% post-action for primary region or a validated cross-region fallback exists.
3) Rollback path: automation must be reversible within 2 minutes (e.g., scale-down, revoke reservation).
4) Approval bit: for Emergency band, automation must pause and require a single on-call confirmation via PagerDuty UI or CLI token.

Actions and thresholds (detailed)
--------------------------------
1) Emergency (Burn > 8x or projection < 60m)
   - Owner: on-call (primary) + platform-sre (secondary)
   - Steps:
     a) Post incident marker: create incident in incident tracker (CLI example below).
     b) Force reserve 20% extra capacity from capacity-allocator: CLI -> capacity-allocator reserve --service inference-gateway --units 0.2 --duration 60m
     c) Redirect 30% of new traffic to read-only model variant or smaller model via request-router dynamic-route: request-router set-weight --route inference-prod --variant small-model --weight 0.3
     d) If regional GPU shortage: trigger cross-region redistribution: scheduler relocate --from eu-west --to us-west --max 50pods
     e) If headroom cannot be recovered within 30m: execute tenant-priority shedding (see table below)
   - Verification: p95 latency reduces to <1.5x baseline and slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes > 240

2) Elevated (Burn 3–8x)
   - Owner: service owner + on-call
   - Steps:
     a) Enable aggressive KV caching: kv-cache promote --policy aggressive --target inference-gateway
     b) Increase batcher concurrency for long-running models: continuous-batcher set-concurrency --service model-worker --delta +2
     c) Start gradual model-quality fallback for non-critical tenants: model-worker set-fallback --tenants tenant-list.yaml
   - Verification: sustained p95 improvement within 10 minutes and error budget burn-rate reduces below 3x.

3) Normal (Burn <3x but trending upward)
   - Owner: service owner
   - Steps:
     a) Tune dynamic batching windows: continuous-batcher set-window --target 5ms
     b) Warm key prefixes into KV cache for anticipated request patterns.
     c) Open a short-lived ticket to follow up on root cause.

Tenant priority shedding table
------------------------------
(Used only as last-resort in Emergency band. Always apply least-impact-first.)
| Priority | Action | Max allowed reduction |
| High (SLA customers) | No shedding unless explicit legal-approved escalation | 0% |
| Medium | Disable model variants >2x cost per token; limit tokens per request to 75% | up to 30% |
| Low | Enforce strict rate-limits, reject low-value batched jobs | up to 70% |

Operational commands and examples
----------------------------------
- Create incident: redwood-ops incident create --title "Hot-path latency surge" --services inference-gateway --priority P1 --assignee @oncall
- Reserve capacity: capacity-allocator reserve --service inference-gateway --units 0.2 --duration 60m --requester @oncall
- Evict best-effort jobs: capacity-allocator evict --queue noncritical-batch --target 30%
- Re-route traffic: request-router set-weight --route inference-prod --variant small-model --weight 0.3
- Revoke reservation (rollback): capacity-allocator release --reservation-id <id>

Verification checklist
----------------------
1) Metrics: p95 and p99 latency trending back toward baseline for 20 minutes.
2) SLO ledger: slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes increased > 240 minutes.
3) Capacity: available_gpu_units >= 10% or cross-region fallback active.
4) Logs/Tracing: no new increases in tail latency traced to the same code path.
5) Customer impact: check customer-facing dashboards for SLA alerts; notify account teams if High customers were impacted.

Post-incident actions and analysis
---------------------------------
1) Postmortem owner: on-call or SLO steward must open an incident review within 48 hours.
2) Evidence to collect: SLO ledger snapshot, graph of burn rate, capacity-allocator decisions, automation logs, and runbook actions timeline.
3) Remediation: create actionable tickets with owners and deadlines for at least the following: root cause fix, automation improvement, and SLO tuning.
4) run a chaos rehearsal that simulates the recovered scenario within 2 weeks if the event required tenant shedding.

Safety notes and constraints
---------------------------
- Never perform tenant shedding for High-priority SLAs without legal and customer success approval.
- All automation that affects model routing must be tested in canary (10% traffic) before full rollout, except during Emergency where safety gates may be overridden with human confirmation.
- Record every manual override in the incident timeline with rationale.

Related pages
-------------
- /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/service-onboarding-runway-and-slo-admission-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/capacity-observability-and-recovery-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/oom-cascade-and-memory-throttling-runbook-2026

Appendices
----------
A. Example JSON for an automated reservation request (safe-mode):
{
  "service": "inference-gateway",
  "units": "0.15",
  "duration_minutes": "60",
  "requester": "@oncall",
  "expiration_action": "auto-release"
}

B. Sample PromQL snippets (copyable):
1) p95 latency:
   histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, service))
2) available GPUs:
   sum(gpu_total_units) - sum(gpu_used_units)

C. Contact list:
- on-call pager: #sre-pager
- platform-sre slack: @platform-sre
- runtime-ops pager: #runtime-ops

End of document

Safe automation gates
---------------------
All automated actions must satisfy the following preconditions before execution:
1) Observability confirm: two independent signals (metrics + tracing slowdown) show the same impact.
2) Capacity sanity: min_headroom >= 5% post-action for primary region or a validated cross-region fallback exists.
3) Rollback path: automation must be reversible within 2 minutes (e.g., scale-down, revoke reservation).
4) Approval bit: for Emergency band, automation must pause and require a single on-call confirmation via PagerDuty UI or CLI token.

Actions and thresholds (detailed)
--------------------------------
1) Emergency (Burn > 8x or projection < 60m)
   - Owner: on-call (primary) + platform-sre (secondary)
   - Steps:
     a) Post incident marker: create incident in incident tracker (CLI example below).
     b) Force reserve 20% extra capacity from capacity-allocator: CLI -> capacity-allocator reserve --service inference-gateway --units 0.2 --duration 60m
     c) Redirect 30% of new traffic to read-only model variant or smaller model via request-router dynamic-route: request-router set-weight --route inference-prod --variant small-model --weight 0.3
     d) If regional GPU shortage: trigger cross-region redistribution: scheduler relocate --from eu-west --to us-west --max 50pods
     e) If headroom cannot be recovered within 30m: execute tenant-priority shedding (see table below)
   - Verification: p95 latency reduces to <1.5x baseline and slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes > 240

2) Elevated (Burn 3–8x)
   - Owner: service owner + on-call
   - Steps:
     a) Enable aggressive KV caching: kv-cache promote --policy aggressive --target inference-gateway
     b) Increase batcher concurrency for long-running models: continuous-batcher set-concurrency --service model-worker --delta +2
     c) Start gradual model-quality fallback for non-critical tenants: model-worker set-fallback --tenants tenant-list.yaml
   - Verification: sustained p95 improvement within 10 minutes and error budget burn-rate reduces below 3x.

3) Normal (Burn <3x but trending upward)
   - Owner: service owner
   - Steps:
     a) Tune dynamic batching windows: continuous-batcher set-window --target 5ms
     b) Warm key prefixes into KV cache for anticipated request patterns.
     c) Open a short-lived ticket to follow up on root cause.

Tenant priority shedding table
------------------------------
(Used only as last-resort in Emergency band. Always apply least-impact-first.)
| Priority | Action | Max allowed reduction |
| High (SLA customers) | No shedding unless explicit legal-approved escalation | 0% |
| Medium | Disable model variants >2x cost per token; limit tokens per request to 75% | up to 30% |
| Low | Enforce strict rate-limits, reject low-value batched jobs | up to 70% |

Operational commands and examples
----------------------------------
- Create incident: redwood-ops incident create --title "Hot-path latency surge" --services inference-gateway --priority P1 --assignee @oncall
- Reserve capacity: capacity-allocator reserve --service inference-gateway --units 0.2 --duration 60m --requester @oncall
- Evict best-effort jobs: capacity-allocator evict --queue noncritical-batch --target 30%
- Re-route traffic: request-router set-weight --route inference-prod --variant small-model --weight 0.3
- Revoke reservation (rollback): capacity-allocator release --reservation-id <id>

Verification checklist
----------------------
1) Metrics: p95 and p99 latency trending back toward baseline for 20 minutes.
2) SLO ledger: slo_projection_reaches_zero_minutes increased > 240 minutes.
3) Capacity: available_gpu_units >= 10% or cross-region fallback active.
4) Logs/Tracing: no new increases in tail latency traced to the same code path.
5) Customer impact: check customer-facing dashboards for SLA alerts; notify account teams if High customers were impacted.

Post-incident actions and analysis
---------------------------------
1) Postmortem owner: on-call or SLO steward must open an incident review within 48 hours.
2) Evidence to collect: SLO ledger snapshot, graph of burn rate, capacity-allocator decisions, automation logs, and runbook actions timeline.
3) Remediation: create actionable tickets with owners and deadlines for at least the following: root cause fix, automation improvement, and SLO tuning.
4) run a chaos rehearsal that simulates the recovered scenario within 2 weeks if the event required tenant shedding.

Safety notes and constraints
---------------------------
- Never perform tenant shedding for High-priority SLAs without legal and customer success approval.
- All automation that affects model routing must be tested in canary (10% traffic) before full rollout, except during Emergency where safety gates may be overridden with human confirmation.
- Record every manual override in the incident timeline with rationale.

Related pages
-------------
- /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/service-onboarding-runway-and-slo-admission-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/capacity-observability-and-recovery-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/oom-cascade-and-memory-throttling-runbook-2026

Appendices
----------
A. Example JSON for an automated reservation request (safe-mode):
{
  "service": "inference-gateway",
  "units": "0.15",
  "duration_minutes": "60",
  "requester": "@oncall",
  "expiration_action": "auto-release"
}

B. Sample PromQL snippets (copyable):
1) p95 latency:
   histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, service))
2) available GPUs:
   sum(gpu_total_units) - sum(gpu_used_units)

C. Contact list:
- on-call pager: #sre-pager
- platform-sre slack: @platform-sre
- runtime-ops pager: #runtime-ops

End of document

title: hallucination-stress-test-suite-and-recovery-playbook-2027
preview:
Overview
========

This document describes the Hallucination Stress Test Suite (HSTS) and an associated Recovery Playbook for operationalizing detection, triage, and automated mitigation of hallucination regressions across Redwood’s inference fleet (hosted, dedicated, private). The HSTS is intended to be integrated with the eval-mesh and probe-orchestrator pipelines to provide deterministic and stochastic stress probes that surface hallucination risk under real-world conditions: prompt priming changes, quantized kernels, KV-cache boundary events, and latency-preserving batching.

Goals
-----
- Provide a repeatable, versioned set of probes that exercise hallucination-prone behaviors.
- Define measurable metrics and pass/fail thresholds for gating rollouts.
- Prescribe triage steps and automated repair actions (prompt rewrites, quant-fallback, enhanced priming) to reduce incident MTTR.
- Integrate with model-onboarding checks and shadow-rollforward evaluation flows.

Scope
-----
Includes: multi-turn knowledge requests, attribute-grounding tests, temporal-fact queries, numeric-precision checks, hallucination amplification under low-context or adversarial priming, and functioncall hallucination detection.
Excludes: safety policy violations and content moderation tests (handled by the safety-sandbox).

Test taxonomy
------------
1) Fact-Falsification Probes (deterministic): prompts that request verifiable facts with expected ground truth answers. Useful for regression delta measurements after quantization or model version swaps.
2) Attributive Drift Probes (context-shift): prompts where entity attributes are swapped or obfuscated in successive turns to surface memory/KV-cache boundary failures.
3) Numeric-Precision Suite: arithmetic and date-time queries that expose precision erosion after low-precision kernels.
4) Adversarial Priming Benchmarks: small perturbations to priming instructions that cause confident but incorrect assertions.
5) Functioncall-Resolve Tests: functioncall-triggering prompts where hallucinated arguments or missing fields indicate generation-time confusion.

Metrics and thresholds
---------------------
We report the following metrics for each probe-run (per model/version/environment):
- Hallucination Rate (HR): fraction of probes where output contradicted accepted ground truth. (Target for production: HR <= 0.5% on canonical slices)
- Confidence-Calibration Drift (CCD): fraction where model assigns >0.8 confidence score to incorrect assertions (Target <= 0.1%)
- Anchor-Consistency Score (ACS): a syntactic/semantic consistency score across repeated runs (Target >= 95%)
- Reproducible-Flip Rate (RFR): probes that flip answer under seed/temperature perturbations (Target <= 2%)

Suggested gating thresholds for canaries and full rollouts:
| Stage | HR threshold | CCD threshold | ACS threshold | RFR threshold |
| Canary | 1.0% | 0.5% | 92% | 4% |
| Production rollout | 0.5% | 0.2% | 95% | 2% |

Test harness integration (engineers)
-----------------------------------
1. Add probe bundle to eval-mesh: place JSON spec in /eval-bundles/hsts/{model-family}/{bundle-version}.json. See Probe Spec Reference below.
2. Schedule: Canary runs -> daily for 48 hours; full-run shadowing -> every 6 hours for first 7 days post-rollout.
3. Execution engines: probe-orchestrator (deterministic), stochastic-sampler (for RFR), and shadow-sequencer (for mixed-traffic mirroring).
4. Artifacting: store outputs and seeds in the evaluation-asset-registry with artifact tags: hsts, model:{ver}, env:{hosted|dedicated|private}.

Probe Spec Reference (JSON example)
---------------------------------
```json
{
  "probe_id": "hsts-fact-001",
  "type": "fact-falsification",
  "input_template": "Who is the CEO of {company} as of {year}?",
  "expected": "{ground_truth}",
  "annotations": ["authoritative_source:wiki", "variant:abbreviated"]
}
```

Runbook: triage and automated recovery
-------------------------------------
When an HSTS run exceeds gating thresholds, follow the steps below. The goal is to rapidly determine whether the regression is model-logic, quantization, prompt-shift, or runtime (cache/batching) related.

Immediate steps (0–15 minutes):
1. Alerting: probing pipeline raises an incident to #ml-regressions with summary: model, ver, env, HR, CCD, top failing probe IDs.
2. Snapshot artifacts: ensure the evaluation-asset-registry snapshot is pinned for reproduce.
3. Run quick differential: execute failing probes against last-known-good model-version and current model-version in an isolated eval sandbox (use eval-cli --artifact-pin).

Hypothesis triage (15–60 minutes):
1. Quantization check: run quant-shadow replay (quant-shadow-fidelity-scan) with full-precision fallback to see if HR improves >50%. If yes, mark quant-failure and escalate to quant-tuning.
2. KV-cache boundary test: replay multi-turn failing probes with cache warmup disabled. If failures correlate, mark cache-boundary bug and create runbook task for runtime team.
3. Prompt-sensitivity: run adversarial-priming variants on the same model; if small phrasing changes change outputs, prioritize prompt-engineering short-term fixes.
4. Seed/Temperature variance: run stochastic-sampler at T=0.0, 0.2, 0.8. High RFR implies instability; may require temperature constraints or guardrails.

Automated mitigations (fast-fix via rollout tooling)
1. Canary rollforward block: set rollout policy to block further traffic to new variant (runtime: smart-routing set to prefer model:previous-stable).
2. Prompt rewrite shim: use the PromptRewrite sidecar (prompt-rewrite-config: enforce-anchoring=true) to force more specific grounding for affected routes for up to 72 hours.
3. Quant-fallback: toggle model-quant-fallback=true in deployment config to route suspect slices to FP16/FP32 pool.
4. Functioncall hardening: enable functioncall-argument-validation hooks to reject outputs with malformed or hallucinated args.

Longer-term remediation (owner teams)
1. If quant: initiate quant-tuner job with increased sample density on failing slices and add quant-profile exceptions.
2. If model-logic: file an issue for model-onboarding to require additional probe coverage during promotion (include failing probe set).
3. If prompt-sensitivity: add targeted prompt-catalog entries and annotate as 'anchoring-required' and add to the priming-registry.
4. If runtime: schedule capacity/team-runbook updates to fix KV-cache boundary handling or batching heuristics.

Operationalizing HSTS for customer-facing services
--------------------------------------------------
- Map probes to per-route SQC (service-quality-contract) entries in the per-route dashboards so Product can see HR deltas per customer.
- Tag high-risk tenant slices in the probe meta so dedicated/private deployments can maintain stricter gating (e.g., block quantized variants).
- Include HSTS pass/fail in release checklist for dedicated and private image promotions.

Monitoring and dashboards
-------------------------
Key dashboards to maintain:
- hsts-summary (overall HR by model-family, last 30d trend)
- hsts-canary-detail (failed-probe list for active canaries)
- hsts-quant-correlation (HR vs quant-profile across slices)

SLO and alerting rules (suggested)
----------------------------------
- Alert: model.HSTS.HR > 1.0% over 60m (warn) and > 2.0% over 60m (critical).
- Alert: CCD > 0.5% over 30m (critical).

Ownership and cadence
---------------------
- Owner: Applied ML / Evaluation team (primary), Runtime team (secondary for cache/batching issues), Quant team (secondary for quantization-related failures).
- Regular cadence: HSTS maintenance meeting every 2 weeks to rotate probe authorship, update ground-truth manifests, and review any high-severity incidents.

Appendix A — canonical failing probe examples
--------------------------------------------
1) Multi-turn attribution: 
- Prompt stream: 
  - Q1: "Alice moved to Cedar City in 2018; which company did she join after moving?"
  - Q2: "Which city did Alice move to in 2018?"
- Expected: Q1 -> 'Acme Systems (joined 2018)'. Q2 -> 'Cedar City'.
- Failure mode: model hallucinates a different company or fabricates dates.

2) Temporal facts: 
- Prompt: "What was the US federal funds rate in March 2019?"
- Expected source: FRED or treasury dataset entry.

Appendix B — ticket template for triage issues
---------------------------------------------
- Title: HSTS Regression: {model-family} {version} HR:{value}%
- Attach: evaluation-asset-registry snapshot id, failing probe list, seeds, quant-profile used, runtime flags.
- Severity: Blocker/High/Medium/Low
- Assignee: (runtime/quant/model-onboarding)

Related pages and links
-----------------------
- See also: evaluation-asset-registry, shadow-sequencer runbook, quant-profile-synthesis-and-safety-playbook (link in eval-bundle index).

Change history
--------------
- 2026-11-04: Initial draft and internal review.
- 2026-12-15: Added numeric-precision suite and functioncall tests.
- 2027-02-03: Finalized gating thresholds and automated mitigation flow; marked published.

If you are implementing this for a specific model or customer route and need help selecting seed bundles or probe density, contact Applied ML (alias: #applied-ml) or add an entry to the HSTS maintenance board.

title: Execution Quiet Mode and Preemption Consensus Policy (ADR)
preview: Defines the Execution Quiet Mode (EQM) concept and an inter-shard preemption consensus policy to reduce tail-latency regressions from aggressive preemption in mixed-priority multi-tenant inference workloads. Specifies contract-level API hints, runtime config surface, telemetry requirements, rollout and rollback steps, and safety gates.
