title: Embedded Feature Simulator: SDK Onboarding Examples & Telemetry Codelets
preview:
Overview

This page describes a lightweight, reproducible simulator pattern for embedding feature flows used in SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and support triage. The simulator ("embedded feature simulator") is a small, language-agnostic harness that mimics common client-side behavior: batching, framing, streaming, and local telemetry emission. It is intended to help SDK authors, support engineers, and sample-app integrators validate behavior against Redwood API expectations before full integration.

Why this exists

- Speed: launch a local stub that reproduces class of SDK runtime problems in under a minute.
- Determinism: control tokenization and response timing to exercise batching and timeout logic.
- Observability alignment: validate telemetry event formats and correlation IDs before customers escalate.
- Canonical fixes: include reproducible codelets that match support playbooks.

Scope and limitations

- The simulator is not a replacement for full e2e tests against the Redwood hosted runtime.
- It focuses on API-level interactions (HTTP/HTTP2/WebSocket-style streaming) and SDK telemetry hooks, not GPU performance.
- Intended for developer machines, CI smoke lanes, and SDK support sandboxes.

Quickstart: run the Python simulator locally

1) Prerequisites
   - Python 3.10+
   - pip install -r requirements.txt (requirements include fastapi, uvicorn, httpx)

2) Start simulator (one command)

   $ python simulator_app.py --port 8085 --latency-ms 40 --stream-chunk-size 16

3) Run sample SDK call (node/python) against http://localhost:8085/v1/generate

   - Expected: immediate 200 with streaming chunks every 40ms and telemetry events logged to /v1/telemetry endpoint.

Simulator features and flags

- --latency-ms: mean response chunk delay to simulate pacing.
- --stream-chunk-size: tokens emitted per chunk in streaming mode.
- --failure-mode: none | intermittent-429 | partial-kv-loss | stuck-kv
- --telemetry-mode: local-log | post-to-endpoint

Design: core components

1) Request ingress matcher
   - Matches incoming HTTP request to an example flow (embedding, generation, stream) by header and body shape.
   - Allows flow overrides for testing features such as model-variant fallback and routing policies.

2) Response generator (deterministic)
   - Template-driven text generation with token boundary awareness so SDK token counters behave the same as real runs.
   - Support for a small set of templates: concise, verbose, hallucination-trigger, truncated.

3) Telemetry emitter
   - Emits canonical telemetry events that SDKs are expected to produce. By default it POSTs to /v1/telemetry on the same simulator.
   - Events follow the telemetry schema in the "Telemetry contract" section below.

4) Failure injector
   - Deterministic toggles for 429 spikes, stream truncation, and retry-after semantics.

Telemetry contract (canonical schema)

- event_time: ISO-8601 timestamp string
- sdk_client_id: string (UUID)
- request_id: string (alphanumeric)
- flow: one of ["generation", "embedding", "rerank"]
- model: string (example: "redwood-1-small")
- tokens_sent: integer (estimated)
- tokens_received: integer (estimated)
- latency_ms: integer
- status: one of ["ok", "partial", "error", "rate_limited"]
- error_code: optional string (e.g., "429_RATE_LIMIT")

Example telemetry event (JSON)

{
  "event_time": "2026-11-07T14:03:22.511Z",
  "sdk_client_id": "b4b1d5fe-7a9a-4e7e-b2c6-9f2d8e5c3f11",
  "request_id": "req_8x9z1a",
  "flow": "generation",
  "model": "redwood-1-small",
  "tokens_sent": 24,
  "tokens_received": 128,
  "latency_ms": 198,
  "status": "ok"
}

Codelets: minimal examples for common scenarios

A) Node.js sample: simulate slow streaming handling

// pseudo-code
const res = await fetch('http://localhost:8085/v1/generate', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ prompt }) });
for await (const chunk of res.body) {
  // assert that SDK resumes progress and appends tokens in-place
}

B) Python sample: verify retry-on-429 with jitter

import httpx

async def call():
  r = await client.post('http://localhost:8085/v1/generate', json=payload)
  if r.status_code == 429:
    # follow SDK backoff policy and assert eventual success or graceful downgrade

C) Telemetry validator (curl + jq)

$ curl -s http://localhost:8085/v1/telemetry | jq '.[] | select(.status != "ok")'

Test matrix and recommended smoke lanes

- Local fast feedback: latency-ms=10, stream-chunk-size=32, failure-mode=none (run on dev machine).
- CI smoke: latency-ms=50, stream-chunk-size=8, failure-mode=intermittent-429 (assert SDK backoff and error counters).
- Support repro: latency-ms=200, failure-mode=partial-kv-loss (reproduce cache/KV-miss patterns).

Operational runbook: triage steps for telemetry mismatches

1) Reproduce with simulator: get SDK to call the local simulator with the same headers and body observed in customer logs.
2) Validate telemetry contract: curl /v1/telemetry and compare fields with customer payloads.
3) If tokens_sent mismatch > 10%: check tokenizer version mismatch in SDK sample. Action: pin tokenizers in SDK dependency and document in release notes. Owner: devx-team.
4) If streaming order incorrect: check framing logic (is-end / seq ids) in SDK. Action: apply canonical stream-merge helper from examples folder. Owner: sdk-runtime team.
5) If rate-limited behavior diverges: compare backoff implementation against "SDK backoff reference" (link in related pages). If needed, ship a hotfix. Owner: platform reliability.

Canonical fixes and example patches

- Fix: streaming reassembly bug in Node adapter
  - Symptom: partial tokens swallowed when multiple concurrent streams share buffer.
  - Patch: use per-request stream buffer and flush-on-boundary. Example patch included in /examples/node/stream-fix.js.

- Fix: telemetry missing sdk_client_id for certain async flows
  - Symptom: telemetry events from nested async tasks lacked the correlation id.
  - Patch: ensure request-scoped context propagation; we include a small codelet for async-local-storage with clear license.

Repository layout (what you get when you clone the examples repo)

- /simulator/simulator_app.py           # Python FastAPI harness
- /examples/node/stream-fix.js           # Node minimal adapter and fixes
- /examples/python/retry_on_429.py      # Python sample with injectable jitter/backoff
- /examples/telemetry/validator.sh       # curl + jq validator for telemetry events
- /docs/README.md                        # Quickstart and troubleshooting tips

Best practices for SDK authors

- Emit request_id early and propagate it to all async boundaries.
- Always record tokens_sent and an estimated tokens_received to help downstream billing and SLO calculations.
- Use local simulator in CI for a lightweight smoke gate: fail builds only on protocol regressions, not textual quality diffs.
- Include a telemetry validator job that compares event shapes to the canonical schema.

Maintenance and ownership

- Ownership: devx-team maintains simulator codelets and examples.
- SLA: update simulator templates for each major SDK release and new streaming feature within two weeks of the release.
- Review cadence: quarterly review with platform-reliability and sdk-runtime teams.

Appendix: troubleshooting tips

- If simulator emits constant 429s: ensure failure-mode was not left on (run with --failure-mode none).
- If tokens mismatch with production samples: verify tokenizer version and shared encoding table.
- If telemetry endpoint returns 5xx from simulator: check local port conflicts and increase logging with --telemetry-mode local-log.

Contacts and escalation

- Primary owner: Priya Natarajan (devx-team) — priya@redwood.ai
- Secondary: Daniel Kim (sdk-runtime) — daniel@redwood.ai
- For urgent customer-facing regressions open the "SDK Support" PagerDuty channel and tag devx on Slack (#devx-oncall).

Related material

- SDK backoff reference and canonical retry windows (linked in related_pages).
- Tokenizer compatibility matrix (refer to product-docs/tokenizer-matrix).

Change log

- 2026-06-01: created initial draft with simulator design and quickstart.
- 2026-07-20: added failure injection modes and telemetry contract.
- 2026-11-07: updated examples to include Node stream-fix codelet and CI smoke lane recommendations.
text:
Overview

This page describes a lightweight, reproducible simulator pattern for embedding feature flows used in SDK onboarding, local integration tests, and support triage. The simulator ("embedded feature simulator") is a small, language-agnostic harness that mimics common client-side behavior: batching, framing, streaming, and local telemetry emission. It is intended to help SDK authors, support engineers, and sample-app integrators validate behavior against Redwood API expectations before full integration.

Why this exists

- Speed: launch a local stub that reproduces class of SDK runtime problems in under a minute.
- Determinism: control tokenization and response timing to exercise batching and timeout logic.
- Observability alignment: validate telemetry event formats and correlation IDs before customers escalate.
- Canonical fixes: include reproducible codelets that match support playbooks.

Scope and limitations

- The simulator is not a replacement for full e2e tests against the Redwood hosted runtime.
- It focuses on API-level interactions (HTTP/HTTP2/WebSocket-style streaming) and SDK telemetry hooks, not GPU performance.
- Intended for developer machines, CI smoke lanes, and SDK support sandboxes.

Quickstart: run the Python simulator locally

1) Prerequisites
   - Python 3.10+
   - pip install -r requirements.txt (requirements include fastapi, uvicorn, httpx)

2) Start simulator (one command)

   $ python simulator_app.py --port 8085 --latency-ms 40 --stream-chunk-size 16

3) Run sample SDK call (node/python) against http://localhost:8085/v1/generate

   - Expected: immediate 200 with streaming chunks every 40ms and telemetry events logged to /v1/telemetry endpoint.

Simulator features and flags

- --latency-ms: mean response chunk delay to simulate pacing.
- --stream-chunk-size: tokens emitted per chunk in streaming mode.
- --failure-mode: none | intermittent-429 | partial-kv-loss | stuck-kv
- --telemetry-mode: local-log | post-to-endpoint

Design: core components

1) Request ingress matcher
   - Matches incoming HTTP request to an example flow (embedding, generation, stream) by header and body shape.
   - Allows flow overrides for testing features such as model-variant fallback and routing policies.

2) Response generator (deterministic)
   - Template-driven text generation with token boundary awareness so SDK token counters behave the same as real runs.
   - Support for a small set of templates: concise, verbose, hallucination-trigger, truncated.

3) Telemetry emitter
   - Emits canonical telemetry events that SDKs are expected to produce. By default it POSTs to /v1/telemetry on the same simulator.
   - Events follow the telemetry schema in the "Telemetry contract" section below.

4) Failure injector
   - Deterministic toggles for 429 spikes, stream truncation, and retry-after semantics.

Telemetry contract (canonical schema)

- event_time: ISO-8601 timestamp string
- sdk_client_id: string (UUID)
- request_id: string (alphanumeric)
- flow: one of ["generation", "embedding", "rerank"]
- model: string (example: "redwood-1-small")
- tokens_sent: integer (estimated)
- tokens_received: integer (estimated)
- latency_ms: integer
- status: one of ["ok", "partial", "error", "rate_limited"]
- error_code: optional string (e.g., "429_RATE_LIMIT")

Example telemetry event (JSON)

{
  "event_time": "2026-11-07T14:03:22.511Z",
  "sdk_client_id": "b4b1d5fe-7a9a-4e7e-b2c6-9f2d8e5c3f11",
  "request_id": "req_8x9z1a",
  "flow": "generation",
  "model": "redwood-1-small",
  "tokens_sent": 24,
  "tokens_received": 128,
  "latency_ms": 198,
  "status": "ok"
}

Codelets: minimal examples for common scenarios

A) Node.js sample: simulate slow streaming handling

// pseudo-code
const res = await fetch('http://localhost:8085/v1/generate', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ prompt }) });
for await (const chunk of res.body) {
  // assert that SDK resumes progress and appends tokens in-place
}

B) Python sample: verify retry-on-429 with jitter

import httpx

async def call():
  r = await client.post('http://localhost:8085/v1/generate', json=payload)
  if r.status_code == 429:
    # follow SDK backoff policy and assert eventual success or graceful downgrade

C) Telemetry validator (curl + jq)

$ curl -s http://localhost:8085/v1/telemetry | jq '.[] | select(.status != "ok")'

Test matrix and recommended smoke lanes

- Local fast feedback: latency-ms=10, stream-chunk-size=32, failure-mode=none (run on dev machine).
- CI smoke: latency-ms=50, stream-chunk-size=8, failure-mode=intermittent-429 (assert SDK backoff and error counters).
- Support repro: latency-ms=200, failure-mode=partial-kv-loss (reproduce cache/KV-miss patterns).

Operational runbook: triage steps for telemetry mismatches

1) Reproduce with simulator: get SDK to call the local simulator with the same headers and body observed in customer logs.
2) Validate telemetry contract: curl /v1/telemetry and compare fields with customer payloads.
3) If tokens_sent mismatch > 10%: check tokenizer version mismatch in SDK sample. Action: pin tokenizers in SDK dependency and document in release notes. Owner: devx-team.
4) If streaming order incorrect: check framing logic (is-end / seq ids) in SDK. Action: apply canonical stream-merge helper from examples folder. Owner: sdk-runtime team.
5) If rate-limited behavior diverges: compare backoff implementation against "SDK backoff reference" (link in related pages). If needed, ship a hotfix. Owner: platform reliability.

Canonical fixes and example patches

- Fix: streaming reassembly bug in Node adapter
  - Symptom: partial tokens swallowed when multiple concurrent streams share buffer.
  - Patch: use per-request stream buffer and flush-on-boundary. Example patch included in /examples/node/stream-fix.js.

- Fix: telemetry missing sdk_client_id for certain async flows
  - Symptom: telemetry events from nested async tasks lacked the correlation id.
  - Patch: ensure request-scoped context propagation; we include a small codelet for async-local-storage with clear license.

Repository layout (what you get when you clone the examples repo)

- /simulator/simulator_app.py           # Python FastAPI harness
- /examples/node/stream-fix.js           # Node minimal adapter and fixes
- /examples/python/retry_on_429.py      # Python sample with injectable jitter/backoff
- /examples/telemetry/validator.sh       # curl + jq validator for telemetry events
- /docs/README.md                        # Quickstart and troubleshooting tips

Best practices for SDK authors

- Emit request_id early and propagate it to all async boundaries.
- Always record tokens_sent and an estimated tokens_received to help downstream billing and SLO calculations.
- Use local simulator in CI for a lightweight smoke gate: fail builds only on protocol regressions, not textual quality diffs.
- Include a telemetry validator job that compares event shapes to the canonical schema.

Maintenance and ownership

- Ownership: devx-team maintains simulator codelets and examples.
- SLA: update simulator templates for each major SDK release and new streaming feature within two weeks of the release.
- Review cadence: quarterly review with platform-reliability and sdk-runtime teams.

Appendix: troubleshooting tips

- If simulator emits constant 429s: ensure failure-mode was not left on (run with --failure-mode none).
- If tokens mismatch with production samples: verify tokenizer version and shared encoding table.
- If telemetry endpoint returns 5xx from simulator: check local port conflicts and increase logging with --telemetry-mode local-log.

Contacts and escalation

- Primary owner: Priya Natarajan (devx-team) — priya@redwood.ai
- Secondary: Daniel Kim (sdk-runtime) — daniel@redwood.ai
- For urgent customer-facing regressions open the "SDK Support" PagerDuty channel and tag devx on Slack (#devx-oncall).

Related material

- SDK backoff reference and canonical retry windows (linked in related_pages).
- Tokenizer compatibility matrix (refer to product-docs/tokenizer-matrix).

Change log

- 2026-06-01: created initial draft with simulator design and quickstart.
- 2026-07-20: added failure injection modes and telemetry contract.
- 2026-11-07: updated examples to include Node stream-fix codelet and CI smoke lane recommendations.

title: Predictive Throttle and Canary Rollback Procedure
preview:
Overview:

This runbook describes Redwood's standardized procedure for applying predictive capacity throttling and performing a controlled canary rollback when early signals indicate SLO risk. It codifies the decision thresholds, required telemetry, automation hooks, and human escalation steps so teams can reduce blast radius while preserving customer-facing availability.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: minimize customer impact by automatically and safely mitigating traffic surges or degraded model performance before error budgets are exhausted.
- Scope: applies to public inference endpoints, Dedicated pools, and Private VPC deployments where automated routing and throttle policies are enabled. Does NOT cover manual DBA/infra maintenance windows.

Key definitions:
- Predictive throttle: automated traffic shaping that reduces request concurrency or drops non-critical request classes based on forecasted capacity shortfalls.
- Canary rollback: automated or manual rollback of a recently deployed model or runtime variant affecting a small percentage of traffic.
- Primary SLOs: p95 latency < 120ms for text-gen, availability >= 99.9%; token-level quality SLOs as defined per model.

Signals used for decisioning (ordered):
1) Telemetry predictions: 15–60m forecast from the capacity-forecaster service indicating >20% probability of exceeding provisioned tokens/sec.
2) SLO burn rate: instantaneous burn rate > 4x and projected to exhaust error budget in < 12h.
3) Latency signal: sustained p95 above threshold for 5m and increasing.
4) Error spike: client error rate (5xx) increased by >200% relative to 1h baseline.
5) Model quality regressions: eval-set quality delta > configured threshold for last 1k requests.

Preconditions and ownership:
- Owning team: service owner listed in the service manifest (ownership on-service page).
- Oncall: primary oncall for the owning team executes human-confirm steps if automation is not enabled.
- Automation owner: infra-runtime team maintains the throttle controller and canary deploy automation.

Decision matrix (quick reference):
| Trigger type | Immediate automated action | Human required? | Escalation window |
|--------------|----------------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Telemetry forecast (>=20% oversub) | Apply soft throttle: reduce non-critical classes by 25% | no | 5 minutes |
| Burn rate >4x & projection <12h | Apply strict throttle (50%) and notify oncall | yes | 2 minutes |
| Latency p95 > threshold (5m) | Mark for canary rollback candidate; keep throttles | yes | 5 minutes |
| 5xx spike >200% | Fail traffic to fallback model variant + throttle | yes | immediate |
| Eval quality delta exceeded | Pause new deployments; start canary rollback | yes | immediate |

Operational steps (automated path):
1. Detection: capacity-forecaster raises alert -> alert rules in PagerDuty (tag: predictive-throttle).
2. Soft throttle: throttle-controller reduces concurrency for non-critical routes by 25% and reroutes lower-tier customers to best-effort pool. CLI: rdwctl throttle apply --service {svc} --class noncritical --percent 25 --reason "forecasted capacity shortfall"
3. Telemetry re-evaluation: after 3m, controller re-evaluates predicted capacity. If projection still >20% oversub, escalate to strict throttle.
4. Strict throttle: throttle-controller enforces 50% reduction and reduces batch sizes to limit token throughput. CLI: rdwctl throttle apply --service {svc} --percent 50 --batchesize 8 --reason "strict policy"
5. Canary selection: router picks 1% of traffic from the affected pool and switches it to the previous stable model variant (canary rollback). Automation: rollout-controller can perform rollback automatically when configured; otherwise oncall triggers rollback.
6. Recovery monitoring: observe SLOs for 15m. If SLOs recover, gradually remove throttles over 30m (linear unwind: 25% -> 0).
7. Post-action: create incident ticket automatically with root cause artifacts attached (traces, flamegraphs, capacity forecasts, sample inputs).

Operational steps (human-assisted path):
1. PagerDuty alert -> acknowledge and join incident channel. Post initial assessment within 2 minutes.
2. Confirm trigger signals. If safe and automation disabled, execute rdwctl throttle apply (soft) and follow automated re-eval steps.
3. If rollback required: perform canary rollback via rollout-controller UI or CLI: rdwctl rollout rollback --service {svc} --target-version {previous} --canary-percent 1 --wait 300s
4. Monitor and escalate to SRE lead after 10 minutes if no improvement.
5. If rollback fails or regression persists, execute full rollback: rdwctl rollout rollback --service {svc} --target-version {previous} --canary-percent 100 --wait 600s

Runbook playbook for capacity planners (when predictive throttles are frequent):
- Frequency > 3 predictive throttle events per 7 days => require capacity retrospective with infra-runtime and owning team.
- Retrospective agenda: 1) Review forecasts vs reality; 2) Examine batching/quantization opportunities; 3) Re-evaluate routing policies; 4) Consider reserved capacity increase or model quantization changes.

SLO and error budget integration:
- When predictive throttle is triggered by forecast only, increment a special "throttle" counter that counts against a monthly operational allowance (not the customer-visible error budget) to track how often service-level mitigation is applied.
- If strict throttle or rollback is required, record event as an error-budget-impacting incident and annotate incident with burn-rate and estimated customer token loss.

Telemetry & dashboards required (minimum):
- SLO dashboard: p50/p95/p99 latency, error rates, burn-rate, error budget remaining.
- Capacity-forecast view: 15m/30m/60m probability distribution, detected anomalies.
- Throttle-controller audit: active throttles, last-applied reason, owner, and unwind schedule.
- Canary rollback dashboard: canary percent, model versions, quality delta, sample responses.

Essential CLI snippets and automation examples:
- Soft throttle: rdwctl throttle apply --service inference-api --class background --percent 25 --reason "forecasted_high"
- Strict throttle: rdwctl throttle apply --service inference-api --percent 50 --batchesize 8 --reason "strict"
- Canary rollback (automated template):
  {
    "service": "inference-api",
    "from_version": "v1.24",
    "to_version": "v1.23",
    "canary_percent": "1",
    "health_check": {
      "slo_check": "p95_latency < 120ms",
      "quality_check": "eval_delta < 0.5%"
    }
  }

Sample throttle policy (YAML):
- name: predictive-throttle-policy
  trigger:
    - forecast_probability: 0.2
      forecast_window: 60m
  actions:
    - type: soft-throttle
      percent: 25
      scope: noncritical
    - type: strict-throttle
      percent: 50
      scope: all
  canary_rollback:
    enabled: true
    canary_percent: 1
    monitor_window: 15m

Incident review and post-incident requirements:
1. If strict throttle or any rollback occurred, open an incident review within 72 hours. Document: timeline, signals, who ran commands (CLI audit), dashboards, and decision rationale.
2. Include the capacity-forecaster prediction artifacts and the throttle-controller audit log.
3. Action items must be recorded with owners and expected completion dates. Track in the SRE reliability board.

Playbook templates for incident reviews (minimum sections):
- Summary and impact
- Timeline of automated/human actions (with timestamps)
- Evidence (telemetry snapshots, sample bad requests, model eval diffs)
- Root cause hypothesis and validation steps
- Preventative changes (code/config/system)
- Postmortem owner and due date for action items

Safety checks and abort conditions:
- If canary rollback causes quality delta > 1% worse for canary traffic, abort rollback and escalate to oncall lead.
- If throttles are applied but SLOs continue to degrade for 30m, trigger emergency capacity reallocation (move traffic to warm standby region).

Appendices:
A. Ownership contacts:
- Throttle-controller SRE oncall: #infra-throttle-pd
- Rollout-controller owner: infra-runtime team (Slack: @infra-runtime)

B. Playbook change log:
- 2026-05-10: Initial published runbook (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-07-01: Added evaluation-based rollback trigger (Lin Zhao).
- 2026-11-02: Tightened strict throttle thresholds after experiment (Marcus Hale).

C. Useful links:
- rollout-controller runbook: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/rollout-controller-operations
- capacity-forecaster docs: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/capacity-forecaster

End of document.

title: Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture
preview:
Overview

This page documents the Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture (LOLMFA) for Redwood Private deployments. The pattern is intended for customers who require sub-50ms median (p50) inference latency for common short requests while preserving the ability to route heavier or higher‑quality requests to central GPU pools.

Goals

- Provide a predictable low-latency path for small/short-text workloads (p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms) using local, quantized model shards.
- Retain correctness and quality by transparently fanouting requests to central inference for long-context or compute‑intensive queries.
- Ensure graceful degradation and clear rollback/upgrade paths for both local and central tiers.
- Minimize customer network egress and meet privacy constraints (models and tokens may remain within customer VPC).

High-level topology

1) Edge nodes (customer-managed in VPC or on-prem):
   - Small GPU or CPU instances running quantized model variants (4–8GB GPU or multi-socket CPU).
   - Serve frequent short requests locally with optimized kernels and minimal batching.
2) Regional aggregator (optional):
   - Responsible for request classification, caching, and short‑circuiting locality decisions.
3) Central Redwood Dedicated pool (hosted or customer collocated):
   - Large-GPU cluster (A100/H100 equivalents) for long-context, high‑quality responses.
4) Control plane connectors:
   - Secure control channel for model metadata, KV cache presence, and rollout policies.

Component details

- Local runtime: a stripped-down Redwood agent providing:
  - quantized-model runtime (int8/4-bit profiles),
  - prefix/KV cache with LRU TTL (default 24h),
  - small-batch path with kernel selection favoring latency.
- Classifier router: lightweight policy process that decides: LOCAL | CACHE_HIT | CENTRAL. Policies include tokens count, prompt length, customer labels, and per-route cost ceilings.
- Fallback policy: automatic reroute to central on local failure, plus degraded-mode reply when central unreachable.
- Observability: token-level traces, request sampling (1%) to central for quality checks, and latency histograms per-tier.

SLOs and thresholds (recommended starting values)

- Local path: p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms for requests with input tokens <= 64.
- Classification decision: < 5ms 99.9% of the time.
- Central path for long requests: p95 < 1.2s (depends on model).
- Cache hit target: >= 30% of local short requests.
- Availability: local agent SLA (operator target) 99.95% during business hours.

Customer requirements (pre-install checklist)

- Network:
  - LAN RTT to local nodes: < 2ms typical.
  - WAN RTT from edge to regional aggregator/central: < 100ms for acceptable fallbacks.
  - Ports: outbound TCP 443 to Redwood control plane and dedicated pool endpoints; open internal gRPC/TCP ports 8000–8020 for inter-agent.
- Hardware:
  - Edge GPU: minimum 4GB VRAM (recommended 8GB) for quantized variants; or 32 vCPUs with AVX512 for CPU-only sites.
  - Disk: 50GB NVMe for model shards, cache, and logs.
- Security & keys:
  - Customer KMS reachable in VPC for secret unwrap; HSM integration optional for FIPS customers.
  - Mutually-authenticated TLS between agents and central (mTLS).
- IAM & service accounts:
  - Dedicated service account with least privilege; key rotation schedule defined.

Deployment playbook (edge node)

1) Preflight (operator):
   - Validate hardware profile: run collect_hardware_profile.sh and store output.
   - Verify network rules and KMS access.
   - Ensure time sync (NTP) and kernel parameters per infra-standards.
2) Install agent:
   - curl -sSL https://packages.redwood.ai/agent/install.sh | sudo bash -s -- --profile=latency-edge
   - Configure /etc/redwood/agent.yaml with:
     - model_variant: "rw-small-quant-v1"
     - kv_cache.ttl: 24h
     - router.policy: "token-count-based"
3) Smoke test:
   - redwoodctl healthcheck --target localhost:8000
   - Run quick latency benchmark: redwoodctl bench --requests 500 --max_tokens 64
4) Canary rollout:
   - Update router policy to include 10% forced central routing to validate end-to-end. Monitor errors for 2 hours before full promotion.

Day-2 operations (operator runbook)

- Monitoring and alerting:
  - Metrics to watch: local_latency_ms (p50/p95), classifier_decision_latency_ms, kv_cache_hit_rate, model_load_errors, agent_restarts.
  - Alerts: kv_cache_hit_rate < 15% sustained 30m; agent_restarts > 3 in 10m; local p95 > 200ms for 15m.
- Cache warmup strategy:
  - Scheduled synthetic workloads during quiet hours to prefill KV caches for top 200 prompt templates.
- Capacity scaling:
  - When local CPU/GPU utilization > 70% for 30m, auto-scale policy triggers adding a hot-spare agent.
- Quality checks:
  - Sampled 1% of local replies are sent to central for quality comparison; alert if semantic drift > threshold.

Upgrade and rollback procedures

- Upgrade (agent or local model):
  1. Stage new model variant in a canary edge node (1 node). Run bench and quality regression checks against a saved prompt set.
  2. Gradually increase canary percentage (1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100%) over multiple hours while monitoring p95 latency and quality diffs.
  3. If quality regression or latency spike occurs, trigger rollback.

- Rollback (fast-fail path):
  - Command: redwoodctl agent rollback --target <agent-id> --to-version <previous-model-semilabel>
  - Automated safety: controller evaluates health for 10m before rolling to additional nodes; if failure persists, enact tenant traffic drain to central with retained cache headers.
  - Evidence capture: collect /var/log/redwood/agent.log, heap profiles, last 100 classifier traces. Attach to incident ticket.

Failure modes and mitigation

- Local model OOM: automatic restart into degraded/no-local mode (route to central) and notify operator.
- KV cache corruption: fallback to central until cache TTL expires and warmup job restores entries.
- Network partition (edge->central): operate in isolated mode, local-only responses; flag degraded quality for audit.

Observability and telemetry contract

- Required metrics exported via Prometheus endpoint:
  - redwood_local_request_latency_ms_bucket
  - redwood_classifier_decision_latency_ms_bucket
  - redwood_kv_cache_hit_ratio
  - redwood_model_load_duration_seconds
- Traces: OpenTelemetry spans must include decision.reason, route.tier, model.variant, and cache.hit boolean.
- Logs: structured JSON lines with request_id, tenant_id, model_variant, duration_ms, and error_codes. Retention default 30d (configurable).

Security & compliance notes

- All model artifacts signed and verified using the customer-side image attestation. See image signing playbook for commands.
- Sensitive telemetry (user prompts) must be redacted before external transmission; send only hashed prompt fingerprints when required.
- Key rotation: follow a 90-day rotation schedule and ensure agents gracefully reload keys without restart.

Ownership and contact points

- Architecture owner: Priya Natarajan (eng-serving-runtime).
- Support handoff: Private Deployments Field Team (private-deploy-support@redwood.ai).
- Oncall escalation: see eng-sre oncall roster; page: /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotation.

Appendix: example router policy (YAML)

---
policy_name: latency_fanout_v1
rules:
  - name: local_short_request
    condition: "input_tokens <= 64 and tenant_priority == 'standard'"
    action: LOCAL
    sampling: 0.01   # 1% sampled to central for QA
  - name: cache_only
    condition: "cache.hit == true"
    action: LOCAL
  - name: heavy_request
    condition: "input_tokens > 64 or requires_retrieval == true"
    action: CENTRAL
  - name: default_fallback
    action: CENTRAL

References and related reading

- Private deploy observability contract: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides/observability-contracts-and-log-sampling-strategy-2026
- Image attestation and signing: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/image-attestation-spec-2026
- Cold start mitigation: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/cold-start-latency-mitigation-for-private-deploys-2026

Change log

- 2026-03-15: Initial draft.
- 2026-06-10: Added SLO targets and cache warmup strategy after field trials.
- 2026-10-12: Finalized rollback commands and telemetry contract.
text:
Overview

This page documents the Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture (LOLMFA) for Redwood Private deployments. The pattern is intended for customers who require sub-50ms median (p50) inference latency for common short requests while preserving the ability to route heavier or higher‑quality requests to central GPU pools.

Goals

- Provide a predictable low-latency path for small/short-text workloads (p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms) using local, quantized model shards.
- Retain correctness and quality by transparently fanouting requests to central inference for long-context or compute‑intensive queries.
- Ensure graceful degradation and clear rollback/upgrade paths for both local and central tiers.
- Minimize customer network egress and meet privacy constraints (models and tokens may remain within customer VPC).

High-level topology

1) Edge nodes (customer-managed in VPC or on-prem):
   - Small GPU or CPU instances running quantized model variants (4–8GB GPU or multi-socket CPU).
   - Serve frequent short requests locally with optimized kernels and minimal batching.
2) Regional aggregator (optional):
   - Responsible for request classification, caching, and short‑circuiting locality decisions.
3) Central Redwood Dedicated pool (hosted or customer collocated):
   - Large-GPU cluster (A100/H100 equivalents) for long-context, high‑quality responses.
4) Control plane connectors:
   - Secure control channel for model metadata, KV cache presence, and rollout policies.

Component details

- Local runtime: a stripped-down Redwood agent providing:
  - quantized-model runtime (int8/4-bit profiles),
  - prefix/KV cache with LRU TTL (default 24h),
  - small-batch path with kernel selection favoring latency.
- Classifier router: lightweight policy process that decides: LOCAL | CACHE_HIT | CENTRAL. Policies include tokens count, prompt length, customer labels, and per-route cost ceilings.
- Fallback policy: automatic reroute to central on local failure, plus degraded-mode reply when central unreachable.
- Observability: token-level traces, request sampling (1%) to central for quality checks, and latency histograms per-tier.

SLOs and thresholds (recommended starting values)

- Local path: p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms for requests with input tokens <= 64.
- Classification decision: < 5ms 99.9% of the time.
- Central path for long requests: p95 < 1.2s (depends on model).
- Cache hit target: >= 30% of local short requests.
- Availability: local agent SLA (operator target) 99.95% during business hours.

Customer requirements (pre-install checklist)

- Network:
  - LAN RTT to local nodes: < 2ms typical.
  - WAN RTT from edge to regional aggregator/central: < 100ms for acceptable fallbacks.
  - Ports: outbound TCP 443 to Redwood control plane and dedicated pool endpoints; open internal gRPC/TCP ports 8000–8020 for inter-agent.
- Hardware:
  - Edge GPU: minimum 4GB VRAM (recommended 8GB) for quantized variants; or 32 vCPUs with AVX512 for CPU-only sites.
  - Disk: 50GB NVMe for model shards, cache, and logs.
- Security & keys:
  - Customer KMS reachable in VPC for secret unwrap; HSM integration optional for FIPS customers.
  - Mutually-authenticated TLS between agents and central (mTLS).
- IAM & service accounts:
  - Dedicated service account with least privilege; key rotation schedule defined.

Deployment playbook (edge node)

1) Preflight (operator):
   - Validate hardware profile: run collect_hardware_profile.sh and store output.
   - Verify network rules and KMS access.
   - Ensure time sync (NTP) and kernel parameters per infra-standards.
2) Install agent:
   - curl -sSL https://packages.redwood.ai/agent/install.sh | sudo bash -s -- --profile=latency-edge
   - Configure /etc/redwood/agent.yaml with:
     - model_variant: "rw-small-quant-v1"
     - kv_cache.ttl: 24h
     - router.policy: "token-count-based"
3) Smoke test:
   - redwoodctl healthcheck --target localhost:8000
   - Run quick latency benchmark: redwoodctl bench --requests 500 --max_tokens 64
4) Canary rollout:
   - Update router policy to include 10% forced central routing to validate end-to-end. Monitor errors for 2 hours before full promotion.

Day-2 operations (operator runbook)

- Monitoring and alerting:
  - Metrics to watch: local_latency_ms (p50/p95), classifier_decision_latency_ms, kv_cache_hit_rate, model_load_errors, agent_restarts.
  - Alerts: kv_cache_hit_rate < 15% sustained 30m; agent_restarts > 3 in 10m; local p95 > 200ms for 15m.
- Cache warmup strategy:
  - Scheduled synthetic workloads during quiet hours to prefill KV caches for top 200 prompt templates.
- Capacity scaling:
  - When local CPU/GPU utilization > 70% for 30m, auto-scale policy triggers adding a hot-spare agent.
- Quality checks:
  - Sampled 1% of local replies are sent to central for quality comparison; alert if semantic drift > threshold.

Upgrade and rollback procedures

- Upgrade (agent or local model):
  1. Stage new model variant in a canary edge node (1 node). Run bench and quality regression checks against a saved prompt set.
  2. Gradually increase canary percentage (1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100%) over multiple hours while monitoring p95 latency and quality diffs.
  3. If quality regression or latency spike occurs, trigger rollback.

- Rollback (fast-fail path):
  - Command: redwoodctl agent rollback --target <agent-id> --to-version <previous-model-semilabel>
  - Automated safety: controller evaluates health for 10m before rolling to additional nodes; if failure persists, enact tenant traffic drain to central with retained cache headers.
  - Evidence capture: collect /var/log/redwood/agent.log, heap profiles, last 100 classifier traces. Attach to incident ticket.

Failure modes and mitigation

- Local model OOM: automatic restart into degraded/no-local mode (route to central) and notify operator.
- KV cache corruption: fallback to central until cache TTL expires and warmup job restores entries.
- Network partition (edge->central): operate in isolated mode, local-only responses; flag degraded quality for audit.

Observability and telemetry contract

- Required metrics exported via Prometheus endpoint:
  - redwood_local_request_latency_ms_bucket
  - redwood_classifier_decision_latency_ms_bucket
  - redwood_kv_cache_hit_ratio
  - redwood_model_load_duration_seconds
- Traces: OpenTelemetry spans must include decision.reason, route.tier, model.variant, and cache.hit boolean.
- Logs: structured JSON lines with request_id, tenant_id, model_variant, duration_ms, and error_codes. Retention default 30d (configurable).

Security & compliance notes

- All model artifacts signed and verified using the customer-side image attestation. See image signing playbook for commands.
- Sensitive telemetry (user prompts) must be redacted before external transmission; send only hashed prompt fingerprints when required.
- Key rotation: follow a 90-day rotation schedule and ensure agents gracefully reload keys without restart.

Ownership and contact points

- Architecture owner: Priya Natarajan (eng-serving-runtime).
- Support handoff: Private Deployments Field Team (private-deploy-support@redwood.ai).
- Oncall escalation: see eng-sre oncall roster; page: /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotation.

Appendix: example router policy (YAML)

---
policy_name: latency_fanout_v1
rules:
  - name: local_short_request
    condition: "input_tokens <= 64 and tenant_priority == 'standard'"
    action: LOCAL
    sampling: 0.01   # 1% sampled to central for QA
  - name: cache_only
    condition: "cache.hit == true"
    action: LOCAL
  - name: heavy_request
    condition: "input_tokens > 64 or requires_retrieval == true"
    action: CENTRAL
  - name: default_fallback
    action: CENTRAL

References and related reading

- Private deploy observability contract: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides/observability-contracts-and-log-sampling-strategy-2026
- Image attestation and signing: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/image-attestation-spec-2026
- Cold start mitigation: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/cold-start-latency-mitigation-for-private-deploys-2026

Change log

- 2026-03-15: Initial draft.
- 2026-06-10: Added SLO targets and cache warmup strategy after field trials.
- 2026-10-12: Finalized rollback commands and telemetry contract.

title: Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge Reference Architecture
preview:
Overview:
This reference architecture describes the Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge: a secure, operator-friendly pattern for capturing tenant traffic traces, KV-cache snapshots, and control-plane metadata from private VPC and on-prem Redwood Private deployments for post-incident analysis, regulatory audits, and model-drift investigations. The design balances minimal performance impact, strong chain-of-custody guarantees, and customer privacy controls.

Goals:
- Provide an immutable, auditable snapshot pipeline for tenant state and request traces.
- Ensure snapshots can be collected with <=5% added tail-latency under normal load via asynchronous capture.
- Maintain tenant data isolation: all forensic snapshots are encrypted with customer KMS/HSM keys where required.
- Offer deterministic retention and evidence packaging for audits (customer-facing artifact).

When to use this architecture:
- Post-incident root cause analysis for private deployments where customer operators require local forensics.
- Regulatory evidence requests (e.g., finserv, healthcare) where full request-correspondence is needed.
- Model quality regression cases where sequence-level KV-caches and prefix traces are necessary to reproduce outputs.

High-level components:
1) Local Capture Agents (agent-snap): per-host process that performs non-blocking request tap, kv-cache snapshot coordination, and local staging.
2) Snapshot Bridge (bridge-proxy): tenant-scoped gateway that aggregates artifacts, signs bundles, and performs envelope encryption for transfer.
3) Forensic Store (immutable-store): append-only object store (S3-compatible) with versioned buckets and retention rules; may be hosted on customer object storage or Redwood-managed vault.
4) Evidence Broker (evidence-api): control-plane service to register retention policies, initiate snapshots, and produce audit manifests.
5) Operator Console (private console extension): UI + APIs for authorized operators to request, view, and package evidence bundles.

Architecture diagram (text):
- Tenant traffic -> Agent-snap (in host network namespace) -> local staging area -> Bridge-proxy (tenant egress to object store) -> Immutable-store (encrypted)
- Evidence Broker coordinates via control-plane channel (signed requests, attestation tokens)

Key design decisions and rationale:
- Asynchronous capture: Agent-snap never blocks request processing. Synchronous forcing would violate latency SLOs.
- Envelope encryption with customer KMS: ensures Redwood cannot read snapshots when customer requires BYOK.
- Minimal sensitive PII in bundled artifacts: by default we capture request-id, tokenized input/response, kv-cache state (configurable). Full plaintext capture requires explicit customer opt-in and attestation.

Detailed flow (step-by-step):
1) Trigger: Evidence request can be operator-initiated (console) or automated (alert rule). Evidence Broker validates the request, checks operator RBAC, and issues a short-lived signed attestation token to bridge-proxy.
2) Local Coordination: Agent-snap receives attestation token and enters \"capture window\" for the tenant (configurable 30s–10m). During the window it records non-blocking traces and initiates a consistent KV-cache snapshot across replicas using the runtime snapshot API.
3) Bundle creation: Agent-snap composes a manifest.json (metadata), a compressed trace file (.ndjson.gz), and a serialized kv-cache snapshot (.snap.gz). Each file is hashed (SHA-256) and recorded.
4) Signing & Encryption: Bridge-proxy validates attestation, signs the manifest with a bridge key, then performs envelope-encryption: file encrypted with data key; data key encrypted with customer KMS public key or Redwood-managed key depending on policy.
5) Upload & Index: Encrypted bundle uploaded to Immutable-store and indexed in Evidence Broker with metadata: tenant-id, originating host, time-range, SLOs at capture time, operator id. Evidence Broker generates an audit entry and exportable chain-of-custody record.
6) Retrieval: Authorized operators request retrieval. Evidence Broker verifies RBAC, returns decryption instructions (if Redwood-managed) or redirects to pre-signed customer storage download links (if BYOK), and records the retrieval event.

Operational SLOs and thresholds:
- Capture startup latency: agent-snap start < 200ms (metric: agent.capture.startup_ms P50/P95).
- Impact on tail latency: added P99 latency <= 5% of baseline for requests during capture windows.
- Upload success rate: 99.9% of bundles must reach Immutable-store within 2 minutes under normal network conditions.
- Chain-of-custody integrity: manifest and bundle signatures validated at time of retrieval or audit.

Alerting and monitoring (suggested):
- Alerts: agent.capture.failure_rate > 0.5% for 5m -> PagerDuty (oncall-owner: runtime-team)
- Alerts: bundle.upload.latency_p95 > 120s for 10m -> SRE team
- Dashboards: per-tenant capture activity, bundle sizes distribution, retention occupancy by customer, failed-encryption rates.

Operator runbooks (concise):
Runbook A — Investigate missing bundle:
1) Check Evidence Broker UI for \"bundle failed\" events. Note bundle_id.
2) SSH to originating host (use operator jumpbox): sudo journalctl -u agent-snap | grep bundle_id.
3) Validate local manifest and hash: sha256sum manifest.json -> compare with Evidence Broker index.
4) If encryption failed: check KMS connectivity (kms-proxy logs) and re-run bundle re-encrypt command: 
   bridge-proxy reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --retries 3
5) After fix, mark bundle re-uploaded in Evidence Broker and verify presence in Immutable-store.

Runbook B — Produce customer audit package:
1) Operator opens Evidence Broker -> select tenant -> time-range -> generate audit package.
2) Evidence Broker returns package_id and a pre-signed download link or decryption instructions.
3) For BYOK: instruct customer to perform local key unwrap and verify manifest signature.
4) Record the audit package delivery in customer QBR artifact store and notify the customer success lead.

Upgrade and rollback procedure (safe-to-execute checklist):
- Scope: agent-snap and bridge-proxy are the only services needing coordinated upgrade. Evidence Broker schema changes are backward-compatible.
- Preflight checks before upgrade:
  - Run synthetic capture test on staging tenant for baseline.
  - Verify backup of current agent config and bridge-proxy signing keys.
  - Confirm Immutable-store retention policies and available disk.
- Rolling upgrade steps (zero-downtime):
  1) Drain node A worker pool for bridge-proxy (k8s: cordon && drain).
  2) Deploy new bridge-proxy container (image tag).
  3) Smoke-test: bridge-proxy self-test endpoint /_health and a synthetic bundle upload.
  4) Uncordon node and proceed to next node.
  5) Staged agent-snap rollout: update DaemonSet with new image and a 30s pause between nodes for warm-cache verification.
- Rollback: revert DaemonSet and deployment images to previous tags. If manifest schema incompatible, Evidence Broker will reject reads; use the compatibility tool: evidence-cli reconcile --bundle <bundle_id> --downgrade-manifest.

Security and compliance considerations:
- All artifacts are signed and hashed; manifests include operator id, timestamp, and attestation token.
- Retention rules are enforced via Immutable-store lifecycle policies; customer-managed retention overrides Redwood defaults.
- Access control: RBAC enforced at Evidence Broker; retrieval requires two-person authorization for restricted datasets.
- For air-gapped/on-prem deployments: snapshot bundles remain on customer storage by default; Redwood-managed transfer is disabled unless customer enables egress and provides attestation.

Customer preflight & requirements checklist (operator-facing):
| Requirement | Minimum | Rationale |
|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Host disk staging | 10 GB per host | Temporary storage for bundle assembly and compression |
| Network egress | 10 Mbps sustained per bridge-proxy | Ensures bundle uploads during capture windows |
| KMS/HSM | Optional BYOK | Required for customers that restrict provider access |
| Time sync | NTP or equivalent, max skew 2s | Chain-of-custody timestamp correctness |
| Operator roles | 2 contacts with private-console access | For two-person approval flow |

Configuration examples (snippets):
- agent-snap capture window (k8s DaemonSet env):
  AGENT_CAPTURE_WINDOW=60 # seconds
  AGENT_MAX_STAGING_SIZE_MB=10240
- bridge-proxy config (yaml):
  encryption:
    mode: envelope
    kms_provider: customer-kms # options: customer-kms, redwood-kms
  upload:
    bucket: \"tenant-forensics-prod\"
    multipart_threshold_mb: 50

CLI examples:
- Initiate an evidence capture for tenant-123 for 120s via Evidence Broker CLI:
  evidence-cli capture --tenant tenant-123 --duration 120 --initiator jdoe@redwood.com
- Re-encrypt a failed bundle on bridge-proxy host:
  sudo bridge-proxyctl reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --force

Ownership and contacts:
- Runtime team (primary): runtime-team@redwood.com — owner for agent-snap and runtime snapshot APIs.
- Observability team (secondary): observability@redwood.com — owner for dashboards and alerting.
- Security & Compliance: security-compliance@redwood.com — owner for chain-of-custody and retention policies.

Appendix A — Example manifest.json (illustrative):
{
  \"bundle_id\": \"bndl-20260915-7f3a\",
  \"tenant_id\": \"tenant-123\",
  \"start_time\": \"2026-09-15T22:10:00Z\",
  \"end_time\": \"2026-09-15T22:12:00Z\",
  \"files\": [\"traces.ndjson.gz\", \"kvcache.snap.gz\"],
  \"hashes\": {\"traces.ndjson.gz\": \"<sha256>\"},
  \"signed_by\": \"bridge-proxy-1\"
}

Appendix B — Frequently asked questions:
Q: Will enabling forensic capture increase costs?
A: Yes — storage, egress (if enabled), and operator time. We provide estimated per-1000-captures cost models during onboarding.

Q: Can customers choose which fields to redact?
A: Yes — redaction policy is configurable per-tenant and applied at agent-snap before bundle assembly. Redaction logs are included in the manifest.

Related artefacts and references:
- Runtime snapshot API spec: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/snapshot-api-spec-2025
- Immutable-store lifecycle policies: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/immutable-store-policies-2026
- Operator RBAC matrix: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/operator-rbac-matrix-2025

Revision history:
- 2026-01-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-04-03: Added chain-of-custody signing details per security review.
- 2026-09-15: Published with BYOK and air-gap clarifications and additional runbooks.

4) If encryption failed: check KMS connectivity (kms-proxy logs) and re-run bundle re-encrypt command: 
   bridge-proxy reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --retries 3
5) After fix, mark bundle re-uploaded in Evidence Broker and verify presence in Immutable-store.

Runbook B — Produce customer audit package:
1) Operator opens Evidence Broker -> select tenant -> time-range -> generate audit package.
2) Evidence Broker returns package_id and a pre-signed download link or decryption instructions.
3) For BYOK: instruct customer to perform local key unwrap and verify manifest signature.
4) Record the audit package delivery in customer QBR artifact store and notify the customer success lead.

Upgrade and rollback procedure (safe-to-execute checklist):
- Scope: agent-snap and bridge-proxy are the only services needing coordinated upgrade. Evidence Broker schema changes are backward-compatible.
- Preflight checks before upgrade:
  - Run synthetic capture test on staging tenant for baseline.
  - Verify backup of current agent config and bridge-proxy signing keys.
  - Confirm Immutable-store retention policies and available disk.
- Rolling upgrade steps (zero-downtime):
  1) Drain node A worker pool for bridge-proxy (k8s: cordon && drain).
  2) Deploy new bridge-proxy container (image tag).
  3) Smoke-test: bridge-proxy self-test endpoint /_health and a synthetic bundle upload.
  4) Uncordon node and proceed to next node.
  5) Staged agent-snap rollout: update DaemonSet with new image and a 30s pause between nodes for warm-cache verification.
- Rollback: revert DaemonSet and deployment images to previous tags. If manifest schema incompatible, Evidence Broker will reject reads; use the compatibility tool: evidence-cli reconcile --bundle <bundle_id> --downgrade-manifest.

Security and compliance considerations:
- All artifacts are signed and hashed; manifests include operator id, timestamp, and attestation token.
- Retention rules are enforced via Immutable-store lifecycle policies; customer-managed retention overrides Redwood defaults.
- Access control: RBAC enforced at Evidence Broker; retrieval requires two-person authorization for restricted datasets.
- For air-gapped/on-prem deployments: snapshot bundles remain on customer storage by default; Redwood-managed transfer is disabled unless customer enables egress and provides attestation.

Customer preflight & requirements checklist (operator-facing):
| Requirement | Minimum | Rationale |
|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Host disk staging | 10 GB per host | Temporary storage for bundle assembly and compression |
| Network egress | 10 Mbps sustained per bridge-proxy | Ensures bundle uploads during capture windows |
| KMS/HSM | Optional BYOK | Required for customers that restrict provider access |
| Time sync | NTP or equivalent, max skew 2s | Chain-of-custody timestamp correctness |
| Operator roles | 2 contacts with private-console access | For two-person approval flow |

Configuration examples (snippets):
- agent-snap capture window (k8s DaemonSet env):
  AGENT_CAPTURE_WINDOW=60 # seconds
  AGENT_MAX_STAGING_SIZE_MB=10240
- bridge-proxy config (yaml):
  encryption:
    mode: envelope
    kms_provider: customer-kms # options: customer-kms, redwood-kms
  upload:
    bucket: \"tenant-forensics-prod\"
    multipart_threshold_mb: 50

CLI examples:
- Initiate an evidence capture for tenant-123 for 120s via Evidence Broker CLI:
  evidence-cli capture --tenant tenant-123 --duration 120 --initiator jdoe@redwood.com
- Re-encrypt a failed bundle on bridge-proxy host:
  sudo bridge-proxyctl reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --force

Ownership and contacts:
- Runtime team (primary): runtime-team@redwood.com — owner for agent-snap and runtime snapshot APIs.
- Observability team (secondary): observability@redwood.com — owner for dashboards and alerting.
- Security & Compliance: security-compliance@redwood.com — owner for chain-of-custody and retention policies.

Appendix A — Example manifest.json (illustrative):
{
  \"bundle_id\": \"bndl-20260915-7f3a\",
  \"tenant_id\": \"tenant-123\",
  \"start_time\": \"2026-09-15T22:10:00Z\",
  \"end_time\": \"2026-09-15T22:12:00Z\",
  \"files\": [\"traces.ndjson.gz\", \"kvcache.snap.gz\"],
  \"hashes\": {\"traces.ndjson.gz\": \"<sha256>\"},
  \"signed_by\": \"bridge-proxy-1\"
}

Appendix B — Frequently asked questions:
Q: Will enabling forensic capture increase costs?
A: Yes — storage, egress (if enabled), and operator time. We provide estimated per-1000-captures cost models during onboarding.

Q: Can customers choose which fields to redact?
A: Yes — redaction policy is configurable per-tenant and applied at agent-snap before bundle assembly. Redaction logs are included in the manifest.

Related artefacts and references:
- Runtime snapshot API spec: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/snapshot-api-spec-2025
- Immutable-store lifecycle policies: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/immutable-store-policies-2026
- Operator RBAC matrix: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/operator-rbac-matrix-2025

Revision history:
- 2026-01-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-04-03: Added chain-of-custody signing details per security review.
- 2026-09-15: Published with BYOK and air-gap clarifications and additional runbooks.

title: Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator
preview:
Summary:

This document defines the design, operational runbook, verification checks, and customer-facing templates for Redwood's Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator (ACIR-SIG). ACIR-SIG produces standardized, auditable customer incident summaries within a defined SLA window after an incident is classified as customer-impacting. The system computes SLO impact automatically from telemetry and timeline evidence, assembles a concise narrative, and supports automated delivery to customers (via Console, email, or support ticket) with human-in-the-loop gating.

Goals and scope:
- Goal: Reduce time-to-customer-summary while preserving accuracy, traceability, and blameless language.
- Scope: infra and inference incidents that affect production API responses (latency, availability, errors). Excludes: billing, account-level policy, or non-production incidents.
- Output: structured incident summary (human-readable + machine JSON payload) and an SLO impact statement.

Ownership and roles:
1) SRE oncall: responsible for verification and approval when gating is required.
2) Incident commander (IC): final sign-off for any customer report that contains mitigation details or security-sensitive statements.
3) Customer Success (CS): decides routing and cadence for customer notifications and escalations.
4) Reporting service (service-owner: ops-tooling): implements ACIR-SIG automation and maintains templates.

Key constraints and principles:
- Always calculate SLO impact from the canonical SLO aggregator (slo-aggregate-service) and telemetry (tracing, api-metrics, regional-health). Do not use local approximations.
- Maintain a human-in-the-loop for incidents where the automated confidence < 92% (see confidence rules).
- Use standardized, blameless language and give measurable impact (requests affected, time windows, and computed %SLO burn).
- Store evidence pointers (trace IDs, query snippets, dashboards) in the report payload for audits and follow-ups.

SLO impact computation (algorithm):
- Inputs:
  - timeseries of successful requests S(t) and total requests T(t) at 1s aggregation or the highest fidelity available.
  - policy: SLO target (e.g., availability 99.9% over 30d) and SLO window used for customer-facing burn calculation (typically rolling-30d).
  - incident window [t_start, t_end] defined by timeline service.
- Steps:
  1) Compute baseline SLO compliance before the incident: baseline_compliance = sum_{t < t_start}(S(t))/sum_{t < t_start}(T(t)) over a baseline period (default 24h).
  2) Compute incident compliance: incident_compliance = sum_{t in [t_start,t_end]}(S(t))/sum_{t in [t_start,t_end]}(T(t)).
  3) Convert to SLO burn for the rolling window:
     - delta_unavailability = max(0, target - incident_compliance) * (duration_of_window_weight)
     - approximate SLO%_burn = (sum_{window} expected_bad_seconds_from_incident) / (total_window_seconds * (1 - target)) * 100
- Simplified formula (used by ACIR-SIG for short reports):
  SLO_burn_percent = round(100 * ((target - incident_compliance) * incident_duration_seconds) / (rolling_window_seconds * (1 - target)), 2)
- Example:
  - target = 99.9% (0.999), rolling_window_seconds = 30*24*3600 = 2,592,000s, incident duration = 900s, incident_compliance = 0.95.
  - SLO_burn_percent = 100 * ((0.999 - 0.95) * 900) / (2,592,000 * (1 - 0.999)) = 100 * (0.049 * 900) / (2,592,000 * 0.001) = 100 * 44.1 / 2592 = 1.70% (rounded).

Confidence scoring and gating rules:
- Confidence inputs (scored 0–100):
  - telemetry_coverage (percentage of requests covered by high-fidelity metrics): weight 40
  - timeline_agreement (percent overlap between automated detection and timeline boundaries): weight 20
  - anomaly_correlation (correlation of errors/latency with topology/event): weight 20
  - evidence_linkage (presence of traces/logs matching top error signatures): weight 20
- Final confidence = weighted_sum(components).
- Gating thresholds:
  - confidence >= 92: auto-generate and auto-send short customer summary (no mitigation detail) after 2h post-incident verification window.
  - 75 <= confidence < 92: auto-generate draft; require SRE oncall verification and IC approval.
  - confidence < 75: do not generate automated customer content; produce internal report only.

Automated customer summary template (short):
- Title: (Service) Incident Summary — (YYYY-MM-DD) — (region)
- One-line impact: During (time window), customers experienced increased (errors/latency/availability) affecting approximately N requests (~X% of requests) in (region).
- What happened: brief description of root cause or leading hypothesis (automated, with confidence).
- What we did: mitigation steps taken and timeframe.
- SLO impact: SLO_burn_percent for rolling window and absolute requests affected.
- Links & evidence: dashboard links, trace IDs, support ticket link for follow-up.
- Next steps: planned remediation and expected ETA when available.

Full machine payload (JSON) — example snippet for automation (ACIR-SIG sends this to CS or support):
{
  \"incident_id\": \"INC-2026-1234\",
  \"service\": \"inference-api\",
  \"region\": \"us-west-2\",
  \"window\": \"2026-08-14T15:12:00Z/2026-08-14T15:27:00Z\",
  \"requests_affected\": \"18234\",
  \"approx_pct_requests\": \"3.6\",
  \"slo_burn_pct\": \"1.7\",
  \"confidence\": \"94\",
  \"evidence\": [\"dash://api-availability/overview\", \"trace://abc123\"],
  \"narrative\": \"A partial control-plane configuration regression caused router miss-paths leading to elevated 5xxs for 15 minutes. Mitigation: rollback of config at T+6.\"
}

Automation architecture (high level):
- Inputs:
  - slo-aggregate-service (canonical SLO metrics)
  - telemetry-mesh (metrics/traces/logs)
  - timeline-service (incident boundaries and annotations)
  - incident-bus (events from PagerDuty/ops)
- Components:
  - generator-worker: computes SLO burn and assembles payloads.
  - confidence-evaluator: calculates confidence and determines gating.
  - draft-store: stores auto-drafts for human review (if needed).
  - delivery-orchestrator: posts to Console, creates CS ticket, or sends email.
- Safety layers:
  - rate limiter: ensures only one auto-customer report per incident, and no more than one per hour for the same customer group.
  - redaction module: strips PII or security-sensitive fields per policy.
  - audit logger: immutable storage of generated payloads and final send event (retention policy defined below).

Runbook: step-by-step operator flow when a draft is generated and human gate required:
1) Alert: oncall receives automated draft notification (Slack + Console).
2) Quick checks (5–10 min): verify top evidence links, ensure traces contain indicative errors, confirm region and affected customers.
3) Approve or reject: Approve via Console button to publish; reject sends back to draft with reason.
4) If approved, CS will receive the published summary and follow-up engagement steps are initiated automatically.
5) If rejected, SRE tags draft with notes and sets auto-resend window to 2h.

Manual override and safety escalation:
- IC can hold customer notifications for up to 24h for incidents involving legal, compliance, or high-sensitivity customers.
- If an automated summary is already sent and later found inaccurate, publish a correction using the same payload schema and include a clear correction header.

Verification checklist (pre-send):
- [ ] Evidence pointers present (>=2 of {dashboard, trace, logs})
- [ ] SLO computation cross-checked by slo-aggregate-service snapshot
- [ ] PII redaction module passed (redaction=false means pass)
- [ ] Confidence >= configured threshold for auto-send or IC approval obtained
- [ ] Delivery channel contact list validated for affected customer segments

Operational observability and metrics for ACIR-SIG itself:
- Metric: acir.reports.generated (count) — label by outcome: auto_sent/draft/rejected/corrected
- Metric: acir.processing.latency (ms) — time from incident-closed to draft ready
- Metric: acir.confidence.distribution — histogram of confidence scores
- Alert: if acir.processing.latency P95 > 45min for 3 hours => paging to ops-tooling

Retention and audit policy:
- Generated payloads and evidence pointers retained for 365 days in immutable audit store.
- Access to full payloads limited by RBAC: engineering/incident-review and CS lead roles, security/legal by approval.

Security and privacy notes:
- Redaction rules are centrally controlled: do not include customer payloads, secrets, or raw logs in customer-facing narrative. Evidence pointers are allowed but must not embed credentials.
- For incidents involving regulated customers, default confidentiality escalates to 'restricted' and a manual IC approval is required before any outgoing message.

Testing and validation cadence:
- Quarterly TT (tabletop) exercises: validate automated outputs against mock incidents.
- Monthly synthetic incidents: run ACIR-SIG in shadow mode to measure drift in confidence and SLO calculation correctness.

Rollout and migration plan for first production release:
1) Phase 1 (shadow): 2 months, run in draft-only mode for a subset (20%) of incidence types.
2) Phase 2 (co-pilot): human-in-the-loop gating required for all drafts; telemetry and metrics validated.
3) Phase 3 (autorelease): enable auto-send for confidence >= 92; monitor metrics and enable roll-back flag for first 30 days.

Appendix A — Example customer-facing short summary (final):
Service: Redwood Inference — Hosted API
Date: 2026-08-14
Impact window: 15:12–15:27 UTC
Summary: Between 15:12–15:27 UTC customers in us-west-2 experienced elevated 5xx errors affecting approximately 18k requests (~3.6% of normal request volume). We rolled back a recent control-plane configuration change at 15:18 which restored normal routing by 15:18:30 and fully recovered by 15:27.
SLO impact: Estimated to consume 1.7% of the 30-day availability budget for Hosted API (99.9% target).
Links: Dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/incident-1234 Trace: trace://abc123 Ticket: https://support.redwood.ai/t/INC-2026-1234
Next steps: Root cause analysis in progress; RCA will be published within 72 hours.

Appendix B — Quick references:
- SLO aggregation playground: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/slo-aggregate-playground
- Incident timeline canonicalization: /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review/incident-hypothesis-repro-environment-spec-2024
- PII redaction policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-management/redaction-policy

Questions or feedback: reach out to the ops-tooling owners (ops-tooling@redwood.ai) or file improvements via the Console suggestions flow.

Change log:
- 2026-01-10: Initial draft (Priya Natarajan)
- 2026-04-02: Added confidence scoring schema and gating thresholds (Jonah Meyer)
- 2026-08-15: Added rollout plan, retention table, and expanded examples (Sofia Alvarez)

title: Emergent Behavior Lab — Operator Simulation Recipes and Dashboard Library
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This page documents a repeatable lab for reproducing and triaging emergent platform behaviors (e.g., cross-tenant KV cache storms, prefix-cache thrash, inter-model tail-latency cascades) and the dashboard artifacts operators should use during exercises and incidents. The lab is designed for platform operators, SREs, and reliability engineers who run postmortems, runbooks, and training.

Scope: covers simulation recipes, dashboard templates, runbook fragments, operator checklists, curriculum for training sessions, and a short roadmap for expanding the lab. This is not a step-by-step system upgrade guide; see the platform runbooks for those procedures.

Goals
- Provide deterministic, reproducible scenarios that manifest realistic emergent failure modes in the runtime.
- Produce operator dashboard views that surface root-cause signals quickly.
- Codify remediation sequences and guarded rollback paths for each scenario.
- Use the lab for quarterly operator training and retro-grade validation.

Safety and constraints
- Always run simulations in the lab tenancy or a dedicated non-production region. Never run high-impact scenarios in regions with live production traffic.
- Each recipe includes kill-switch steps and timeboxed windows. Default simulation window: 15 minutes.
- Customer-sensitive datasets are not allowed; use synthetic prompt sets and seeded synthetic tenants.

Owner and contacts
- Lab owner: Priya Natarajan (Platform Reliability)
- Primary operators: Platform Operator Guild (pager: #platform-ops)
- Escalation: Eng-SRE on-call -> Platform Lead (paging policy documented in /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-handbook)

Simulation Recipes (high level)
1) KV prefix cache storm (short-tail eviction)
- Objective: cause heavy prefix-cache eviction across a shard cohort to observe cold-start latency and downstream queueing.
- Preconditions: lab kv-cache configured with 4 shards; synthetic tenant cohort of 20 tenants.
- Steps:
  1. Seed workloads: run prefix-generator tool (see CLI below) to create 10k unique prefixes across tenants over 60s.
  2. Start a rapid churn workload: 30 RPS per tenant for 120s with random prompt prefixes.
  3. At t=45s, inject a throttled background prefetch disable command to force read-throughs (operator flag: runtime.prefetch.enabled=false).
  4. Monitor dashboards for eviction-rate, cold-start P50/P95/P99, and prefix-miss ratio.
- Expected signals:
  - Eviction-rate spikes > 10k/s.
  - Cold-start P99 > 800ms for the model-serving layer.
  - Request queue length increases on shard nodes.
- Guard rails:
  - Kill-switch: runtime.prefetch.enabled=true; warmup sweep script run; abort workload.

2) Cross-tenant token-burst cascade
- Objective: replicate cross-tenant token surges causing multi-tenant scheduler inversion and unfair throughput.
- Preconditions: multi-tenant allocator with soft-weights; quota-buckets set.
- Steps:
  1. Create 3 high-priority tenants (A/B/C) and 20 low-priority tenants.
  2. Ramp Tenant A from 0 -> 300 RPS within 30s while simultaneously starting Tenant B/C at 200 RPS each.
  3. Observe tenant fairness metrics, allocator preemption rate, and tenant-drop events.
  4. Trigger a burst-mitigation script that applies temporary weights to rebalance (operator action).
- Expected signals:
  - Soft-weight overrides engaged; tenant-throttle events logged.
  - SLO burn for affected low-priority tenants.
- Guard rails:
  - Auto-scaler override (operator): scale-dedicated-pool --tenant=low-priority --add 2 GPUs
  - If scale fails, move low-priority tenants to cold lane via routing rule.

3) Kernel-swap jitter and JIT kernel fallback
- Objective: cause a runtime kernel selection misprediction leading to increased per-token latency and GPU utilization spikes.
- Preconditions: runtime configured with JIT kernel switch enabled.
- Steps:
  1. Deploy a model variant with a new kernel profile to the canary pool.
  2. Feed mixed sequence-length traffic (mean 1024, variance 200) for 10 minutes.
  3. Force a partial kernel mismatch via a mocked sequence-length spike generator.
  4. Observe kernel-swap counts, per-kernel latency histograms, and GPU SM utilization.
- Expected signals: kernel-swap rate > baseline by 5x, P99 token latency uplift.
- Rollback: revert kernel profile in canary and trigger warmup pass.

Dashboard Library (templates)
- Purpose: prebuilt dashboard panels for rapid triage. Each panel should be grouped into an incident view: Health, Admission, Scheduling, Execution, Cost.

Suggested panels (table):
| Group | Panel name | Important KPIs | Thresholds | Suggested pinning |
| Health | Cluster node-loss | node_down_count, avg_node_load | node_down_count > 0 | Top-left |
| Admission | Token-admit-rate | admitted_tokens/s, admission_latency_p50/p99 | admission_latency_p99 > 200ms | Top row |
| Scheduling | Allocator-preemption-rate | preemptions/s, tenant_eviction_count | preemptions/s > 5 | Middle |
| Execution | GPU-sm-utilization | gpu_sm_pct, per_gpu_queue_length | sm_pct > 90% | Middle-right |
| Cost | Token-cost-delta | token_cost/minute, token_rate_change_1m | token_cost/min > +5% baseline | Bottom row |

Dashboard variables and derived metrics
- Derived metric: cold_start_ratio = cold_start_count / total_requests (use pipeline tag: request_type=cold_start).
- Variable examples: tenant (dropdown), model_version (dropdown), region, heap_shard_id.

Runbook fragments (actionable sequences)
- Triage template (first 5 minutes):
  1. Identify scope: use tenant variable to check cross-tenant impact.
  2. Isolate admission vs execution: compare admission_latency_p99 vs token-p90-exec-latency.
  3. If admission_latency_p99 high -> investigate admission queue and upstream rate limits.
  4. If execution latency high and GPU sm > 85% -> consider soft-eviction and scale-out.
  5. Document all operator actions in the incident log with timestamps.

- Eviction mitigation sequence (when eviction-rate abnormal):
  1. Apply tenant soft-weights to the top offending tenants: allocator.apply_weights --tenant <id> --weight 0.5
  2. If prefetch disabled, re-enable prefetch per shard: runtime.prefetch.enabled=true
  3. Warm up evicted prefixes via warmup-sweeper: warmup-sweeper --shard <id> --duration 180
  4. Monitor cold_start_ratio until it returns to baseline.

Operator checklists (to be printed in on-call pack)
- Pre-run checks before simulation: verify lab tenancy, check quota tokens, ensure canary flag off, confirm SSO tokens valid.
- During run checks: confirm kill-switch availability, maintain Slack channel for play-by-play, record timestamps for critical metric inflection points.
- Post-run checks: run telemetry sanity sweep, archive dashboard snapshots, create a retro note and attach incident artifacts.

Training curriculum (quarterly)
- 0:30 — Intro and safety brief.
- 1:00 — Walkthrough of dashboard templates and variables.
- 2:00 — Run one KV prefix cache storm scenario (operators practice triage).
- 3:00 — Run one token-burst cascade (coverage of allocator actions).
- 4:00 — Debrief and fill out training assessment form.

Retro and expected outcomes
- Each simulation run requires a brief retro: what signals were missing, which dashboards were misleading, operator cognitive load items (time to decision), and automation candidates.
- Capture agreed action items with owners and due dates in the lab backlog (Board: platform-lab/EMERGENT-LAB).

Telemetry and instrumentation checklist (minimum)
- Required traces and metrics:
  - request.latency.{p50,p95,p99} (tagged by tenant, model_version)
  - runtime.kernel_swap.count (tagged by kernel_id, model_version)
  - kvcache.eviction_rate, kvcache.miss_rate, kvcache.prefetch_enabled
  - allocator.preemptions, allocator.weight_changes
  - gpu.sm_utilization, gpu.queue_length_per_device
- Logs: correlate service-request-id across admission -> scheduler -> executor. Include tenant id and model_version in log headers.

Sample CLI snippets
- Prefix generator (Python entrypoint):
  ./lab-tools/prefix-generator --tenants 20 --prefixes 10000 --duration 60 --rps 30

- Warmup sweep example (YAML job spec):
  job_name: warmup-sweep
  target_shards: [0,1,2,3]
  duration_seconds: 180
  concurrency: 4

Sample alert rules (text)
- Alert: KVCache-Eviction-Spike
  - Expr: increase(kvcache_eviction_rate[1m]) > 10000
  - For: 1m
  - Labels: severity=page, team=platform-ops
  - Runbook: link to eviction mitigation sequence above

Roadmap (next 12 months)
- Q3 2026: Add synthetic tenant fidelity matrix (mapping synthetic traffic to customer workload archetypes). Owner: Platform Lab.
- Q4 2026: Integrate lab scenarios with CI (PR gated synthetic smoke tests for runtime changes). Owner: Eng-Platform CI.
- Q1 2027: Expand dashboard templates to include a guided-runbook mode: in-dashboard operator actions and one-click kill-switch.

Maintenance and governance
- Lab assets (dashboards, scripts, workload definitions) live in repo: git.redwood/platform-lab (branch protected).
- Review cadence: quarterly review of scenarios and dashboards. Responsible: Platform Lab Steering committee.

Appendix: example incident timeline (abridged)
- 00:00 Start token-burst ramp (Tenant A)
- 00:45 Admission latency P99 begins to rise
- 01:10 Preemptions detected and low-priority SLOs burn
- 01:13 Operator applies soft-weight override
- 01:18 Low-priority SLOs stabilize, P99 admission latency improves
- 01:25 Warmup-sweeper started to remediate cold prefixes

References and links
- Platform lab repo: git.redwood/platform-lab
- Runbooks: /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks
- Dashboard gallery: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts

Revision history
- 2026-01-12 — doc created by Priya Natarajan
- 2026-04-02 — added kernel-swap recipe and dashboard table (reviewed by Liam Chen)
- 2026-07-15 — updated training curriculum and roadmap (reviewed by Marisol Vega)
