- rw.watermark.incoming.count
- rw.watermark.parsed.failure.count
- rw.shedding.action.count (labeled by action=Accept/Degrade/Partial/Reject)
- rw.latency.p99, rw.latency.p999 (compare with budget_ms quantiles)
- rw.fidelity.applied.count (type=quant/int8/early-exit/partial-cache)

Alerts:
1) P99 latency > budget_ms target for 5m AND increase in Degrade+Partial actions > 15% -> P1 page to runtime oncall.
2) Watermark parse failure rate > 1% -> P2 to api-router.

Client / API guidance
---------------------
- SDKs will include helper to encode watermarks and a validator for common mistakes.
- Customers are encouraged to set budget_ms for latency-sensitive routes and use priority tiers sparingly.
- Documented examples: short synchronous chat completions should set budget_ms 150-250ms for 99th percentile on hosted, embeddings can be higher.

Testing and compatibility
-------------------------
- Unit tests for encoder/decoder, property tests for budget rounding.
- Integration tests: synthetic traffic with mixed priorities asserting deterministic shedding fingerprints.
- Long-running chaos test: randomly induce GPU preemption and verify that shedding actions preserve SLOs better than baseline.

Security and privacy considerations
----------------------------------
- Watermark payload contains no customer PII by design. trace_id is ephemeral and used only for telemetry correlation; it must not be stored in logs beyond 30 days unless linked to an incident ticket.
- For private deployments: watermark enforcement can be disabled by configuration, but we strongly recommend enabling it for consistent behavior.

Open issues and future work
--------------------------
- v2 schema: compressed binary encoding to reduce header size and permit cryptographic signing for tamper resistance.
- Explore exposing an explicit API for clients to query expected budget_ms for a route (helpful for dynamic clients).
- Per-tenant adaptive budgets based on historical p99 consumption.

Owners and contact
------------------
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (runtime team)
- Implementation owner: Runtime scheduler and api-router teams (shared)
- Oncall escalation: see eng-sre runbook for runtime shedding (linked in related_pages).

References
----------
- See 'runtime-workload-signatures-and-slo-derivation-playbook' for deriving budget_ms per route.
- Related ADRs: request-hedging decision lattice; progressive degradation playbook.

Appendix: example X-RW-WM codec (pseudocode)
--------------------------------------------
1) Python encode:
   import json, base64
   payload = json.dumps({"v":"1","budget_ms":"200","fidelity_hint":"reduced","priority_tier":"standard","trace_id":"123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000"})
   header = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(payload.encode()).decode()

2) Edge decode validation rules:
   - parse base64 -> json, ensure v=="1"
   - budget_ms must be int between 50 and 10000
   - fidelity_hint must be one of allowed set
   - priority_tier must be known

This ADR is intentionally prescriptive at the schema and contract boundary while leaving algorithmic scheduling and exact thresholds for the runtime-team to refine with experiments.

title: Mitigation: Provisional credit reversal causing autopay hold (RevRec & Billing)
preview:
## Purpose
This page documents the operational mitigation and finance/legal guidance for incidents where a support-issued provisional credit is automatically reversed during reconciliation, leading to a negative invoice balance and an autopay hold. It consolidates learnings from SUP-121212 (Acme Health) and establishes a repeatable response for RevRec, Billing Engineering, and Customer Support.

## Background (what happened)
In SUP-121212, a provisional credit issued for hourly smoothing was reversed automatically during an overnight/hourly reconciliation run. The reversal produced a negative balance on the customer invoice and triggered an automated autopay hold. For a dedicated customer, the payment-hold state also restricted production API keys and degraded integrations.

### Reference timeline (example case: Acme Health)
- 2026-03-10 22:14 UTC: Support issued provisional credit (REF: PC-4431) for $125.00 USD.
- 2026-03-11 02:07 UTC: Hourly postback/reconciler job ran (run_id=20260311-0207).
- 2026-03-11 02:09 UTC: Accounting audit recorded a provisional credit reversal (reason=duplicate_credit_detected).
- 2026-03-11 03:30 UTC: Autopay hold applied due to negative balance (-$125.00).
- 2026-03-13 09:15 UTC: Manual credit applied to restore positive balance; autopay hold cleared; keys restored.

### Symptoms / signals
- Invoice shows a credit reversal line matching the amount of a recent provisional credit.
- Accounting audit trail contains events similar to: provisional_credit_reversal reason=duplicate_credit_detected.
- Reconciler logs warn about multiple postbacks for the same org/window (e.g., sources include both legacy and v1 identifiers).
- Finance automation applies autopay hold shortly after negative balance is computed.

## Root cause (technical + process)
### Primary technical root cause
A reconciler deduplication rule incorrectly matched postback source aliases and treated a support-issued provisional credit as a duplicate credit, triggering automatic reversal during reconciliation. The issue was exacerbated when both legacy and v1 postback sources were present for the same usage window.

### Contributing factors
- Dedup signature matching was tightened in the dedup rule set and did not account for legacy postback source aliases.
- Provisional credits issued via Support were not explicitly excluded from dedup reversal logic.
- Autopay hold automation acted on negative balances without an emergency override path for time-sensitive customer-impacting situations.

## Current status (as of last_updated)
- Engineering fix tracked under INT-4421 has been deployed to production to normalize/accept legacy postback aliases in signature matching.
- Dedup rule set has been updated to prevent automatic reversal of support-issued provisional credits when the credit is tagged as Support-issued and within an allowed adjustment window.
- Finance automation policy has been updated for dedicated customers to allow a limited “emergency release” process with Billing approval for small amounts when customer availability is impacted.

## Immediate mitigation playbook (when an incident occurs)
### 1) Confirm the condition
1. Identify the most recent provisional credit ID (e.g., PC-####) and amount.
2. Confirm reversal in accounting audit trail and/or invoice lines.
3. Confirm the autopay hold trigger reason is negative_balance (and note the amount).
4. Confirm whether the customer is dedicated and whether keys/endpoints are restricted.

Evidence to collect (attach to the case):
- Invoice ID, org_id, and the credit + reversal line items.
- Reconciler run_id and time window.
- Accounting audit event(s) for credit issuance and reversal.

### 2) Stabilize customer operations
Goal: remove payment-hold restrictions as quickly as possible while preserving auditability.

Approved stabilization actions:
- Apply a manual provisional credit (or adjustment) to restore balance to >= $0.00.
- Request Finance to clear the autopay hold after the balance is corrected.

Constraints:
- If the adjustment is > $100 USD (or local equivalent), Billing must provide written approval in the ticket before Finance executes the override.
- All actions must be recorded in the support case with: actor, timestamp (UTC), amount, rationale, and references to evidence.

### 3) Communicate to the customer (Support-owned)
Minimum customer update should include:
- Confirmation that the account balance was corrected and the autopay hold was cleared.
- High-level explanation: automated reconciliation incorrectly reversed a support-issued provisional credit.
- Assurance: engineering fix in progress or deployed; monitoring added.

Do not share internal rule names/commit hashes with customers. Use non-technical phrasing and keep it factual.

## Permanent remediation (required follow-ups)
### Engineering (Billing/SRE)
- Maintain alias normalization in postback signature matching to prevent false duplicate detection.
- Maintain explicit exclusion logic so support-issued provisional credits are not eligible for automatic dedup reversal.
- Add regression tests covering: legacy + v1 source alias combinations, support-issued credit tagging, and reconciliation edge cases.
- Add monitoring/alerting on accounting audit events matching: provisional_credit_reversal where reason includes duplicate_credit_detected.

### Finance/RevRec
- Ensure reversal/credit adjustments are appropriately classified for revenue recognition and that customer communications do not imply revenue-impacting promises without approval.
- Maintain an emergency override procedure for dedicated customers where availability is impacted.
- Periodically review autopay hold thresholds and automation sensitivity for known false-negative-balance scenarios.

### Support/Customer Success
- Maintain a standard customer response template (see “Customer communication template”).
- For dedicated customers, confirm whether any production restrictions occurred and document the window.

## Policy: Autopay hold emergency release (dedicated customers)
### When to use
- Dedicated customer with production impact (keys restricted, endpoint errors, or integrations degraded), and
- Negative balance was caused by a suspected erroneous reversal of a support-issued provisional credit.

### Approval and controls
- Billing on-call approval required for adjustments > $100 USD.
- Finance executes hold clearance only after balance is restored to non-negative.
- All actions must be logged in the ticket and in the finance adjustment system with cross-references.

### Audit requirements
For each emergency release:
- Ticket link (SUP-####)
- Invoice ID and org_id
- Amount and currency
- Reason code: “ERRONEOUS_REVERSAL_SUSPECTED”
- Approver (name) and timestamp (UTC)

## Customer communication template
Subject: Billing hold cleared; provisional credit reversal investigation

Message:
- We identified that an automated billing process reversed a provisional credit that had been applied to your account.
- We have corrected your balance and cleared the automatic payment hold.
- Our engineering team has implemented (or is implementing) a fix to prevent recurrence and we are monitoring for similar events.
- If you observed any service impact, please share timestamps; we will validate and follow up.

## Appendix A: Example log indicators (internal)
- billing.reconciler: starting reconciliation run (run_id=...)
- billing.reconciler: detected multiple postbacks (sources: postback-v1, postback-legacy)
- accounting.service: provisional_credit_reversal credit_id=PC-#### amount=... reason=duplicate_credit_detected
- finance.automation: autopay_hold applied due_to negative_balance ...

## Appendix B: Links / tracking
- Primary incident example: SUP-121212
- Engineering tracking: INT-4421
- Runbook: Hourly Postback Reconciler Runbook (see related_pages)

## Change log
- 2026-03-14: Initial publication based on SUP-121212.
- 2026-03-18: Updated to reflect production deployment of alias normalization and provisional-credit exclusion behavior.
- 2026-03-22: Added Finance emergency release controls and audit requirements; updated customer template.

title: Few-shot priming economics and sample compression guide
preview:
Summary:
This guide documents Redwood's recommended approach to designing few-shot prompts and compressed sample sets for production evals. It balances quality (coherence, instruction-following) against unit-costs (tokens per probe, annotation overhead) and operational constraints (throughput, latency for streaming evals). The guidance is intended for evaluator engineers, model-onboarding owners, and probe stewards.

Goals:
- Reduce per-probe token cost by 20–60% without regressing key quality slices (intent accuracy, safety sentinel pass-rate).
- Provide an auditable compression workflow so that downstream continuous evals and shadow deployments use reproducible priming sets.
- Define metrics and an acceptance gate for compressed few-shot sets.

Background and tradeoffs:
Few-shot priming increases model context and improves alignment accuracy on many instruction tasks, but it also inflates token usage and can amplify quantization sensitivity. Compression here means generating a smaller representative set of examples or a condensed priming representation (templates, canonicalized examples, or sketch encodings) that preserves signal for the target slices.

Key principles:
1) Slice-first fidelity: prioritize preserving performance on high-priority slices (billing-sensitive, safety-critical) when compressing.
2) Incremental compression: perform staged compression with an explicit acceptance gate at each step.
3) Telemetry-driven selection: use real traffic fingerprints and evaluator telemetry to pick candidate exemplars.
4) Quant-compatibility testing: run compacted priming through quantized-shadow evaluation to detect sensitivity early.

Definitions:
- exemplar: a single few-shot example used in the prompt.
- compaction: any operation that reduces token footprint of a priming set (e.g., truncation, templating, summarization).
- fidelity delta: observed change in slice-level metric after compaction (positive or negative).

Recommended workflow (high level):
1. Assemble seed exemplar pool from: production probes, human-curated anchor examples, and synthetic perturbations. Target pool size: 500–5k per slice.
2. Compute exemplar fingerprints: token length distribution, semantic embedding, label anchoring, and contextual importance score.
3. Rank exemplars per slice using a hybrid score: w1 * embedding-diversity + w2 * label-coverage + w3 * recency. Default weights: [0.5, 0.3, 0.2].
4. Apply staged compaction strategies (see table).
5. Run micro-canary evals across: float baseline, INT8/INT4 quant-shadow, cached vs cold contexts.
6. Accept/reject with acceptance gate (see Acceptance gate).

Compaction strategies (choose 1–3, apply incrementally):
| Strategy | Typical reduction | When to use | Risks |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Truncate long examples (keep minimal context) | 20–40% | Long narrative examples with low label density | may omit crucial reasoning steps |
| Template extraction (replace verbose spans with slot templates) | 30–70% | Repetitive instruction scaffolds across examples | over-simplification, instruction ambiguity |
| Example summarization (LLM-based abstractive) | 40–80% | Long multi-paragraph demos | hallucination in summaries |
| Representative exemplar selection (diversity-based sampling) | 50–90% | Large exemplar pool with redundancy | misses rare edge behaviors |
| Sketch encoding (compact structured tokens for reasoning chains) | 60–95% | Controlled tasks with repeatable reasoning patterns | requires runtime deterministic decoder |

Config example (YAML) - compress-config.yaml:
---
slice: "account-inquiry"
seed_pool_path: "s3://eval-probes/seed/account-inquiry-v2.jsonl"
fingerprint: [embedding_model: "embed-v3", tokenizer: "cl100k_base"]
rank_weights: [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]
max_tokens_after_compaction: 2400
stages:
  - name: "template-extract"
    strategy: "template-extraction"
    params: {max_template_slots: 3}
  - name: "select-representatives"
    strategy: "k-center-sampling"
    params: {k: 64, diversity_metric: "cosine"}
  - name: "summarize-examples"
    strategy: "abstractive-summarization"
    params: {max_length: 80, model: "summarizer-2b"}
acceptance_gate:
  target_metric: "intent_accuracy"
  delta_threshold: -0.015  # reject if accuracy drops >1.5%
  additional_checks: ["safety_sentinel_pass", "embedding drift < 0.02"]
---

Acceptance gate (detailed):
- Primary metric: slice-level intent accuracy or task-specific correctness.
- Allowed fidelity delta: default -1.5% relative to full-shot baseline for non-S safety slices. For S (safety/finance/legal) slices the allowed delta is -0.2%.
- Checkpoints:
  1. Local micro-canary: 256 probes, compare float baseline (full shot) vs compressed.
  2. Shadow canary: run 2,000 probes against quant-shadow configuration.
  3. Production probe escrow (optional): limited roll-forward on 0.1% of traffic for 24 hours.
- Rollback triggers: fidelity delta > threshold, safety sentinel failures increase by >0.1%, or latency regression > 25ms p95.

Metrics and telemetry to capture:
- token_savings_pct (per-probe)
- fidelity_delta_by_slice (intent_accuracy, topk_coverage, safety_pass_rate)
- quant_sensitivity_score (change from float to quantized on critical prompts)
- exemplar_coverage (percent of original labels represented)
- sampling_entropy (indicator of selection diversity)

Procedural checklist: compress-and-certify (numbered):
1. Create seed pool and store versioned manifest in S3 with sha256 checksums.
2. Run fingerprinting job (embedding + tokenizer stats) and persist results to probe-catalog.
3. Run automated ranking; inspect top-100 exemplars manually for critical slices.
4. Execute first compaction stage (template extraction).
5. Launch micro-canary: 256 examples, run against float baseline. Record metrics and attach run artifacts.
6. If pass, execute second stage (representative selection) and run shadow canary.
7. If shadow canary passes, attach compressed set to promotion PR and request two reviewers (probe steward + model owner).
8. On approval, schedule a 24h prod-escrow roll-forward (0.1% traffic) with observability hooks.
9. If production signals are green, tag the compressed set as promoted and update eval catalog.
10. Archive the original seed pool and link to compressed set for traceability.

Pseudocode: exemplar ranking (simplified)
```
def score(example):
    emb_div = diversity_score(example.embedding, shard_embeddings)
    label_cov = label_coverage(example.labels)
    recency = recency_score(example.timestamp)
    return 0.5*emb_div + 0.3*label_cov + 0.2*recency

ranked = sorted(pool, key=score, reverse=True)
selected = k_center_sample(ranked, k=K, metric="cosine")
```

Acceptance test matrix (decision table):
| Stage | Dataset size | Baseline | Quant modes | Accept if | Action |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| micro-canary | 256 | float | none | delta >= -0.015 for non-S slices | proceed |
| shadow-canary | 2,000 | float | INT8, INT4 | all quant modes within thresholds | proceed to PR |
| prod-escrow | 0.1% traffic | quantized serving | INT8 | no safety sentinel regressions, p95 latency within 25ms | promote |

Ownership and roles:
- Probe Steward: owns seed pool curation and manual inspection (team: applied-ml).
- Evaluator Engineer: owns ranking pipeline and micro-canary execution (team: eng-evals).
- Model Owner: final approval on promotion PR and rollout gating.
- SRE/Observability: ensures telemetry hooks and alerting during prod-escrow.

Rollout checklist for model onboards that will use compressed few-shot sets:
- Add compressed primer reference in service eval-contracts.yaml.
- Include a quant-sensitivity job in CI that runs on PRs (INT8 + INT4).
- Instrument per-route token_savings_pct and fidelity_delta_by_slice to Redwood Console dashboards.

Troubleshooting and recovery patterns:
- If quant_sensitivity_score spikes after compaction: revert to previous primer and run targeted exemplar augmentation for the adversarial sub-slice.
- If safety sentinel failures increase: immediately pause roll-forward, revert serving primer, and open an incident with applied-ml and safety SMEs.
- If latency p95 increases during prod-escrow: evaluate whether decoder complexity from sketch encodings is the cause; prefer template-extraction fallback.

Auditability and reproducibility:
- All compression runs must produce a manifest: {seed_manifest_sha, compaction_steps, random_seed, compressed_set_sha}.
- Store manifests in eval-catalog S3 and link the compressed_set_sha from the model promotion PR.

Open questions and future work:
- Better automated exemplar-to-template extraction that preserves reasoning chains without hallucination.
- Compact sketch encodings that are runtime-decodable across different serving runtimes.
- Integration of exemplar-level explainability signals to prefer exemplars with clear token-level attribution for decisions.

References and related docs:
- See eval-harness throughput/fidelity playbook for aligning micro-canary sizes.
- See quantization-profiles checklist for mixed-precision rollout guidance.

Appendix: quick CLI snippets
- Fingerprint job (example):
  rci eval-fingerprint --input s3://eval-probes/seed/account-inquiry-v2.jsonl --embed-model embed-v3 --out s3://eval-catalog/fingerprints/account-inquiry-v2.jsonl
- Run micro-canary:
  rci canary-run --set compressed-v1 --size 256 --mode float --out s3://eval-artifacts/canary/account-inquiry/micro-2026-03-10.json

Contact and ownership:
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (applied-ml)
- Reviewers: Marco Alvarez, Diane Chen
- For operational help: ping #eng-evals on Slack and tag the oncall evaluator engineer.

Change log:
- 2026-02-12: created initial draft.
- 2026-03-10: updated acceptance thresholds and added quant-sensitivity_score metric.

Config example (YAML) - compress-config.yaml:
---
slice: "account-inquiry"
seed_pool_path: "s3://eval-probes/seed/account-inquiry-v2.jsonl"
fingerprint: [embedding_model: "embed-v3", tokenizer: "cl100k_base"]
rank_weights: [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]
max_tokens_after_compaction: 2400
stages:
  - name: "template-extract"
    strategy: "template-extraction"
    params: {max_template_slots: 3}
  - name: "select-representatives"
    strategy: "k-center-sampling"
    params: {k: 64, diversity_metric: "cosine"}
  - name: "summarize-examples"
    strategy: "abstractive-summarization"
    params: {max_length: 80, model: "summarizer-2b"}
acceptance_gate:
  target_metric: "intent_accuracy"
  delta_threshold: -0.015  # reject if accuracy drops >1.5%
  additional_checks: ["safety_sentinel_pass", "embedding drift < 0.02"]
---

Acceptance gate (detailed):
- Primary metric: slice-level intent accuracy or task-specific correctness.
- Allowed fidelity delta: default -1.5% relative to full-shot baseline for non-S safety slices. For S (safety/finance/legal) slices the allowed delta is -0.2%.
- Checkpoints:
  1. Local micro-canary: 256 probes, compare float baseline (full shot) vs compressed.
  2. Shadow canary: run 2,000 probes against quant-shadow configuration.
  3. Production probe escrow (optional): limited roll-forward on 0.1% of traffic for 24 hours.
- Rollback triggers: fidelity delta > threshold, safety sentinel failures increase by >0.1%, or latency regression > 25ms p95.

Metrics and telemetry to capture:
- token_savings_pct (per-probe)
- fidelity_delta_by_slice (intent_accuracy, topk_coverage, safety_pass_rate)
- quant_sensitivity_score (change from float to quantized on critical prompts)
- exemplar_coverage (percent of original labels represented)
- sampling_entropy (indicator of selection diversity)

Procedural checklist: compress-and-certify (numbered):
1. Create seed pool and store versioned manifest in S3 with sha256 checksums.
2. Run fingerprinting job (embedding + tokenizer stats) and persist results to probe-catalog.
3. Run automated ranking; inspect top-100 exemplars manually for critical slices.
4. Execute first compaction stage (template extraction).
5. Launch micro-canary: 256 examples, run against float baseline. Record metrics and attach run artifacts.
6. If pass, execute second stage (representative selection) and run shadow canary.
7. If shadow canary passes, attach compressed set to promotion PR and request two reviewers (probe steward + model owner).
8. On approval, schedule a 24h prod-escrow roll-forward (0.1% traffic) with observability hooks.
9. If production signals are green, tag the compressed set as promoted and update eval catalog.
10. Archive the original seed pool and link to compressed set for traceability.

Pseudocode: exemplar ranking (simplified)
```
def score(example):
    emb_div = diversity_score(example.embedding, shard_embeddings)
    label_cov = label_coverage(example.labels)
    recency = recency_score(example.timestamp)
    return 0.5*emb_div + 0.3*label_cov + 0.2*recency

ranked = sorted(pool, key=score, reverse=True)
selected = k_center_sample(ranked, k=K, metric="cosine")
```

Acceptance test matrix (decision table):
| Stage | Dataset size | Baseline | Quant modes | Accept if | Action |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| micro-canary | 256 | float | none | delta >= -0.015 for non-S slices | proceed |
| shadow-canary | 2,000 | float | INT8, INT4 | all quant modes within thresholds | proceed to PR |
| prod-escrow | 0.1% traffic | quantized serving | INT8 | no safety sentinel regressions, p95 latency within 25ms | promote |

Ownership and roles:
- Probe Steward: owns seed pool curation and manual inspection (team: applied-ml).
- Evaluator Engineer: owns ranking pipeline and micro-canary execution (team: eng-evals).
- Model Owner: final approval on promotion PR and rollout gating.
- SRE/Observability: ensures telemetry hooks and alerting during prod-escrow.

Rollout checklist for model onboards that will use compressed few-shot sets:
- Add compressed primer reference in service eval-contracts.yaml.
- Include a quant-sensitivity job in CI that runs on PRs (INT8 + INT4).
- Instrument per-route token_savings_pct and fidelity_delta_by_slice to Redwood Console dashboards.

Troubleshooting and recovery patterns:
- If quant_sensitivity_score spikes after compaction: revert to previous primer and run targeted exemplar augmentation for the adversarial sub-slice.
- If safety sentinel failures increase: immediately pause roll-forward, revert serving primer, and open an incident with applied-ml and safety SMEs.
- If latency p95 increases during prod-escrow: evaluate whether decoder complexity from sketch encodings is the cause; prefer template-extraction fallback.

Auditability and reproducibility:
- All compression runs must produce a manifest: {seed_manifest_sha, compaction_steps, random_seed, compressed_set_sha}.
- Store manifests in eval-catalog S3 and link the compressed_set_sha from the model promotion PR.

Open questions and future work:
- Better automated exemplar-to-template extraction that preserves reasoning chains without hallucination.
- Compact sketch encodings that are runtime-decodable across different serving runtimes.
- Integration of exemplar-level explainability signals to prefer exemplars with clear token-level attribution for decisions.

References and related docs:
- See eval-harness throughput/fidelity playbook for aligning micro-canary sizes.
- See quantization-profiles checklist for mixed-precision rollout guidance.

Appendix: quick CLI snippets
- Fingerprint job (example):
  rci eval-fingerprint --input s3://eval-probes/seed/account-inquiry-v2.jsonl --embed-model embed-v3 --out s3://eval-catalog/fingerprints/account-inquiry-v2.jsonl
- Run micro-canary:
  rci canary-run --set compressed-v1 --size 256 --mode float --out s3://eval-artifacts/canary/account-inquiry/micro-2026-03-10.json

Contact and ownership:
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (applied-ml)
- Reviewers: Marco Alvarez, Diane Chen
- For operational help: ping #eng-evals on Slack and tag the oncall evaluator engineer.

Change log:
- 2026-02-12: created initial draft.
- 2026-03-10: updated acceptance thresholds and added quant-sensitivity_score metric.

title: Tenant Impact Scorer & Priority Gating Runbook
preview:
Overview:

This runbook defines the Tenant Impact Scorer (TIS), a deterministic scoring algorithm and automation flow that:
- Quantifies incident impact at the tenant/customer level.
- Converts impact into a prioritized remediation level (P0..P3).
- Drives automated gating of non-critical deployments and throttles via the gatekeeper-service when error budgets or capacity signals cross thresholds.

Goal:
- Reduce time-to-prioritize for multi-tenant incidents.
- Ensure capacity/economics aware mitigation (avoid global throttles for a small set of high-error tenants).
- Provide a reproducible signal for oncall and product owners to take action.

Scope:
- Applies to all customer-facing inference endpoints and long-running background jobs that surface tenant errors (service prefixes: inference-api/*, embeddings/*, reranking/*).
- Not applicable to internal platform-only pipelines (use eng-sre/internal-playbook).

Owners and Contacts:
- Primary owner: eng-sre (oncall alias: eng-sre-oncall@redwood.com)
- Gatekeeper service owner: infra-platform (owner: Aaron Patel)
- Product escalation: product-support@redwood.com
- Incident CLI & automation maintainer: Liam Chen

Definitions and Signals:
- tenant_error_rate: percentage of failing requests over last 5m for a tenant (Prometheus metric: inference_request_errors_total / inference_request_total by (tenant_id)).
- tenant_traffic_share: tenant requests / total requests over 1h.
- error_budget_burn_rate: rate at which a tenant is consuming its Service SLO error budget (30m exponential window).
- active_complaints: number of customer support tickets opened in last 30m for the tenant (from Zendesk API).
- tier_score: numeric mapping for plan tier: enterprise=1.0, standard=0.6, free=0.2 (enterprise gets higher weight because impact is higher).

Scoring formula (deterministic):
- Normalize each signal into 0..1 range using soft-floor/ceiling per metric (see params below).
- TenantImpactScore = 0.35*norm_error_rate + 0.25*norm_traffic_share + 0.20*norm_burn_rate + 0.10*norm_active_complaints + 0.10*tier_score

Parameter normalization thresholds (default):
- norm_error_rate: 0% -> 0, 1% -> 0.25, 5% -> 0.75, 20% -> 1.0
- norm_traffic_share: 0% -> 0, 0.5% -> 0.2, 5% -> 0.7, 30% -> 1.0
- norm_burn_rate: 1x -> 0, 2x -> 0.25, 5x -> 0.75, 20x -> 1.0
- norm_active_complaints: 0 -> 0, 1 -> 0.4, 3 -> 0.8, 10 -> 1.0

Priority mapping (TenantImpactScore -> Action):
- P0 (>= 0.85): Immediate incident response; page SRE; auto-gate deploys for tenant; apply temporary traffic diversion or aggressive throttles.
- P1 (0.6 - 0.85): High priority; assign incident commander within 10 minutes; auto-throttle to soft limit; notify account owner and CS.
- P2 (0.35 - 0.6): Monitor and mitigate; schedule regression test, consider targeted retry/backoff policies for affected tenant.
- P3 (<0.35): Low priority; record and window into next reliability retrospective.

Automated gating and policy enforcement:
- gatekeeper-service: internal HTTP API that accepts decisions to quarantine tenants or set dynamic throttles.
- Default gate actions: {
    "quarantine": {"traffic_cap_pct": X},
    "throttle": {"qps_limit": N},
    "pause_deploys": true/false
  }

Sample gatekeeper request (curl):

curl -X POST https://internal-api.redwood.com/gatekeeper/v1/tenants/action 
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer $GATEKEEPER_TOKEN' 
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' 
  -d '{"tenant_id":"acct_1234", "action":"quarantine", "params": {"traffic_cap_pct": 20}, "reason":"TIS P0 auto-respond"}'

PromQL examples used by scorer (replace $TENANT with tenant label):
- tenant_error_rate: sum(increase(inference_request_errors_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m])) / sum(increase(inference_request_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m]))
- tenant_traffic_share: sum(increase(inference_request_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[1h])) / sum(increase(inference_request_total[1h]))
- tenant_burn_rate: rate(error_budget_consumed_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[5m]) / (error_budget_total{tenant="$TENANT"} / 3600)

Zendesk quick lookup (example curl):
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $ZENDESK_TOKEN" 
  "https://redwood.zendesk.com/api/v2/search.json?query=type:ticket organization:acct_1234 created>now-30m" 
  | jq '.results | length'  # returns active_complaints

Operational runbook: step-by-step
1) Detect: TIS runs every 60s and emits tenant_impact_score{tenant_id}. Alert fires when any tenant score crosses 0.6 (P1) or 0.85 (P0).
2) Page: For P0 -> page eng-sre-oncall and product-owner of tenant. For P1 -> page eng-sre-oncall. Include a generated summary (top signals, top endpoints, recent deploys).
3) Observe: Run automated diagnostic queries (PromQL + slowlog extraction) via the incident CLI:
   - incident-cli query --tenant acct_1234 --duration 15m --metrics errors,latency,traffic
4) Decide: Use the decision matrix below to choose gate action. The first responder should confirm suggested gate in Slack #oncall-decisions with the JSON payload.
5) Enforce: POST to gatekeeper-service (see sample) and record action in incident tracker (Jira/Confluence incident page).
6) Mitigate: Depending on action, apply fixes: rollback last deploy for tenant routing, increase capacity if capacity-related, or roll out a targeted config patch.
7) Validate: Verify tenant metrics return to baseline for 30m before removing quarantine.
8) Post-incident: Create an incident review using templates in /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review. Include TIS telemetry and gate decisions as artifacts.

Decision matrix (quick reference):
- If TenantImpactScore >= 0.85 -> gate: quarantine to 10-25% traffic and pause deploys; escalate to P0.
- If 0.7 <= score < 0.85 and error_budget_burn_rate > 5x -> gate: throttle to safe qps and notify billing/CS.
- If score driven primarily by traffic spike (traffic_share > 10% but low error rate) -> consider autoscale/allocate burst pool instead of quarantining.
- If score driven by active_complaints > 3 -> immediately notify account owner and enable crash-only logging for tenant requests for 1h.

Safety knobs and overrides:
- human_override: any SRE with role "incident_responder" can annotate the gate action with reason: human_override=true and set manual expire timestamp.
- auto_ttl: automated gate actions default to 30 minutes unless extended by explicit operator or a follow-up automation that keeps sampling.
- dry_run_mode: during holiday weekends or platform upgrades, TIS runs in "dry_run" and will not call gatekeeper-service but will still page and log decisions. Toggle via feature flag: feature.tis.enable_gating=false

Telemetry and dashboards:
- Dashboards: Tenant Impact Overview (Grafana board id: SRE-TIS-012) showing top 25 tenants by score, trend sparkline, and active gates.
- Alerts: TIS-P1 (score>=0.6) and TIS-P0 (score>=0.85) in PagerDuty. Alert payload includes: tenant_id, computed_score, top contributing signal, last_deploy_id.

Rollout plan for the scorer and gating automation:
1) Developer preview (week 1-2): run TIS in dry_run against production data; gather feedback from top 50 enterprise tenants.
2) Gradual enable (week 3-6): enable auto-gating for internal/test tenants only; schema and webhook contract validated.
3) Canary (week 7-10): enable gating for low-risk customer buckets; monitor false positive rate < 2% over 2 weeks.
4) Full launch (week 11+): enable for all tenants with monitoring guardrails; SRE oncall trained on playbook.

Testing guidance and QA:
- Unit test scoring logic with synthetic tenants: include edge cases (zero traffic, heavy error burn for free tier, sudden bursty traffic).
- Load test gatekeeper-service at 10k ops/s and ensure <200ms median response.
- Chaos tests: simulate API gateway failures and ensure TIS degrades to read-only (no gating) and still pages SRE.

Runbook snippets & templates (Confluence copy candidates):
- Incident summary template: include computed TIS score, rationale, gate action, and validation checklist.
- Postmortem artifact checklist: log of gate actions, PromQL queries, deploy ids, and Zendesk ticket ids.

Example incident timeline (illustrative):
- 09:02: TIS detects acct_1234 score 0.88 (P0) due to 12% error rate and 8% traffic share.
- 09:03: Eng-sre-oncall paged and auto-suggested action: quarantine 20% traffic + pause deploys.
- 09:04: SRE lead approves in #oncall-decisions and gatekeeper-service POSTs quarantine action.
- 09:20: Errors fall to 1.2% for tenant; score drops to 0.45.
- 09:55: Gate auto-ttl expires; first responder validates 30m baseline and removes quarantine.

Audit and compliance notes:
- All gatekeeper actions are logged to audit-log-service (retention 13 months).
- For regulated customers, additional manual approval is required to apply quarantines (see security-and-compliance/gated-actions-policy).

Appendix: Example scoring pseudo-code

# python-style pseudo-code

def normalize(value, thresholds):
    # thresholds = [(x1, s1), (x2, s2), ...] linear interp clamp
    # returns 0..1
    ...

def compute_tis(tenant_id):
    err = query_promql(tenant_error_rate)
    traffic = query_promql(tenant_traffic_share)
    burn = query_promql(tenant_burn_rate)
    complaints = query_zendesk_count(tenant_id)
    tier_score = lookup_tier(tenant_id)
    return 0.35*norm(err) + 0.25*norm(traffic) + 0.20*norm(burn) + 0.10*norm(complaints) + 0.10*tier_score

# CI/CD gating example (GitHub Actions snippet)
name: Deploy
on: [push]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Query gatekeeper before deploy
        run: |
          curl -s -X POST https://internal-api.redwood.com/gatekeeper/v1/ci/check \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer $GATEKEEPER_TOKEN" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{"target":"svc/inference-api", "tenant_id":"${{ matrix.tenant }}"}' \
          | jq '.status'

Notes and future work:
- Integrate tenant sentiment from support conversations (NLP score) to increase sensitivity for enterprise customers.
- Add machine-learning calibration to dynamically tune normalization thresholds per-service.
- Expand the gate actions to include fine-grained model-variant fallback for inference endpoints.

References:
- Grafana board: SRE-TIS-012
- Gatekeeper API docs: https://internal-api.redwood.com/docs/gatekeeper
- Incident CLI: https://internal-tools.redwood.com/incident-cli

End of document

title: Tiered-fallback-routing-and-eviction-architecture-2026
preview:
Overview:

This document describes the Tiered Fallback Routing and Eviction Architecture (TFREA) used by Redwood’s serving runtime to meet multi-dimensional SLOs (latency percentiles, cost budget, and availability) while degrading predictably during capacity pressure or downstream failure modes. TFREA unifies routing policies, candidate model variants, eviction heuristics, and runtime-informed kernel scheduling hints into a single, observable control loop.

Goals:
- Maintain 99th-percentile latency SLOs per route under normal load.
- Bound hosted per-token cost within configured customer cost tiers.
- Provide deterministic, monotonic quality degradation (higher-quality -> lower-quality) with automated rollback when pressure subsides.
- Make fallback decisions explainable and traceable per request for post-incident analysis.

Scope:
- Applies to hosted and Dedicated modes; Private deployments can enable TFREA via configuration flag.
- Covers routing layer, model-variant catalog, eviction/placement heuristics, kernel scheduling hints, and observability integration.

Key concepts and components:
1) Routing Policy Engine (RPE)
- Policy primitives: latency-priority, cost-priority, isolation-priority. Each route has a profile: e.g., {latency: 0.7, cost: 0.2, isolation: 0.1}.
- Decision inputs: request metadata (route, token budget, customer plan), node telemetry (utilization, queue depth), model health (regression alerts), and global capacity signals.
- Outputs: selected pool (hot, warm, cold), model variant (full, quantized, distilled), and optional dual-write fallback (emit to both selected and fallback model asynchronously for A/B quality logging).

2) Model Variant Catalog and Contracts
- Each canonical model version registers 1..N variants with contract metadata:
  - quality_score (0.0-1.0) derived from eval harness
  - compute_cost_factor (relative tokens/second)
  - warmup_time_seconds
  - compatible_kernels (list of kernels / tensor layouts)
- Example registration YAML snippet: 
  id: llama-3-20b:v2026-02
  variants:
    - id: llama-3-20b_fp16
      quality_score: "0.98"
      cost_factor: "1.0"
      warmup: "18"
      kernels: [matmul-fused, alpa-attn-optimized]
    - id: llama-3-20b_8bitq
      quality_score: "0.92"
      cost_factor: "0.55"
      warmup: "6"
      kernels: [quant-8q-conv]

3) Tiered Pools (hot / warm / cold)
- Hot: low-latency nodes, prewarmed KV caches, continuous batching source; used for latency-priority traffic. SLO target: p99 < 120ms for short requests (<=128 tokens).
- Warm: on-demand nodes with partial KV warmup and batched scheduling; SLO target: p99 < 260ms.
- Cold: preemptible or burst capacity; best-effort latency with strong cost savings. SLO target: p99 < 1s (best-effort).

4) Eviction and Placement Heuristics
- Eviction signals: node memory pressure, KV cold-start ratio > 0.4, kernel queue growth rate > 20% over 30s, and per-tenant cost throttle events.
- Heuristic ordering when evicting: least-recently-used variant replicas weighted by (quality_score * route_priority) to avoid evicting high-quality routes.
- Soft-eviction step: move from hot->warm; Hard-eviction: terminate replica (cold) and optionally nominate a lower-cost variant.

5) Kernel Scheduling Hints and Interaction
- RPE emits kernel hints: preferred_kernel, concurrency_target, slice_budget. These are advisory fields consumed by kernel-and-scheduling layer to optimize batch slicing and preemption.
- Example hint: {preferred_kernel: "matmul-fused", concurrency_target: "6", slice_budget_ms: "40"}.

6) Observability and Decision Tracing
- Every routing decision logs a DecisionTrace ID attached to request spans. Trace contains inputs, scoring vector, selected_variant, and fallback_candidates. Stored in the decision-trace index for 30 days by default.
- Key dashboards: Per-route fallback rate, per-variant quality delta (A/B logs), pool occupancy and eviction rate, kernel hint adoption rate.

SLOs and thresholds (operative values):
- Route-level p50/p95/p99 SLOs are configured per route. Default intent for conversational routes: p50 < 30ms, p95 < 95ms, p99 < 250ms.
- Cost guardrails: per-customer monthly token-cost target with soft watermarks at 50% and 80% where TFREA may progressively bias cost-priority.
- Fallback budget: No route should experience fallback > 10% of requests sustained over 5 minutes without triggering operator notification.

Operational procedures and runbooks:
1) Gradual rollout checklist (owner: runtime-release)
1. Create canary pool with 5% traffic weight.
2. Enable decision-trace sampling at 5% for canary.
3. Monitor: fallback-rate, p99 latency, kernel-queue-depth for 15 minutes.
4. If fallback_rate > 1% or p99 regresses > 15% over baseline, abort and roll back.
5. If stable, ramp 5% every 10 minutes to target.

2) Incident triage playbook: Unexpected fallback surge
- Pager triggers at fallback_rate > 10% sustained for 2 minutes.
- Triage steps:
  1. Query decision-trace for last 5 minutes (filter by route). CLI: redwoodctl traces query --route <r> --since 5m --filter fallback=true
  2. Check pool telemetry: node_util, kv_hit_rate, kernel_queue_growth.
  3. If KV cold-start > 50%: trigger warmup job for top-5 variants: redwoodctl runtime warmup --variants <ids> --target-pool hot
  4. If node OOMs or watchdog hits: cordon nodes and spin up replacement nodes (see gpu-fleet-and-capacity runbook).
  5. Temporarily set route policy to latency-priority and pin to hot pool for critical tenants while investigating.

3) Performance regression investigation (owner: runtime-profiling)
- Collect sample logs and kernel traces with sampler strategy: 1:1000 baseline, 1:50 on regression window.
- Use profiling commands: profiler collect --trace-id <trace> --duration 30s --output s3://runtime-profiles/<id>.tar.gz
- Correlate with batch-slice histograms and kernel latency percentiles.

Benchmarking and validation suite:
- The TFREA validation harness is a sub-suite in the performance suite: includes mixed-workload load generator, KV-warmup simulator, and variant quality regression tests.
- Key scenarios:
  - Steady-state with mixed short/long sequences (60/30/10 split by request length).
  - Rapid burst (2x sustained) with concurrent model-variant promotion/demotion.
  - Engineered wholesale kernel regression: inject a slow kernel variant to ensure sentinel metrics and fallbacks work.
- Release gate: TFREA must pass canary e2e smoke and two automated burst simulations without p99 SLO breaches before canary ramp.

Release notes summary (2026-02-15):
- Introduced Tiered Fallback Routing and Eviction Architecture (TFREA) to reduce tail latency and control cost during capacity pressure.
- Added DecisionTrace indexing and trace-based runbook utilities.
- New model-variant contract fields (quality_score, warmup_time_seconds) and kernel hint API.

Known issues and workarounds (as of 2026-03-02):
- Issue: Under certain mixed-tenant workloads, eviction ranking can bias too heavily toward routes with frequent short requests, causing long-tail quality regressions for background batch routes.
  - Workaround: Set explicit route affinity in route config to protect background batch routes, e.g., affinity: {protect_until: "2026-06-01"}.
- Issue: Kernel hint adoption sometimes lags on heterogeneous pools where the kernel mapper is updating; mismatch can cause suboptimal batch slicing.
  - Workaround: Restart kernel-mapper after variant promotion: systemctl restart kernel-mapper (one node at a time) and allow 60s for re-registration.

Implementation notes and APIs:
- New API: /v1/runtime/route-evaluate (internal) returns DecisionTrace and selected_variant. Schema: {route, inputs, scoring, selected, fallbacks, hints}.
- redwoodctl examples:
  - Query fallback stats: redwoodctl runtime stats fallback --route <route> --window 10m
  - Force promote variant to hot pool: redwoodctl runtime promote --variant <id> --pool hot --reason "emergency-fix"

Ownership and SLAs:
- Owners: runtime-architecture (Priya Natarajan), runtime-scheduling (Marcus Liu), runtime-profiling (Zara Ortega).
- Support: eng-sre oncall for 24/7 pager coverage.
- Documentation and training: rollout workshops scheduled for 2026-03-10 and 2026-03-17.

Appendix A — route policy example (YAML):
route: "conversations/v1/generate"
policy:
  latency: "0.75"
  cost: "0.20"
  isolation: "0.05"
  affinity: {pools: [hot, warm], protect_until: "2026-04-01"}

Appendix B — decision scoring example (csv):
input,weight,score,contrib
latency_priority,0.75,0.82,0.615
cost_priority,0.20,0.34,0.068
isolation_priority,0.05,0.9,0.045
final_score,, ,0.728

Appendix C — escalation contact matrix:
- Pager 1: eng-sre (primary)
- Pager 2: runtime-architecture (Priya Natarajan)
- Email: runtime-discuss@redwood.ai

Related pages and next steps:
- Add kernel hint adoption metrics to runtime-consolidation dashboard and alert if adoption < 80% across a pool.
- Prototype ML-driven fallback scorer (Q2 2026) to replace hand-tuned weighting.

If you are implementing or operating TFREA and see any unexpected behaviors, file a ticket in the runtime backlog (component: runtime-fallback) and include DecisionTrace IDs for at least 5 example requests.

title: Cross-region signal fusion and fallback runbook
preview:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. It codifies signal sources, fusion heuristics, alert thresholds, operator actions, and automated fallback configuration. The goal is to preserve customer-facing SLOs (p95 latency < 300ms for interactive routes; token cost drift alerts at > +30% delta) while limiting unnecessary cross-region failovers and cost overruns.

Scope
-----
- Services in scope: runtime-gateway, batch-router, kv-prefix-cache, regional-allocator, traffic-shepherd.
- Regions: us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Applies to hosted and dedicated deployments; private deployments may adapt the heuristics but should follow the same escalation flow.

Signal sources and priorities
----------------------------
1) Latency telemetry (highest priority for interactive SLOs)
   - Metrics: request_p95_ms, request_p99_ms from runtime-gateway and regional-edge-proxy.
   - Dashboards: dash/cross-region-latency-fusion -> panel "p95 by region & route".
2) Token cost / execution-time signals (priority for cost-driven routing)
   - Metrics: avg_tokens_per_request, cost_per_1k_tokens_estimated from billing-proxy.
3) Queue depth and backpressure (priority for safety/failure detection)
   - Metrics: queue_depth, slow_consumer_count from batch-router.
4) KV-cache-hit-rate and prefetch cohort fidelity
   - Metrics: kv_cache_hit_ratio, prefetch_miss_streak.

Fusion heuristics (how alerts are decided)
-----------------------------------------
- The fusion engine (service: signal-fuser) computes a composite score per region R:

  composite_score(R) = w1 * normalized_p95(R) + w2 * normalized_cost_delta(R) + w3 * backpressure_penalty(R) - w4 * cache_health_bonus(R)

  where (recommended weights): w1=0.6, w2=0.2, w3=0.15, w4=0.05.

- Normalization: each input is mapped to 0..1 using rolling baseline windows (last 24h).
- Composite score thresholds:
  - Informational: score > 0.35
  - Action recommended: score > 0.55
  - Auto-fallback candidate: score > 0.75 (AND persistent for >= 5 minutes)

Alerting and dashboards
-----------------------
- Alert definitions (Grafana/Alerter):
  1) alert: CrossRegionLatencyWave_ActionRecommended
     expr: composite_score > 0.55 for 5m
     severity: P2 (Platform Operational)
     runbook: this doc -> Operator actions (page anchor)
  2) alert: CrossRegionFallbackCandidate
     expr: composite_score > 0.75 for 5m AND avg(queue_depth) > 50% for 3m
     severity: P1 (Immediate)
     auto-remediation: initiate canary re-route to fallback model/region (see automated fallbacks).
- Dashboard link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
- Panels to check: p95 by region, composite heatmap, queue depth heatmap, token-cost delta by tenant.

Operator actions (ordered)
-------------------------
1) Triage
   - Open the cross-region dashboard and confirm composite score growth is not caused by a single noisy tenant.
   - Query: topk(10, sum by (tenant) (rate(request_tokens[5m]))) and compare to baseline.
2) Contain
   - If backpressure present on batch-router, increase worker concurrency for the affected allocation:

     kubectl -n runtime scale deployment batch-router-uswest2 --replicas=6

   - If kv-prefix-cache shows sustained prefetch_miss_streak > 0.2, flush targeted prefixes:

     curl -X POST "https://ops-api.internal/cache/flush" -d '{"prefixes": ["tenant-1234-user-ctx"]}'

3) Mitigate (manual fallback)
   - If composite_score > 0.75 and operators confirm regional instability, perform a controlled reroute using traffic-shepherd:

     # preview
     traffic-shepherd preview-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10

     # commit (staggered):
     traffic-shepherd apply-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10 --stagger 2m --owner @oncall-platform

   - Record the reroute ID and annotate the incident ticket.
4) Escalate
   - If reroute does not restore p95 within 10 minutes or causes cost > +40% vs baseline, escalate to Platform SRE on-call and open incident channel #inc-platform-regional-failover.

Automated fallback configuration
-------------------------------
- Auto-fallbacks are enabled when alert CrossRegionFallbackCandidate fires and composite_score is sustained for 7m. Auto-fallback policy:
  - Step 1: Canary 5% traffic to fallback region/model for 3m.
  - Step 2: If fallback improves composite score by >= 20% within 3m, ramp to 30% for 5m.
  - Step 3: If still improving, ramp to 100% over 10 minutes.
- Overrides: tenants with "no-fallback" contract label are exempt. The auto-fallback actor uses the service regional-allocator and writes an audit entry to fallback-log.

Rollback and safety checks
-------------------------
- Always validate output quality for canary traffic using the canary-eval job:

  kubectl -n eval-job logs canary-eval-<id>

  The job compares a predefined prompt-set S against baseline and reports 3 metrics: semantic_similarity (>=0.92 pass), generation_stability (<0.15 drift), latency_improvement_ms.
- If semantic_similarity < 0.90 for canary, abort fallback and revert routing:

  traffic-shepherd revert-reroute --id <reroute-id> --owner @oncall-platform --reason "canary-quality-failure"
- Safety circuit: any automated fallback that increases estimated token-cost > +60% triggers immediate rollback and hands control to operators.

Post-incident remediation checklist
----------------------------------
1) Annotate the incident ticket with composite_score graphs, reroute IDs, eviction/restart events, and canary-eval summaries.
2) Run a root-cause hypothesis session (30–60m) with owners from runtime, allocator, and infra. Capture findings in: /confluence/eng-platform/retrospectives/<incident>-postmortem (link).
3) If the incident involved cache fidelity, schedule a prefetch-cohort audit and add to backlog with priority P2.

Runbook run-through (rapid script)
----------------------------------
1) Open: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
2) Confirm composite_score alert and top offending tenants.
3) Run: kubectl get deployment batch-router-uswest2 -o wide
4) If queue_depth high: scale batch-router replicas (see contain step).
5) If composite_score persists: preview and apply traffic-shepherd reroute (see mitigate).
6) Monitor canary-eval and artifact logs for 15 minutes before ramping.

Ownership and contacts
----------------------
- Primary owner: platform-ops (oncall rotation via PagerDuty: Platform SRE).
- Secondary owner: runtime-team (Slack: #runtime).
- Fallback automation owner: allocator-team (PagerDuty: Allocator SRE).

Appendix: CLI snippets and JSON examples
---------------------------------------
- Example composite_score query (PromQL):

  sum by(region) (
    0.6 * (request_p95_ms / (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[24h]))))) +
    0.2 * cost_delta_normalized +
    0.15 * clamp_max(queue_depth / 1000, 1) -
    0.05 * kv_cache_hit_ratio
  )

- Sample fallback audit entry (JSON):

  {
    "reroute_id": "rr-20260302-84f2",
    "from": "us-west-2",
    "to": "us-east-1",
    "routes": ["interactive-text", "chat-generic"],
    "percent": 10,
    "trigger": "CrossRegionFallbackCandidate",
    "canary_eval": {"semantic_similarity": 0.948, "stability": 0.08},
    "operator": "@oncall-platform"
  }

References and related reading
----------------------------
- Regional failure mode mapping: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/regional-failure-mode-mapping-and-observability-patterns-2026
- Multiregion tenant routing guide: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/multiregion-latency-aware-tenant-routing-guide-2025
- Prefetch cohort best-practices: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

Change history
--------------
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2026-02-10: Added automated fallback policy and canary-eval tie-in (reviewers: Liam Chen).
- 2026-03-02: Finalized weights and example queries; published.

-----------------------------------------
- The fusion engine (service: signal-fuser) computes a composite score per region R:

  composite_score(R) = w1 * normalized_p95(R) + w2 * normalized_cost_delta(R) + w3 * backpressure_penalty(R) - w4 * cache_health_bonus(R)

  where (recommended weights): w1=0.6, w2=0.2, w3=0.15, w4=0.05.

- Normalization: each input is mapped to 0..1 using rolling baseline windows (last 24h).
- Composite score thresholds:
  - Informational: score > 0.35
  - Action recommended: score > 0.55
  - Auto-fallback candidate: score > 0.75 (AND persistent for >= 5 minutes)

Alerting and dashboards
-----------------------
- Alert definitions (Grafana/Alerter):
  1) alert: CrossRegionLatencyWave_ActionRecommended
     expr: composite_score > 0.55 for 5m
     severity: P2 (Platform Operational)
     runbook: this doc -> Operator actions (page anchor)
  2) alert: CrossRegionFallbackCandidate
     expr: composite_score > 0.75 for 5m AND avg(queue_depth) > 50% for 3m
     severity: P1 (Immediate)
     auto-remediation: initiate canary re-route to fallback model/region (see automated fallbacks).
- Dashboard link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
- Panels to check: p95 by region, composite heatmap, queue depth heatmap, token-cost delta by tenant.

Operator actions (ordered)
-------------------------
1) Triage
   - Open the cross-region dashboard and confirm composite score growth is not caused by a single noisy tenant.
   - Query: topk(10, sum by (tenant) (rate(request_tokens[5m]))) and compare to baseline.
2) Contain
   - If backpressure present on batch-router, increase worker concurrency for the affected allocation:

     kubectl -n runtime scale deployment batch-router-uswest2 --replicas=6

   - If kv-prefix-cache shows sustained prefetch_miss_streak > 0.2, flush targeted prefixes:

     curl -X POST "https://ops-api.internal/cache/flush" -d '{"prefixes": ["tenant-1234-user-ctx"]}'

3) Mitigate (manual fallback)
   - If composite_score > 0.75 and operators confirm regional instability, perform a controlled reroute using traffic-shepherd:

     # preview
     traffic-shepherd preview-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10

     # commit (staggered):
     traffic-shepherd apply-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10 --stagger 2m --owner @oncall-platform

   - Record the reroute ID and annotate the incident ticket.
4) Escalate
   - If reroute does not restore p95 within 10 minutes or causes cost > +40% vs baseline, escalate to Platform SRE on-call and open incident channel #inc-platform-regional-failover.

Automated fallback configuration
-------------------------------
- Auto-fallbacks are enabled when alert CrossRegionFallbackCandidate fires and composite_score is sustained for 7m. Auto-fallback policy:
  - Step 1: Canary 5% traffic to fallback region/model for 3m.
  - Step 2: If fallback improves composite score by >= 20% within 3m, ramp to 30% for 5m.
  - Step 3: If still improving, ramp to 100% over 10 minutes.
- Overrides: tenants with "no-fallback" contract label are exempt. The auto-fallback actor uses the service regional-allocator and writes an audit entry to fallback-log.

Rollback and safety checks
-------------------------
- Always validate output quality for canary traffic using the canary-eval job:

  kubectl -n eval-job logs canary-eval-<id>

  The job compares a predefined prompt-set S against baseline and reports 3 metrics: semantic_similarity (>=0.92 pass), generation_stability (<0.15 drift), latency_improvement_ms.
- If semantic_similarity < 0.90 for canary, abort fallback and revert routing:

  traffic-shepherd revert-reroute --id <reroute-id> --owner @oncall-platform --reason "canary-quality-failure"
- Safety circuit: any automated fallback that increases estimated token-cost > +60% triggers immediate rollback and hands control to operators.

Post-incident remediation checklist
----------------------------------
1) Annotate the incident ticket with composite_score graphs, reroute IDs, eviction/restart events, and canary-eval summaries.
2) Run a root-cause hypothesis session (30–60m) with owners from runtime, allocator, and infra. Capture findings in: /confluence/eng-platform/retrospectives/<incident>-postmortem (link).
3) If the incident involved cache fidelity, schedule a prefetch-cohort audit and add to backlog with priority P2.

Runbook run-through (rapid script)
----------------------------------
1) Open: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
2) Confirm composite_score alert and top offending tenants.
3) Run: kubectl get deployment batch-router-uswest2 -o wide
4) If queue_depth high: scale batch-router replicas (see contain step).
5) If composite_score persists: preview and apply traffic-shepherd reroute (see mitigate).
6) Monitor canary-eval and artifact logs for 15 minutes before ramping.

Ownership and contacts
----------------------
- Primary owner: platform-ops (oncall rotation via PagerDuty: Platform SRE).
- Secondary owner: runtime-team (Slack: #runtime).
- Fallback automation owner: allocator-team (PagerDuty: Allocator SRE).

Appendix: CLI snippets and JSON examples
---------------------------------------
- Example composite_score query (PromQL):

  sum by(region) (
    0.6 * (request_p95_ms / (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[24h]))))) +
    0.2 * cost_delta_normalized +
    0.15 * clamp_max(queue_depth / 1000, 1) -
    0.05 * kv_cache_hit_ratio
  )

- Sample fallback audit entry (JSON):

  {
    "reroute_id": "rr-20260302-84f2",
    "from": "us-west-2",
    "to": "us-east-1",
    "routes": ["interactive-text", "chat-generic"],
    "percent": 10,
    "trigger": "CrossRegionFallbackCandidate",
    "canary_eval": {"semantic_similarity": 0.948, "stability": 0.08},
    "operator": "@oncall-platform"
  }

References and related reading
----------------------------
- Regional failure mode mapping: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/regional-failure-mode-mapping-and-observability-patterns-2026
- Multiregion tenant routing guide: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/multiregion-latency-aware-tenant-routing-guide-2025
- Prefetch cohort best-practices: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

Change history
--------------
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2026-02-10: Added automated fallback policy and canary-eval tie-in (reviewers: Liam Chen).
- 2026-03-02: Finalized weights and example queries; published.

title: Operator Interactive Regression Debugger — Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook describes the Operator Interactive Regression Debugger: a low-latency, human-in-the-loop protocol and toolkit for investigating production-quality regressions that show up in eval observability or customer signals. It complements automated bisect and probe tooling by enabling an operator or engineer to rapidly confirm causal factors, inspect intermediate artifacts (prompts, tokenization, KV cache traces), and run guarded micro-rollouts or targeted mitigations.

Scope:
- Intended for use by SREs, Applied ML engineers, and on-call evaluators responding to model quality regressions or severe fidelity shifts.
- Covers interactive investigation steps, CLI examples, automated helper scripts, telemetry checks, and safe rollback/mitigation actions.
- Not a replacement for automated bisect; use after initial triage if regression persists or is high-severity.

When to use this debugger:
- Alert example: response-stability-index (RSI) for 'billing-invoice-parsing' slice drops > 8% vs baseline within 6 hours.
- Customer-visible correctness regressions with high support volume or P0 product impact.
- Regression bisect returns ambiguous results or multiple suspect commits/quant profiles.

Key goals and guardrails:
- Fast, reproducible experiments (target: operator-to-insight in < 60 minutes).
- Non-disruptive by default: default mode is read-only inspection and simulation against shadow datasets.
- All writes (rollouts, toggles) require explicit two-step confirmation and audit logging (audit event emitted to /audit/logs/regression-debugger).

Ownership and roles:
- Primary operator: oncall SRE or evaluator (role: trigger and lead investigation).
- Supporting owners: model owner (applied-ml), runtime owner (serving-runtime), infra owner (eng-infra).
- Escalation path: if confidence > 70% of production breakage cause, escalate to Model Rollback channel and Product Impact lead.

Signals and preconditions (what to gather before interactive steps):
1) Alert payload: RSI delta, slice id, example probe ids, and time window.
2) Baseline artifacts: golden prompt set for slice, production model id and commit hash, quant-profile id, last successful probe timestamps.
3) Telemetry extracts: token-latency distribution, KV cache hit ratio, embedding drift score, and trace id for an affected request.

Required telemetry and fast checks (run these first):
- rwd-metrics query examples:
  - rwd-metrics query "RSI(slice=billing-invoice, window=6h)"
  - rwd-metrics query "kv_cache_hit_rate(model=v2, window=1h)"
- Quick thresholds to validate: 
  - RSI drop > 5%: high priority.
  - Median token latency increase > 50ms: possible runtime regression.
  - KV cache hit ratio change > 10%: suspect cache policy/serialization change.

Interactive investigation steps (operator workflow):
1) Isolate representative probes
   - Command: rwd-eval probe-select --slice billing-invoice --severity high --limit 20 --output probes.json
   - Inspect a sample of probes for immediate pattern changes (format, tokenization anomalies, hallucination markers).
2) Re-run probes against shadow model variants
   - Run: rwd-eval debug run --probes probes.json --model rwd-model:v2 --shadow-only --parallel 8 --output run-v2.json
   - Then run against previous model: rwd-eval debug run --probes probes.json --model rwd-model:v1 --shadow-only --output run-v1.json
   - Diff: jq -S '.responses[] | {id: .probe_id, text: .text}' run-v2.json > out-v2.ndjson; jq -S '.responses[] | {id: .probe_id, text: .text}' run-v1.json > out-v1.ndjson; diff out-v1.ndjson out-v2.ndjson
3) Inspect intermediate artifacts
   - Tokenization: rwd-inspect tokenizer --model rwd-model:v2 --input "<example prompt>"
   - KV cache trace (sample): rwd-trace kv --trace-id TRACE-12345 --limit 200
   - Kernel scheduling: rwd-trace kernels --trace-id TRACE-12345
4) Hypothesis generation
   - Use artifacts above to produce 1–3 hypotheses (e.g., quantized attention kernel introduced off-by-one, prompt truncation due to new tokenization).
5) Quick micro-experiments (non-disruptive)
   - Use local simulation flags: rwd-eval debug run --model rwd-model:v2 --probe probes.json --flags "--disable-quant-fallback --force-512-context" --shadow-only
   - If hypothesis relates to quantization, try a hybrid fallback: rwd-deploy canary --model rwd-model:v2 --quant-profile q-safe --target-pct 0.5 --shadow-beta
6) Decision matrix
   - If difference between v1 and v2 for critical probes > severity threshold (default 10% relative failure), recommend one of:
     a) Immediate rollback to v1 (if customer impact is P0 and mitigation urgent).
     b) Canary with reduced quant aggressively disabled and close monitoring.
     c) Patch model prompt wrapper (short-term) + medium-term model retraining.
7) Safe rollback checklist (if chosen)
   - Owner confirmation: model owner acknowledges rollback finger.
   - Runbooks: rwd-deploy rollback --model rwd-model:v1 --force --change-id RB-<ticket> 
   - Post-rollback monitoring: open-monitoring dashboard "rollback-watch" and alert on RSI for 30 minutes.
8) Postmortem and tagging
   - Create a short incident entry with probe ids, hypothesis, experiment artifacts, and final remediation. Tag model artifacts with label: "regression-2026-<shortid>".

Heuristics for blame (quick guide):
- If tokenization diffs align with changes in token-lexicon or new special tokens: blame upstream tokenizer/merge.
- If answers diverge only after 200 tokens and KV cache hit rate drops: blame KV cache eviction policy or cache serialization changes.
- If deterministic seeds produce nondeterministic outputs across runs: suspect scheduling/kernel regression in runtime.
- If differences appear only with quant profiles: suspect quantization thresholds or emergency mixed-precision fallback.

Automated helpers (scripts & services):
- rwd-eval fast-diff: produces slice-level delta report (path: /tools/rwd-eval/fast-diff).
- rwd-debugger-ui: dashboard that mounts probe population and allows side-by-side comparison (internal URL: https://console/internal/debugger).
- scripts/regression-hunt/auto-gather.sh — orchestrates telemetry gathers for an alert id.

SLOs and thresholds (operational):
| Metric | Alert threshold | Operator action | Escalation window |
|-------|-----------------|----------------|------------------|
| RSI (slice) | drop > 5% absolute or > 8% relative | Start interactive debugger | 30 min |
| Median token latency | +50ms | Inspect runtime traces | 60 min |
| KV cache hit rate | -10% | Inspect cache policy | 60 min |

Safe rollout recommendations:
- Prefer shadow-only experiments for hypothesis validation.
- Canary target size should not exceed 1% of production traffic unless safety validated.
- Any canary that changes quant profile must be labeled and limited to < 0.2% traffic until 12h of telemetry passes.

Audit and compliance:
- All debugger commands that mutate production state must emit audit_event with fields: operator, alert_id, action, before_model_id, after_model_id, ticket_id.
- Audit log location: s3://redwood-audit/regression-debugger/YYYY/MM/DD/<ticket>.json

Runbook snippets (examples):
- Start a focused debug session and gather probes:
  rwd-eval session start --alert RSI-4573 --owner priya.natarajan --mode interactive --out session-4573.tar.gz
- Re-run probes against previous artifact:
  rwd-eval debug run --probes session-4573/probes.json --model rwd-model:v1 --shadow-only --parallel 12 --out re-run-v1.json
- Canary deploy with quant fallback disabled:
  rwd-deploy canary --model rwd-model:v2 --quant-profile q-safe --target-pct 0.5 --reason "regression-debug" --ticket RG-4573

Post-incident tasks and knowledge capture:
1) Populate the regression index card (template path: /templates/regression-index-card).
2) Add annotated probe examples to the canonical prompt set for the slice.
3) If change was code/infra, open an ADR referencing the root cause and mitigation plan.

Appendix: Useful debugging commands and endpoints
- Telemetry queries: rwd-metrics query "metric(args)"
- Trace viewer: https://console/internal/traces?trace_id=TRACE-<id>
- Debug UI: https://console/internal/debugger
- Audit viewer: https://console/internal/audit?source=regression-debugger

Related pages
- /applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/probe-farm-orchestration-for-rare-regressions-2026
- /applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/shadow-to-prod-eval-continuity-guidelines-2026

Contact and escalation
- Oncall SRE: #oncall-sre (Slack)
- Model owners: team-applied-ml@redwood.com
- Emergency Pager: PagerDuty — Redwood ML Critical

This playbook is reviewed quarterly or after any P0 regression event.
