title: Converged Platform Capability Catalog and Ownership Matrix
preview:
Summary:
This document defines a converged catalog of platform capabilities (reusable services, sidecars, and shared abstractions), the ownership model for each capability, required SLOs and monitoring signals, integration contracts, and a lightweight onboarding + deprecation process. Its goal is to reduce duplication, clarify operational responsibility, and provide a repeatable checklist for teams that want to adopt or extend platform capabilities.

Audience:
- Platform engineers and service owners
- Product/feature teams integrating platform capabilities
- Platform SRE & oncall operators

Scope:
- Leaf capabilities provided by the platform runtime and platform-managed sidecars (e.g., prefix-cache, batching service, tenant-slice controller, cost-telemetry), and the integration contracts that consumers must implement.
- Does NOT include customer-managed application business logic or ephemeral developer tooling.

Key definitions:
- Capability: A platform-provided, versioned service or contract (API, sidecar, runtime hook) intended for cross-team reuse.
- Owner: The team responsible for runbook, SLOs, changes, and rollouts for a capability.
- Consumer: Any product/service that depends on a capability.

1) Capability catalog (summary table)
| Capability | Description | Owner team | Minimum SLOs | Primary telemetry | Integration type | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Prefix/KV Cache | Shared prefix caching for repeated prompt prefixes and KV state | eng-serving-runtime | 99.9% availability, 95th pct tail RTT < 20ms | cache_hit_rate, prefix_miss_ratio, p95_latency | Sidecar + runtime hook | Supports shard hints for multi-tenant isolation |
| Batcher | Continuous batching service for small requests | eng-platform | 99.95% availability, tail latency SLO see per-tenant contracts | batch_size_dist, queue_wait_ms, drop_rate | Sidecar / local agent | Batching policy configurable per-route |
| Tenant-Slicer | Priority slicing and credit accounting for tenants | eng-platform | 99.9% availability | tenant_throughput, credit_balance, evictions | Controller + policy DSL | Critical for dedicated pools |
| Cost Telemetry Aggregator | Token-level cost attribution service and export | eng-platform/finance-infra | 99.9% delivery to warehouse (24h) | tokens_attributed, attribution_delay_seconds | HTTP push + kafka export | Produces daily cost-report artifacts |
| Model-Variant Router | Runtime routing policy engine for model fallbacks | eng-serving-runtime | 99.95% policy-eval success | routing_decisions, fallback_count, eval_latency | Library + config | Supports canary and A/B tag semantics |
| Observability Envelope | Standardized SLI/trace headers & context propagation | eng-platform | 99.99% header preservation | trace_propagation_rate, header_trim_rate | Library + sidecar | Used by all other capabilities for consistent dashboards |

2) Ownership matrix (detailed responsibilities)
- eng-serving-runtime: owning teams operate the runtime components (prefix-cache, model-variant router). Responsibilities: kernel compatibility testing, release notes, perf bench artifacts, hotfixes, capacity planning.
- eng-platform: owns tenant-slicing, batching orchestration, cost telemetry ingestion, and platform SDKs. Responsibilities: SLA definition, rollout orchestration, quota semantics, runbooks, and billing signals.
- finance-infra (cross-functional): owns the downstream cost schema and warehouse exports. Responsibilities: reconciliation, cost model changes, cross-team billing questions.

3) SLOs, SLIs, and alerting guidance (per capability)
- Always define a primary availability SLI (successful API or RPC path) and at least one latency SLI (p95 or p99 depending on capability).
- Example (Prefix/KV Cache):
  - Availability SLI: successful cache GET/SET / total attempts; target 99.9% monthly.
  - Latency SLI: p95 end-to-end cache lookup latency < 20ms.
  - Alert thresholds: pages at SLO burn > 10% sustained for 30m, warn at 3%.

- Monitoring signals to emit for every capability:
  - availability (success rate),
  - p50/p95/p99 latency,
  - error class distribution (5xx, 4xx),
  - consumer-side backpressure metrics (queue_wait_ms, drop_rate),
  - capacity headroom (utilization ratio).

4) Integration contract template (example)
- Consumers must:
  1. Adopt the platform observability envelope (trace headers + tagging).
  2. Implement the capability adapter interface (example shim below).
  3. Provide a capability-consumer registration PR that declares expected QPS, typical request size, and latency requirements.

Sample adapter (YAML) for Batcher integration:
-----BEGIN SAMPLE YAML-----
adapter_version: v1
consumer_service: payments-api
expected_qps: 120
avg_payload_tokens: 48
batching_policy:
  max_delay_ms: 10
  max_batch_tokens: 4096
  fairness: weighted
-----END SAMPLE YAML-----

5) Onboarding checklist for adding a new consumer
1. Fill the consumer registration template and submit to the capability owner (link to registration form in team wiki).
2. Run integration tests using the local-sidecar shim (steps below).
3. Deploy to canary with traffic < 2% for 24h, monitor SLI dashboards.
4. Confirm cost/telemetry artifacts are visible in the Cost Telemetry Aggregator.
5. Gradually ramp to target traffic following the rollout playbook; document the rollout in the consumer’s PR.

Local sidecar test commands (example):
- Start shim container: docker run --rm -v $(pwd)/shim-config.yaml: /etc/shim/config.yaml platform/local-batcher-shim:latest
- Run smoke tests: ./tools/platform-integration-test --capability=batcher --consumer=payments-api

6) Rollout & release policy for capabilities
- Semantic versioning for capability contracts (major.minor.patch).
- Backwards-incompatible (major) changes require:
  - 90-day deprecation announcement, automated analyzers to detect consumers, and a migration guide.
- Minor changes: feature flags + opt-in during canary.
- Patch changes: immediate; owners must publish release notes.

7) Monitoring dashboards & alert playbook (recommended panels)
Per-capability dashboard should include:
- SLI trend (30d), burn rate panel, top contributing error codes, consumer-level throughput table, recent deploys timeline, and cost per-tenant heatmap.
Alert playbook pattern:
1. Paging alert -> page oncall for owning team if SLO burn > 10% sustained 30m.
2. Secondary notify channel to consumer owners if consumer-specific errors exceed threshold.
3. Automated mitigation: temporary throttle or routed fallback to a compatible variant.

8) Cost and chargeback signals
- Emit a token attribution row per request to the Cost Telemetry Aggregator with fields: timestamp, tenant_id, tokens_consumed, capability_id, cost_model_version.
- Owners must produce a daily cost rollup export that finance-infra can consume.
- Dashboards: monthly cost by tenant, cost per-1000 tokens, top-10 cost accounts.

9) Risk assessment & fallback strategies
- For every capability provide:
  - A compatible fallback variant (e.g., cache miss -> direct model call with rate-limit),
  - A grace degradation path (serve stale KV for read-only lanes),
  - A safety circuit breaker that transitions to degraded mode and notifies owners.

10) Capability lifecycle (add, change, deprecate)
- Add: capability owner publishes spec -> consumer onboarding template -> integration & canary -> production.
- Change: follow semantic versioning; for non-breaking changes owners must provide migration notes.
- Deprecate: 90-day notice + automated consumer discovery + mandatory migration plan.

11) Runbook excerpt (operational play steps)
Situation: sudden rise in prefix-cache miss rate and p99 latency
- Immediate steps:
  1. Check prefix-cache dashboard for sharding imbalance and evictions.
  2. Run: ./tools/cache-inspect --shard-heatmap | jq .top_evictions
  3. If a single shard > 80% eviction: trigger shard rebalancer (manual safe-mode): /ops/rebalancer reassign --shard X --drain-rate 0.2
  4. If overall p99 > 500ms and traffic > 80% of capacity: enable temporary read-through rate limit: curl -X POST https://platform/api/controls/throttle -d '{"capability":"prefix-cache","target_pct":80}'
  5. Page eng-serving-runtime oncall and attach the cache diagnostics bundle.

12) Roadmap alignment (next 12 months)
- Q2 2026: Standardize observability-envelope v2 and adopt by 80% of capabilities.
- Q3 2026: Launch capability-contract-linter to detect breaking contract changes in PRs.
- Q4 2026: Automated consumer discovery pipeline for deprecation notices.

13) Governance & cross-team decision flow
- Capability owners propose major changes via a lightweight ADR; reviews required from consumer representatives and platform SRE.
- Quarterly capability review: owners present SLO health, onboarding velocity, and cost trends.

14) Contacts and escalation
- Primary owner (eng-platform): platform-capabilities@redwood.ai (pager: #platform-oncall)
- eng-serving-runtime oncall: #runtime-oncall
- Finance-infra: finance-infra@redwood.ai

Appendices
A) Example capability-consumer registration checklist (bullet form)- Service name, owner, expected qps, avg tokens, acceptable tail latency, required availability, retry semantics, canary schedule.

B) Useful queries (example)
- Top consumers by token spend (BigQuery):
  SELECT tenant_id, SUM(tokens) AS tokens FROM `redwood.cost_events` WHERE event_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY tokens DESC LIMIT 50;

C) Change log
- 2026-02-10: Created initial catalog and onboarding checklist (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-03-01: Clarified SLO thresholds and added shard rebalancer steps (Daniel Park).

Related reading:
- /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-snapshot-and-rollback-patterns-2026
- /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

Summary:
This document defines a converged catalog of platform capabilities (reusable services, sidecars, and shared abstractions), the ownership model for each capability, required SLOs and monitoring signals, integration contracts, and a lightweight onboarding + deprecation process. Its goal is to reduce duplication, clarify operational responsibility, and provide a repeatable checklist for teams that want to adopt or extend platform capabilities.

Audience:
- Platform engineers and service owners
- Product/feature teams integrating platform capabilities
- Platform SRE & oncall operators

Scope:
- Leaf capabilities provided by the platform runtime and platform-managed sidecars (e.g., prefix-cache, batching service, tenant-slice controller, cost-telemetry), and the integration contracts that consumers must implement.
- Does NOT include customer-managed application business logic or ephemeral developer tooling.

Key definitions:
- Capability: A platform-provided, versioned service or contract (API, sidecar, runtime hook) intended for cross-team reuse.
- Owner: The team responsible for runbook, SLOs, changes, and rollouts for a capability.
- Consumer: Any product/service that depends on a capability.

1) Capability catalog (summary table)
| Capability | Description | Owner team | Minimum SLOs | Primary telemetry | Integration type | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Prefix/KV Cache | Shared prefix caching for repeated prompt prefixes and KV state | eng-serving-runtime | 99.9% availability, 95th pct tail RTT < 20ms | cache_hit_rate, prefix_miss_ratio, p95_latency | Sidecar + runtime hook | Supports shard hints for multi-tenant isolation |
| Batcher | Continuous batching service for small requests | eng-platform | 99.95% availability, tail latency SLO see per-tenant contracts | batch_size_dist, queue_wait_ms, drop_rate | Sidecar / local agent | Batching policy configurable per-route |
| Tenant-Slicer | Priority slicing and credit accounting for tenants | eng-platform | 99.9% availability | tenant_throughput, credit_balance, evictions | Controller + policy DSL | Critical for dedicated pools |
| Cost Telemetry Aggregator | Token-level cost attribution service and export | eng-platform/finance-infra | 99.9% delivery to warehouse (24h) | tokens_attributed, attribution_delay_seconds | HTTP push + kafka export | Produces daily cost-report artifacts |
| Model-Variant Router | Runtime routing policy engine for model fallbacks | eng-serving-runtime | 99.95% policy-eval success | routing_decisions, fallback_count, eval_latency | Library + config | Supports canary and A/B tag semantics |
| Observability Envelope | Standardized SLI/trace headers & context propagation | eng-platform | 99.99% header preservation | trace_propagation_rate, header_trim_rate | Library + sidecar | Used by all other capabilities for consistent dashboards |

2) Ownership matrix (detailed responsibilities)
- eng-serving-runtime: owning teams operate the runtime components (prefix-cache, model-variant router). Responsibilities: kernel compatibility testing, release notes, perf bench artifacts, hotfixes, capacity planning.
- eng-platform: owns tenant-slicing, batching orchestration, cost telemetry ingestion, and platform SDKs. Responsibilities: SLA definition, rollout orchestration, quota semantics, runbooks, and billing signals.
- finance-infra (cross-functional): owns the downstream cost schema and warehouse exports. Responsibilities: reconciliation, cost model changes, cross-team billing questions.

3) SLOs, SLIs, and alerting guidance (per capability)
- Always define a primary availability SLI (successful API or RPC path) and at least one latency SLI (p95 or p99 depending on capability).
- Example (Prefix/KV Cache):
  - Availability SLI: successful cache GET/SET / total attempts; target 99.9% monthly.
  - Latency SLI: p95 end-to-end cache lookup latency < 20ms.
  - Alert thresholds: pages at SLO burn > 10% sustained for 30m, warn at 3%.

- Monitoring signals to emit for every capability:
  - availability (success rate),
  - p50/p95/p99 latency,
  - error class distribution (5xx, 4xx),
  - consumer-side backpressure metrics (queue_wait_ms, drop_rate),
  - capacity headroom (utilization ratio).

4) Integration contract template (example)
- Consumers must:
  1. Adopt the platform observability envelope (trace headers + tagging).
  2. Implement the capability adapter interface (example shim below).
  3. Provide a capability-consumer registration PR that declares expected QPS, typical request size, and latency requirements.

Sample adapter (YAML) for Batcher integration:
-----BEGIN SAMPLE YAML-----
adapter_version: v1
consumer_service: payments-api
expected_qps: 120
avg_payload_tokens: 48
batching_policy:
  max_delay_ms: 10
  max_batch_tokens: 4096
  fairness: weighted
-----END SAMPLE YAML-----

5) Onboarding checklist for adding a new consumer
1. Fill the consumer registration template and submit to the capability owner (link to registration form in team wiki).
2. Run integration tests using the local-sidecar shim (steps below).
3. Deploy to canary with traffic < 2% for 24h, monitor SLI dashboards.
4. Confirm cost/telemetry artifacts are visible in the Cost Telemetry Aggregator.
5. Gradually ramp to target traffic following the rollout playbook; document the rollout in the consumer’s PR.

Local sidecar test commands (example):
- Start shim container: docker run --rm -v $(pwd)/shim-config.yaml: /etc/shim/config.yaml platform/local-batcher-shim:latest
- Run smoke tests: ./tools/platform-integration-test --capability=batcher --consumer=payments-api

6) Rollout & release policy for capabilities
- Semantic versioning for capability contracts (major.minor.patch).
- Backwards-incompatible (major) changes require:
  - 90-day deprecation announcement, automated analyzers to detect consumers, and a migration guide.
- Minor changes: feature flags + opt-in during canary.
- Patch changes: immediate; owners must publish release notes.

7) Monitoring dashboards & alert playbook (recommended panels)
Per-capability dashboard should include:
- SLI trend (30d), burn rate panel, top contributing error codes, consumer-level throughput table, recent deploys timeline, and cost per-tenant heatmap.
Alert playbook pattern:
1. Paging alert -> page oncall for owning team if SLO burn > 10% sustained 30m.
2. Secondary notify channel to consumer owners if consumer-specific errors exceed threshold.
3. Automated mitigation: temporary throttle or routed fallback to a compatible variant.

8) Cost and chargeback signals
- Emit a token attribution row per request to the Cost Telemetry Aggregator with fields: timestamp, tenant_id, tokens_consumed, capability_id, cost_model_version.
- Owners must produce a daily cost rollup export that finance-infra can consume.
- Dashboards: monthly cost by tenant, cost per-1000 tokens, top-10 cost accounts.

9) Risk assessment & fallback strategies
- For every capability provide:
  - A compatible fallback variant (e.g., cache miss -> direct model call with rate-limit),
  - A grace degradation path (serve stale KV for read-only lanes),
  - A safety circuit breaker that transitions to degraded mode and notifies owners.

10) Capability lifecycle (add, change, deprecate)
- Add: capability owner publishes spec -> consumer onboarding template -> integration & canary -> production.
- Change: follow semantic versioning; for non-breaking changes owners must provide migration notes.
- Deprecate: 90-day notice + automated consumer discovery + mandatory migration plan.

11) Runbook excerpt (operational play steps)
Situation: sudden rise in prefix-cache miss rate and p99 latency
- Immediate steps:
  1. Check prefix-cache dashboard for sharding imbalance and evictions.
  2. Run: ./tools/cache-inspect --shard-heatmap | jq .top_evictions
  3. If a single shard > 80% eviction: trigger shard rebalancer (manual safe-mode): /ops/rebalancer reassign --shard X --drain-rate 0.2
  4. If overall p99 > 500ms and traffic > 80% of capacity: enable temporary read-through rate limit: curl -X POST https://platform/api/controls/throttle -d '{"capability":"prefix-cache","target_pct":80}'
  5. Page eng-serving-runtime oncall and attach the cache diagnostics bundle.

12) Roadmap alignment (next 12 months)
- Q2 2026: Standardize observability-envelope v2 and adopt by 80% of capabilities.
- Q3 2026: Launch capability-contract-linter to detect breaking contract changes in PRs.
- Q4 2026: Automated consumer discovery pipeline for deprecation notices.

13) Governance & cross-team decision flow
- Capability owners propose major changes via a lightweight ADR; reviews required from consumer representatives and platform SRE.
- Quarterly capability review: owners present SLO health, onboarding velocity, and cost trends.

14) Contacts and escalation
- Primary owner (eng-platform): platform-capabilities@redwood.ai (pager: #platform-oncall)
- eng-serving-runtime oncall: #runtime-oncall
- Finance-infra: finance-infra@redwood.ai

Appendices
A) Example capability-consumer registration checklist (bullet form)- Service name, owner, expected qps, avg tokens, acceptable tail latency, required availability, retry semantics, canary schedule.

B) Useful queries (example)
- Top consumers by token spend (BigQuery):
  SELECT tenant_id, SUM(tokens) AS tokens FROM `redwood.cost_events` WHERE event_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY tokens DESC LIMIT 50;

C) Change log
- 2026-02-10: Created initial catalog and onboarding checklist (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-03-01: Clarified SLO thresholds and added shard rebalancer steps (Daniel Park).

- /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-snapshot-and-rollback-patterns-2026
- /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

title: Latency and Token-Budgeted Prompt Design Guide
preview:
Summary:
This guide defines a repeatable design and validation workflow for authoring prompts when you have hard latency or token-budget constraints. It covers principled prompt composition, token accounting, cache-priming patterns, integration tests for the eval harness, and rollout checks for model promotion. The target reader is engineers and applied-ML owners preparing prompts for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).

Scope and objectives:
- Scope: customer-facing text- and chat-style prompts used in production inference paths (single-turn and multi-turn). Does not cover multimodal binary payload formats.
- Objectives:
  - Ensure prompts meet latency SLOs and token budgets for cost predictability.
  - Maximize reuse of KV/prefix caches and enable safe quantized deployments.
  - Provide testable templates and an automated validation checklist for onboarding.

Key definitions:
- Token budget: maximum average tokens per request (input + expected output) allocated to a route.
- Latency SLO: 95th percentile latency target for generation portion of a request (example: 500 ms for 128-token responses on p4d).
- Priming slice: compact context intended to be cached (prefix) and not sent every request.

Principles for budgeted prompt design:
1) Make context scannable and compressible:
   - Prefer concise, semantically dense instructions (one short sentence per instruction).
   - Replace long enumerations with named anchors and include a short lookup when needed.
2) Partition mutable vs stable context:
   - Place stable facts, policy, or persona in a priming slice (cacheable).
   - Place request-specific data in the transient context (per-request tokens).
3) Budget first, optimize second:
   - Choose a hard token budget per route (example budgets below).
   - If quality drops, prefer targeted prompt edits and reranking over increasing budget.
4) Design for cache hits and graceful degradation:
   - Structure prompts so missing cache entries are safe: fallbacks should still be valid albeit more verbose.
5) Test under quantized kernels and batching:
   - Validate prompts with the quantization profile(s) that will be used in production.

Token budget examples (suggested starting points):
| Route class | Input budget (tokens) | Output budget (tokens) | Notes |
|-------------|----------------------:|-----------------------:|-------|
| Low-latency short response | 64 | 64 | Mobile search snippets, chat suggestions |
| Medium-latency assistant | 256 | 256 | Feature assistants with context and follow-ups |
| High-throughput embedding-driven | 128 | 64 | Embedding-based rerank + short answer |

Prompt composition templates (text):
- Compact instruction template (cacheable prefix):
  "SYSTEM: You are an assistant that answers concisely. STYLE: bullet-brief.
   FACTS: [stable facts — place in priming slice].
   --END PREFIX--
   USER: <customer prompt or example>"

- Split-prompt example for KV caching:
  1) Priming (cached): "Docs summary: <3-line abstract>. Safety: refuse hallucinations. Style: concise."
  2) Ephemeral (per-request): "User query: {query}. Context snippets: {topK passages}. Task: answer in <= 64 tokens."

Token accounting rules (practical):
- Count tokens using the production tokenizer for the model family.
- Include: system + user + instruction + expected output budget. Exclude: wire/transport framing added by SDKs (we assert those are negligible but verify).
- Formula (string to implement in CI):
  expected_total = ceil(tokenize(prefix)) + ceil(max_input_tokens) + ceil(max_output_tokens) + safety_margin
  where safety_margin = max(16, ceil(0.05 * expected_total))

Service-level SLO guidance (example defaults):
- p95 generation latency: 500 ms for <= 128 output tokens on Dedicated p4d (single-request, no batching).
- Token budget compliance: <1% of requests exceed the configured budget in steady-state testing.
- Cache hit contribution: primed routes should target >= 60% cache-hit rate in steady traffic to justify the split.

Validation checklist (pre-promotion):
1) Tokenize all templates with production tokenizer and confirm expected_total <= route_budget for 99th pct of test inputs.
2) Run the sample prompt family through the quantized model artifacts (if using quant). Compare response-quality metrics (ROUGE/EM or task-specific thresholds) against baseline model.
3) Shadow-run the route for at least 24h (or 1M requests equivalent) with eval harness:
   - Verify p95 latency meets SLO under target batching levels.
   - Verify token-budget exceedance rate <1%.
   - Collect cache-hit telemetry.
4) Execute functional tests: safety-sentinels, hallucination probes, and continuity checks for multi-turn sequences.
5) Record rollout plan and rollback criteria in the release ticket.

Automated eval harness integration (example YAML):
- name: prompt-priming-budget-check
  type: shadow-scan
  routes: ["assistant-short", "assistant-medium"]
  samples: sampler.lookup("production-probe-anchored")
  checks:
    - name: token-budget-violation
      assert: percentile(tokens_total, 99) <= ${route.token_budget}
    - name: latency-p95
      assert: p95_generation_latency_ms <= ${route.p95_slo_ms}
    - name: quality-delta
      metric: fidelity_score
      assert: delta <= 0.03

Design patterns and anti-patterns:
- Pattern: "Prefix compression" — move stable long lists into a single-line compressed annotation with an index reference. This yields better cache behavior and smaller prefixes.
- Pattern: "Two-stage answering" — generate a compact plan in the first micro-call (<= 32 tokens) and expand in an allowed secondary call when more tokens are required; use only when latency SLOs permit.
- Anti-pattern: embedding a long dump of unrelated documentation in the per-request context — kills cacheability and unpredictably increases cost.

Rolling out with quantized/models mix:
- If deploying a quantized artifact, add a quant-fallback policy:
  - Route config should declare compatible variants: [fp16, int8-alpha].
  - On increased error budget or unexpected fidelity regressions, traffic must automatically revert to the last-known-good variant.
- Example rollout policy: gradual: 0% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 4 days with canary quality gates at each step.

Monitoring and telemetry (required):
- Per-route: p50/p95/p99 generation latency, tokens_in, tokens_out, token_budget_exceed_rate, cache_hit_rate, fidelity_delta.
- Alerts: token_budget_exceed_rate > 1% for 30m; p95 latency breach > 20% of SLO for 15m; fidelity_delta > configured threshold.

On-call playbook (short):
1) Triage alert: check route telemetry dashboard and recent eval harness run.
2) If token budget exceedances: roll traffic to smaller output_budget or enable autoscaling; if immediate fix required, rollback to previous prompt template tag.
3) If fidelity regressions observed under quant: trigger auto-revert to fp16 variant and open a regression triage ticket.

Ownership and lifecycle:
- Prompt owners: the product/feature team that owns the surface area. They are responsible for template changes and providing sample probes.
- Applied ML: responsible for quantitative validation (eval harness runs, quant checks).
- Runtime team: ensures metrics, SLOs, and cache telemetry are available for the route.

Appendix A — Example short prompt family: 
- Priming slice (cached):
  "Assistant role: concise technical helper. Allowed formats: bullet list, short paragraph. Refuse to invent facts. 
   Policy: prefer public documentation only."
- Dynamic slice (per request):
  "Question: {user_query}
   TopK: {top3_docs}
   Task: answer in <= 64 tokens, include one-line citation."

Appendix B — Example token-accounting pseudocode (run in CI):
```
# pseudo
def token_budget_check(prefix, sample_inputs, max_output):
    tokenizer = load_prod_tokenizer()
    prefix_tokens = tokenizer.count(prefix)
    for s in sample_inputs:
        total = prefix_tokens + tokenizer.count(s) + max_output
        margin = max(16, int(0.05 * total))
        assert total + margin <= ROUTE_BUDGET
```

References and links:
- See eval harness runbook for shadow scans and orchestration.
- Quantization profiling playbooks for per-workload checks.

Change log:
- 2026-02-10: created (initial draft).
- 2026-03-01: updated token accounting rules and added YAML integration example.

Contact: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai for questions or template merge requests.

   Task: answer in <= 64 tokens, include one-line citation."

Appendix B — Example token-accounting pseudocode (run in CI):
```
# pseudo
def token_budget_check(prefix, sample_inputs, max_output):
    tokenizer = load_prod_tokenizer()
    prefix_tokens = tokenizer.count(prefix)
    for s in sample_inputs:
        total = prefix_tokens + tokenizer.count(s) + max_output
        margin = max(16, int(0.05 * total))
        assert total + margin <= ROUTE_BUDGET
```

References and links:
- See eval harness runbook for shadow scans and orchestration.
- Quantization profiling playbooks for per-workload checks.

Change log:
- 2026-02-10: created (initial draft).
- 2026-03-01: updated token accounting rules and added YAML integration example.

Contact: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai for questions or template merge requests.

title: Entitlement Chronicle and Log Preservation Guidance
preview:
## Overview

Purpose: This document defines the "Entitlement Chronicle" concept — a canonical, time-ordered record of entitlement lifecycle events (grants, revocations, role changes, delegation), and the operational guidance for preserving authorization-related telemetry and artifacts to satisfy compliance, incident response, and forensic needs. It covers log capture, retention/archival policy, chain-of-custody procedures, SDLC integration points, and runbook steps for producing compliance evidence bundles.

Scope: Applies to all Redwood Inference services and control planes that issue or enforce entitlements (console authn/authz, service-to-service IAM, admin UI, provisioning pipelines, and third-party plugins). Does not replace existing retention policies for privacy-sensitive user content — it augments audit retention with explicit custody and preservation steps for authz telemetry.

Audience: security engineers, platform owners, SREs, compliance, legal, and incident responders.

---

## Definitions

- Entitlement Chronicle: an append-only stream and index for authorization-related events (examples: role.create, role.bind, access.activate, privilege-escalation-request, jita-activation).
- Chronicle Record: single event with provenance metadata (actor, credential, approval id, request id, timestamp, region, resource identifier).
- Evidence Bundle: curated archive produced for audits or incidents containing a filtered slice of chronicle records, supporting logs, snapshots, and attestations.
- Preservation Marker: an immutable snapshot record that indicates a legal hold or preservation request for a set of chronicle records.

---

## Ownership and Roles

- Security (owner): defines retention windows, chain-of-custody procedures, legal-hold coordination, and approves exception requests.
- Platform/SRE (operators): implement chronicle ingestion, backup/immutable storage, alerting, and snapshot mechanics.
- Service Owners (owners): annotate events with service identifiers, map resources to residency requirements, and validate that events include required attributes.
- Compliance/Legal: request preservation markers, consume evidence bundles, and trigger long-term archival.

Contact list: security-oncall@redwood.example, sres-platform@redwood.example, compliance-requests@redwood.example.

---

## Chronicle Event Model (required fields)

Each event ingested to the Entitlement Chronicle MUST include the following attributes:

1. event_id (UUID v4)
2. timestamp (ISO-8601 UTC)
3. actor (user/service principal id)
4. actor_type (human | machine | system)
5. action (enum: grant | revoke | modify | request | approve | deny | escalate | delegate)
6. target_resource (type:id)
7. before_state (JSON blob or hash, nullable)
8. after_state (JSON blob or hash, nullable)
9. request_id (correlation id across systems)
10. approval_reference (approval workflow id, optional)
11. region (cloud region or on-prem cluster id)
12. residency_tag (data-residency label required for mapping retention)
13. signature (ed25519 signature or equivalent provenance token)

Example JSON snippet (for documentation only):

{
  "event_id": "6f7b8d3e-9a2c-4c7a-b6e5-1c2f4ab8c0d1",
  "timestamp": "2026-02-28T14:23:45Z",
  "actor": "svc-deploy-bot@redwood",
  "actor_type": "machine",
  "action": "grant",
  "target_resource": "project:analytics-prod",
  "after_state": "{\"roles\":[\"viewer\"]}",
  "request_id": "req-20260228-3392",
  "approval_reference": "approvals/12345",
  "region": "us-west-2",
  "residency_tag": "eu-sv-dr",
  "signature": "sig:v1:ABCD..."
}

Note: the Chronicle accepts both structured blobs and stable content-hash references to object storage for large 'before_state'/'after_state' payloads.

---

## Ingestion and Immutable Storage

- Ingestion pipeline: services must forward entitlement events to the Chronicle ingest topic (kafka/internal-chronicle-authz). Messages are validated, enriched (add service owner tags, residency mapping), signed, and written to an append-only segmented store (immutable object store + index).
- Immutable retention: ingested segments are committed to Write-Once Object Storage (WORM) with immutability flags for the configured retention window.
- Backup cadence: daily incremental snapshots with weekly full snapshots. Snapshot artifacts are signed and checksummed.

Operational SLOs:
- 99.9% of entitlement events validated and indexed within 30s of emission.
- 99.99% durability for archived Chronicle segments over retention window.

---

## Retention and Archival Matrix

| Entitlement Class | Typical retention | Location | Legal/Compliance Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Administrative (privileged grants, role edits) | 7 years | WORM-archive (primary), encrypted cold storage | Default for regulated customers and SOC2
| Operational (service-to-service role rotations) | 3 years | WORM-archive (regional) | May be reduced for low-risk internal services with documented exception
| Short-lived activations (JITA, temp tokens) | 180 days (index); 7 years (preserved on legal hold) | Indexed fast-lookup + periodic compact archive | Must include approval_reference for traceability
| Decommissioned resource histories | 1 year after resource deletion | regional cold storage | Retained for forensic reconstruction only

Retention exceptions: All retention reductions require an explicit documented exception reviewed by Security + Legal. Exceptions are recorded as preservation_marker events and included in the Chronicle.

---

## Data Residency Mapping Rules

1. Event locality: Chronicle events inherit the residency tag of the target resource. If the target lacks a residency tag, merge behavior is: service-owner hint -> fallback to ingest-region.
2. Cross-border replication: Only index metadata may be replicated globally for search; raw payloads for events with residency_tag != global must remain within the specified jurisdiction (encrypted at rest with region KMS).
3. Access controls: Evidence bundles that include records from multiple jurisdictions require dual-authorization for export; the bundle generation service enforces this.

---

## Chain of Custody and Preservation Markers

- Preservation marker: A special chronicle record added when an incident/ audit requires a frozen snapshot. Fields: marker_id, preserved_range (start_event_id, end_event_id), requester, legal_hold_id, timestamp, snapshot_location, attestations (signatures).
- Snapshots are generated as immutable artifacts with accompanying manifest.json having checksums and signer identities.
- All preservation activities are logged to a separate preservation-audit log that itself is WORM-protected.

Chain-of-custody checklist for legal hold:
1. Security or Legal issues request preservation marker via compliance-requests@redwood.example.
2. Platform operator creates snapshot: run `chronicle-snapshot --start <event-id> --end <event-id> --hold-id <LH123>` and verifies manifest digest.
3. Preservation artifact uploaded to WORM store; manifest and signatures circulated to requestor and security-oncall.
4. Preservation-audit entry created and cross-referenced to the incident ticket.

---

## Integration with Secure SDLC

- Build-time annotations: Pipeline agents must emit entitlement-affecting approvals and gating decisions into the Chronicle (example: terraform apply approvals, RBAC policy changes in code reviews).
- Commit hooks: Git commit hook integrates with approvals service to append approval_reference to Chronicle when infrastructure RBAC files are merged.
- Release gates: Deployment systems must verify there are no outstanding legal holds against resources targeted by a rollout; the deployment engine queries the Chronicle Preservation API before proceeding.

Example pipeline step (bash):

```bash
# append approval metadata to chronicle before promotion
curl -s -X POST $CHRONICLE_INGEST/v1/event \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $PIPELINE_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"event_id":"'$EVENT_ID'","timestamp":"'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'","actor":"ci-pipeline@redwood","action":"approve","target_resource":"infra:rbac-policy","approval_reference":"pull/4567"}'
```

---

## Incident Response: Triage and Preservation Runbook

1. Triage (0-30 minutes):
   - Confirm scope: determine impacted services and resources.
   - Identify quick-preserve set: list of event ranges and candidate preservation markers.
   - Contact Security and Legal if cross-border or PII is involved.
2. Preservation (30-120 minutes):
   - Create Preservation Marker via `chronicle-snapshot` (see chain-of-custody checklist).
   - Export Evidence Bundle (see section below).
3. Investigation (hours-days):
   - Use chronicle query interface to reconstruct timeline (correlate request_id, approval_reference, and service traces).
   - Collect supplementary artifacts: service logs, API gateway traces, workload snapshots, and config versions.
4. Remediation and Reporting (days-weeks):
   - Produce final Evidence Bundle and submit to Compliance with manifest and hash.
   - Document lessons and update Chronicle ingestion/validation rules if gaps found.

Escalation thresholds: if a preservation marker covers >1000 events or crosses >2 jurisdictions, escalate to Security Director and Legal within 2 hours.

---

## Evidence Bundle Creation (how to produce a defensible archive)

1. Select event range(s) and related artifacts (traces, logs, approvals, snapshots).
2. Generate snapshot(s) and manifest(s) with checksums (SHA-256) and signer identities.
3. Produce an index file (ndjson) mapping event_id -> artifact_location.
4. Apply export controls (redaction for PII if required) and record the redaction policy in the manifest.
5. Deliver bundle to Compliance via secure transfer channel (SFTP over mutual TLS) or upload to secure evidence vault.

CLI example to generate bundle: 

chronicle-bundle --start <event-id> --end <event-id> --include-traces --output /tmp/bundle-LH123.tar.gz --signer svc-preserve@redwood

---

## Forensic Queries and Example Playbook

- Quick timeline query (by request_id):

Example: search for events correlated to a request

SELECT event_id, timestamp, actor, action, target_resource, approval_reference
FROM chronicle_index
WHERE request_id = 'req-20260228-3392'
ORDER BY timestamp ASC;

- Find privileged grants in date window:

SELECT event_id, timestamp, actor, target_resource
FROM chronicle_index
WHERE action = 'grant' AND target_resource LIKE 'project:%' AND timestamp BETWEEN '2026-02-01' AND '2026-02-28';

Operational note: queries that return >10k events require pre-approval and should instead be executed as a snapshot job to avoid load on index clusters.

---

## Alerts and Operational Metrics

- Alerts to create:
  - High-volume entitlement churn: >500 entitlement changes in window (5m) for a single service.
  - Unsigned events detected (missing signature).
  - Preservation snapshot failure or manifest mismatch.
  - Cross-jurisdiction export attempt without dual authorization.

- Dashboards to maintain: ingestion latency (P50/P95/P99), index error rates, preservation jobs success rate, number of active legal holds.

---

## Change Management and Risk Exceptions

- Any change to Chronicle schema or retention defaults MUST be documented in an ADR and reviewed by Security + Compliance.
- Risk exceptions for reduced retention require: justification, compensating controls, review by Security, and approval recorded in the Chronicle as an exception_event with a 3-year maximum active window.

---

## Appendix A: Quick Commands

- Create preservation snapshot:
  chronicle-snapshot --start <event-id> --end <event-id> --hold-id <LH123> --signer svc-preserve@redwood

- Verify manifest digest:
  openssl dgst -sha256 -hex manifest.json

- Generate evidence bundle (signed):
  chronicle-bundle --start <event-id> --end <event-id> --output /securevault/bundle-LH123.tar.gz --signer svc-preserve@redwood

---

## Appendix B: Frequently Asked Questions

Q: When should I request a preservation marker?
A: Immediately after identifying an incident with potential regulatory impact, cross-border access issues, or when Legal requests a hold. Early preservation reduces risk that relevant events are overwritten or compacted.

Q: Can production teams reduce retention for a low-sensitivity service?
A: Only via a documented exception; the default retention policy stands until an approved exception is in the Chronicle.

---

## Revision History

- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-02-15: Added SDLC integration examples (Anika Bose).
- 2026-03-01: Clarified cross-jurisdiction export controls and escalation thresholds (Liam O'Connor).

title: SLO to Customer Impact Classifier and Remediation Workflow
preview:
Overview

This page defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 200ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defined rolling window.
- Customer-impact tier: a 1–4 classification used to route and prioritize incident response.

Customer-impact tiers (quick guide)
| Tier | User-visible symptoms | Business effect | Escalation urgency | Owner to notify |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Widespread request failures or complete service outage across regions | Severe revenue/customer retention impact | Immediate (page) | SRE lead + Product PM + CS Director |
| 2 | Large subset of customers impacted (major tenants) or major feature down | Significant business disruption for key customers | High (page + slack) | SRE oncall + Product owner + CS manager |
| 3 | Localized errors, degraded latency for non-critical endpoints | Minor customer annoyance, limited support tickets | Medium (slack alert) | On-call SRE + Feature owner |
| 4 | Minor latency increase or transient errors with no customer tickets | No immediate business impact | Low (ticket) | On-call SRE |

SLO catalog excerpt (example mapping)
| SLO name | Metric | Target | Error-budget window | Default customer-impact tier |
|----------|--------|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| request_latency_p95_public | request_latency_p95{route=~"/v1/.*"} | <= 200ms | 30d | 2 |
| api_error_rate_5m | sum(rate(api_errors[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests[5m])) | <= 0.5% | 7d | 1 |
| embedding_throughput_tail | embedding_infer_latency_p99 | < 1.2s | 14d | 3 |
| console_ui_availability | synthetic_check(console_login) | 99.5% monthly | 30d | 3 |

Classification algorithm (operational)
1) Alert receives telemetry: detector alerts on SLI breach or error-budget burn (alerting config).
2) Metrics enrichment: pull impact signals (affected tenant count, region distribution, top N tenants by RPS, open support tickets matching pattern).
3) Heuristics engine evaluates the following scores (0–100): failure-scope, severity, customer-exposure.
4) Combined score maps to Tier: Score >= 80 -> Tier 1; 60–79 -> Tier 2; 30–59 -> Tier 3; < 30 -> Tier 4.
5) Attach classification to incident and follow the tiered remediation workflow below.

Heuristics inputs (examples)
- affected_tenant_ratio: percent of total tenant RPS impacted.
- p95_latency_delta: percent change vs baseline.
- p99_error_spike: absolute increase in error count at p99.
- active_cs_tickets: number of open tickets tagged /inference or /api.

Operational remediations by tier
Tier 1 (Immediate response)
- Page the SRE oncall and SRE lead.
- Open an incident in the incident-management system using template: tag=CUSTOMER_IMPACT_TIER_1.
- Run mitigation playbook: fallback-to-compatible-model-variant (see runbook link).
- If capacity-related: trigger immediate capacity burst policy (see CLI below).
- Notify CS and Product leadership within 15 minutes with templated Slack/Jira.

Tier 2 (High-priority)
- Notify on-call via high-severity Slack alert.
- Execute targeted throttles or tenant fair-share configuration.
- Begin canary rollback of recent model or config changes (if correlated).

Tier 3 (Operational handling)
- Create ticket and schedule on-call investigation within 30–60 minutes.
- Apply mitigations from the runbook: restart worker pool, clear KV cache, or disabled newly-deployed feature flag.

Tier 4 (Monitoring)
- Monitor metrics and let automated remediation run. If unresolved after SLA window (6 hours), escalate to Tier 3.

Sample alert rule (Prometheus-style YAML)
alert: HighAPIErrorRate
expr: |
  sum(rate(api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.005
for: 2m
labels:
  severity: page
  impact: "possible-customer-impact"
annotations:
  summary: "API error rate >0.5% over 5m"
  runbook: "https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation"

Enrichment query examples (pseudo-SQL / analytics)
- Tenants affected in last 10m: SELECT tenant_id, count(*) FROM requests WHERE status >= 500 AND timestamp > now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 50;
- Support ticket correlation: count tickets where created_at >= now()-10m AND tags IN ('api','inference')

Capacity escalation knobs (examples)
| Trigger | Condition | Automated action | Escalation threshold |
|---------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| headroom_exhausted | available_gpu_slots < 5% | trigger-burst-alloc --pool=shared-burst | immediate (Tier1) |
| model_warm_pool_empty | warm_pool_size < configured_min | scale-up warm pool by 2 nodes | Tier2 |
| p95_latency_spike | p95 > baseline*1.5 for 10m | enable-prefetch-kv-cache=true | Tier2 |

CLI examples
- Trigger burst capacity (operators only):
  rcmd infra capacity burst apply --pool shared-burst --reason "Tier1 SLO breach" --expires 30m
- Re-route traffic to fallback models:
  rcmd infra routing promote --from model-xyz-v2 --to model-xyz-v1 --weight 100

Communication templates
Slack template (Tier1)
- Channel: #incidents
- Message: :rotating_light: *Tier 1 SLO breach* — request_error_rate_5m at 1.2% impacting ~32% of tenants. IC: @oncall. Mitigation in progress: toggling fallback routing. Please notify CS lead @renee.zhao. Incident link: <INCIDENT_URL>

Jira template fields
- Priority: P0 for Tier1, P1 for Tier2, P2 for Tier3/4
- Components: runtime, routing, capacity, product-feature
- Labels: slo-breach, customer-impact-tier-<n>, automated-enrichment

Post-incident / Error-budget reconciliation
1) Add classification evidence (metrics, tenant list, tickets) to the incident story.
2) Compute error-budget consumption attributable to the incident window.
3) For Tier1/Tier2 incidents: schedule a joint SRE/Product/CS triage within 72 hours. The triage determines whether to take a governance action (e.g., customer credits, temporary throttle, emergency capacity commit).
4) Document remediation plan and assign owners with due dates.

Postmortem checklist (to include in incident review)
- Summary of classifier result and whether the initial tier matched customer reports.
- Timeline of detection -> mitigation -> recovery.
- Root cause and contributing factors including capacity and SLO misconfigurations.
- Action items (owner + due date) and follow-up on error-budget policy.
- Evidence attachments: metrics, logs, enrichment query results.

Automation and operational tooling notes
- The classifier runs in the telemetry enrichment pipeline (owner: telemetry-team). Inputs are Prometheus alerts, request traces, and support ticket API.
- All automated classification decisions must be auditable: store the computed scores and inputs in incident metadata.
- Use feature flags to disable automatic Tier upgrades in sensitive customer accounts.

Ownership and governance
- SLO owner: Each SLO must list a primary owner (SRE or Product) and a secondary owner. Owners are responsible for maintaining mapping in this catalog.
- Classification engine owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com.
- Incident review owner for Tier1/Tier2: SRE lead on rotation.

Runbook links (examples)
- API error mitigation: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation
- Fallback routing: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/fallback-routing-and-rollbacks
- Capacity burst playbook: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/capacity-burst-and-burst-rollback

Change log
- 2026-01-15 — Initial draft published (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-20 — Added capacity knobs and CLI examples (review: Liam O'Connor).
- 2026-03-01 — Finalized communication templates and post-incident checklist (review: Renee Zhao).

Appendix: sample incident evidence payload (JSON snippet)
{
  \"incident_id\": \"INC-2026-0115-001\",
  \"slo\": \"api_error_rate_5m\",
  \"computed_scores\": { \"failure_scope\": 78, \"severity\": 85, \"customer_exposure\": 60 },
  \"mapped_tier\": 1,
  \"top_affected_tenants\": [\"tenant-acme\", \"tenant-vela\"],
  \"evidence_queries\": [\"tenant_impact_query_2026-01-15-10m\"]
}

If you are an on-call SRE and this workflow appears to misclassify an incident, immediately create a ticket in the SRE backlog and tag the telemetry-owner for a review.

Contact
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.com)
- Telemetry owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com
- Incident management: incidents@redwood.com
text:
Overview

This page defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 200ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defined rolling window.
- Customer-impact tier: a 1–4 classification used to route and prioritize incident response.

Customer-impact tiers (quick guide)
| Tier | User-visible symptoms | Business effect | Escalation urgency | Owner to notify |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Widespread request failures or complete service outage across regions | Severe revenue/customer retention impact | Immediate (page) | SRE lead + Product PM + CS Director |
| 2 | Large subset of customers impacted (major tenants) or major feature down | Significant business disruption for key customers | High (page + slack) | SRE oncall + Product owner + CS manager |
| 3 | Localized errors, degraded latency for non-critical endpoints | Minor customer annoyance, limited support tickets | Medium (slack alert) | On-call SRE + Feature owner |
| 4 | Minor latency increase or transient errors with no customer tickets | No immediate business impact | Low (ticket) | On-call SRE |

SLO catalog excerpt (example mapping)
| SLO name | Metric | Target | Error-budget window | Default customer-impact tier |
|----------|--------|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| request_latency_p95_public | request_latency_p95{route=~"/v1/.*"} | <= 200ms | 30d | 2 |
| api_error_rate_5m | sum(rate(api_errors[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests[5m])) | <= 0.5% | 7d | 1 |
| embedding_throughput_tail | embedding_infer_latency_p99 | < 1.2s | 14d | 3 |
| console_ui_availability | synthetic_check(console_login) | 99.5% monthly | 30d | 3 |

Classification algorithm (operational)
1) Alert receives telemetry: detector alerts on SLI breach or error-budget burn (alerting config).
2) Metrics enrichment: pull impact signals (affected tenant count, region distribution, top N tenants by RPS, open support tickets matching pattern).
3) Heuristics engine evaluates the following scores (0–100): failure-scope, severity, customer-exposure.
4) Combined score maps to Tier: Score >= 80 -> Tier 1; 60–79 -> Tier 2; 30–59 -> Tier 3; < 30 -> Tier 4.
5) Attach classification to incident and follow the tiered remediation workflow below.

Heuristics inputs (examples)
- affected_tenant_ratio: percent of total tenant RPS impacted.
- p95_latency_delta: percent change vs baseline.
- p99_error_spike: absolute increase in error count at p99.
- active_cs_tickets: number of open tickets tagged /inference or /api.

Operational remediations by tier
Tier 1 (Immediate response)
- Page the SRE oncall and SRE lead.
- Open an incident in the incident-management system using template: tag=CUSTOMER_IMPACT_TIER_1.
- Run mitigation playbook: fallback-to-compatible-model-variant (see runbook link).
- If capacity-related: trigger immediate capacity burst policy (see CLI below).
- Notify CS and Product leadership within 15 minutes with templated Slack/Jira.

Tier 2 (High-priority)
- Notify on-call via high-severity Slack alert.
- Execute targeted throttles or tenant fair-share configuration.
- Begin canary rollback of recent model or config changes (if correlated).

Tier 3 (Operational handling)
- Create ticket and schedule on-call investigation within 30–60 minutes.
- Apply mitigations from the runbook: restart worker pool, clear KV cache, or disabled newly-deployed feature flag.

Tier 4 (Monitoring)
- Monitor metrics and let automated remediation run. If unresolved after SLA window (6 hours), escalate to Tier 3.

Sample alert rule (Prometheus-style YAML)
alert: HighAPIErrorRate
expr: |
  sum(rate(api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.005
for: 2m
labels:
  severity: page
  impact: "possible-customer-impact"
annotations:
  summary: "API error rate >0.5% over 5m"
  runbook: "https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation"

Enrichment query examples (pseudo-SQL / analytics)
- Tenants affected in last 10m: SELECT tenant_id, count(*) FROM requests WHERE status >= 500 AND timestamp > now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 50;
- Support ticket correlation: count tickets where created_at >= now()-10m AND tags IN ('api','inference')

Capacity escalation knobs (examples)
| Trigger | Condition | Automated action | Escalation threshold |
|---------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| headroom_exhausted | available_gpu_slots < 5% | trigger-burst-alloc --pool=shared-burst | immediate (Tier1) |
| model_warm_pool_empty | warm_pool_size < configured_min | scale-up warm pool by 2 nodes | Tier2 |
| p95_latency_spike | p95 > baseline*1.5 for 10m | enable-prefetch-kv-cache=true | Tier2 |

CLI examples
- Trigger burst capacity (operators only):
  rcmd infra capacity burst apply --pool shared-burst --reason "Tier1 SLO breach" --expires 30m
- Re-route traffic to fallback models:
  rcmd infra routing promote --from model-xyz-v2 --to model-xyz-v1 --weight 100

Communication templates
Slack template (Tier1)
- Channel: #incidents
- Message: :rotating_light: *Tier 1 SLO breach* — request_error_rate_5m at 1.2% impacting ~32% of tenants. IC: @oncall. Mitigation in progress: toggling fallback routing. Please notify CS lead @renee.zhao. Incident link: <INCIDENT_URL>

Jira template fields
- Priority: P0 for Tier1, P1 for Tier2, P2 for Tier3/4
- Components: runtime, routing, capacity, product-feature
- Labels: slo-breach, customer-impact-tier-<n>, automated-enrichment

Post-incident / Error-budget reconciliation
1) Add classification evidence (metrics, tenant list, tickets) to the incident story.
2) Compute error-budget consumption attributable to the incident window.
3) For Tier1/Tier2 incidents: schedule a joint SRE/Product/CS triage within 72 hours. The triage determines whether to take a governance action (e.g., customer credits, temporary throttle, emergency capacity commit).
4) Document remediation plan and assign owners with due dates.

Postmortem checklist (to include in incident review)
- Summary of classifier result and whether the initial tier matched customer reports.
- Timeline of detection -> mitigation -> recovery.
- Root cause and contributing factors including capacity and SLO misconfigurations.
- Action items (owner + due date) and follow-up on error-budget policy.
- Evidence attachments: metrics, logs, enrichment query results.

Automation and operational tooling notes
- The classifier runs in the telemetry enrichment pipeline (owner: telemetry-team). Inputs are Prometheus alerts, request traces, and support ticket API.
- All automated classification decisions must be auditable: store the computed scores and inputs in incident metadata.
- Use feature flags to disable automatic Tier upgrades in sensitive customer accounts.

Ownership and governance
- SLO owner: Each SLO must list a primary owner (SRE or Product) and a secondary owner. Owners are responsible for maintaining mapping in this catalog.
- Classification engine owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com.
- Incident review owner for Tier1/Tier2: SRE lead on rotation.

Runbook links (examples)
- API error mitigation: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation
- Fallback routing: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/fallback-routing-and-rollbacks
- Capacity burst playbook: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/capacity-burst-and-burst-rollback

Change log
- 2026-01-15 — Initial draft published (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-20 — Added capacity knobs and CLI examples (review: Liam O'Connor).
- 2026-03-01 — Finalized communication templates and post-incident checklist (review: Renee Zhao).

Appendix: sample incident evidence payload (JSON snippet)
{
  \"incident_id\": \"INC-2026-0115-001\",
  \"slo\": \"api_error_rate_5m\",
  \"computed_scores\": { \"failure_scope\": 78, \"severity\": 85, \"customer_exposure\": 60 },
  \"mapped_tier\": 1,
  \"top_affected_tenants\": [\"tenant-acme\", \"tenant-vela\"],
  \"evidence_queries\": [\"tenant_impact_query_2026-01-15-10m\"]
}

If you are an on-call SRE and this workflow appears to misclassify an incident, immediately create a ticket in the SRE backlog and tag the telemetry-owner for a review.

Contact
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.com)
- Telemetry owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com
- Incident management: incidents@redwood.com

title: Service poison-pill safety and isolation guide
preview:
Summary
-------
This guide defines detection, automated mitigation, and operator isolation procedures for "poison-pill" requests and workloads in the Redwood inference platform. A poison-pill is any request or workload pattern that rapidly escalates resource usage or error propagation and risks cross-tenant/service degradation (examples: pathological prompts that exhaust KV caches, malformed inputs that trigger infinite loops in model runtime, or tenant-driven token-surges).

Scope & applicability
---------------------
Applies to: inference-gateway, batching-worker, kv-prefix-cache, model-eval-sandbox, and the routing layer (router and model-variant-fallbacker). Does NOT replace tenant-level security policies; complements quota and billing controls.

Goals
-----
- Detect poison-pill behavior quickly with deterministic signals.
- Isolate affected tenants/requests before systemic impact.
- Provide safe automated mitigation with operator-safe kill paths.
- Offer clear post-incident steps and test harnesses to prevent regressions.

Definitions
-----------
- Poison-pill: a request or set of requests that cause runaway CPU/memory, unbounded token generation, excessive I/O, or errorstorm cascades.
- Sweeper: an automated operator process that identifies and quarantines suspect traffic.
- Quarantine: short-term routing and resource restrictions that allow investigation without impacting other tenants.

Detecting poison-pills (signals)
--------------------------------
Primary signals (alert if condition sustained for 30s unless otherwise noted):
1) Error amplification: service-level 5xx rate > 2% AND contributing request path error growth > 5x baseline over 1 minute.
2) Resource surge: container memory growth > 30% in 60s or resident set > 85% of container limit.
3) Token explosion: single request token generation > 50k tokens OR token-emit-rate > 10k tokens/s.
4) KV miss storm: kv-prefix-cache read latency > 500ms and miss ratio > 60% for a tenant prefix.
5) Tail latency spike: p99 latency increases > 10x baseline and correlated across > 3 nodes in a pool.
6) Circuit-breaker saturation: > 75% of router circuits tripped for a tenant.

Automated safeguards (default actions)
-------------------------------------
- Fast-fail fences: inference-gateway applies per-request synthetic limits (timeout=120s, token_limit=20000) unless tenant override is configured.
- Token-rate limiter: burstable token bucket per-tenant (ref config table below). Exceeded -> 429 + route to canary fallback.
- Quarantine-router: when threshold breached, request routing shifts tenant to isolated queue slice with reduced concurrency (default concurrency=2) and cached fallback model.
- Safe preemption: batching-worker will evict the largest in-flight requests from offending tenant first (LIFO within tenant).
- Memory guard: kernel oom_score_adj review hook and cgroup soft kill paths that prefer restarting the worker process over whole-node reboot.

Operator isolation and remediation runbook (step-by-step)
---------------------------------------------------------
Trigger: 'Quarantine-router' alert OR manual detection via observability console.
1) Triage (0-2 minutes)
   a) Open incident channel: #platform-ops / incident-{YYYYMMDD}-{short}.
   b) Run quick query: 
      - kubectl top pods -n inference | grep <pool-name>
      - curl -sS "https://observability/api/query?expr=token_emit_rate{tenant=\"TENANT_ID\"}" | jq .
   c) Identify tenant_id, request_id(s), and service(s) involved.
2) Immediate isolation (2-5 minutes)
   a) Apply enforced tenant throttling: 
      - opa policy patch: opa eval --data policies.json 'patch-policy("tenant/limit/ID")'
   b) Move traffic to quarantine slice: 
      - rtrctl isolate-tenant --tenant TENANT_ID --slice quarantine-2026-03 --concurrency 2
   c) If KV miss-storm detected: mark prefix read-only and enable warmup cache for other tenants.
3) Stabilize (5-20 minutes)
   a) If memory leak suspected: capture heap profile and stacktrace: 
      - kubectl exec -it pod/<pod> -- /bin/sh -c "curl localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap > /tmp/heap.pprof"
   b) If inflated token counts: temporarily enable model-variant-fallbacker to a conservative model: 
      - rtrctl swap-fallback --service inference-gateway --from model-xlarge --to model-xsmall --reason "poison-pill mitigation"
   c) Restart only the offending worker process where possible: 
      - kubectl rollout restart deployment/batching-worker -n inference --field-selector metadata.name=<pod> --grace-period=30
4) Remediation and business follow-up (20-90 minutes)
   a) Collect request payloads (sanitizing PII): s3://redwood-incident-bucket/{inc}/payloads/
   b) Run deterministic replay in the model-eval-sandbox with instrumentation (cpu/mem/token traces).
   c) If caused by customer prompt abuse, open support ticket and apply billing/contract actions per customer policy.
5) Restore and monitor (90-240 minutes)
   a) Gradually lift quarantine by shifting 10% traffic increments every 10 minutes while observing SLOs.
   b) Post-restore synthetic test: run 100 synthetic inferences with worst-case prompt families.

Safe-kill checklist (quick reference)
------------------------------------
- Has traffic been routed away from shared pools? (yes/no)
- Are core dumps and pprof collected? (yes/no)
- Is restart targeted to container/process rather than node? (yes/no)
- Have relevant owners been paged? (yes/no)
- Is rollback plan prepared (stable image / config)? (yes/no)

Config table: default thresholds and owner contacts
-------------------------------------------------
| component            | primary_threshold                   | auto-action                          | owner_team | oncall |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------|--------|
| inference-gateway    | p99 > 2s OR token_rate > 10k/s     | quarantine-traffic, swap-fallback    | serving    | pg-serv |
| batching-worker      | memory growth > 30% / 60s          | evict-inflight restart worker        | infra      | pg-infra |
| kv-prefix-cache      | miss-rate > 60% & latency > 500ms  | prefix-readonly, prefetch warm cache | platform   | pg-cache |
| model-eval-sandbox   | token-gen > 50k/request            | throttle, sandboxed-replay           | research   | pg-ml |
| router               | circuits tripped > 75%             | route to quarantine slice            | platform   | pg-router |

(Owners map: serving=eng-serving-runtime; infra=eng-infra; platform=eng-platform; research=eng-applied-ml)

CLI examples and snippets
-------------------------
- Isolate tenant: rtrctl isolate-tenant --tenant T-42 --slice quarantine-2026-03 --concurrency 2
- Capture trace for request: curl "https://inference-gateway/internal/trace?req_id=REQ123" -H "Authorization: Bearer $ANALYST_TOKEN" > /tmp/REQ123.trace
- Query token emit rate (Grafana API): 
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $GRAF_TOKEN" "https://grafana/api/datasources/proxy/1/api/v1/query?query=token_emit_rate%7Btenant%3D%27T-42%27%7D" | jq .

Rollout and feature gating for a new "sweeper"
------------------------------------------------
Purpose: introduce a controlled automatic quarantining component that can be toggled by region/cluster.
Phased rollout:
1) Feature flag off in prod, enabled in staging with synthetic poison-pills.
2) Canary: enable for 1% of traffic in us-west with verbose logging.
3) Metrics gating: require stable latency, no cross-tenant error correlation, and <0.1% false positive rate for 48h.
4) Gradual ramp: 1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 7 days with daily review.
Fallback: immediate feature-flag off + revert config if SLO regression observed.

Testing and exercise plan
-------------------------
- Chaos experiments: weekly gameday simulating token-surges and kv-prefix storms against a non-prod replica.
- Unit/integration: simulate OOM via fault-injector and assert quarantine transitions.
- Synthetic library: maintain a corpus of pathological prompts (stored in repo infra/poison-corpus) used in CI for release gating.

Post-incident RCA template (to be attached to incident)
------------------------------------------------------
- Incident ID:
- Start / End:
- Services impacted:
- Root cause summary (2-3 sentences):
- Detection timeline (timestamps):
- Mitigation actions taken and times:
- Why the mitigation worked / did not work:
- Persistent changes planned (config, code, policy):
- Owner for follow-up tasks and ETA:

Roles & ownership
------------------
- First responder: platform oncall (pg-platform). Responsible for initial isolation actions.
- Runtime engineer: eng-serving-runtime. Responsible for heap traces and kernel-level restarts.
- Cache owner: eng-platform (kv-team). Responsible for prefix freeze and warmups.
- Customer success: engage for customer-facing communication if tenant is externally managed.

References & links
------------------
- Observability SLI guide: ../dashboards-and-alerts/token-burst-forensics-and-debugging-workflow-2026.json
- Emergency model swap runbook: ../runbooks/emergency-model-swap-and-token-surge-mitigation-2026.json
- KV cache rebalancer: ../systems-and-services/operator-workflows-for-llm-kv-cache-sharding-and-rebalancing-2026.json

Appendix: Example OPA policy (sanitized)
----------------------------------------
{
  \"id\": \"tenant-rate-limit\",
  \"effect\": \"deny\",
  \"conditions\": { \"tenant\": \"T-42\", \"token_rate\": { \"gt\": 10000 } },
  \"action\": \"isolate\"
}

Document maintenance
--------------------
- Owner: eng-platform (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: quarterly or after any major poison-pill incident.
- Additions to the synthetic corpus must be reviewed by eng-applied-ml and infra.

This guide should be used in conjunction with per-service runbooks and security policy. If you find gaps or have observed a new poison pattern, file a PR against this page and tag the reviewers listed above.
