| component            | primary_threshold                   | auto-action                          | owner_team | oncall |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------|--------|
| inference-gateway    | p99 > 2s OR token_rate > 10k/s     | quarantine-traffic, swap-fallback    | serving    | pg-serv |
| batching-worker      | memory growth > 30% / 60s          | evict-inflight restart worker        | infra      | pg-infra |
| kv-prefix-cache      | miss-rate > 60% & latency > 500ms  | prefix-readonly, prefetch warm cache | platform   | pg-cache |
| model-eval-sandbox   | token-gen > 50k/request            | throttle, sandboxed-replay           | research   | pg-ml |
| router               | circuits tripped > 75%             | route to quarantine slice            | platform   | pg-router |

(Owners map: serving=eng-serving-runtime; infra=eng-infra; platform=eng-platform; research=eng-applied-ml)

CLI examples and snippets
-------------------------
- Isolate tenant: rtrctl isolate-tenant --tenant T-42 --slice quarantine-2026-03 --concurrency 2
- Capture trace for request: curl "https://inference-gateway/internal/trace?req_id=REQ123" -H "Authorization: Bearer $ANALYST_TOKEN" > /tmp/REQ123.trace
- Query token emit rate (Grafana API): 
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $GRAF_TOKEN" "https://grafana/api/datasources/proxy/1/api/v1/query?query=token_emit_rate%7Btenant%3D%27T-42%27%7D" | jq .

Rollout and feature gating for a new "sweeper"
------------------------------------------------
Purpose: introduce a controlled automatic quarantining component that can be toggled by region/cluster.
Phased rollout:
1) Feature flag off in prod, enabled in staging with synthetic poison-pills.
2) Canary: enable for 1% of traffic in us-west with verbose logging.
3) Metrics gating: require stable latency, no cross-tenant error correlation, and <0.1% false positive rate for 48h.
4) Gradual ramp: 1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 7 days with daily review.
Fallback: immediate feature-flag off + revert config if SLO regression observed.

Testing and exercise plan
-------------------------
- Chaos experiments: weekly gameday simulating token-surges and kv-prefix storms against a non-prod replica.
- Unit/integration: simulate OOM via fault-injector and assert quarantine transitions.
- Synthetic library: maintain a corpus of pathological prompts (stored in repo infra/poison-corpus) used in CI for release gating.

Post-incident RCA template (to be attached to incident)
------------------------------------------------------
- Incident ID:
- Start / End:
- Services impacted:
- Root cause summary (2-3 sentences):
- Detection timeline (timestamps):
- Mitigation actions taken and times:
- Why the mitigation worked / did not work:
- Persistent changes planned (config, code, policy):
- Owner for follow-up tasks and ETA:

Roles & ownership
------------------
- First responder: platform oncall (pg-platform). Responsible for initial isolation actions.
- Runtime engineer: eng-serving-runtime. Responsible for heap traces and kernel-level restarts.
- Cache owner: eng-platform (kv-team). Responsible for prefix freeze and warmups.
- Customer success: engage for customer-facing communication if tenant is externally managed.

References & links
------------------
- Observability SLI guide: ../dashboards-and-alerts/token-burst-forensics-and-debugging-workflow-2026.json
- Emergency model swap runbook: ../runbooks/emergency-model-swap-and-token-surge-mitigation-2026.json
- KV cache rebalancer: ../systems-and-services/operator-workflows-for-llm-kv-cache-sharding-and-rebalancing-2026.json

Appendix: Example OPA policy (sanitized)
----------------------------------------
{
  \"id\": \"tenant-rate-limit\",
  \"effect\": \"deny\",
  \"conditions\": { \"tenant\": \"T-42\", \"token_rate\": { \"gt\": 10000 } },
  \"action\": \"isolate\"
}

Document maintenance
--------------------
- Owner: eng-platform (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: quarterly or after any major poison-pill incident.
- Additions to the synthetic corpus must be reviewed by eng-applied-ml and infra.

This guide should be used in conjunction with per-service runbooks and security policy. If you find gaps or have observed a new poison pattern, file a PR against this page and tag the reviewers listed above.

title: Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI) and Annotator Adaptation Manual
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI), describes a standardized annotator adaptation protocol, and provides operational runbooks for integrating QSPI into our promotion/evaluation pipeline for quantized artifacts. The goal is to detect, quantify, and remediate semantic or task-specific signal loss introduced by quantization changes while minimizing annotation overhead and preserving production fidelity.

Why this matters:
- Quantization reduces cost but can change subtle tokenization/representation behavior that impacts downstream labelers, rerankers, and function-call correctness.
- Existing throughput-driven checks (latency/peak tokens) miss graded signal degradation affecting annotator agreement and quality SLAs.
- QSPI gives a single, explainable index and a fast path to adapt annotators and probe sets where signal loss is concentrated.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to model artifacts and runtime variants intended for production promotion (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Intended for mixed-precision and integer quant (e.g., FP16->INT8, 4-bit transforms) and algorithmic kernel changes.
- Not a replacement for full fidelity human-in-the-loop evals — a prioritization and gating metric that maps to annotator effort budgets.

Contents of this manual:
1) Definition and computation of QSPI
2) Probe selection and weighting rules for QSPI
3) Annotator adaptation protocol (AA-1) and fast re-training checklist
4) Thresholds, SLOs, and gating rules for promotion
5) Triage and rollback playbook with CLI examples
6) Observability and dashboarding recommendations
7) Ownership and decision matrix

1) Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI)
Definition: QSPI is a weighted score in [0,100] where 100 == no detectable behavioral difference between baseline (float or golden artifact) and candidate (quantized) across a prioritized probe set. Lower scores indicate larger expected annotation overhead or user-impact risk.
Computation (high-level):
- For each probe i, compute a per-probe similarity s_i in [0,1]. Similarity uses task-aware metrics: exact-match for structured outputs, BLEU/ROUGE/EM for textual templates, embedding cosine + classifier-concordance for rerankers, and functioncall-schema-match for tool-calling.
- Apply a probe weight w_i reflecting traffic, business-criticality, and sensitivity. Weights sum to 1.
- QSPI = 100 * (sum_i w_i * s_i)^alpha where alpha is a conservatism factor (default 1.0).
Example: 50 probes with concentrated weight on top-10 business-critical slices.

2) Probe selection and weighting rules
- Probe types: production-mirror probes (live-anchored), synthetic edgecases (seeded), and canonical unit probes (deterministic). Use the following mix by default: 60% production-mirror, 30% synthetic edgecases, 10% canonical unit probes.
- Weighting heuristic:
  - w_i = base_weight(slice_traffic_rank) * impact_multiplier
  - impact_multiplier = 2.0 for financial/legal slices, 1.5 for functioncall-heavy, 0.8 for low-risk conversational slices.
- Minimum probe coverage: ensure each critical slice has at least 12 probes across seeds/primings to stabilize s_i.

3) Annotator Adaptation Protocol (AA-1)
Objective: minimize re-annotation cost by adapting existing annotator guides and lightweight retraining instead of wholesale relabels when QSPI indicates localized degradation.
Steps (numbered runbook):
1. Trigger: QSPI < 95 for a candidate artifact. Create a QSPI incident in the evaluation queue.
2. Automated triage: run per-slice delta analysis to produce a prioritized table: slice, delta-score, top failure-mode (e.g., omission, hallucination, schema drift).
3. Rapid annotation rehearsal (2-day window): sample up to 50 failing probes per top-5 slices and run a calibration session with annotators. Use the following checklist during rehearsal:
   - Present side-by-side baseline vs candidate responses.
   - Annotators flag root-cause (formatting vs content vs predicate mismatch).
   - Track time-per-annotation and inter-annotator-agreement (IAA).
4. Adaptation decision matrix:
   - If IAA >= 0.8 and time-per-annotation <= baseline * 1.2, proceed to targeted guide updates and re-run QSPI.
   - If IAA < 0.6 or time > baseline * 1.5, escalate to model-team for quantization fallback or refined kernel selection.
5. If targeted guide updates lead to QSPI >= 97, proceed with promotion. Otherwise, follow rollback path.

Annotator training artifacts to generate:
- Short "delta guide" (1 page) describing specific failure patterns and canonical corrections.
- 10-shot micro-training bundle (JSONL) for retraining lightweight classifier head or annotation heuristics.
- Annotator time-cost estimate and re-run probes list.

4) Thresholds, SLOs, and gating rules
- Gate levels (default):
  - Safe: QSPI >= 98 — automatic promotion allowed after automated acceptance suite passes.
  - Caution: QSPI 95-97 — require AA-1 and QSPI re-evaluation.
  - Block: QSPI < 95 — block promotion, require model-team investigation or fallback to higher-precision artifact.
- Production alerting: if promoted artifact later records a slice-level drift (slice delta > 0.12 in live telemetry) within first 72 hours, open P0 evaluation and auto-rollforward to pre-quantized variant.

5) Triage and rollback playbook (step-by-step with CLI examples)
Runbook summary: identify affected slices, run fast-scan, apply AA-1 rehearsal, and either remediate or rollback.
Example commands (eval harness CLI):
- Run QSPI on candidate:
  rwd-eval qspi --baseline=artifact:float-v2 --candidate=artifact:quant-4bit-v1 --probe-set=qspi-top50 --out=qspi-report.json
- Generate per-slice deltas:
  rwd-eval slice-diff --input=qspi-report.json --format=table > slice-deltas.csv
- Launch annotator rehearsal (scheduler API):
  rwd-ops schedule-rehearsal --slices slice-deltas.csv --annotator-pool=annotators-core --ttl=48h
Rollback / rollforward:
- If rollback required:
  rwd-deploy promote --artifact=artifact:float-v2 --route=canary --reason=\"QSPI-block: <95\"
- If rollforward after fix:
  rwd-deploy promote --artifact=artifact:quant-4bit-v1 --route=canary --notes=\"QSPI remediated via AA-1; QSPI=97.8\"

6) Observability and dashboarding recommendations
Minimum telemetry to publish on model-promotions dashboard:
- QSPI overall score (trend, last 30 days)
- Per-slice s_i and weight w_i trendlines
- Annotator metrics: IAA (krippendorff alpha), median time-per-annotation, training bundle reuse count
- Production feedback loop: slice-level user complaint rate and automatic rollback triggers
Suggested dashboard panels:
| Panel | Purpose | Owner | SLO |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| QSPI trend | Track artifact drift across promotions | Applied ML | >98 day-over-day | 
| Slice heatmap | Highlight concentrated losses | Model SRE | per-slice alert if delta > 0.12 |
| Annotator health | Track IAA and throughput | Annotation Ops | IAA >= 0.75 |

7) Ownership and decision matrix
- Primary owners:
  - Metric steward: Applied ML (Priya Natarajan) — maintains QSPI spec and default alpha/weights.
  - Operational steward: Annotation Ops (María Alvarez) — runs AA-1 and trains annotators.
  - Promotion gate owner: Model Release (Ethan Cole) — final promotion authority.
- Escalation path: If model-team disagrees with AA-1 outcome, open an evaluation scoping ticket and convene a cross-functional review within 48 hours.

Appendices
A) YAML manifest example for probe set specification:
---
probe_set_name: qspi-top50
probes: 50
mix: {production_mirror: 0.6, synthetic_edge: 0.3, canonical: 0.1}
weighting: {financial: 2.0, functioncall: 1.5, conversational: 0.8}
alpha: 1.0
---

B) Quick reference: remediation decision table
| Condition | Action |
| QSPI >= 98 | Auto-promote after automated checks |
| 95 <= QSPI < 98 and AA-1 passes | Targeted promotion with annotation guardrail |
| QSPI < 95 | Block promotion and open quant-investigation |

C) Example JSON snippet for micro-training bundle (1-line):
{"prompt": "User: Extract amount and currency. Text: \"Transfer $20 to savings.\"", "label": {"amount": 20, "currency": "USD"}}

D) Glossary:
- QSPI: Quantization Signal Preservation Index
- AA-1: Annotator Adaptation Protocol version 1
- IAA: Inter-Annotator Agreement

Related work and references:
- See the quantization-profiles folder for kernel-specific notes and existing probe manifests.
- See eval-harness/quantized-artifact-eval-gate-and-rollout-2026 for integration with CI gating.

Questions / feedback:
- Ping @priya.natarajan on Slack (#applied-ml) for metric changes or sample-set requests.

Document history:
- 2026-01-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan
- 2026-02-10: Added AA-1 protocol after annotator rehearsal pilot (María Alvarez)
- 2026-03-01: Published; thresholds adjusted after shadow-run results (Ethan Cole)

title: Service boundary evaluation and quantization migration checklist
preview:
Overview

This checklist defines the cross-functional operational playbook for evaluating service boundaries and migrating model serving from floating-point artifacts to quantized artifacts (8-bit, 4-bit, mixed) with minimal user-facing regression risk. The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements while preserving quality and observability contracts. This document ties together: eval-harness checks, prompt and benchmark selection, model onboarding gates, quality guardrails, quantization profile validation, and regression triage steps.

Scope

- Applies to any model promotion that changes runtime numeric representation (FP16/FP32 -> INT8/PACT/4-bit) for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).
- Includes single-model migrations and model-variant rollouts where a quantized variant will be routed by policy.
- Excludes research-only artifacts and offline-only embeddings refreshes.

Primary owners and contacts

- Service owner: Priya Natarajan (ML Infra / Applied ML) - priya@redwood.example.com
- SRE/Performance owner: Jamal Carter - jamal@redwood.example.com
- Evaluation lead: Ethan Miller - ethan@redwood.example.com

Priorities and acceptance criteria (summary)

- Safety/Quality: Zero critical semantic regressions on production prompt families; no increase in severity-1 (S1) safety sentinels.
- Latency: P95 token generation latency should not exceed baseline by more than 10% under nominal load.
- Cost: Expected per-token cost reduction (est. 20–60%) must be validated in a 24h shadow run.
- Observability: Traces, token-level cost breakdowns, and eval-sentinels must be active and firing against baseline slices.

Pre-migration checklist (must be satisfied before building quant artifacts)

1) Workload and boundary analysis
   - Identify the service boundary (API route/model key) and all downstream consumers.
   - Produce a baseline workload fingerprint: token length distribution, function-calls ratio, streaming vs batch, cold start rate.
   - Tag the route with a migration candidate label in the config catalog.

2) Prompt & benchmark selection
   - Select a representative probe set (recommended 5k–20k probes) split across: high-traffic slices, safety-sensitive prompts, long-context prompts, multi-turn threads, and rare edgecases.
   - For each probe include: canonical prompt, expected anchor tokens, pass/fail sentinel, and a golden response reference where applicable.

3) Eval harness configuration
   - Configure a dedicated eval job with: deterministic seeds, temperature and max_tokens matching production, and tokenization parity checks.
   - Enable both delta-metrics and qualitative human review queues for slices with >X% regression (X default 5%).

Quant profiling & artifact build checklist

- Quant profile selection: list candidate quant profiles (per-layer or per-kernel) and annotate tradeoffs. Example table: 

  | Candidate | Precision | Notes | Expected cost delta | Owner |
  |-----------|-----------|-------|---------------------|-------|
  | qp-int8   | 8-bit     | Default per-tensor symmetric | -40% | infra-quant |
  | qp-4bit   | 4-bit     | Aggressive, requires KV-cache tuning | -60% | infra-quant |
  | mixed-8-4 | hybrid    | FP16 for attention, 4-bit for FFN | -50% | infra-quant |

- Locally validate tensor parity (per-layer L2 and activation distribution checks) and record artifacts in the quant-catalog location.
- Attach quant metadata to model manifest: quant_profile_id, sample-seed, compressor-version, and build-recipe id.

Shadow/validation stage (golden path)

1) Shadow run (no traffic impact):
   - Run the quantized model in shadow for 24–72 hours against live traffic sampling (recommended 2–10% traffic sample).
   - Capture the following telemetry for both baseline and quantized variant: token-level latency (p50/p95/p99), response-stability-index (RSI), embedding cosine drift on embedding routes, safety sentinel hit-rate, and cost per token.

2) Quality gating using eval harness:
   - Delta metrics to compute: exact-match rate, semantic-similarity delta (BERTScore or proprietary similarity), hallucination proxy (unexpected-entity-rate), and RSI.
   - Gate criteria (default):
     * semantic-similarity delta <= 2.5 absolute points on production slices
     * RSI drop <= 0.03
     * Safety sentinel rate not increased by more than 0.1x absolute
     * Latency p95 regression <= 10%

3) Human-in-the-loop review
   - For slices failing any gate, auto-queue for annotator triage with a sample size of 200 examples or until 95% confidence is reached.

Canary rollout and progressive promotion

1) Micro-canary (1–2% traffic) for 2–4 hours
   - Route a small percentage of traffic via routing policy to quantized variant. Monitor SLOs and quality-sentinels.

2) Macro-canary (5–15% traffic) for 24 hours
   - Increase traffic and run full pipeline (eval harness repeat on live samples + human review of failure samples).

3) Gradual promotion (increments of 10–20% every 12–24h)
   - Stop and roll back if: any S1 sentinels are triggered, semantic-similarity delta exceeds threshold, or latency p95 increases beyond escalation threshold.

Rollback criteria and emergency process

- Immediately rollback if any of the following occur: S1 safety sentinel, customer-facing error-rate > 0.5% absolute increase, or unresolvable semantic regressions on a critical slice.
- Triage steps in rollback event:
  1) Capture failing request IDs and attach full traces + model-side logs.
  2) Run targeted eval-harness replay of failing probes across baseline and candidate quant-artifact.
  3) If root cause is quantization, escalate to infra-quant and model-onboarding owners; prepare hot-fix (less aggressive quant profile or mixed-precision fallback).

Regression triage quickplay (for triage teams)

- Initial assessment (first 30 minutes):
  * Confirm whether regression is reproducible on the eval-harness using the failing probe set.
  * Check KV-cache alignment and tokenization parity for the failing requests.

- Bisect matrix:
  * Axis 1: quant_profile (int8, 4bit, mixed)
  * Axis 2: sequence length bucket (short/medium/long)
  * Axis 3: temperature/decoding settings
  Run a batched grid to isolate when the failure appears.

- Rapid mitigation patterns:
  1) Route failing slices to baseline float model via routing policy.
  2) Enable per-route quant-fallback setting (prefer mixed-precision) for affected model key.
  3) Open a regression incident with title prefixed: [REGRESSION][quant] <service-route> and assign to infra-quant.

Operational dashboards and alerts (minimum required)

- Dashboards:
  * Migration overview: traffic split, P50/P95 latency baseline vs quant, cost per token, safety sentinel delta, RSI trend.
  * Slice heatmap: top 20 slices by volume and quality delta.

- Alerts (recommended thresholds):
  * Quality delta alert: semantic-similarity delta > 2.5 points (severity P2)
  * Safety sentinel increase: absolute increase > 0.1% (severity P1)
  * Latency regression: p95 > baseline * 1.10 (severity P2)
  * Unexpected error-rate: > 0.5% absolute (severity P1)

Post-promotion verification and documentation

- Run a 7-day monitored window where weekly aggregated metrics are reviewed by the service owner and evaluation lead.
- Update model manifest and production catalog with final quant_profile, effective date, and observed cost savings.
- Publish a short post-mortem or migration summary including: decision rationale, measured deltas, any rollback incidents, and recommended next steps for future migrations.

Appendix: example commands and snippets

- Eval harness run (example):
  > ./eval-harness run --manifest manifests/service-xyz.json --probes probes/service-xyz/production-probes.json --seed 42 --compare-to baseline-artifact-id

- Example routing policy (YAML excerpt):

  route: /v1/generate/service-xyz
  variants:
    - id: baseline-fp16
      weight: 80
    - id: quant-qp-int8
      weight: 20
  fallback_policy: route-to-baseline-on-sentinel

- Example quick-check JSON for similarity metric: 
  {
    "probe_id": "12345",
    "baseline_embedding_cos": 0.9234,
    "quant_embedding_cos": 0.8952,
    "similarity_delta": -0.0282
  }

Related policies and references

- See the quantization profile library for approved profiles and binary provenance.
- Follow the eval-harness seeding and determinism rules when building comparison runs.
- Use the regression-triage incident template when opening incidents for production regressions.

Notes and future work

- Next iteration: automate quant-profile A/B scoring via the eval-harness and integrate with rollout policy to support automated gradual promotion when deltas remain within a conservative band.
- Investigate more robust embedding-drift alarms that map directly to downstream retrieval quality impact.
text:
Overview

This checklist defines the cross-functional operational playbook for evaluating service boundaries and migrating model serving from floating-point artifacts to quantized artifacts (8-bit, 4-bit, mixed) with minimal user-facing regression risk. The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements while preserving quality and observability contracts. This document ties together: eval-harness checks, prompt and benchmark selection, model onboarding gates, quality guardrails, quantization profile validation, and regression triage steps.

Scope

- Applies to any model promotion that changes runtime numeric representation (FP16/FP32 -> INT8/PACT/4-bit) for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).
- Includes single-model migrations and model-variant rollouts where a quantized variant will be routed by policy.
- Excludes research-only artifacts and offline-only embeddings refreshes.

Primary owners and contacts

- Service owner: Priya Natarajan (ML Infra / Applied ML) - priya@redwood.example.com
- SRE/Performance owner: Jamal Carter - jamal@redwood.example.com
- Evaluation lead: Ethan Miller - ethan@redwood.example.com

Priorities and acceptance criteria (summary)

- Safety/Quality: Zero critical semantic regressions on production prompt families; no increase in severity-1 (S1) safety sentinels.
- Latency: P95 token generation latency should not exceed baseline by more than 10% under nominal load.
- Cost: Expected per-token cost reduction (est. 20–60%) must be validated in a 24h shadow run.
- Observability: Traces, token-level cost breakdowns, and eval-sentinels must be active and firing against baseline slices.

Pre-migration checklist (must be satisfied before building quant artifacts)

1) Workload and boundary analysis
   - Identify the service boundary (API route/model key) and all downstream consumers.
   - Produce a baseline workload fingerprint: token length distribution, function-calls ratio, streaming vs batch, cold start rate.
   - Tag the route with a migration candidate label in the config catalog.

2) Prompt & benchmark selection
   - Select a representative probe set (recommended 5k–20k probes) split across: high-traffic slices, safety-sensitive prompts, long-context prompts, multi-turn threads, and rare edgecases.
   - For each probe include: canonical prompt, expected anchor tokens, pass/fail sentinel, and a golden response reference where applicable.

3) Eval harness configuration
   - Configure a dedicated eval job with: deterministic seeds, temperature and max_tokens matching production, and tokenization parity checks.
   - Enable both delta-metrics and qualitative human review queues for slices with >X% regression (X default 5%).

Quant profiling & artifact build checklist

- Quant profile selection: list candidate quant profiles (per-layer or per-kernel) and annotate tradeoffs. Example table: 

  | Candidate | Precision | Notes | Expected cost delta | Owner |
  |-----------|-----------|-------|---------------------|-------|
  | qp-int8   | 8-bit     | Default per-tensor symmetric | -40% | infra-quant |
  | qp-4bit   | 4-bit     | Aggressive, requires KV-cache tuning | -60% | infra-quant |
  | mixed-8-4 | hybrid    | FP16 for attention, 4-bit for FFN | -50% | infra-quant |

- Locally validate tensor parity (per-layer L2 and activation distribution checks) and record artifacts in the quant-catalog location.
- Attach quant metadata to model manifest: quant_profile_id, sample-seed, compressor-version, and build-recipe id.

Shadow/validation stage (golden path)

1) Shadow run (no traffic impact):
   - Run the quantized model in shadow for 24–72 hours against live traffic sampling (recommended 2–10% traffic sample).
   - Capture the following telemetry for both baseline and quantized variant: token-level latency (p50/p95/p99), response-stability-index (RSI), embedding cosine drift on embedding routes, safety sentinel hit-rate, and cost per token.

2) Quality gating using eval harness:
   - Delta metrics to compute: exact-match rate, semantic-similarity delta (BERTScore or proprietary similarity), hallucination proxy (unexpected-entity-rate), and RSI.
   - Gate criteria (default):
     * semantic-similarity delta <= 2.5 absolute points on production slices
     * RSI drop <= 0.03
     * Safety sentinel rate not increased by more than 0.1x absolute
     * Latency p95 regression <= 10%

3) Human-in-the-loop review
   - For slices failing any gate, auto-queue for annotator triage with a sample size of 200 examples or until 95% confidence is reached.

Canary rollout and progressive promotion

1) Micro-canary (1–2% traffic) for 2–4 hours
   - Route a small percentage of traffic via routing policy to quantized variant. Monitor SLOs and quality-sentinels.

2) Macro-canary (5–15% traffic) for 24 hours
   - Increase traffic and run full pipeline (eval harness repeat on live samples + human review of failure samples).

3) Gradual promotion (increments of 10–20% every 12–24h)
   - Stop and roll back if: any S1 sentinels are triggered, semantic-similarity delta exceeds threshold, or latency p95 increases beyond escalation threshold.

Rollback criteria and emergency process

- Immediately rollback if any of the following occur: S1 safety sentinel, customer-facing error-rate > 0.5% absolute increase, or unresolvable semantic regressions on a critical slice.
- Triage steps in rollback event:
  1) Capture failing request IDs and attach full traces + model-side logs.
  2) Run targeted eval-harness replay of failing probes across baseline and candidate quant-artifact.
  3) If root cause is quantization, escalate to infra-quant and model-onboarding owners; prepare hot-fix (less aggressive quant profile or mixed-precision fallback).

Regression triage quickplay (for triage teams)

- Initial assessment (first 30 minutes):
  * Confirm whether regression is reproducible on the eval-harness using the failing probe set.
  * Check KV-cache alignment and tokenization parity for the failing requests.

- Bisect matrix:
  * Axis 1: quant_profile (int8, 4bit, mixed)
  * Axis 2: sequence length bucket (short/medium/long)
  * Axis 3: temperature/decoding settings
  Run a batched grid to isolate when the failure appears.

- Rapid mitigation patterns:
  1) Route failing slices to baseline float model via routing policy.
  2) Enable per-route quant-fallback setting (prefer mixed-precision) for affected model key.
  3) Open a regression incident with title prefixed: [REGRESSION][quant] <service-route> and assign to infra-quant.

Operational dashboards and alerts (minimum required)

- Dashboards:
  * Migration overview: traffic split, P50/P95 latency baseline vs quant, cost per token, safety sentinel delta, RSI trend.
  * Slice heatmap: top 20 slices by volume and quality delta.

- Alerts (recommended thresholds):
  * Quality delta alert: semantic-similarity delta > 2.5 points (severity P2)
  * Safety sentinel increase: absolute increase > 0.1% (severity P1)
  * Latency regression: p95 > baseline * 1.10 (severity P2)
  * Unexpected error-rate: > 0.5% absolute (severity P1)

Post-promotion verification and documentation

- Run a 7-day monitored window where weekly aggregated metrics are reviewed by the service owner and evaluation lead.
- Update model manifest and production catalog with final quant_profile, effective date, and observed cost savings.
- Publish a short post-mortem or migration summary including: decision rationale, measured deltas, any rollback incidents, and recommended next steps for future migrations.

Appendix: example commands and snippets

- Eval harness run (example):
  > ./eval-harness run --manifest manifests/service-xyz.json --probes probes/service-xyz/production-probes.json --seed 42 --compare-to baseline-artifact-id

- Example routing policy (YAML excerpt):

  route: /v1/generate/service-xyz
  variants:
    - id: baseline-fp16
      weight: 80
    - id: quant-qp-int8
      weight: 20
  fallback_policy: route-to-baseline-on-sentinel

- Example quick-check JSON for similarity metric: 
  {
    "probe_id": "12345",
    "baseline_embedding_cos": 0.9234,
    "quant_embedding_cos": 0.8952,
    "similarity_delta": -0.0282
  }

Related policies and references

- See the quantization profile library for approved profiles and binary provenance.
- Follow the eval-harness seeding and determinism rules when building comparison runs.
- Use the regression-triage incident template when opening incidents for production regressions.

Notes and future work

- Next iteration: automate quant-profile A/B scoring via the eval-harness and integrate with rollout policy to support automated gradual promotion when deltas remain within a conservative band.
- Investigate more robust embedding-drift alarms that map directly to downstream retrieval quality impact.

title: Retail & Commerce Inference Sales Kit — Positioning, Pitch, Pricing, and Security FAQ
preview:
Overview

This sales kit is designed for sellers and solutions engineers working outbound or responding to RFPs in Retail & Commerce. It combines crisp positioning, a competitive brief, a pitch-deck outline, price/TCO guidance, a one-page case study, a demo recipe, and a short Security FAQ tailored for procurement and technical evaluation.

Audience and target personas

- Head of Product / Director of Search: cares about relevance, conversion lift, latency and cost per query.
- Engineering Manager (ML/Infra): needs predictable inference runtimes, versioned rollouts, and observability.
- Platform/Cloud Architect: evaluates network, VPC, and deployment models (hosted vs dedicated vs private).
- Procurement / Legal: looks for pricing predictability, SLAs, and security/compliance evidence.

Key positioning and messaging (Retail-focused)

Elevator pitch (15s): Redwood Inference gives retail teams enterprise-grade LLM serving that improves search relevance and personalization while keeping latency low and operating costs predictable — across hosted, reserved, or private deployments.

Top line value props (for pitch & collateral):
- Conversion-first inference: optimized runtimes and caching reduce median query latency to 50–120ms at scale, improving add-to-cart and checkout conversion.
- Cost predictability: reserved/Dedicated GPU pools and unit economics tooling reduce per-query cost up to 6x vs naïve cloud-hosted endpoints for high QPS workloads.
- Safe rollouts and regressions guardrails: canary rollouts, eval integration, and quality monitoring prevent relevance regressions in production.
- Hybrid deployment flexibility: self-hosted/private options for PII-sensitive catalogs and hosted API for rapid POC.

Use cases and value metrics (quick bullets for discovery)

1) Query rewriting & relevance tuning
- Outcome: 8–20% improvement in click-through and add-to-cart for long-tail queries.
- Metrics to ask for: daily search QPS, current median latency, % long-tail queries, baseline conversion rate.

2) Conversational shopping / chat assistants
- Outcome: reduced contact center volume, increased AOV via guided upsell flows.
- Ask for: monthly conversations, average session turns, SLA for response latency.

3) Product summarization & auto-descriptions (catalog enrichment)
- Outcome: faster catalog onboarding, consistent description quality, better SEO.
- Ask for: catalog size, ingestion frequency, human review ratio.

4) Personalization & ranking re-rank
- Outcome: lift in basket size and time-on-site; reranking latency budgets.
- Ask for: rerank QPS, batch vs online constraints, click-through baselines.

Competitive brief (battlecard)

- Major cloud LLM endpoints (AWS/Azure/GCP managed LLM services):
  - Strengths: easy integration, broad platform services, enterprise contracts.
  - Weaknesses / friction: limited fine-grained performance tuning for inference, less control over GPU stack, and fewer integrated caching/batching optimizations that matter at high QPS.
  - Redwood counter: Our runtime selects kernels, manages KV cache and continuous batching automatically and exposes predictable unit economics for production retail workloads.

- Open-source self-hosted stacks + internal infra teams:
  - Strengths: full control, potential low model licensing costs.
  - Weaknesses: high operational burden, variance in latency, ongoing engineering cost for GPU ops and rollout tooling.
  - Redwood counter: Offers the same deployment flexibility with turnkey tooling (rollouts, metrics, SLOs) and a productized private option to reduce ops lift.

- Specialized inference vendors (inference runtimes / orchestration startups):
  - Strengths: high-performance primitives, tight optimization claims.
  - Weaknesses: narrow scope; may lack product integrations (console, evals, pricing tooling, enterprise SLAs).
  - Redwood counter: Combines a production-first platform (console, observability, rollouts) with kernel and runtime optimizations—bridging engineering and product needs.

Win themes to emphasize during discovery

- Measureable conversion impact (show a short case-driven ROI)
- Predictable unit economics at scale (dedicated capacity + Optimize recommendations)
- Low-friction deployment path: POC on hosted API -> reserved Dedicated pool -> private deployment if needed
- Enterprise controls: VPC, KMS integration, audit logs, role-based access

Pricing guidance and sample TCOs (retail scenarios)

High-level guidance: Redwood offers three pricing levers: hosted-per-use (best for POC & low-volume), Dedicated reserved capacity (best for steady high-QPS workloads), and Private (enterprise licensing for regulated deployments). Use these worked examples in commercial conversations.

Scenario A: Mid-market retailer running search + personalization (steady 200 QPS, ~30M tokens/day)
- Recommended plan: Dedicated reserved capacity (monthly commit) with 2x burst capacity
- Expected benefits: 3–5x cost reduction vs unmanaged hosted endpoints at this scale; stable latency SLOs for search.

Scenario B: Enterprise omnichannel retailer (peak 2000 QPS during promotions, heavy rerank workloads)
- Recommended plan: Hybrid approach — hosted API for low-priority workloads, Dedicated pools for search/rerank, optional Private for PII-sensitive catalog processing.
- Commercial terms to pursue: 6–12 month commit with burst engineering support, SLA add-on for promotional periods.

Pricing examples (do not treat as final — use for guidance in deals):

- Hosted: $0.0008–$0.003 per token-equivalent depending on model class and throughput.
- Dedicated base: monthly capacity payment + reduced per-token compute for steady-state throughput. Typical committed monthly price starts in low five-figures for a production small retailer up to mid-six-figures for enterprise.

Use these numbers only as directional; always route to pricing@redwood or solutions-engineering for formal quotes.

Pitch deck outline (10 slides recommended)

1. Title + one-line value prop
2. Retail challenges: conversion, latency, cost, quality regressions
3. Redwood solution overview (hosted/dedicated/private)
4. How it works: runtime + caching + routing + rollouts (1-slide architecture)
5. Case study / customer outcomes (numbers)
6. Demo: search rerank + conversational quick flow (link to demo recipe)
7. Pricing approach & deployment path
8. Security & compliance (VPC, KMS, audit logs)
9. Implementation plan & timeline (POC -> pilot -> production)
10. Ask and next steps

Demo recipe (solutions engineer playbook)

Objective: Show a live search rerank POC that reduces latency and increases relevance for business-critical queries in 45 minutes.

Prep steps (30–60 minutes prior):
1. Ask customer to provide a small catalog CSV (<= 2k SKUs) and a sample of 200 real search queries with click labels.
2. Preload catalog into a demo index and enable a small hosted model instance.
3. Prepare two routes: baseline (current ranking) and Redwood model + rerank.

During demo (20–25 minutes):
1. Quick context (2m): what we measure and success metric (e.g., position-weighted CTR).
2. Live queries (10m): run sample queries side-by-side and show latency, token usage and result changes.
3. Observability (5m): show per-route token cost, latency percentiles and error rates in Console.
4. Next steps (3m): touch on Dedicated option and timelines.

Objection handling (short scripts)

- "We can self-host and save money."
  - Reply: Self-hosting can save model licensing but operational and engineering costs for GPU ops, kernel tuning, and building rollouts usually exceed savings for production-grade throughput. We offer private deployments and transfer operational knowledge during onboarding.

- "Latency during peak sales is non-negotiable."
  - Reply: We support SLO-driven routing and Dedicated capacity with burst policies; for major promotional events we can pre-stage capacity and provide SLA add-ons.

Customer case snapshot (one-pager you can hand to prospects)

Title: "How Northside Apparel cut search latency by 55% and lifted add-to-cart by 12%"
- Customer: Northside Apparel (hypothetical profile: omnichannel retailer, 12M annual sessions)
- Problem: outdated search infra with high tail latency and poor mobile conversion
- Solution: POC using Redwood hosted API for rerank + Dedicated pool for production; KV prefix caching for common queries and a canary rollout.
- Outcomes (90-day): median search latency from 410ms -> 180ms, add-to-cart +12%, cost per search down 35% vs previous managed endpoint.

Security FAQ for prospect evaluations (concise answers)

Q: Can Redwood run inside our VPC / on-prem?
A: Yes. Redwood Private provides a customer-managed control plane with optional on-prem packaging for air-gapped environments. Many enterprise retail customers run catalog ingestion and PII-sensitive transforms in private deployments.

Q: What encryption & key management options exist?
A: All data at rest and in transit is encrypted. We integrate with customer KMS/HSM for private deployments and support customer-managed keys for Dedicated and Private offerings.

Q: How do you handle access controls and auditing?
A: Role-based access control (RBAC) at the Console level, API keys with scoped privileges, and audit logs that capture request metadata, model versions, and operator actions — exportable to SIEM.

Q: Do you persist user prompts or customer PII?
A: For hosted API, data persistence policies are configurable per contract; we provide options for non-retention for PII and can route sensitive processing to Private deployments. See evidence pack for specific retention policies.

Q: What certifications and compliance evidence do you provide?
A: We publish SOC2 Type II and maintain a compliance evidence portal for enterprise procurement. For sector-specific requirements we work with customers on addendum contracts and data residency commitments.

Q: Can Redwood prove no model regression after updates?
A: Yes. Our rollout system integrates with evals and regression monitoring; canary rollouts and automatic rollback rules are available to prevent quality regressions.

Sales motion and recommended next steps (seller checklist)

1. Discovery: collect QPS, latency targets, catalog size, and sample queries.
2. Business case framing: show conversion-impact scenarios and TCO examples tailored to their metrics.
3. POC offer: 2–4 week hosted POC with provided sample prompt sets and eval harness.
4. Pilot: Dedicated reserved pool for production pilot with SLA and support.
5. Enterprise conversion: negotiate private deployment or 12-month dedicated commit, finalize security addenda.

Appendix and links (internal)

- Demo assets: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/retail-demo-assets
- Pricing ops: pricing@redwood.internal
- Security evidence portal: /confluence/security-and-compliance/compliance-evidence

Contact

- Solutions lead: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.internal)
- SE on-call for demos: se-oncall@redwood.internal

Last updated: 2026-03-01 (controls and pricing guidance reviewed with finance and product)

title: SDK: Batching & Streaming Playbook — Patterns, Quickstarts, and Canonical Fixes
preview:
Summary:
This playbook consolidates recommended SDK patterns for streaming + batching usage with Redwood Inference APIs, a short set of quickstart templates for client teams, and canonical fixes for the top support issues we observe in the field. The goal is to reduce token cost without compromising latency SLOs and to provide reproducible steps for common incidents.

Scope:
- SDKs covered: python, node, and go (patterns are portable).
- Traffic modes: low-latency single-request streaming, throughput-oriented batched synchronous requests, hybrid streaming-with-batch setups.
- Not covered: model tuning, model architecture changes.

Intended audience:
- Application engineers integrating Redwood APIs.
- SDK maintainers and support engineers.

1) Quick primer: when to stream vs batch
- Stream when per-request latency (time-to-first-token) is the priority (chat, UI feedback, interactive assistants). Target: TTF token < 200 ms for UI-grade experience.
- Batch when throughput and cost-per-token are the priority (offline generation, high-concurrency inference pipelines). Target: cost efficiency at the expense of slightly higher tail latency.
- Hybrid: use streaming for first N tokens to satisfy TTF SLO and then switch to batched generation for long outputs.

2) Recommended defaults (opinionated)
- Per-tenant latency SLO: 95th percentile TTF < 250ms (hosted tier). Track via console traces and token-level metrics.
- Default batch size by workload (recommendation table):
| Workload Type | Preferred batch size | Max acceptable batch | Notes |
| --- | ---: | ---: | --- |
| Short chat turns (<= 128 tokens) | 8 | 16 | Keep small to preserve TTF |
| Document embedding (dense) | 64 | 256 | Throughput-oriented; use asynchronous workers |
| Long form generation (>512 tokens) | 4 | 8 | Use streaming + chunking for UX |
| Reranking (many short sequences) | 32 | 128 | Use vectorized scoring endpoints when available |

3) SDK quickstart templates
- Python streaming quickstart (minimal):
```python
from redwood import RedwoodClient
client = RedwoodClient(api_key="$REDWOOD_KEY")
# lightweight stream handler
with client.chat.stream(model="redwood-2x", conversation_id="ui-123") as stream:
    stream.send({"role":"user","content":"Summarize latest sales deck"})
    for chunk in stream.receive():
        # update UI chunk-by-chunk
        print(chunk.text, end="", flush=True)
```

- Node batch generator (worker):
```js
const { RedwoodClient } = require('@redwood/inference');
const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_KEY });
async function processBatch(inputs){
  // inputs: [{prompt, meta}, ...]
  const res = await client.generate.batch({model: 'open-rlhf-1', inputs, batchSize: 32, timeoutMs: 30000});
  return res.outputs;
}
```

- CLI to reproduce throughput locally (load test):
$ redwood-cli loadtest --model redwood-2x --concurrency 32 --rps 200 --prompt-file ./samples/prompts.txt --batch-size 16

4) Streaming implementation notes (SDK authors)
- Implement backpressure: when network or downstream UI cannot process chunks fast enough, provide a pause/resume API to the consumer.
- Idempotency: attach a client-generated request_id to streaming sessions so reconnecting clients can resume from the last acknowledged token.
- Heartbeats: emit heartbeat frames every 10s of inactivity to keep connections alive behind proxies/load balancers.
- Chunk size: prefer many small chunks (< 2 KB) for interactive UX; combine tokens only when network overhead is high.

5) Batching implementation notes (SDK + service rules)
- Continuous batching window: 8–20ms for interactive workloads; 50–200ms for higher throughput jobs. Tunable via SDK config.
- Max batch composition rules: do not mix model families with incompatible KV cache sizes in the same batch. SDK must group by model variant, seq_len, and quantization profile.
- Backoff policy: when the service returns 429 or capacity fallback, SDK should implement a jittered exponential backoff with max backoff 10s and initial backoff 50–150ms.

6) Top observed issues and canonical fixes (support playbook)
Issue A: Very high TTF for interactive chat after SDK upgrade
- Symptom: 95p TTF jumps from 120ms to 800ms, users report UI lag.
- Root causes to check (in order):
  1. SDK default batch window increased (check config: batchWindowMs).
  2. Client-side streaming disabled (client using blocking generate call).
  3. New kernel selection caused model to run with cold KV cache due to mixing quantized and FP variants in batch.
- Canonical fixes:
  - Confirm SDK config: set batchWindowMs=12 for chat-tier clients and enable streaming=true.
  - Add batching grouping key: model_name + seq_len_bucket.
  - Rollback the SDK library to previous minor if fix in SDK is needed; escalate to runtime if kernel changes are implicated.

Issue B: Spikes of 429s when autoscaling events happen
- Symptom: Short periods of 429s followed by recovery; usually at scale events during deployments.
- Root causes to check:
  1. Global autoscaler lag on Dedicated pools.
  2. Burst window misconfiguration in client's reserved capacity settings.
  3. Clients not honoring Retry-After header.
- Canonical fixes:
  - Respect Retry-After and implement exponential backoff.
  - For Dedicated customers, validate burstCapacity=true settings and confirm SLA tier.
  - If rollout to new model variant caused capacity fragmentation, use canary rollout (see rollout section).

Issue C: Token-count mismatches and double-chunking in transcripts
- Symptom: Clients display repeated tokens when reconnecting to streams or after chunk stitching.
- Causes & fixes:
  - Ensure each chunk contains absolute token offsets. Use the provided stream_offset metadata to stitch deterministically.
  - Provide client-side deduplication based on token offset and request_id.

7) Troubleshooting checklist (for on-call & support)
1. Reproduce locally with the same SDK version and a captured request/response trace.
2. Check SDK config: batch_size, batchWindowMs, streaming flag, timeoutMs.
3. Inspect service logs for kernel fallbacks, 429 rates, and queue depth (trace tags: request_id, tenant_id).
4. If related to Dedicated: verify pool utilization and scaling events in eng-sre dashboard.
5. If regression after deployment: bisect via canary rollback (rollout id in trace).

8) Rollout and rollback guidance for SDK changes
- Canary strategy: start at 1% of traffic for 24 hours, increase to 10% for 6–12 hours, then 50% for 3–6 hours before full rollout. Monitor: 95p TTF, 99p error rate, 429 rate, and cost-per-1k tokens.
- Emergency rollback steps:
  1. Create rollback release in SDK package manager (npm/pypi/go proxy).
  2. Patch server-side routing policy to pin traffic to previous SDK-compatible model variant for flagged tenants.
  3. Notify affected customers and enable temporary Dedicated burst capacity if needed.

9) Observability and dashboards to add (minimum set)
- Per-SDK-version: 95p TTF, 95p request latency to first token, 99p error rate, and average batch size.
- Tracing tags: sdk_version, batch_id, streaming_session_id, request_id, model_variant.
- Alerts (recommended thresholds):
  - 95p TTF > 350ms for 5m -> P1 oncall notified.
  - 429 rate > 1% of requests for 10m -> P2.
  - Avg batch size < 50% of configured target for 5m (indicates fragmentation) -> investigate routing/grouping.

10) Example configuration snippets (YAML)
# client-config.yaml
sdk:
  version: v2
  streaming: true
  batchWindowMs: 12
  targetBatchSize: 16
  backoff:
    initialMs: 100
    factor: 2.0
    maxMs: 10000
  resume_on_disconnect: true

11) Ownership and escalation
- SDK patterns & templates: eng-developer-experience (primary).
- Runtime fallback and kernel issues: eng-serving-runtime (secondary).
- Scaling & Dedicated pool issues: eng-sre (ops).
- Oncall escalation path: support -> eng-developer-experience -> eng-serving-runtime -> eng-sre.

12) How to add a new canonical fix (process)
1. Create a PR in the docs repo with a reproducible test case and a short patch (code + test).
2. Add the issue to the ‘SDK canonical fixes’ project board and assign reviewers.
3. After merge, mark the doc updated and notify the support channel with reproducer.

Appendix A — Compact checklist card (copy/paste for Slack)
Title: SDK streaming/batching incident triage\- Step 1: Capture request_id and SDK version\- Step 2: Run local repro via redwood-cli with same flags\- Step 3: Check batchWindowMs and streaming flag in client config\- Step 4: Inspect service traces for kernel fallback or 429s\- Step 5: Apply canonical fix if matched; escalate if not

Appendix B — Sample request/response excerpt (JSON)
{ "request_id": "req-9a3b2f", "model": "redwood-2x", "batch_id": "b-123", "chunks": [ { "offset": 0, "text": "Hello ", "seq": 1 }, { "offset": 6, "text": "world!", "seq": 2 } ] }

Notes & history:
- 2024-11-03: first internal draft created during cross-team postmortem for chat regressions.
- 2025-06-10: added hybrid streaming recommendation to improve UX on long-form outputs.
- 2026-02-18: public release of playbook with canonical fixes and default config recommendations.

Contact:
- eng-developer-experience channel on Slack (#eng-devx) and doc owner Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai).

Related artifacts:
- redwood-cli (internal): repo link in platform wiki.
- SDK test harness: /infra/ci/loadtests/sdk-loadtest-suite (internal link).

title: Developer runtime snippet kits and publish playbook
preview:
Summary

This page is a compact, production-oriented kit for Developer Experience (DX) engineers and SDK maintainers: a set of vetted runtime snippets, small quickstart templates, documentation-publishing checks, and a canonical troubleshooting list for the most frequent developer-reported inference integration problems. The goal is a single place to grab copy-paste-ready code and followable publish/run/playback steps that keep examples consistent across languages and the docs site.

Who should use this

- SDK owners and maintainers updating examples or adding new SDK surfaces.
- Documentation authors publishing live examples to the docs site.
- Support engineers triaging reproducible developer bugs.
- Solutions engineers building customer PoCs who need reliable starter templates.

When to pick a kit vs full quickstart

1. Use a snippet kit when you need short, 15–60 line, runnable examples embedded inline in docs.
2. Use a quickstart when the developer needs a multi-file project (local dev harness, docker-compose, CI checks).
3. Use the publish playbook when merging changes that include live examples, redirects, or new code sandboxes.

The kit (what's included)

- Language snippets: Python, TypeScript, and Curl for all common flows (init, streaming, embeddings, function/tool calling).
- Small templates: local-dev harness, retry/idempotency wrapper, small serverless handler (FastAPI / Next.js API route).
- Docs site checklist: static validation commands, snippet test harness commands, and the merge/publish sequence.
- Canonical fixes: curated fixes for the top 8 reproducible support issues tied to examples.

Usage examples (copy/paste)

1) Minimal Python initialization + streaming example

```python
# file: examples/python/streaming_init.py
import redwood  # placeholder package name used in SDK examples

client = redwood.Client(api_key="${REDWOOD_API_KEY}")

with client.stream_completions(model="redwood-1.1-large") as stream:
    stream.send_prompt("Summarize the following text:
- Keep it under 50 words.")
    for chunk in stream.iter_text():
        print(chunk, end="")

# Notes: run with: REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk_... python streaming_init.py
```

2) TypeScript: retry wrapper + small inference helper (Node 18+)

```ts
// file: examples/ts/retry-helper.ts
import { RedwoodClient } from '@redwood-inference/sdk';

const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY });

export async function callWithRetry(payload: object, retries = 2) {
  for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= retries; attempt++) {
    try {
      return await client.completions.create(payload);
    } catch (err) {
      if (attempt === retries) throw err;
      // exponential backoff (simple)
      await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 200 * Math.pow(2, attempt)));
    }
  }
}
```

3) Curl example to reproduce a bad tokenization/encoding issue

```bash
curl -X POST 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
  https://api.redwood-inference.internal/v1/completions 
  -d '{"model":"redwood-1.1-large","input":"Emoji test: 😊 — make sure it returns UTF-8 safe output"}'
```

Docs site operational notes (publish playbook)

Follow these steps when merging documentation changes that include runnable code snippets or new quickstarts: 

1) Local validation (author)
- Run snippet lint: tools/snippet-lint --path examples/ --format markdown
- Execute snippet smoke tests: ./tools/snippet-test-runner --languages python,ts --files examples/
- Confirm all sandboxed calls use the TEST_API_KEY (not personal prod keys).

2) CI gates (automated)
- CI runs: snippet-test-runner (containerized); failures block merges.
- Create a sandbox recording: tools/create-sandbox-recording --flow create_completions --seed 42. Store recording id in PR description.

3) Review (manual)
- Code/Doc owners must verify: language parity (each highlighted feature appears in at least two languages), and performance notes (e.g., expected latency for the snippet on shared sandbox).

4) Staging publish
- Push to docs-staging: ./scripts/publish-docs --staging --include-examples. The staging site will be reachable at https://staging.docs.redwood.local.
- Run the staged smoke tests: tools/staged-smoke --recording <id>. This verifies snippet output shape and no flaky network calls.

5) Production rollout
- After approvals, merge to main; CI will run full publish and invalidate cache.
- Monitor Docs Health dashboard for 24 hours for snippet-related errors tagged docs-snippet.*.

Snippet testing harness (how it works)

- Each snippet directory includes metadata.yaml with: language, run-command, run-timeout, mocked-responses (optional). Example:
runtime: python
run-command: REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY python streaming_init.py
run-timeout: 30s

- The harness runs snippets inside ephemeral containers and captures stdout/stderr and exit code. If a snippet uses network, it must either be recorded or explicitly marked as "allow-network: true" (rare).

Top reported issues (canonical fixes)

These are the eight most frequent problems encountered when developers follow examples. Each entry has a reproducible symptom, root cause, and canonical fix they can perform themselves.

1) Authentication 401 in example runs
- Symptom: Curl or SDK sample returns 401 with message "Invalid API key".
- Root cause: Example used a placeholder value or an expired test key; env var not exported in the shell.
- Fix: export REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY; ensure CI uses masked secrets. Update snippet header to show run command.

2) Streaming snippets hang indefinitely
- Symptom: Example process does not exit when stream completes.
- Root cause: Missing graceful-close/await on the streaming iterator; some SDKs require stream.close().
- Fix: Add explicit close/await in the snippet and update inline comment. Add a 60s timeout in snippet metadata.

3) Encoding/emoji corruption in curl examples
- Symptom: Emojis or special unicode render as question marks.
- Root cause: Incorrect Content-Type or curl not using --data-binary.
- Fix: Use -H 'Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8' and --data-binary instead of -d. Confirm container locale is UTF-8.

4) Mismatched parameter names across SDKs (model vs model_name)
- Symptom: Python example uses "model" while TypeScript example used "model_name" causing confusion.
- Root cause: SDK surface drift during a refactor.
- Fix: Align to canonical parameter names: use "model" everywhere. Add a schema lint that flags parameter-name differences.

5) CI snippet tests intermittently fail due to rate limits
- Symptom: Tests fail in CI with 429 errors.
- Root cause: Shared sandbox exhausted ephemeral throughput.
- Fix: Add retries in snippet harness and set TEST_QPS cap via env var. For CI, allocate parallel sandbox tokens.

6) Local quickstart fails with Docker overlay error
- Symptom: docker-compose up fails on Mac with overlayfs or permission denied.
- Root cause: Host filesystem permissions or conflicting local docker versions.
- Fix: Document known Mac workarounds in README (chmod the project dir, use docker-desktop > 4.0). Provide a non-root compose variant in templates.

7) Examples leak real keys into recorded artifacts
- Symptom: Sandbox recordings contain non-masked API keys.
- Root cause: Author ran snippet locally with a prod key when creating recording.
- Fix: Reject PRs that include recordings with unmasked secrets. Add a pre-commit hook to scrub keys (tools/scrub-recordings).

8) Examples produce different text across runs (flaky outputs)
- Symptom: Assertion on example output shape fails due to slight variation.
- Root cause: Using deterministic seeds not set or relying on non-deterministic logit sampling.
- Fix: Use deterministic parameters in CI (temperature=0, top_k/top_p fixed) for recorded assertions; in inline docs, call out that outputs are stochastic.

Ownership and escalation

- Example owners: 
  - Python snippets: @priya (owner)
  - TypeScript snippets: @ethan (owner)
  - Docs publishing: @maria (owner)
- If CI snippet test failures are systemic, open an incident in eng-developer-experience and tag "docs-snippet-failure"; SRE will investigate infrastructure quotas or container flakiness.

Template catalog (short descriptions)

- local-dev-harness/ : docker-compose, seeded sandbox, env examples. 
- retry-wrapper/ : language-specific retry logic + best-practice comments.
- serverless-handler/ : minimal server-side snippet for hosting a model call (FastAPI and Next.js).
- embedding-starter/ : sample for embeddings + simple vector store (sqlite) + example search.

Appendix: common commands and test vectors

- Run all snippet tests locally: ./tools/snippet-test-runner --all --languages python,ts
- Create a test sandbox: tools/create-sandbox --ttl 2h --seed 12345
- Quick smoke (python): REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY python examples/python/streaming_init.py
- Quick smoke (ts): REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY node examples/ts/retry-helper.ts
- Validate docs build: yarn workspace docs build --check-examples

Frequently asked questions

Q: What is the recommended snippet length?
A: Keep inline snippets under 60 lines (ideally 15–40). For longer flows, link to the quickstart project.

Q: Are recorded sandbox outputs allowed in PRs?
A: Yes — recordings are encouraged for CI stability. Always scrub keys and mark recordings with the sandbox id.

Q: How do we ensure language parity?
A: For any new API surface or feature, publish at least two language examples (Python + JS preferred). Add a docs-lint rule that checks presence.

Related resources

- tools/snippet-test-runner README (internal)
- docs authoring guide: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/docs-site-content-lifecycle-and-local-preview-2026
- API contract: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/

Change log

- 2025-10-12: Draft created (initial set of snippets and smoke-run notes).
- 2025-11-30: Added retry-wrapper and staging publish steps.
- 2026-02-18: Added canonical fixes list and tightened CI expectations.

If you have a snippet you want added or a recurring support pattern to include, file a PR against this directory and ping the reviewer group listed above.

Usage examples (copy/paste)

1) Minimal Python initialization + streaming example

```python
# file: examples/python/streaming_init.py
import redwood  # placeholder package name used in SDK examples

client = redwood.Client(api_key="${REDWOOD_API_KEY}")

with client.stream_completions(model="redwood-1.1-large") as stream:
    stream.send_prompt("Summarize the following text:
- Keep it under 50 words.")
    for chunk in stream.iter_text():
        print(chunk, end="")

# Notes: run with: REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk_... python streaming_init.py
```

2) TypeScript: retry wrapper + small inference helper (Node 18+)

```ts
// file: examples/ts/retry-helper.ts
import { RedwoodClient } from '@redwood-inference/sdk';

const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY });

export async function callWithRetry(payload: object, retries = 2) {
  for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= retries; attempt++) {
    try {
      return await client.completions.create(payload);
    } catch (err) {
      if (attempt === retries) throw err;
      // exponential backoff (simple)
      await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 200 * Math.pow(2, attempt)));
    }
  }
}
```

3) Curl example to reproduce a bad tokenization/encoding issue

```bash
curl -X POST 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
  https://api.redwood-inference.internal/v1/completions 
  -d '{"model":"redwood-1.1-large","input":"Emoji test: 😊 — make sure it returns UTF-8 safe output"}'
```

Docs site operational notes (publish playbook)

Follow these steps when merging documentation changes that include runnable code snippets or new quickstarts: 

1) Local validation (author)
- Run snippet lint: tools/snippet-lint --path examples/ --format markdown
- Execute snippet smoke tests: ./tools/snippet-test-runner --languages python,ts --files examples/
- Confirm all sandboxed calls use the TEST_API_KEY (not personal prod keys).

2) CI gates (automated)
- CI runs: snippet-test-runner (containerized); failures block merges.
- Create a sandbox recording: tools/create-sandbox-recording --flow create_completions --seed 42. Store recording id in PR description.

3) Review (manual)
- Code/Doc owners must verify: language parity (each highlighted feature appears in at least two languages), and performance notes (e.g., expected latency for the snippet on shared sandbox).

4) Staging publish
- Push to docs-staging: ./scripts/publish-docs --staging --include-examples. The staging site will be reachable at https://staging.docs.redwood.local.
- Run the staged smoke tests: tools/staged-smoke --recording <id>. This verifies snippet output shape and no flaky network calls.

5) Production rollout
- After approvals, merge to main; CI will run full publish and invalidate cache.
- Monitor Docs Health dashboard for 24 hours for snippet-related errors tagged docs-snippet.*.

Snippet testing harness (how it works)

- Each snippet directory includes metadata.yaml with: language, run-command, run-timeout, mocked-responses (optional). Example:
runtime: python
run-command: REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY python streaming_init.py
run-timeout: 30s

- The harness runs snippets inside ephemeral containers and captures stdout/stderr and exit code. If a snippet uses network, it must either be recorded or explicitly marked as "allow-network: true" (rare).

Top reported issues (canonical fixes)

These are the eight most frequent problems encountered when developers follow examples. Each entry has a reproducible symptom, root cause, and canonical fix they can perform themselves.

1) Authentication 401 in example runs
- Symptom: Curl or SDK sample returns 401 with message "Invalid API key".
- Root cause: Example used a placeholder value or an expired test key; env var not exported in the shell.
- Fix: export REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY; ensure CI uses masked secrets. Update snippet header to show run command.

2) Streaming snippets hang indefinitely
- Symptom: Example process does not exit when stream completes.
- Root cause: Missing graceful-close/await on the streaming iterator; some SDKs require stream.close().
- Fix: Add explicit close/await in the snippet and update inline comment. Add a 60s timeout in snippet metadata.

3) Encoding/emoji corruption in curl examples
- Symptom: Emojis or special unicode render as question marks.
- Root cause: Incorrect Content-Type or curl not using --data-binary.
- Fix: Use -H 'Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8' and --data-binary instead of -d. Confirm container locale is UTF-8.

4) Mismatched parameter names across SDKs (model vs model_name)
- Symptom: Python example uses "model" while TypeScript example used "model_name" causing confusion.
- Root cause: SDK surface drift during a refactor.
- Fix: Align to canonical parameter names: use "model" everywhere. Add a schema lint that flags parameter-name differences.

5) CI snippet tests intermittently fail due to rate limits
- Symptom: Tests fail in CI with 429 errors.
- Root cause: Shared sandbox exhausted ephemeral throughput.
- Fix: Add retries in snippet harness and set TEST_QPS cap via env var. For CI, allocate parallel sandbox tokens.

6) Local quickstart fails with Docker overlay error
- Symptom: docker-compose up fails on Mac with overlayfs or permission denied.
- Root cause: Host filesystem permissions or conflicting local docker versions.
- Fix: Document known Mac workarounds in README (chmod the project dir, use docker-desktop > 4.0). Provide a non-root compose variant in templates.

7) Examples leak real keys into recorded artifacts
- Symptom: Sandbox recordings contain non-masked API keys.
- Root cause: Author ran snippet locally with a prod key when creating recording.
- Fix: Reject PRs that include recordings with unmasked secrets. Add a pre-commit hook to scrub keys (tools/scrub-recordings).

8) Examples produce different text across runs (flaky outputs)
- Symptom: Assertion on example output shape fails due to slight variation.
- Root cause: Using deterministic seeds not set or relying on non-deterministic logit sampling.
- Fix: Use deterministic parameters in CI (temperature=0, top_k/top_p fixed) for recorded assertions; in inline docs, call out that outputs are stochastic.

Ownership and escalation

- Example owners: 
  - Python snippets: @priya (owner)
  - TypeScript snippets: @ethan (owner)
  - Docs publishing: @maria (owner)
- If CI snippet test failures are systemic, open an incident in eng-developer-experience and tag "docs-snippet-failure"; SRE will investigate infrastructure quotas or container flakiness.

Template catalog (short descriptions)

- local-dev-harness/ : docker-compose, seeded sandbox, env examples. 
- retry-wrapper/ : language-specific retry logic + best-practice comments.
- serverless-handler/ : minimal server-side snippet for hosting a model call (FastAPI and Next.js).
- embedding-starter/ : sample for embeddings + simple vector store (sqlite) + example search.

Appendix: common commands and test vectors

- Run all snippet tests locally: ./tools/snippet-test-runner --all --languages python,ts
- Create a test sandbox: tools/create-sandbox --ttl 2h --seed 12345
- Quick smoke (python): REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY python examples/python/streaming_init.py
- Quick smoke (ts): REDWOOD_API_KEY=TEST_API_KEY node examples/ts/retry-helper.ts
- Validate docs build: yarn workspace docs build --check-examples

Frequently asked questions

Q: What is the recommended snippet length?
A: Keep inline snippets under 60 lines (ideally 15–40). For longer flows, link to the quickstart project.

Q: Are recorded sandbox outputs allowed in PRs?
A: Yes — recordings are encouraged for CI stability. Always scrub keys and mark recordings with the sandbox id.

Q: How do we ensure language parity?
A: For any new API surface or feature, publish at least two language examples (Python + JS preferred). Add a docs-lint rule that checks presence.

Related resources

- tools/snippet-test-runner README (internal)
- docs authoring guide: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/docs-site-content-lifecycle-and-local-preview-2026
- API contract: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/

Change log

- 2025-10-12: Draft created (initial set of snippets and smoke-run notes).
- 2025-11-30: Added retry-wrapper and staging publish steps.
- 2026-02-18: Added canonical fixes list and tightened CI expectations.

If you have a snippet you want added or a recurring support pattern to include, file a PR against this directory and ping the reviewer group listed above.

title: ADR - Telemetry Stencil & Feature-Gate Emission Contract
preview:
Summary:
This ADR defines a compact, enforceable contract between runtime feature gates (feature flags/rollouts) and telemetry producers. We introduce the concept of a 'telemetry stencil' - a minimal, versioned emission schema that accompanies each gated feature. The stencil ensures predictable ingestion cost, bounded cardinality, and privacy-safe defaults while enabling meaningful observability during staged rollouts.

Problem statement and motivation:
- Incidents in 2024-2025 exposed two repeating failure modes: runaway telemetry cardinality from experimental features and accidental emission of PII fields when new gate logic touched request structures. Both increased ingestion costs and created compliance risk.
- Teams want low-friction rollouts but need guardrails that prevent telemetry spikes, make ownership explicit, and integrate with rollout automation (canary, staged percentages, rollback).

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to Redwood server-side runtimes (hosted, Dedicated) and to runtime sidecars used in Private deployments where the sidecar is responsible for telemetry emission. SDKs and client-side events are out-of-scope for this ADR but should adopt the same principles in a companion doc.

Decision (contract overview):
We formalize two small artifacts that must accompany any new runtime gate: a Gate Manifest and a Telemetry Stencil. Both are small JSON documents registered in the feature registry and validated at CI time.

1) Gate Manifest (required fields):
- gate_id (string): stable, namespaced id (example: runtimes/prio-hedging/v1).
- owner (string): team alias and Pager duty rotation.
- rollout_strategy (string): one of canary | staged | percentage.
- telemetry_stencil_id (string): reference to a registered stencil.
- default_state (string): enabled | disabled.
- safe_fallback (string): identifier of fallback behavior to revert to if SLO or telemetry constraints breach.

Validation rule: Missing telemetry_stencil_id or a non-existent stencil reference causes CI to fail for changes that add or modify gates.

2) Telemetry Stencil (required fields):
- stencil_id (string): namespaced id (example: stencils/prio-hedging/basic-v1).
- version (string): semver-like ("1.0.0").
- field_list (list of strings): ordered descriptors of fields the runtime will emit while the gate is active. Field descriptor format: "name|type|cardinality_hint|privacy_hint". Example: "prompt_hash|string|low|pii-free".
- max_estimated_egress_per_minute (string): numeric estimate in MB/min.
- privacy_profile (string): one of pii-free | pii-reduced | pii-allowed.
- retention_days (string): retention in days as numeric string.

Field cardinality and privacy hints are guidance used by ingestion and alerting pipelines. The runtime must not emit fields outside field_list except in an explicit dev/debug mode that is disabled for production.

Operational guarantees and enforcement:
- Bounded cost: Each stencil includes an egress estimate. The runtime and ingestion pipeline will account emissions against the stencil limit. If measured egress for a gate exceeds 2x the declared max, the runtime will trigger an automated mitigation (see Rollout & Runbook).
- Privacy safe-by-default: For hosted deployments, only stencils with privacy_profile in {pii-free, pii-reduced} are allowed without additional legal review. Private deployments may accept pii-allowed on approval.
- Observability fidelity: During initial canary the default sampling is 100% for the stencil fields to ensure visibility; after canary success, teams should reduce sampling to a post-canary target (e.g., 10%) based on cost/SLO tradeoffs.

Registration and CI workflow (authoring pattern):
1) Developer creates stencil JSON and gate manifest and opens a PR in the feature registry repo.
2) CI validates: schema conformance, egress estimate plausibility (compares declared estimate to historical baselines for similar gates/models), owner/LDAP resolution, and no PII hints for hosted-approved stencils.
3) On merge, the registry returns a manifest fingerprint that is embedded in deployment artifacts to guarantee rollout uses the approved stencil.

Runtime behavior specification:
- Load: Runtime loads stencil metadata at startup and caches by stencil_id + version.
- Emit: When a gate is active, runtime will only emit fields in field_list. Unexpected fields are dropped and an internal metric 'telemetry.emission.unexpected_fields' is incremented.
- Annotate: Traces and logs must include gate_id and stencil_version to aid filtering.
- Accounting: The runtime emits per-gate egress counters and reports them to the telemetry-cost pipeline at 1m granularity.

Rollout and runbook (executable policy):
1) Canary: default 1% traffic, 2 cycles (default cycle = 1 hour), telemetry sampling 100% for stencil fields.
2) Monitor: dashboards include: per-gate egress rate, per-field cardinality, SLOs (p50/p95 latency, error rate).
3) Mitigation thresholds:
   - Warning: measured_egress > 1.25 * declared_max => reduce telemetry sampling to 10% and notify owner.
   - Critical: measured_egress > 2.0 * declared_max OR SLO regression > 5% sustained for 3 cycles => automated rollback to safe_fallback and on-call page.

Rollback pseudo-code (runtime):
if measured_egress(gate_id) > 2.0 * declared_max:
  emit_alert("telemetry-egress-critical", gate_id)
  trigger_rollout_action(gate_id, action="rollback-to-fallback")

Example stencil (textual example you can paste into a registry):
{
  "stencil_id": "stencils/prio-hedging/basic-v1",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "field_list": [
    "gate_id|string|low|pii-free",
    "request_id|string|low|pii-free",
    "heuristic_score|float|low|pii-reduced",
    "latency_ms|int|low|pii-free"
  ],
  "max_estimated_egress_per_minute": "0.75",
  "privacy_profile": "pii-reduced",
  "retention_days": "30"
}

Configuration defaults (recommended):
- rollout.canary_percent = 1
- telemetry.default_sampling = 100
- telemetry.post_canary_sampling = 10
- telemetry.egress_throttle_ratio = 2.0

Migration plan and compatibility:
- Existing gates without stencils are given a 6-month remediation window. During this period the platform will auto-attach a conservative system stencil (pii-reduced, max_estimated_egress_per_minute=0.1MB/min) and surface a remediation ticket.
- Private deployments may relax privacy_profile enforcement but must maintain stencil registration and CI validation against the private registry.

Consequences and trade-offs:
- Pros: predictable telemetry cost from feature rollouts; fewer surprise ingestion spikes; clearer ownership and earlier detection of PII leakage.
- Cons: additional authoring overhead for teams; potential slowdown for rapid experiments. Mitigation: provide a minimal "fast-track" stencil template and CLI tooling to scaffold stencils quickly.

Open questions (to be addressed in follow-ups):
- Accurate egress estimation for bursty long-completion models: propose a conservatively-derived worst-case token multiplier and an automated historical multiplier refinement job.
- Scoped debug sampling: should individual request IDs be eligible for elevated tracing? Proposal: enable a gated debug channel limited by role-based access and short TTLs.

Ownership and success metrics:
- Owner: runtime-observability team (primary contact: Priya Natarajan).
- Adoption target: 100% of new gates registered with stencils after the 6-month migration window.
- Reliability target: zero severe telemetry incidents attributable to new gate rollouts year-over-year.

Related documentation and links:
- Telemetry ingestion and retention guidelines (see related_pages).
- Runtime sidecar contract for private deployments.

Reviewers: Evan Park, Maya Raghavan, Jordan Liu

Status: published
