title: Multi-tenant Shared NAT & Egress Crediting Allocator — Design, IaC, and Runbook
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes the architecture, Terraform/IaC patterns, monitoring, and on-call runbook for a Multi-tenant Shared NAT + Egress Crediting Allocator (the Allocator). The Allocator provides fair, auditable egress crediting and NAT port assignment for tenant workloads that share a pool of NAT gateways (or egress appliances). It is intended for Redwood-hosted GPU and inference tenant workloads that must control egress spend while preserving tenant isolation and predictable QoS.

Goals
-----
- Meter per-tenant egress at high cardinality (per account / environment / cluster) with sub-minute accuracy for billing and throttling.
- Enforce predictable fairness by allocating reusable NAT port ranges and egress credit quotas per tenant.
- Provide a Terraform module and CI/CD pattern for managing allocator pools across accounts/regions.
- Offer an on-call runbook that reduces pager fatigue and supports safe rollback of crediting/policing rules.

Non-goals
---------
- Replacing provider-level billing (this augments it for product-level chargebacks).
- Deep packet inspection or payload-level policy.

Architecture overview
---------------------
1) Control-plane service (Allocator API)
   - Stateless HTTP service providing: allocate_port_range, refresh_quota, report-egress, reconcile-tenant-assignments.
   - Backed by a single sharded Postgres (or Dynamo) table for assignments and credits.
   - Exposes a read-only metrics stream for Prometheus and an audit log to S3/BigQuery.

2) Data-plane (shared NAT appliances / managed gateway)
   - NAT appliances (cloud-managed NAT gateway or self-hosted egress proxies) configured with multiple public IPs and port mapping.
   - A sidecar agent (or iptables management daemon) that requests a port-range from Allocator and applies per-tenant SNAT rules.

3) Accounting/charging pipeline
   - Periodic batch job consumes audit logs and per-tenant report-egress events to create invoiceable line items and chargeback metrics.

Key entities and invariants
---------------------------
- Tenant: (org_id, project_id, env) string.
- PortRange: contiguous ephemeral TCP/UDP port block (e.g., 1000 ports).
- CreditBucket: egress bytes reserved per tenant per billing-period.
- Invariant: port assignment is unique per NAT IP + port range to avoid collisions.

Operational SLOs and thresholds
-------------------------------
- Allocator API availability: 99.95% (global)
- Allocation latency: 95th percentile < 300ms for allocate_port_range API call
- Egress metering latency: events visible in Prometheus within 60s of occurrence
- Billing accuracy: <0.5% drift versus provider egress billing for aggregated daily totals

Prometheus metrics to publish
----------------------------
- allocator.allocations_total (labels: tenant, region, outcome)
- allocator.allocation_latency_seconds_bucket (labels: tenant, region)
- allocator.egress_report_bytes_total (labels: tenant, region, ip)
- allocator.quota_exhausted_count (labels: tenant, region)
- allocator.audit_events_published_total

Terraform / IaC patterns (example)
---------------------------------
This section shows the recommended module layout and a compact example. The actual module shipped in our org follows the pattern below and lives in the terraform monorepo (module path: modules/egress-allocator).

Table: Module inputs and defaults
- name: module_input - description: default
- name: region - Target region for NAT pool - default: us-west-2
- name: nat_pool_size - Number of public IPs in pool - default: "4"
- name: port_block_size - Ports reserved per tenant - default: "1024"
- name: db_connection_string - Allocator DB - default: ""

Example usage snippet (HCL)
- module "egress_allocator" {
-   source = "git::ssh://git@repo/modules/egress-allocator.git//cloud-account?ref=v1.3.0"
-   region = "us-west-2"
-   nat_pool_size = 8
-   port_block_size = 2048
- }

CI/CD pattern and gates
----------------------
1. PRs to module require: unit tests, terratest integration (ephemeral infra), and a smoke deploy using a sandbox account.
2. Promotion pipeline (canary -> staging -> prod) enforced with a feature flag for new allocator behavior.
3. Runbook step must be referenced in the promotion merge commit message and checked off in CI before promotion.

Reconciliation and data integrity checks (daily job)
---------------------------------------------------
- Verify no overlapping port ranges across all NAT public IPs.
- Compare aggregated egress bytes by provider API vs our aggregated credit events; emit drift alert if >0.5%.
- Validate that credit buckets with churn >20% get a rebalance recommendation.

On-call runbook (Pager triggers and immediate response)
-----------------------------------------------------
Pager conditions (alerts and actions):
1) allocator.api_5xx_rate > 1% sustained for 3m\   - Action: page oncall infra-engineer, acknowledge in alert system, increase allocator replicas (k8s HPA), check DB connectivity, fail open to conservative default port assignment if DB unreachable.
2) allocation_latency_seconds (p95) > 300ms for 5m\   - Action: page infra oncall, check service heap/cpu, recent deploys, scale horizontally, sample trace for slow SQL queries.
3) quota_exhausted_count spikes for a tenant by >100x baseline\   - Action: notify customer-success and rate-limit tenant via policy; open emergency ticket for billing team to reconcile credits.
4) egress_drift_alert (daily) > 0.5%\   - Action: run reconciliation script (see Appendix), escalate to finance if persistent for 48 hours.

Step-by-step: Recovering from allocator DB outage
1) Identify: Pager shows allocator.api_5xx_rate alert.
2) Quick mitigation: enable local ephemeral port-assignment fallback in sidecars (config flag: ALLOCATOR_FALLBACK=local_range).
3) If DB is write-unavailable, configure Allocator to serve read-only and queue writes to a durable S3 buffer (feature toggle).
4) After DB restore: drain buffer consumer job to apply queued events; run data integrity checks; reconcile drift.
5) Postmortem: publish root cause, timeline, and the corrective IaC change if rollout exposed a deployment race.

Safety & gradual rollout checklist
--------------------------------
- Canary rollout to 3 tenants with high egress but non-critical SLAs for 72h.
- Monitor top-10 egress tenants for port collisions and latency.
- Enable crediting in billing pipeline for canary tenants only.
- After 72h with no anomalies, switch on broader promotion with 24h ramp steps.

Ownership and responsibilities
------------------------------
- Allocator control-plane: eng-infra/networking (primary), with oncall rotation in infra-team.
- NAT appliance configuration: cloud-accounts team (ownership of cloud account and VPC).
- Billing pipeline: finance + eng-data for reconciliation jobs.
- Terraform modules & CI: eng-infra/ci-cd team (module authors), with CD pipeline enforced in repo.

Appendix A — Debugging commands and snippets
-------------------------------------------
- Check allocations for tenant (SQL): SELECT tenant, ip, port_start, port_end, assigned_at FROM allocations WHERE tenant = 'org-123' ORDER BY assigned_at DESC LIMIT 50;
- Reconciliation helper (python psuedocode):
-   requests.get("https://allocator/api/v1/reconcile?period=24h")

Appendix B — Example sidecar config (yaml)
- sidecar:
-   allocator_endpoint: "https://allocator.internal:443"
-   request_timeout: 2s
-   port_block_size: 2048
-   fallback_mode: "disabled"

Appendix C — Audit and compliance notes
- Audit logs: all allocate_port_range and refresh_quota API calls are written to s3://redwood-allocator-audit/{region}/YYYY/MM/DD with per-event signed checksums.
- Retention: 365 days cold, 7 years for restricted tenants.

Appendix D — Known limitations and future work
- UDP-heavy workloads with unpredictable ephemeral port churn may require per-flow NAT session tracking; currently unsupported.
- Plan to introduce token-bucket soft throttles to smooth bursty egress and avoid hard throttling.

Change history
--------------
- 2025-09-12: Initial design and runbook (Priya Natarajan).
- 2025-11-03: Added Terraform promotion gating and reconciliation job (Carlos Mendes).
- 2026-02-15: Clarified DB outage fallback and retention policy (Ava Chen).

Related diagrams and attachments
--------------------------------
- Diagram: allocator-sequence-diagram.drawio (stored in confluence attachments).
- Terraform module source: git@repo:infra/modules/egress-allocator.git (internal).

Questions / feedback
--------------------
For questions about rollout timeline or tenant mapping, ping @priya.natarajan or add comments on this page.
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes the architecture, Terraform/IaC patterns, monitoring, and on-call runbook for a Multi-tenant Shared NAT + Egress Crediting Allocator (the Allocator). The Allocator provides fair, auditable egress crediting and NAT port assignment for tenant workloads that share a pool of NAT gateways (or egress appliances). It is intended for Redwood-hosted GPU and inference tenant workloads that must control egress spend while preserving tenant isolation and predictable QoS.

Goals
-----
- Meter per-tenant egress at high cardinality (per account / environment / cluster) with sub-minute accuracy for billing and throttling.
- Enforce predictable fairness by allocating reusable NAT port ranges and egress credit quotas per tenant.
- Provide a Terraform module and CI/CD pattern for managing allocator pools across accounts/regions.
- Offer an on-call runbook that reduces pager fatigue and supports safe rollback of crediting/policing rules.

Non-goals
---------
- Replacing provider-level billing (this augments it for product-level chargebacks).
- Deep packet inspection or payload-level policy.

Architecture overview
---------------------
1) Control-plane service (Allocator API)
   - Stateless HTTP service providing: allocate_port_range, refresh_quota, report-egress, reconcile-tenant-assignments.
   - Backed by a single sharded Postgres (or Dynamo) table for assignments and credits.
   - Exposes a read-only metrics stream for Prometheus and an audit log to S3/BigQuery.

2) Data-plane (shared NAT appliances / managed gateway)
   - NAT appliances (cloud-managed NAT gateway or self-hosted egress proxies) configured with multiple public IPs and port mapping.
   - A sidecar agent (or iptables management daemon) that requests a port-range from Allocator and applies per-tenant SNAT rules.

3) Accounting/charging pipeline
   - Periodic batch job consumes audit logs and per-tenant report-egress events to create invoiceable line items and chargeback metrics.

Key entities and invariants
---------------------------
- Tenant: (org_id, project_id, env) string.
- PortRange: contiguous ephemeral TCP/UDP port block (e.g., 1000 ports).
- CreditBucket: egress bytes reserved per tenant per billing-period.
- Invariant: port assignment is unique per NAT IP + port range to avoid collisions.

Operational SLOs and thresholds
-------------------------------
- Allocator API availability: 99.95% (global)
- Allocation latency: 95th percentile < 300ms for allocate_port_range API call
- Egress metering latency: events visible in Prometheus within 60s of occurrence
- Billing accuracy: <0.5% drift versus provider egress billing for aggregated daily totals

Prometheus metrics to publish
----------------------------
- allocator.allocations_total (labels: tenant, region, outcome)
- allocator.allocation_latency_seconds_bucket (labels: tenant, region)
- allocator.egress_report_bytes_total (labels: tenant, region, ip)
- allocator.quota_exhausted_count (labels: tenant, region)
- allocator.audit_events_published_total

Terraform / IaC patterns (example)
---------------------------------
This section shows the recommended module layout and a compact example. The actual module shipped in our org follows the pattern below and lives in the terraform monorepo (module path: modules/egress-allocator).

Table: Module inputs and defaults
- name: module_input - description: default
- name: region - Target region for NAT pool - default: us-west-2
- name: nat_pool_size - Number of public IPs in pool - default: "4"
- name: port_block_size - Ports reserved per tenant - default: "1024"
- name: db_connection_string - Allocator DB - default: ""

Example usage snippet (HCL)
- module "egress_allocator" {
-   source = "git::ssh://git@repo/modules/egress-allocator.git//cloud-account?ref=v1.3.0"
-   region = "us-west-2"
-   nat_pool_size = 8
-   port_block_size = 2048
- }

CI/CD pattern and gates
----------------------
1. PRs to module require: unit tests, terratest integration (ephemeral infra), and a smoke deploy using a sandbox account.
2. Promotion pipeline (canary -> staging -> prod) enforced with a feature flag for new allocator behavior.
3. Runbook step must be referenced in the promotion merge commit message and checked off in CI before promotion.

Reconciliation and data integrity checks (daily job)
---------------------------------------------------
- Verify no overlapping port ranges across all NAT public IPs.
- Compare aggregated egress bytes by provider API vs our aggregated credit events; emit drift alert if >0.5%.
- Validate that credit buckets with churn >20% get a rebalance recommendation.

On-call runbook (Pager triggers and immediate response)
-----------------------------------------------------
Pager conditions (alerts and actions):
1) allocator.api_5xx_rate > 1% sustained for 3m\   - Action: page oncall infra-engineer, acknowledge in alert system, increase allocator replicas (k8s HPA), check DB connectivity, fail open to conservative default port assignment if DB unreachable.
2) allocation_latency_seconds (p95) > 300ms for 5m\   - Action: page infra oncall, check service heap/cpu, recent deploys, scale horizontally, sample trace for slow SQL queries.
3) quota_exhausted_count spikes for a tenant by >100x baseline\   - Action: notify customer-success and rate-limit tenant via policy; open emergency ticket for billing team to reconcile credits.
4) egress_drift_alert (daily) > 0.5%\   - Action: run reconciliation script (see Appendix), escalate to finance if persistent for 48 hours.

Step-by-step: Recovering from allocator DB outage
1) Identify: Pager shows allocator.api_5xx_rate alert.
2) Quick mitigation: enable local ephemeral port-assignment fallback in sidecars (config flag: ALLOCATOR_FALLBACK=local_range).
3) If DB is write-unavailable, configure Allocator to serve read-only and queue writes to a durable S3 buffer (feature toggle).
4) After DB restore: drain buffer consumer job to apply queued events; run data integrity checks; reconcile drift.
5) Postmortem: publish root cause, timeline, and the corrective IaC change if rollout exposed a deployment race.

Safety & gradual rollout checklist
--------------------------------
- Canary rollout to 3 tenants with high egress but non-critical SLAs for 72h.
- Monitor top-10 egress tenants for port collisions and latency.
- Enable crediting in billing pipeline for canary tenants only.
- After 72h with no anomalies, switch on broader promotion with 24h ramp steps.

Ownership and responsibilities
------------------------------
- Allocator control-plane: eng-infra/networking (primary), with oncall rotation in infra-team.
- NAT appliance configuration: cloud-accounts team (ownership of cloud account and VPC).
- Billing pipeline: finance + eng-data for reconciliation jobs.
- Terraform modules & CI: eng-infra/ci-cd team (module authors), with CD pipeline enforced in repo.

Appendix A — Debugging commands and snippets
-------------------------------------------
- Check allocations for tenant (SQL): SELECT tenant, ip, port_start, port_end, assigned_at FROM allocations WHERE tenant = 'org-123' ORDER BY assigned_at DESC LIMIT 50;
- Reconciliation helper (python psuedocode):
-   requests.get("https://allocator/api/v1/reconcile?period=24h")

Appendix B — Example sidecar config (yaml)
- sidecar:
-   allocator_endpoint: "https://allocator.internal:443"
-   request_timeout: 2s
-   port_block_size: 2048
-   fallback_mode: "disabled"

Appendix C — Audit and compliance notes
- Audit logs: all allocate_port_range and refresh_quota API calls are written to s3://redwood-allocator-audit/{region}/YYYY/MM/DD with per-event signed checksums.
- Retention: 365 days cold, 7 years for restricted tenants.

Appendix D — Known limitations and future work
- UDP-heavy workloads with unpredictable ephemeral port churn may require per-flow NAT session tracking; currently unsupported.
- Plan to introduce token-bucket soft throttles to smooth bursty egress and avoid hard throttling.

Change history
--------------
- 2025-09-12: Initial design and runbook (Priya Natarajan).
- 2025-11-03: Added Terraform promotion gating and reconciliation job (Carlos Mendes).
- 2026-02-15: Clarified DB outage fallback and retention policy (Ava Chen).

Related diagrams and attachments
--------------------------------
- Diagram: allocator-sequence-diagram.drawio (stored in confluence attachments).
- Terraform module source: git@repo:infra/modules/egress-allocator.git (internal).

Questions / feedback
--------------------
For questions about rollout timeline or tenant mapping, ping @priya.natarajan or add comments on this page.

title: Private upgrades: rollback loop (RRB-17)  Detection, workaround, and fix
preview:
# Overview
This page documents the "rollback loop" failure mode in Redwood Private upgrades tracked as **RRB-17** (also described as "stuck rollback" or "rollback keeps re-triggering").

This is customer-impacting because the installer can retry rollbacks indefinitely, leaving the cluster in a non-converged state.

# Symptoms
- Console/installer status shows **Rollback in progress** for >30 minutes
- Installer controller logs repeat messages like "scheduling rollback retry"
- No forward progress after multiple rollback attempts

# Affected versions
- Observed in: 2.9.0 upgrades from 2.8.0
- Potentially affects other upgrade paths when CRD migration partially applies

# Detection
## Log indicators
Search installer-controller logs for repeated sequences:
- `status=FAILED`
- `scheduling rollback retry`
- references to `installer-rollback-lock`

## Operational threshold
Treat as rollback loop if either:
1) >3 rollback retries in 10 minutes, or
2) rollback has been in-progress >30 minutes with no state change

# Workaround (customer-safe)
**Pre-checks:**
- Confirm you are operating in the customers Private cluster
- Confirm namespace: `redwood-private`

**Commands:**
1) Delete the rollback lock ConfigMap:
   `kubectl -n redwood-private delete cm installer-rollback-lock`
2) Restart controller:
   `kubectl -n redwood-private rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`

**Warnings:**
- Do not delete arbitrary ConfigMaps. The name must match exactly: `installer-rollback-lock`.
- If deletion fails due to RBAC, coordinate with the customers cluster admin.

# Root cause (RRB-17)
Rollback cleanup hook can exit non-zero before clearing the rollback lock. The controller interprets rollback as failed and schedules another rollback attempt, which sees the existing lock and repeats.

# Fix
Planned/implemented changes:
- Best-effort lock clearing even if migration revert fails
- Lock TTL + loop detection to stop infinite retries and surface an actionable status
- Metric: `installer.rollback.loop_detected` and alerting in SRE dashboards

# References
- Jira: INT-330
- Slack: #incidents (INC-2147)
- Customer comms: coordinate with CS before suggesting kubectl commands

title: Kernel variant split-testing and safety contract
preview:
Summary

This document defines the safety contract, telemetry requirements, and a step-by-step split-testing procedure for rolling out new kernel variants in the serving runtime. It is intended for runtime engineers, release owners, and on-call SREs. The goal is to enable controlled experiments that validate latency, correctness, and resource-stability properties while providing deterministic rollback triggers.

Goals

- Allow safe A/B (split) testing of kernel variants (e.g., tiled-attention-v2, quantized-matmul-approx, async-dispatch) against production workloads.
- Define automated and human-reviewed rollback criteria that preserve customer SLOs and prevent resource exhaustion.
- Standardize telemetry and alerting required to make split decisions reproducible and auditable.
- Provide a compact, executable checklist for runbook-driven canary experiments.

Scope and non-goals

- Scope: kernel-level variants and scheduler policy plugins deployed through the runtime-coordinator. Applies to hosted and dedicated environments; private deployments may adopt with adjustments for networked telemetry.
- Non-goals: model topology changes, dataset-level model weight variations, or hardware firmware updates (those follow separate change control).

Safety contract (mandatory)

All kernel variant rollouts must satisfy the following contract before traffic is diverted beyond a baseline canary:

1) Binary and configuration signoff: build artifact signed and checksum verified.
2) Unit/regression tests: pass the kernel microbench suite (latency delta < +5% for median on representative microbench) and correctness tests (numerical tolerance/bitmask pass).
3) Telemetry gates: new telemetry keys instrumented (see Telemetry section) and validated in dev shadow traffic.
4) Resource guardrails: per-GPU memory delta must be within +/- 8% of baseline under synthetic steady-state; no additional persistent allocations.

Telemetry (required signals)

All kernel variants must emit the following metrics to the observability-bridge with the specified cardinality and dimensions:

- kernel.exec.latency.p50/p95/p99 (ns) — dims: model, sequence_len_bucket, cohort, kernel_variant
- kernel.exec.gpu_memory.delta (MB) — dims: node_id, kernel_variant
- kernel.exec.error.rate (count / 1m) — dims: error_code, kernel_variant
- kernel.exec.preemptions (count) — dims: kernel_variant, priority_class
- kernel.exec.cpu-offload.time (ms) — dims: kernel_variant, host_type

Validation requirements

- Telemetry ingest must be validated end-to-end for a sample of traffic before any gradual ramp: test query must appear within 90s in the monitoring pipeline and show correct tags.
- Aggregation freshness: p50/p95 must update within 120s for canary cohorts.

Split-testing procedure (step-by-step)

Preflight (offline)

1) Build and sign kernel artifact. Record SHA256 in release notes.
2) Run microbench suite (baseline vs candidate) across sequence length buckets: 8, 32, 128, 512. Attach results to experiment ticket.
3) Validate correctness test harness (token-level diffs on deterministic prompts).

Canary (controlled production)

1) Canary cohort creation: create a dedicated cohort named cvt-YYYYMMDD-<short> limited to 0.5% traffic or up to 3 clients, whichever is smaller.
2) Deploy kernel variant to a subset of dispatcher shards via runtime-coordinator with flag kernel_variant=candidate; ensure a rollback flag is present in the same change set.
3) Shadow traffic warmup: mirror production requests for 10 minutes to validate end-to-end trace tagging and K/V cache behavior.
4) Ramp schedule: 0.5% -> 2% (30m) -> 5% (1h) -> 10% (2h). Pause at each step for at least one monitoring evaluation interval (see Monitoring checks).

Monitoring checks (automated)

At each ramp step the following automated checks run and must all pass for the ramp to continue:

- Latency: p95(candidate)/p95(baseline) <= 1.15 for the canary cohort across sequence_len_bucket median.
- Error rate: error.rate candidate <= baseline + 0.05% absolute and not > 2x baseline for any error_code.
- Memory: rolling 5m mean gpu_memory.delta within +/- 8% of baseline.
- Preemptions: preemptions increase <= 20% relative baseline.
- SLO proxy: request-level SLO (90th percentile latency for end-to-end) must not regress by more than 8%.

Monitoring checks (human review)

- After the 5% and 10% steps, an engineer from the owning team must perform a manual review of traces, histograms, and example failure traces. Approvers: the release owner and at least one peer reviewer from kernel-scheduling.

Rollback and abort criteria (automated immediate abort)

Any of the following triggers an immediate automated rollback to the previous variant and pages on-call:

1) p99 latency increase > 35% over baseline for >3 minutes.
2) absolute error rate increase of >0.2% and sustained for 2 minutes.
3) GPU memory delta > +15% for any node for 60s (indicates potential leak).
4) Sudden spike in preemptions causing >30% throughput drop for >2 minutes.

Abort (human-initiated)

- A release owner or SRE may abort the rollout at any time based on qualitative evidence in traces or customer reports. Document rationale in the experiment ticket.

Postmortem and artifact capture

- On any rollback, capture the following artifacts automatically into the experiment ticket: kernel logs (last 1h), kernel telemetry snapshots, runtime-coordinator change set, and example trace IDs (at least 5).
- Run the failure triage checklist (see Triage section) within 24 hours.

Configuration matrix (example)

| Parameter | Baseline value | Candidate constraints | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| initial_cohort_size | 0.5% | 0.5% | Small exposure for first validation |
| max_cohort_size | 10% | <= 10% | Default upper bound for automated ramp |
| max_ramp_duration | 4h | <= 8h | Total time from canary start to max_cohort_size |
| memory_delta_threshold | +/-8% | candidate must be within | Guardrail measured on synthetic steady-state |
| error_rate_abs_threshold | +0.05% | smaller is better | Absolute increase over baseline |

Example CLI and config snippet

YAML used by runtime-coordinator to create a canary: 

```yaml
canary: 
  name: cvt-20260215-async-tiling
  kernel_variant: tiled-attention-v2
  cohort: cvt-20260215
  initial_weight: 0.005
  ramp_steps:
    - weight: 0.02
      duration_minutes: 30
    - weight: 0.05
      duration_minutes: 60
    - weight: 0.10
      duration_minutes: 120
  rollback_policy: automated
  telemetry_tags: ["kernel_variant", "cohort", "release_owner"]
```

CLI example (ops):

$ rictl canary apply -f cvt-20260215-async-tiling.yaml
$ rictl canary status cvt-20260215-async-tiling

Triage checklist (on rollback)

1) Confirm rollback completed and previous variant active.
2) Collect last 60m of kernel logs and telemetry snapshots.
3) Identify top 5 trace IDs correlated with p99 latency or errors.
4) Run microbench candidate vs baseline with match of failing sequence lengths.
5) Decide next step: revert permanently, patch and retry, or abort experiment.

Ownership and escalation

- Release owner: assign in the experiment ticket (engineer responsible for rollout).
- Kernel-scheduling on-call: first responder for automated pages triggered by rollback.
- Runtime SRE: responsible for cross-cluster mitigations and capacity actions.

Rollout signoff checklist (pre-production to production)

- Dev and CI: microbench and unit tests green.
- Observability: telemetry keys instrumented and pipe validated.
- Capacity: synthetic steady-state memory validation complete.
- Security: artifact signed and signed-by key verified.
- Approvals: release owner + kernel-scheduling peer reviewer approve in ticket.

References and related pages

- Scheduler policy language and declarative profiles (see internal contract)
- Observability bridge telemetry tagging guide
- Runtime-coordinator deployment and change set conventions

Appendix: example alerts to create for canary cohorts

- alert: CanaryKernelLatencyP99Spike
  expr: increase(kernel_exec_latency_p99{cohort="cvt-20260215"}[2m]) > 0.35
  for: 3m
  labels: {severity: page}

- alert: CanaryKernelMemoryLeakCandidate
  expr: kernel_exec_gpu_memory_delta_bytes{kernel_variant="tiled-attention-v2"} > 15728640
  for: 1m
  labels: {severity: page}

Notes and rationale

This spec attempts to codify pragmatic thresholds identified from historical regressions: latency tail behavior, memory leaks manifesting under steady-state, and preemption amplification when background CPU offload patterns change. Thresholds prioritize customer-facing SLO preservation over incremental throughput gains.

If changes to the contract are proposed, they must be reviewed by kernel-scheduling and runtime-sre and published with a changelog entry and example experiment ticket templates.

Document history

- 2025-11-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-01-10: Added memory guardrail guidance and CLI examples (review by Evan Clark).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized automated rollback thresholds and published.

| memory_delta_threshold | +/-8% | candidate must be within | Guardrail measured on synthetic steady-state |
| error_rate_abs_threshold | +0.05% | smaller is better | Absolute increase over baseline |

Example CLI and config snippet

YAML used by runtime-coordinator to create a canary: 

```yaml
canary: 
  name: cvt-20260215-async-tiling
  kernel_variant: tiled-attention-v2
  cohort: cvt-20260215
  initial_weight: 0.005
  ramp_steps:
    - weight: 0.02
      duration_minutes: 30
    - weight: 0.05
      duration_minutes: 60
    - weight: 0.10
      duration_minutes: 120
  rollback_policy: automated
  telemetry_tags: ["kernel_variant", "cohort", "release_owner"]
```

CLI example (ops):

$ rictl canary apply -f cvt-20260215-async-tiling.yaml
$ rictl canary status cvt-20260215-async-tiling

Triage checklist (on rollback)

1) Confirm rollback completed and previous variant active.
2) Collect last 60m of kernel logs and telemetry snapshots.
3) Identify top 5 trace IDs correlated with p99 latency or errors.
4) Run microbench candidate vs baseline with match of failing sequence lengths.
5) Decide next step: revert permanently, patch and retry, or abort experiment.

Ownership and escalation

- Release owner: assign in the experiment ticket (engineer responsible for rollout).
- Kernel-scheduling on-call: first responder for automated pages triggered by rollback.
- Runtime SRE: responsible for cross-cluster mitigations and capacity actions.

Rollout signoff checklist (pre-production to production)

- Dev and CI: microbench and unit tests green.
- Observability: telemetry keys instrumented and pipe validated.
- Capacity: synthetic steady-state memory validation complete.
- Security: artifact signed and signed-by key verified.
- Approvals: release owner + kernel-scheduling peer reviewer approve in ticket.

References and related pages

- Scheduler policy language and declarative profiles (see internal contract)
- Observability bridge telemetry tagging guide
- Runtime-coordinator deployment and change set conventions

Appendix: example alerts to create for canary cohorts

- alert: CanaryKernelLatencyP99Spike
  expr: increase(kernel_exec_latency_p99{cohort="cvt-20260215"}[2m]) > 0.35
  for: 3m
  labels: {severity: page}

- alert: CanaryKernelMemoryLeakCandidate
  expr: kernel_exec_gpu_memory_delta_bytes{kernel_variant="tiled-attention-v2"} > 15728640
  for: 1m
  labels: {severity: page}

Notes and rationale

This spec attempts to codify pragmatic thresholds identified from historical regressions: latency tail behavior, memory leaks manifesting under steady-state, and preemption amplification when background CPU offload patterns change. Thresholds prioritize customer-facing SLO preservation over incremental throughput gains.

If changes to the contract are proposed, they must be reviewed by kernel-scheduling and runtime-sre and published with a changelog entry and example experiment ticket templates.

Document history

- 2025-11-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-01-10: Added memory guardrail guidance and CLI examples (review by Evan Clark).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized automated rollback thresholds and published.

title: Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan
preview:
Summary
This page defines the Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan: a standardized, automated validation and exercise suite for verifying that service contracts (API request/response schemas, streaming semantics, model-variant compatibility) survive realistic failure and degradation modes, and that graceful-degradation policies keep downstream applications safe.

Purpose and scope
- Purpose: Ensure changes to APIs, model variants, runtime kernels, or infra do not cause silent client breakage or unacceptable user-facing quality regressions when the platform degrades (capacity loss, quantization fallbacks, network partitions, high tail latency).
- Scope: Applies to: Redwood public REST/streaming API endpoints, model-serving runtime variants (fp16, int8, quantized kernels), routing/fallback policies, and platform-side contract transforms (stream chunking, partial result checkpointing). Excludes: third-party managed endpoints and external partner SDKs.

Key definitions
- Contract survivability: The ability of a client and service to continue to communicate successfully (with defined semantics) despite service degradation, version differences, or partial failures.
- Graceful degradation: Controlled reduction of service fidelity (e.g., shorter max tokens, fallback to smaller model, send partial results) instead of outright failures.
- Degradation threshold: A measurable limit (latency, error rate, quality metric) that triggers a degradation policy.

Goals and non-goals
- Goals:
  1) Provide a runnable test matrix that exercises all supported compatibility modes and failure profiles.
  2) Verify observability coverage (telemetry, logs, traces) for each degraded path.
  3) Declare pass/fail criteria tied to SLOs, quality delta limits, and error budgets.
  4) Integrate tests into CI gating and canary pipelines for releases and infra upgrades.
- Non-goals: This document does not specify model retraining, per-customer contractual SLAs, or security certification steps.

Test strategy overview
- Three layers of validation:
  1) Contract unit checks: static schema validation + golden response matching. Fast, runs on PR.
  2) Survivability scenarios: synthetic fault injection (GPU drain, quantization fallback, kernel failover, partial KV cache loss) executed in staging clusters. Medium time-to-run.
  3) Degradation chaos+load: long-running experiments under realistic traffic mixes (chat, embeddings, reranking) to measure quality drift, latency tail behavior, and cascading effects.

Compatibility and test matrix
- Dimensions to cover (matrix columns): client API version, streaming vs non-streaming, response schema version, model binary variant (float16, int8, bfloat16), kernel family, region, and tenant isolation mode (hosted/dedicated/private).
- Example matrix rows (textual table):
  | Scenario | Client v1 | Streaming | Model var | Kernel | Failure injected | Expected survival behavior |
  |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
  | Short-circuit fallback | v1 | streaming | fp16 -> int8 | fused-attn-v2 | quantize fallback during generation | Continue streaming with truncated tokens + warning header |
  | Partial KV loss | v2 | non-stream | bfloat16 | kv-cache-v1 | KV cache eviction on half of shards | Fall back to recompute prefix with <=30% latency penalty; maintain correctness |

Pass/fail criteria and thresholds
- Latency SLOs (staging baseline vs experiment):
  - 50th percentile: not > 1.25x baseline
  - 95th percentile: not > 1.5x baseline
  - 99th percentile: not > 2.0x baseline or absolute 5s (whichever is lower)
- Error budget and rates:
  - Service errors (5xx): experiment must not exceed 0.2% absolute increase over baseline in a 30-minute window.
  - Client-visible schema errors: zero tolerated for contract-major-mode changes.
- Quality delta (for generative outputs):
  - Automated evals on canary prompt set: BLEU/ROUGE/Hallucination proxy delta must be <= 5% relative degradation for critical prompt subset; for non-critical prompts allow up to 10% with explicit stakeholder signoff.
- Observability completeness:
  - All degradations must emit a structured event: event_type=degradation_experiment, failure_mode, affected_model_variant, request_id. Missing events are a hard fail.

Test cases (representative)
1) Quantize-Fallback-Stream-Test
Preconditions: staging cluster with both fp16 and int8 model families deployed behind routing policy.
Steps:
   1. Start a 10-min steady streaming workload (chat prompts at 50 req/s) using streaming client v1.
   2. Mid-run, trigger quantization fallback by toggling node-level kernel preference (simulate degraded GPU memory).
   3. Monitor stream continuity, chunking headers, and final truncation messages.
   Expected result: clients receive valid stream chunks, final message contains a header 'x-degradation-warning: quantize-fallback' and token truncation does not exceed configured limit (default: 128 tokens).

2) KV-Cache-Partial-Evict-Test
Preconditions: KV cache warm with repeated prefix workloads.
Steps:
   1. Run synthetic repeat-prefix traffic at 200 rps for 20 minutes.
   2. Evict KV cache on 40% of serving shards.
   3. Measure latency delta and validate correctness via deterministic checksum of first 64 tokens on a reserved prompt set.
   Expected result: deterministic checksum match, p95 latency within threshold, eviction logged per-shard.

3) Streaming-Edge-Chunk-Reassembly
Steps:
   1. Induce network jitter and drop 1 in 50 stream packets at the edge proxy.
   2. Verify client resume semantics: partial-result-checkpointing token offsets allow resumption without reissuing entire prompt.
   Expected: successful resume in <= 2s and no duplicate tokens.

Automation and CI integration
- Unit contract checks: run as part of PR pipeline via 'make contract-unit-checks'. Fail fast.
- Survivability smoke: nightly GitHub Action suite running core scenarios (quantize fallback, KV eviction) against ephemeral staging clusters. Tag results and publish to the release dashboard.
- Release gating: any change to routing/fallback policy or kernel family must run the full survivability scenarios in the release canary phase. A failed scenario blocks rollout by automated policy in the deployment pipeline.

CI snippets and examples
- Example CI job (YAML):
  jobs:
    survivability-smoke:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - name: Run quantize fallback smoke
          run: ./tools/survive/run_quantize_smoke.sh --cluster staging --duration 600

- Example curl for schema validation (non-streaming):
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
    -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" 
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
    -d '{"model":"rwd-7b-fp16","prompt":"What is the capital of France?","max_tokens":32}'

Observability and telemetry requirements
- Telemetry events must include the following structured fields: service, request_id, client_version, model_variant, kernel_family, failure_mode, degradation_policy, latency_ms, snippet_hash.
- Trace spans: experiments must produce a top-level span named 'degradation-experiment' that includes child spans for 'routing', 'cache-lookup', 'kernel-select', and 'fallback-action'.
- Dashboards and alerts: Create a dedicated dashboard 'Contract Survivability' with panels: p95 latency by scenario, quality-delta heatmap, structured event rate by failure_mode. Alert rules: when structured event missing for >10 runs raise severity P2.

Rollout, canaries and rollback policy
- Canary policy for runtime/kernel changes:
  - Stage 1 (micro-canary): 0.1% traffic, run contract unit checks and survivability smoke for 30 minutes.
  - Stage 2 (regional canary): 2% traffic for 2 hours, run degradation chaos tests in parallel.
  - Stage 3 (ramped): 10% for 24 hours, monitor quality delta and SLOs.
- Automatic rollback triggers:
  - p95 latency > 1.5x baseline AND p99 > 2.0x baseline for 5 consecutive minutes.
  - Any client-visible schema error for requests in the canary cohort.

Ownership and runbooks
- Test harness owners: Eng QA (primary), Eng Serving Runtime (secondary).
- On-call escalation: If a gating test fails during a release, pager to runtime-oncall team with tag 'survivability-block'.
- Runbook pointers:
  - /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/eviction-and-recoveries
  - /confluence/eng-infra/gpu-fleet-and-capacity/gpu-node-autoscaling-eviction-playbook-2026

Appendix: example evaluation config (YAML snippet)
- prompts_file: 'canary_prompts_v2.txt'
- metrics:
  - name: p95_latency_ms
    threshold: 1500
  - name: quality_delta_pct
    threshold: 5
- failure_modes: ['quantize_fallback','kv_evict','network_jitter']
- run_duration_minutes: 30

Change history and review notes
- 2025-11-06: initial draft authored by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-12-10: added automated gating rules and CI job examples (review by Carlos Mendez).
- 2026-02-14: expanded observability fields and added rollback triggers (approved by Asha Rao).

References and related guidance
- See: request-fanout-attenuation-and-resilience-guardrails-2026 (performance standards).
- See: model-slicing-and-fallback-routing-guidelines-2026 (model-serving standards).
- See: sequence-length-aware-kernel-selection-guidelines-2026 (runtime architecture).

Contact
- For questions or runbook updates, contact: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai or the eng-qa Slack channel (#eng-qa).

title: Field Acceptance & Safety Certification Playbook for Offline (Air‑gapped) Deploys
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines the field acceptance and safety certification criteria for offline / air‑gapped private deployments of the Redwood runtime. It is intended for delivery engineers, customer site operators, and SREs who perform the final acceptance verification and produce evidence for compliance packages.

Scope

- Applies to: on‑prem or isolated VPC deployments where control‑plane connectivity is intentionally restricted (air‑gapped or dark site).
- Excludes: hosted Redwood API, fully connected Dedicated deployments.
- Goal: provide a deterministic, repeatable test suite and sign‑off template to ensure the deployment meets safety, availability, and traceability requirements prior to customer handover.

Quick summary of acceptance outcomes (must all PASS to sign off)

1) Functional health: all core services report healthy and APIs respond to local queries.
2) Safety resilience: node restart, power loss, and network partition recovery tested.
3) Observability: required telemetry artifacts produced and retained locally as evidence.
4) Security baseline: image signatures validated, KMS/HSM handshake validated (when applicable), minimal privileged access verified.
5) Performance sanity: representative inference workload meets configured SLOs for p50/p95/p99.

Roles and responsibilities

- Delivery Engineer (owner): run acceptance suite, collate artifacts, mark preliminary PASS/FAIL.
- Customer Site Operator: provide physical access and power/networking support, verify facility safety steps.
- SRE Field Reviewer: audit evidence, approve final certification.
- Product Security: validate signature / KMS evidence if restricted.

Prerequisites (before starting the playbook)

- Images delivered and verified by checksum/signature. Provide signed manifest (sha256) in /opt/redwood/manifests/.
- Hardware included in the delivery manifest and power/cooling validated by customer on site.
- Basic network cabling and switch configuration completed. Out‑of‑band management (BMC) reachable from operator network.
- Local admin user created: redwood-admin (sudo without password restricted by time window).

Required artifacts to produce during the run (evidence bundle)

- signed-manifest.txt (sha256 checksums + signer identity)
- health-checks.json (service health endpoints snapshot)
- telemetry-snapshot.tar.gz (metrics/ logs / traces for the test window)
- firmware-and-drivers.txt (nvidia-smi / lspci / dmidecode outputs)
- power-fail-recovery.log (step trace for power tests)
- acceptance-signoff.md (signed by delivery engineer and customer operator)

Service names referenced in this playbook

- redwood-runtime.service (primary inference runtime)
- redwood-agent.service (node agent handling KV cache and local routing)
- control-plane-proxy (local proxy for private control plane connectivity, may be disabled in true airgap)
- kv-cache-daemon (local at-rest cache service)
- node-watchdog.service (operator safety watchdog for unattended reboots)

Acceptance tests (step‑by‑step)

1) Baseline verification (10–20 minutes)
   a) Verify signed images: sha256sum -c /opt/redwood/manifests/signed-manifest.txt
      - Expected: all OK. Document signer and checksum.
   b) Validate service units: systemctl status redwood-runtime redwood-agent node-watchdog
      - Expected: active (running) for each. Capture `systemctl show -p ActiveState,MainPID` output.
   c) Health endpoint checks: curl --unix-socket /var/run/redwood.sock http://localhost/health | jq .
      - Expected: {"overall":"ok","components":[...] }

2) Functional smoke (20–40 minutes)
   a) Local inference request: use the bundled smoke client. Example: /opt/redwood/bin/smoke-client --model local-l2 --prompt "Hello" --max-tokens 16
      - Expected: valid token stream within 200ms median for small model; capture output (stdout) to health-checks.json.
   b) KV cache warmup: run 100 short requests and confirm cache hit rate > 60%. Use /opt/redwood/bin/cache-stats --window 60s.

3) Performance sanity (1–2 hours depending on hardware)
   a) Run the workload profile supplied for the customer (profile file location: /opt/redwood/tests/profiles/<customer>-baseline.yaml).
   b) Observe p50/p95/p99 latency and GPU utilization. SLO targets for acceptance (configurable per contract):
      - p50 inference latency: <= 35ms (small L1 models)
      - p95 inference latency: <= 90ms
      - p99 inference latency: <= 250ms
      - throughput stability: <10% jitter over 15 min window
   c) Collect metrics: curl http://localhost:9090/metrics | grep -E "(inference_latency|gpu_util|cache_hit)" > metrics.txt

4) Safety & resilience tests (sequential; coordinate with customer operator)
   Note: these tests intentionally induce disruptions. Ensure customer signs the safety waiver section in acceptance-signoff.md before proceeding.

   a) Graceful node restart
      - Command: sudo systemctl restart redwood-runtime && sudo journalctl -u redwood-runtime --since "5m" --no-pager > restart-recovery.log
      - Acceptance: runtime resumes within configured restart window (default 60s) and recovers KV state; no data corruption in local cache.

   b) Simulated power failure (controlled)
      - Procedure: use remote PDU to cut power for a single node for 30s, then restore.
      - Acceptance: node boots to operational state within site SLA (default 10 minutes), node-watchdog performed expected reboot, logs captured to power-fail-recovery.log.

   c) Network partition/failover
      - Procedure: isolate tenant network from site management network for 90s. If control-plane-proxy is enabled, confirm the runtime can continue processing local queued requests and that fallback policies engage.
      - Acceptance: no more than 1% request failure during partition; queued requests are replayed; alerts fired into local alerting backend. Capture partition-recovery.log.

   d) Disk corruption detection (read-only simulation)
      - Procedure: mount a loop device with read-only flag to simulate partial disk failure for /var/lib/redwood/cache.
      - Acceptance: runtime detects read-only filesystem, transitions affected services to degraded mode and emits evidence to health-checks.json.

5) Security & image attestation tests (30 minutes)
   a) Verify image signature with customer KMS/HSM or provided signer. Command example: cosign verify-blob --key /opt/redwood/keys/cosign.pub /opt/redwood/images/redwood-runtime.tar.gz
      - Expected: verification success.
   b) RBAC smoke: attempt an elevated operation from non-admin account; expect denial. Capture sudoers and audit logs.

Observability and evidence capture (always collect)

- Metrics snapshot: /opt/redwood/bin/dump-metrics --output telemetry-snapshot/metrics.prom
- Log snapshot: tar -czf telemetry-snapshot/logs.tar.gz /var/log/redwood/* (include system/journal logs for the test window)
- Traces: if local jaeger-enabled, export span collection: /opt/redwood/bin/export-spans --window 30m --out telemetry-snapshot/traces.jaeger
- Attachments: hardware outputs: nvidia-smi -q > firmware-and-drivers.txt; dmidecode > firmware-and-drivers.txt (append).

Minimum telemetry retention for certification: 90 days local + offsite transfer instructions included in runbook if customer requests.

Failure modes and rollback criteria

- If more than one of (power fail, network partition, disk failure) causes permanent data loss or unrecoverable runtime state, abort acceptance.
- If performance SLOs fail for p99 by >40% consistently after tuning, classify as FAIL and open a ticket to Eng Platform for remediation with priority P1.
- For security verification failures (image signature mismatch, unexpected privileged processes), stop and revert to verified images.

Safe rollback checklist (if required)

1) Quiesce incoming traffic at local load balancer.
2) Snapshot local KV state (if possible): /opt/redwood/bin/kv-snapshot --out /tmp/kv-snap-<ts>.tar.gz
3) Restore verified image from signed-manifest to node: /opt/redwood/bin/apply-image --manifest signed-manifest.txt
4) Bring up services in dependency order: redwood-agent -> kv-cache-daemon -> redwood-runtime.
5) Re-run smoke tests and attach artefacts.

Sign‑off template (fill in fields, append signatures)

- Deployment id: <deployment-id>
- Customer: <customer-name>
- Site: <site-location>
- Hardware kit id: <kit-id>
- Delivery Engineer: <name> (signature)
- Customer Operator: <name> (signature)
- SRE Reviewer: <name> (signature)
- Acceptance result: PASS | FAIL
- Notes: freeform field for deviations, mitigations, follow-ups.

Common troubleshooting commands and expected artifacts

- Check GPU: nvidia-smi -q > /tmp/gpu.txt (verify driver >= 515.0)
- Runtime logs: sudo journalctl -u redwood-runtime -o short-precise --since "1 hour" > /tmp/runtime.log
- KV cache status: /opt/redwood/bin/cache-stats --json > /tmp/cache.json
- Heartbeat: curl -s --unix-socket /var/run/redwood.sock http://localhost/health | jq .uptime

Safety and physical site considerations (non‑technical)

- Ensure qualified electrical staff performs power PDU tests.
- Follow local permits and safety rules for on-site battery/UPS maintenance.
- All induced failure tests must have a signed waiver before execution.

Appendix A — Example commands (copyable)

1) Verify manifest: sha256sum -c /opt/redwood/manifests/signed-manifest.txt > /tmp/manifest-verify.out 2>&1
2) Health check: curl --unix-socket /var/run/redwood.sock http://localhost/health | jq . > /tmp/health.json
3) Export metrics: curl http://localhost:9090/metrics | grep inference_latency > /tmp/inference-metrics.txt
4) Create evidence bundle: tar -czf /tmp/acceptance-bundle-<deployment-id>.tgz signed-manifest.txt /tmp/health.json telemetry-snapshot/* firmware-and-drivers.txt power-fail-recovery.log

Appendix B — Minimum hardware/firmware table (acceptance baseline)

- Minimum GPU driver: 515.0
- Minimum CUDA toolkit: 11.7
- Disk: NVMe >= 1TB per node (mirrored or enterprise grade)
- RAM: >= 256GB per inference node for large models; smaller models may accept 128GB
- BIOS: latest vendor-recommended revision; secure boot optional but recommended
- TPM/KMS recommended for image attestation

References and related runbooks

- Ephemeral boot and attestation (onprem packaging): /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/ephemeral-boot-and-attestation-playbook-2026
- Field operational runway and safety windows: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/field-operational-runway-and-safety-windows-2026
- Controlled rollforward & rollback guidance: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/upgrade-and-rollback/orchestrated-rollforward-and-customer-safeguards-2026

Change history

- 2025-07-10: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2025-09-02: Added performance sanity thresholds and KV cache guidance (reviewer: Carlos Mendez).
- 2026-02-12: Updated safety test windows and added evidence retention guidance (reviewer: Aisha Rahman).

End of document
text:
Overview

This playbook defines the field acceptance and safety certification criteria for offline / air‑gapped private deployments of the Redwood runtime. It is intended for delivery engineers, customer site operators, and SREs who perform the final acceptance verification and produce evidence for compliance packages.

Scope

- Applies to: on‑prem or isolated VPC deployments where control‑plane connectivity is intentionally restricted (air‑gapped or dark site).
- Excludes: hosted Redwood API, fully connected Dedicated deployments.
- Goal: provide a deterministic, repeatable test suite and sign‑off template to ensure the deployment meets safety, availability, and traceability requirements prior to customer handover.

Quick summary of acceptance outcomes (must all PASS to sign off)

1) Functional health: all core services report healthy and APIs respond to local queries.
2) Safety resilience: node restart, power loss, and network partition recovery tested.
3) Observability: required telemetry artifacts produced and retained locally as evidence.
4) Security baseline: image signatures validated, KMS/HSM handshake validated (when applicable), minimal privileged access verified.
5) Performance sanity: representative inference workload meets configured SLOs for p50/p95/p99.

Roles and responsibilities

- Delivery Engineer (owner): run acceptance suite, collate artifacts, mark preliminary PASS/FAIL.
- Customer Site Operator: provide physical access and power/networking support, verify facility safety steps.
- SRE Field Reviewer: audit evidence, approve final certification.
- Product Security: validate signature / KMS evidence if restricted.

Prerequisites (before starting the playbook)

- Images delivered and verified by checksum/signature. Provide signed manifest (sha256) in /opt/redwood/manifests/.
- Hardware included in the delivery manifest and power/cooling validated by customer on site.
- Basic network cabling and switch configuration completed. Out‑of‑band management (BMC) reachable from operator network.
- Local admin user created: redwood-admin (sudo without password restricted by time window).

Required artifacts to produce during the run (evidence bundle)

- signed-manifest.txt (sha256 checksums + signer identity)
- health-checks.json (service health endpoints snapshot)
- telemetry-snapshot.tar.gz (metrics/ logs / traces for the test window)
- firmware-and-drivers.txt (nvidia-smi / lspci / dmidecode outputs)
- power-fail-recovery.log (step trace for power tests)
- acceptance-signoff.md (signed by delivery engineer and customer operator)

Service names referenced in this playbook

- redwood-runtime.service (primary inference runtime)
- redwood-agent.service (node agent handling KV cache and local routing)
- control-plane-proxy (local proxy for private control plane connectivity, may be disabled in true airgap)
- kv-cache-daemon (local at-rest cache service)
- node-watchdog.service (operator safety watchdog for unattended reboots)

Acceptance tests (step‑by‑step)

1) Baseline verification (10–20 minutes)
   a) Verify signed images: sha256sum -c /opt/redwood/manifests/signed-manifest.txt
      - Expected: all OK. Document signer and checksum.
   b) Validate service units: systemctl status redwood-runtime redwood-agent node-watchdog
      - Expected: active (running) for each. Capture `systemctl show -p ActiveState,MainPID` output.
   c) Health endpoint checks: curl --unix-socket /var/run/redwood.sock http://localhost/health | jq .
      - Expected: {"overall":"ok","components":[...] }

2) Functional smoke (20–40 minutes)
   a) Local inference request: use the bundled smoke client. Example: /opt/redwood/bin/smoke-client --model local-l2 --prompt "Hello" --max-tokens 16
      - Expected: valid token stream within 200ms median for small model; capture output (stdout) to health-checks.json.
   b) KV cache warmup: run 100 short requests and confirm cache hit rate > 60%. Use /opt/redwood/bin/cache-stats --window 60s.

3) Performance sanity (1–2 hours depending on hardware)
   a) Run the workload profile supplied for the customer (profile file location: /opt/redwood/tests/profiles/<customer>-baseline.yaml).
   b) Observe p50/p95/p99 latency and GPU utilization. SLO targets for acceptance (configurable per contract):
      - p50 inference latency: <= 35ms (small L1 models)
      - p95 inference latency: <= 90ms
      - p99 inference latency: <= 250ms
      - throughput stability: <10% jitter over 15 min window
   c) Collect metrics: curl http://localhost:9090/metrics | grep -E "(inference_latency|gpu_util|cache_hit)" > metrics.txt

4) Safety & resilience tests (sequential; coordinate with customer operator)
   Note: these tests intentionally induce disruptions. Ensure customer signs the safety waiver section in acceptance-signoff.md before proceeding.

   a) Graceful node restart
      - Command: sudo systemctl restart redwood-runtime && sudo journalctl -u redwood-runtime --since "5m" --no-pager > restart-recovery.log
      - Acceptance: runtime resumes within configured restart window (default 60s) and recovers KV state; no data corruption in local cache.

   b) Simulated power failure (controlled)
      - Procedure: use remote PDU to cut power for a single node for 30s, then restore.
      - Acceptance: node boots to operational state within site SLA (default 10 minutes), node-watchdog performed expected reboot, logs captured to power-fail-recovery.log.

   c) Network partition/failover
      - Procedure: isolate tenant network from site management network for 90s. If control-plane-proxy is enabled, confirm the runtime can continue processing local queued requests and that fallback policies engage.
      - Acceptance: no more than 1% request failure during partition; queued requests are replayed; alerts fired into local alerting backend. Capture partition-recovery.log.

   d) Disk corruption detection (read-only simulation)
      - Procedure: mount a loop device with read-only flag to simulate partial disk failure for /var/lib/redwood/cache.
      - Acceptance: runtime detects read-only filesystem, transitions affected services to degraded mode and emits evidence to health-checks.json.

5) Security & image attestation tests (30 minutes)
   a) Verify image signature with customer KMS/HSM or provided signer. Command example: cosign verify-blob --key /opt/redwood/keys/cosign.pub /opt/redwood/images/redwood-runtime.tar.gz
      - Expected: verification success.
   b) RBAC smoke: attempt an elevated operation from non-admin account; expect denial. Capture sudoers and audit logs.

Observability and evidence capture (always collect)

- Metrics snapshot: /opt/redwood/bin/dump-metrics --output telemetry-snapshot/metrics.prom
- Log snapshot: tar -czf telemetry-snapshot/logs.tar.gz /var/log/redwood/* (include system/journal logs for the test window)
- Traces: if local jaeger-enabled, export span collection: /opt/redwood/bin/export-spans --window 30m --out telemetry-snapshot/traces.jaeger
- Attachments: hardware outputs: nvidia-smi -q > firmware-and-drivers.txt; dmidecode > firmware-and-drivers.txt (append).

Minimum telemetry retention for certification: 90 days local + offsite transfer instructions included in runbook if customer requests.

Failure modes and rollback criteria

- If more than one of (power fail, network partition, disk failure) causes permanent data loss or unrecoverable runtime state, abort acceptance.
- If performance SLOs fail for p99 by >40% consistently after tuning, classify as FAIL and open a ticket to Eng Platform for remediation with priority P1.
- For security verification failures (image signature mismatch, unexpected privileged processes), stop and revert to verified images.

Safe rollback checklist (if required)

1) Quiesce incoming traffic at local load balancer.
2) Snapshot local KV state (if possible): /opt/redwood/bin/kv-snapshot --out /tmp/kv-snap-<ts>.tar.gz
3) Restore verified image from signed-manifest to node: /opt/redwood/bin/apply-image --manifest signed-manifest.txt
4) Bring up services in dependency order: redwood-agent -> kv-cache-daemon -> redwood-runtime.
5) Re-run smoke tests and attach artefacts.

Sign‑off template (fill in fields, append signatures)

- Deployment id: <deployment-id>
- Customer: <customer-name>
- Site: <site-location>
- Hardware kit id: <kit-id>
- Delivery Engineer: <name> (signature)
- Customer Operator: <name> (signature)
- SRE Reviewer: <name> (signature)
- Acceptance result: PASS | FAIL
- Notes: freeform field for deviations, mitigations, follow-ups.

Common troubleshooting commands and expected artifacts

- Check GPU: nvidia-smi -q > /tmp/gpu.txt (verify driver >= 515.0)
- Runtime logs: sudo journalctl -u redwood-runtime -o short-precise --since "1 hour" > /tmp/runtime.log
- KV cache status: /opt/redwood/bin/cache-stats --json > /tmp/cache.json
- Heartbeat: curl -s --unix-socket /var/run/redwood.sock http://localhost/health | jq .uptime

Safety and physical site considerations (non‑technical)

- Ensure qualified electrical staff performs power PDU tests.
- Follow local permits and safety rules for on-site battery/UPS maintenance.
- All induced failure tests must have a signed waiver before execution.

Appendix A — Example commands (copyable)

1) Verify manifest: sha256sum -c /opt/redwood/manifests/signed-manifest.txt > /tmp/manifest-verify.out 2>&1
2) Health check: curl --unix-socket /var/run/redwood.sock http://localhost/health | jq . > /tmp/health.json
3) Export metrics: curl http://localhost:9090/metrics | grep inference_latency > /tmp/inference-metrics.txt
4) Create evidence bundle: tar -czf /tmp/acceptance-bundle-<deployment-id>.tgz signed-manifest.txt /tmp/health.json telemetry-snapshot/* firmware-and-drivers.txt power-fail-recovery.log

Appendix B — Minimum hardware/firmware table (acceptance baseline)

- Minimum GPU driver: 515.0
- Minimum CUDA toolkit: 11.7
- Disk: NVMe >= 1TB per node (mirrored or enterprise grade)
- RAM: >= 256GB per inference node for large models; smaller models may accept 128GB
- BIOS: latest vendor-recommended revision; secure boot optional but recommended
- TPM/KMS recommended for image attestation

References and related runbooks

- Ephemeral boot and attestation (onprem packaging): /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/ephemeral-boot-and-attestation-playbook-2026
- Field operational runway and safety windows: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/field-operational-runway-and-safety-windows-2026
- Controlled rollforward & rollback guidance: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/upgrade-and-rollback/orchestrated-rollforward-and-customer-safeguards-2026

Change history

- 2025-07-10: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2025-09-02: Added performance sanity thresholds and KV cache guidance (reviewer: Carlos Mendez).
- 2026-02-12: Updated safety test windows and added evidence retention guidance (reviewer: Aisha Rahman).

End of document

title: Tenant failover, observability & cost-safeguard contract
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the tenant-level contract between Redwood and enterprise customers for automated failover, observability telemetry, and cost-safeguard behavior. It is intended for customer success, platform engineers, SREs, and partner integration teams.

Goals:
- Keep customer-facing inference traffic available during service/resource degradation with predictable, auditable behavior.
- Ensure minimal false-positive escalation by defining telemetry thresholds and synthetic checks.
- Provide clear economic safeguards to prevent runaway billing while preserving critical availability.

Scope:
- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants only (hosted tier has separate defaults).
- Covers ingress failover, model fallback, burst throttling, billing-protection limits, and telemetry signals used for QBRs.
- Excludes cross-region DR for air-gapped on-prem installs unless explicitly contracted.

Ownership and Contacts:
- Primary owner: Customer Success (Priya Natarajan)
- Platform/Egress owner: Eng-Platform (oncall: platform-ops@redwood.com)
- SRE escalation: eng-sre-oncall@redwood.com
- Billing guard owner: Finance-legal (billing-ops@redwood.com)

Contracted SLOs and Thresholds (tenant-level):
| Metric | Target | Alert threshold | Pager | Notes |
|--------|--------|-----------------|-------|-------|
| 95th percentile token latency (p95-token-latency-ms) | < 220ms | > 350ms for 5m | SRE | measured at tenant egress gateway
| Request success rate (http 2xx) | > 99.9% | < 99.5% for 10m | Customer Success | Includes model-fallback responses as success unless response is 5xx
| Embedding index sync lag (sec) | < 30s | > 300s | Platform | Applies to enterprise embedding sync jobs
| Tenant cost rate (USD/hr) | N/A (billing) | > committed_rate * 1.5 for 1h | Billing Ops | triggers cost-safeguard (see below)

Telemetry contract (required signals and query examples):
- Required signals to be forwarded by the tenant to Redwood telemetry pipeline: tenant_id, route, model_variant, request_id, request_size_tokens, response_size_tokens, duration_ms, upstream_error_code, billing_cost_cents.
- Example PromQL queries used by Redwood and the customer in QBRs and alerting: 
  - Failed request rate (10m): sum(rate(http_requests_total{tenant="$TENANT",status=~"5.."}[10m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[10m]))
  - Synthetic journey latency (p95, 15m): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(synthetic_request_latency_bucket{tenant="$TENANT"}[15m])) by (le))
  - Cost rate (1h): increase(billing_cost_cents_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[1h])

Failover behavior (automated, ordered):
1) Prefix and KV cache reuse: if a model node becomes unavailable, the router attempts local cache reuse and replays using warm KV where possible (no tenant-visible change).
2) Model variant fallback: route to preconfigured lower-cost or quantized model variant when latency SLOs risk breach. Configuration: model_fallback_policy in tenant config.
3) Cross-pool fanout: for Dedicated tenants with reserved capacity, span requests across standby pool in same region.
4) Tenant ingress backpressure: when request queue exceeds 1000 concurrent requests or p95-token-latency > 400ms, adaptive rate-limiter returns 429 with Retry-After.
5) Graceful degradation: streaming/partial responses permitted if application has opted in.

Cost-safeguard policies (tenant-configurable):
- Billing guard level (enum): warn | throttle | hard-stop
- Default guard: warn at 125% of committed_rate for 30m; throttle at 150% for 1h; hard-stop only if explicitly requested in contract.
- Throttle behavior: dynamically downgrade non-critical model variants and apply token rate limit per-tenant to reduce cost growth while preserving low-volume interactive calls.
- Audit trail: all cost-safeguard actions emit audit events with fields: tenant_id, action, reason, previous_rate, new_rate, timestamp.

Example tenant config snippet (YAML):
---
tenant_id: "acme-corp-42"
model_fallback_policy:
  - prefer: [redwood-quant-3b, redwood-8k-quant]
  - max_latency_ms: 350
cost_safeguard:
  guard_level: throttle
  thresholds:
    warn_pct: 125
    throttle_pct: 150
    hard_stop_pct: 300
synthetic_checks:
  - name: login-journey
    frequency: 1m
    max_p95_ms: 300
---

Required integration steps (for customer success / integration teams):
1) Confirm tenant contract level (Dedicated vs Private) and committed_rate.
2) Exchange telemetry schema and sampling decision with the customer; ensure required signals are instrumented.
3) Configure tenant config (example above) in provisioning template and apply via provisioning API.
   - CLI example: curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d @tenant-config.yaml https://controlplane.redwood/api/v1/tenants
4) Enable synthetic checks and verify p95 and success-rate metrics for 24h before go-live.
5) Run a failover dry-run game-day to validate fallback policies. Document results in the tenant runbook.

Failover dry-run checklist (run by Customer Success + Platform):
- Simulate node failure and confirm: prefix cache reuse occurs; model fallback engaged within 30s; no silent data loss; all audit events are recorded.
- Validate synthetic journey failure handling and Retry-After semantics.
- Confirm cost-safeguard warning is emitted and stakeholder emails are sent.

Escalation matrix (tenant-impacting):
- Severity P1 (tenant offline / >30% error rate for 15m): page SRE (pager), notify CS Lead, open bridge.
- Severity P2 (degraded latency / increased cost risk): notify CS and Platform via Slack; create ticket and runbook entry.
- Severity P3 (informational / billing warning): email to Finance-ops and CS, no page.

Runbooks and automation hooks (examples):
- runbook:tctl tenant-failover --tenant $TENANT --simulate (dry-run)
- Automation play: on alert "tenant_high_cost_rate" -> run lambda reduce-tenant-model-variants($TENANT) -> create ticket in JIRA and send notification to billing-ops.

Observability checklist (minimum dashboards and alerts required before go-live):
- Per-tenant p95/p99 latency heatmap
- Per-tenant success rate and 5xx breakdown by upstream component
- Synthetic journey health panel with per-step timings
- Cost burn rate panel (USD/hr) with committed vs actual overlay
- Audit events stream filtered by tenant_id

QBR artifacts and expected deliverables (monthly/quarterly):
- Monthly: cost burn analysis, top-5 requests by token volume, synthetic pass rate summary.
- Quarterly (QBR): incident RCA summary, model quality regressions, proposed adjustments to committed_rate or guard levels. Include dashboard links and agreed follow-up action items.

Acceptance criteria for go-live (all must pass):
1) Telemetry contract verified and flowing to Redwood pipeline for 72h.
2) Synthetic suite passes 95% of journeys at p95 < committed threshold for 24h.
3) Failover dry-run shows model fallback < 30s and no data loss.
4) Cost-safeguard test triggers warning and throttle behavior in a controlled test.

Post-go-live monitoring cadence:
- First 72 hours: 30-minute ops checks and daily customer sync.
- First 30 days: weekly QBR-lite with executive summary and any proposed parameter tuning.
- Ongoing: monthly cost and telemetry checkpoint.

FAQ:
Q: What happens to streaming connections during a model fallback?
A: If the client has opted into streaming fallback, partial stream frames will be returned with a final frame indicating a fallback model was used. Transparent field `x-redwood-fallback` is set to true.

Q: Can a customer opt out of throttling?
A: Customers can opt out (hard-stop disabled) only if contract explicitly allows and Finance signs off on overage liability. Otherwise the default is warn -> throttle.

Change log:
- 2026-01-11: Initial version (Priya Natarajan)
- 2026-02-12: Added synthetic-checks section and clarified throttle thresholds (Marco Alvarez)

Related references:
- Provisioning API docs: https://docs.redwood.ai/provisioning-api#tenant-config
- Observability playbook: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/tenant-observability-standards-2025
- Billing guard design: /confluence/finance-and-legal/billing-and-guardrails/tenant-billing-guards-2025

Appendix: sample alert definitions (Prometheus alert rules YAML snippet):
- alert: TenantHighCostRate
  expr: increase(billing_cost_cents_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[1h]) > (committed_rate_cents{tenant="$TENANT"} * 1.5)
  for: 1h
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Tenant {{ $labels.tenant }} cost exceeded 150% of committed rate"
