Q: What happens to streaming connections during a model fallback?
A: If the client has opted into streaming fallback, partial stream frames will be returned with a final frame indicating a fallback model was used. Transparent field `x-redwood-fallback` is set to true.

Q: Can a customer opt out of throttling?
A: Customers can opt out (hard-stop disabled) only if contract explicitly allows and Finance signs off on overage liability. Otherwise the default is warn -> throttle.

Change log:
- 2026-01-11: Initial version (Priya Natarajan)
- 2026-02-12: Added synthetic-checks section and clarified throttle thresholds (Marco Alvarez)

Related references:
- Provisioning API docs: https://docs.redwood.ai/provisioning-api#tenant-config
- Observability playbook: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/tenant-observability-standards-2025
- Billing guard design: /confluence/finance-and-legal/billing-and-guardrails/tenant-billing-guards-2025

Appendix: sample alert definitions (Prometheus alert rules YAML snippet):
- alert: TenantHighCostRate
  expr: increase(billing_cost_cents_total{tenant="$TENANT"}[1h]) > (committed_rate_cents{tenant="$TENANT"} * 1.5)
  for: 1h
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Tenant {{ $labels.tenant }} cost exceeded 150% of committed rate"

title: RBAC Evidence Accelerator and Failure-Mode Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook describes a structured, automation-first approach for: (1) ensuring RBAC and authorization changes are safe to deploy, (2) capturing immutable compliance evidence across failures and cross-border failovers, and (3) integrating audit logging, data-residency constraints, and SDLC gating into incident response and risk exception processes. It is intended for engineering SREs, security engineers, release managers, and compliance owners.

Scope
- Applies to: service accounts, role templates, entitlement matrices, role-binding changes, attribute-based access policy updates, and key release/build artifacts for model-serving and control-plane components.
- Environments: staging, canary, production, and cross-region failover (multi-region deployments).
- Exclusions: ad-hoc developer test accounts (see Access Sandbox policy).

Why this exists
- We observed gaps in evidence capture during several cross-region incidents: missing correlation between audit events and deployment artifacts; inconsistent retention applied by region; and lack of a single automatable artifact bundle for auditors. This playbook codifies the steps and tool primitives to close those gaps and minimize time-to-evidence during incidents and audits.

Ownership and SLAs
- Playbook owner: Security Engineering (Priya Natarajan)
- Oncall reviewer: SRE (Engineering)
- Evidence availability SLO: 95% of requests return a complete evidence bundle within 48 hours after incident declaration.
- Audit logging durability target: event replication to immutable index within 120 seconds for production-critical services.

Key Concepts and Definitions
- Evidence Bundle: Signed tarball containing: deployment manifest, CI pipeline run log, RBAC change diff, audit-event slice (time-bounded), KMS-access logs (key one-time actions), residency map.
- Correlation ID: 128-bit hex token attached to every RBAC change request and propagated in the pipeline, change API, and audit events.
- Residency Tag: Per-request geo tag (e.g., eu, na, ap) attached at ingress and enforced by policy.

Pre-deployment Controls (SDLC Gates)
1) Change Request and Ticketing
   - All RBAC/entitlement changes require a pre-authorized TFR (Technical Feature Request) with: justification, list of affected service-ids, residency impact matrix, and rollback plan.
2) CI/CD Policy Checks (automated)
   - Gate jobs: rbac-static-analysis, residency-impact, audit-propagation-checks. Failure of any job blocks promotion.
   - Example CLI: 
     - ./ci/gate-check --pr 1931 --jobs rbac-static-analysis,residency-impact
3) Signed Artifact Requirement
   - All release artifacts touching authz config must be GPG-signed and produce an artifact signature entry stored in the evidence catalog.

Operational Procedures: Standard Change (non-incident)
1) Author change in feature branch with change-spec (YAML):
   - change-spec fields: id, author, target-roles[], reason, residency-scopes[], correlation_id (auto-generated)
2) Run CI gates; attach correlation_id to pipeline metadata.
3) Canary rollout (10%) for 12 hours; collect audit slice: events where correlation_id = X.
4) If no anomalies (see detection thresholds below), proceed to full rollout and automatically generate Evidence Bundle.
5) Bundle publishing: evidence-bundler --correlation-id X --out s3://evidence-buckets/locked/{id}.tar.gz --kms-key arn:aws:kms:region:acct:key/aa11bb22

Detection thresholds and anomaly signals
- Unexpected 5xx in auth service after change: >0.5% increase over 1 hour window compared to baseline.
- Entitlement spike: more than 3x the historical attach rate for a role in 30 minutes.
- Cross-border residency violation: any event where request.residency_tag != resource.required_residency.

Incident response: RBAC or Residency Failure Mode
Purpose: This section covers both security incidents caused by authorization misconfiguration and operational failover events (region outage requiring residency override).

Declaration criteria
- Incident declared when: (A) a critical service experiences authorization failures impacting >20% of user requests for >5 minutes, or (B) an active cross-border residency breach is detected.

Immediate actions (0–15 minutes)
1) Pager escalation: security-oncall + oncall-sre + product-owner.
2) Run the fast-capture script: ./tools/incident/capture-evidence --correlation-id {id} --window now-15m --include-logs --include-deploy --out /tmp/{id}-fast-bundle.tar.gz
3) If service is degraded, enable fallback policy (policy id: fallback-std-2024) to route to read-only or lower-entitlement model variants. Document action in the incident ticket.

Containment (15–60 minutes)
1) Snapshot active role bindings: authctl list-bindings --format json --out /tmp/{id}-bindings.json
2) Lock further role changes by setting change-control mode to maintenance: ./auth-api set-maintenance true --reason "incident {id}"
3) If incident is a residency breach, trigger geo-fence: ./residency block-region --region <code> --apply-affected-resources=true
4) Start evidence orchestration task which will: collect audit slice, pipeline runs, KMS logs, and network egress metrics for the window [-30m, +30m].

Forensic collection and packaging (60–180 minutes)
- Evidence collector specification (table):
| Artifact | Retriever | Retention location | Responsible |
| -------- | --------- | ------------------ | ----------- |
| Audit events (auth-service) | audit-query --q "correlation_id:{id}" --start {t0} --end {t1} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/audit.ndjson | Oncall SRE |
| CI pipeline logs | ci-api fetch --build {build_id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/ci.log | Release Eng |
| RBAC diff | git diff --name-only {pre} {post} && git show {post}:auth/roles.yaml | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/rbac.yaml | Security Eng |
| KMS access audit | kms-audit --filter correlation_id={id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/kms.log | Security Eng |
| Network egress metrics | net-metrics --flow filter cid:{correlation_id} | s3://evidence/immutable/{id}/egress.csv | NetOps |

- All artifacts must be SHA256-summed and the manifest signed by security-oncall. Example manifest line: "sha256:3b2f...  audit.ndjson"

Evidence bundling and legal hold
1) After collection, invoke: evidence-signer --bundle s3://evidence/immutable/{id} --signer /keys/evidence-signer-key --out s3://evidence/locked/{id}.tar.gz
2) Apply legal-hold tag if incident class >= P2: aws s3api put-object-tagging --bucket evidence --key locked/{id}.tar.gz --tagging "LegalHold=true&Retention=10y"
3) Notify Legal and Compliance channels with bundle link and summary.

Risk and Exception Handling (pre-authorized deviations)
- Risk acceptance template (must be attached to TFR): impact, compensating controls, monitoring plan, expiration date, approver (security+compliance).
- Temporary residency exception process: approval required from Regional Data Custodian and CISO; exception artifacts must be included in Evidence Bundle with a signed attestation.
- Automatic rollback triggers: if anomaly thresholds are exceeded during canary, system must automatically invoke rollback within 5 minutes and produce rollback evidence.

Audit Logging Schema (auth-service minimal required fields)
- JSON event example:
{ "timestamp": "2026-02-01T15:12:10Z", "service": "auth-service", "correlation_id": "a3f9c5e2-...", "actor": { "type": "service_account", "id": "sa-deployer-prod" }, "action": "role.bind", "target": { "type": "role", "id": "role/model-inference-writer" }, "residency": "eu", "outcome": "success|failure", "request_id": "req_...", "metadata": { "pipeline_build": 7213 } }

Retention and Residency Mapping (summary table)
| Artifact Class | Default Retention | Cross-border constraints |
| -------------- | ----------------- | ----------------------- |
| Audit events (auth) | 7 years (immutable store) | EU events stored in EU primary + replicated index in NA (read-only), replication only after redaction | 
| CI logs | 3 years | Build artifacts containing customer data do not leave region without exception |
| Evidence Bundles | 10 years (legal hold support) | Bundles that contain residency-protected data must store primary copy in required jurisdiction |

Automation and Tooling primitives (examples)
- capture-evidence: collects time-bounded slices from all sources and generates signed manifests.
- evidence-signer: wraps and signs bundles with KMS and records signer principal.
- rbac-audit-linter: static analyzer that flags excessive role-permissions, missing residency tags, and suspicious role-attach patterns.

Post-incident: Review and Remediation
1) RCA playbook: SRE and Security to co-author timeline, root cause, and evidence index within 5 business days.
2) Remediation ticket must include: code fixes, SDLC gate improvements, additional monitoring, and a compliance evidence re-run to validate capture.
3) If failures are due to a process gap, update this playbook and re-run a tabletop in the next weekly game-day.

Checklist: Minimum Items for an Auditor Request (quick hit)
- Signed Evidence Bundle (S3 locked URL)
- Correlation ID and change-spec (YAML)
- Audit-event slice covering the incident window (ndjson)
- CI pipeline run + build artifact signatures
- RBAC pre/post diffs and access snapshots
- KMS access log slice and signer manifest
- Residency attestation or exception approval (if applicable)

Appendix A: Sample Change-spec (YAML)
---
id: rbac-change-2026-0420
author: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai
correlation_id: 9f2b1a...
reason: Enable model serving write role for predictor-v2
target_services: [predictor-v2, inference-router]
target_roles: [role/model-inference-writer]
residency_scopes: [eu]
rollout_strategy:
  canary_percent: 10
  canary_duration_h: 12
  auto_rollback_on_threshold: true
---

Appendix B: Common Playbook Commands (cheat sheet)
- Lock role changes: ./auth-api set-maintenance true --reason "incident {id}"
- Snapshot bindings: authctl list-bindings --format json --out /tmp/{id}-bindings.json
- Build evidence bundle: evidence-bundler --correlation-id {id} --out s3://evidence/locked/{id}.tar.gz
- Apply legal hold: aws s3api put-object-tagging --bucket evidence --key locked/{id}.tar.gz --tagging "LegalHold=true&Retention=10y"

Related reading and references
- RBAC pattern library: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth
- Audit schema contract: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/event-schema-contract-and-retention-runbook-2025
- SDLC gating standards: /confluence/security-and-compliance/secure-sdlc/sdlc-security-gates-rbac-audit-residency-checklist-2026

Revision history
- 2025-09-15: Created (initial draft)
- 2025-11-02: Added automation CLI examples and retention table
- 2026-02-10: Finalized evidence bundle signing flow and legal-hold integration

title: governance-lattice-and-procedure-templates-2026
preview:
Overview

This document defines Redwood Inference’s cross-functional governance lattice: a compact, operational catalog of company-wide policies and the workflow templates teams must use for access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement and expenses, vendor management, and travel. It is intended for process owners, managers, and auditors who run or depend on these corporate functions.

Scope

- Applies to all full-time employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to Redwood systems and data.
- Covers the following policy families: Access & Permissions, Change Management, Data Management, Procurement & Expenses, Vendor Management, Travel, and shared Templates used by teams.

Why a governance lattice?

The lattice model privileges intersecting ownership (technical owners + business owners + legal/compliance) rather than a single central gatekeeper. This reduces single points of friction while preserving auditability and consistent controls.

Executive summary of controls

- Least privilege by default: role-based groups in IAM with quarterly attestation.
- Change management: three-tier change model (low, standard, critical) with automated gating for critical changes.
- Data classification: four tiers (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) with handling procedures and retention windows.
- Procurement & spend: delegated approval matrix with automated PO creation for purchases > $5,000.
- Vendor onboarding: security, legal, and procurement must sign off before production access.
- Travel: pre-approval, expense caps, and required receipt policy for reimbursements.

Table: Policy Owners (text table)

Role | Primary Owner | Secondary Owner | Review Cadence
- Access & Permissions | Identity & Access Team | Eng Security | Quarterly
- Change Management | Eng Platform | SRE | Monthly
- Data Management | Data Governance | Legal | Annually
- Procurement & Expenses | Finance | Procurement Ops | Quarterly
- Vendor Management | Procurement | Security | On onboarding
- Travel Policy | People Ops | Finance | Annually

Access & Permissions

Principles
- Default to least privilege.
- Use group-based roles; avoid ad-hoc user-level permissions.
- All privileged roles require MFA and hardware-backed keys for production access.

Processes
1) Requesting access:
   - Submit: Use the Access Request template (see Templates section).
   - Approval chain: team lead -> service owner -> IAM admin (automated where possible).
   - Duration: temporary access expires automatically; permanent requests require justification and attestation.
2) Quarterly attestation:
   - Managers receive a review bundle showing current access for their teams; re-certify or request revocation within 7 business days.
3) Emergency access (break-glass):
   - Time-limited (max 8 hours), logged in the Emergency Access ledger, and requires post-use review by Security and the responsible manager within 48 hours.

Technical controls and SLOs
- All console logins to production services require MFA and are logged to AuditLog service (retention: 365 days).
- Provisioning latency SLO: 95% of standard role grants executed in < 4 hours.

Change Management

Change tiers
- Low (config tweaks, documentation edits) — no formal CAB; auto-approve after CI test pass.
- Standard (non-disruptive infra changes, model rollout config updates) — requires automated test results + owner approval.
- Critical (schema migrations, runtime changes affecting >5% of traffic, kernel updates) — requires formal RFC, pre-deploy canary, and CAB approval.

Procedure for Standard and Critical changes
1) Create Change Request (CR) using the Change Request template. Include rollback plan, safety checks, and traffic impact analysis.
2) Run automated preflight tests in staging.
3) Schedule canary: 5–10% traffic for at least 30 minutes with health metrics and monitoring hooks.
4) CAB review (for Critical): CAB includes Eng Platform, SRE, Security, Product owner, and a Finance representative for high-cost rollouts.
5) Post-deploy verification and 24h watch.

SLOs and thresholds
- Canary health: error rate must remain within 2x baseline; CPU/GPU usage increases must be < 30% for the canary window.
- Rollback threshold: 3x baseline error rate or sustained latency above SLO for 10 minutes.

Data Management

Classification and handling
- Public: safe to publish. No retention beyond business need.
- Internal: operational data for employees. Access: company directory groups. Retention: default 2 years.
- Confidential: customer or internal IP. Encryption at rest and transit mandatory. Retention: per contract (minimum 1 year).
- Restricted: regulated PII/PHI or customer-sensitive secrets. Access requires documented business justification and periodic attestation.

Data lifecycle controls
- Ingest: source classification, schema validation, and provenance metadata required.
- Storage: encryption keys managed in KMS; restricted-tier data must be isolated in dedicated storage namespaces.
- Retention & deletion: automated retention jobs; deletion requests must follow Data Deletion Request template with owner approval.
- Auditing: all accesses to Restricted-tier data yield detailed audit trails retained for 3 years.

Change of classification
- Any re-classification to a higher sensitivity requires a Data Impact Assessment and Legal review.

Procurement & Expenses

Approval matrix (text)
- Individual contributor: up to $500 — manager approval.
- Manager: up to $5,000 — manager + finance notification.
- Director: up to $25,000 — director + procurement approval.
- VP+: > $25,000 — procurement + finance + VP sign-off.

Process highlights
- All vendor spend must be raised via the Procurement portal to generate a PO before service activation for amounts > $5,000.
- Corporate card use: receipts required for all transactions; monthly reconciliation due 10 business days after month end.
- Capital expense vs operating expense: Finance retains final classification authority and will flag exceptions during monthly close.

Vendor Management

Onboarding checklist (required)
1) Vendor information: legal name, tax ID, contractual contact.
2) Security assessment: complete Security Questionnaire and provide SOC2/ISO evidence where applicable.
3) Legal: NDA and Master Services Agreement signed.
4) Procurement: PO issued and terms captured.
5) Access: limiting principle — vendors granted only the minimal access necessary; all vendor access must be time-bound and logged.

Ongoing reviews
- High-risk vendors: quarterly security posture review and annual business review.
- Low-risk vendors: annual contract review.

Travel Policy (summary)

Booking & approvals
- All travel must be pre-approved via the Travel Request template. Approvers: direct manager for domestic travel; director-level approval required for international travel.
- Preferred suppliers: use corporate Travel Portal to receive negotiated rates.

Expense reimbursement
- Receipts required for all meals, transportation, and lodging. Card transactions without receipts must be explained in the Expense Note and may be denied.
- Per diem: set per region; exceptions require pre-approval.

Safety & Security
- Emergency contact: People Ops + Security on call.
- Travel to high-risk regions requires Security sign-off and mandatory pre-travel briefing.

Templates & Example Artifacts

- Access Request template (example snippet):

  {
    "requester": "<email>",
    "resource": "<service-name>",
    "role_requested": "<role>",
    "justification": "<business reason>",
    "duration": "<hours/days/perm>",
    "manager_approval": "<manager-email>"
  }

- Change Request template (example snippet):

  Title: <short title>
  Owner: <service owner name/email>
  Tier: low | standard | critical
  Description: <what and why>
  Impact: <approx traffic/users/costs>
  Rollback plan: <steps>
  Approval signatures: <list>

- Data Deletion Request (example snippet):

  Requestor: <email>
  Data scope: <table of datasets>
  Reason: <business/legal>
  Desired deletion date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
  Data owner sign-off: <name>

Standard process flow (text diagram)
- Request -> Triage -> Owner review -> Automated tests -> Preflight -> Canary -> CAB (if required) -> Deploy -> Post-deploy watch -> Close

Roles & Responsibilities

- Service owner: technical owner accountable for feature behavior, deployment, and rollback plans.
- Process owner: maintains the policy text, templates, and review cadence. Usually mapped to a central function (e.g., Finance for procurement).
- Approvers: named individuals authorized to sign on approvals in the matrix.
- IAM admin: operationally executes access changes and maintains automation.

Escalation path

1) First-line: team lead and service owner (0–1 hour)
2) Second-line: process owner and security/finance as appropriate (1–4 hours)
3) Executive escalation: VP-level (4+ hours)

Implementation & Adoption Plan

- Q1: Rollout of Access attestation automation and procurement PO enforcement for > $5,000.
- Q2: Enforce Change Request gating for critical changes and integrate canary automation into CI.
- Q3: Complete Data classification tagging across datasets and begin Restricted-tier isolation.

Audit & Compliance

- Monthly compliance snapshot shared with Execs: access compliance %, outstanding attestation items, open high-risk vendor issues.
- Annual external audit for SOC2 readiness mapped to Data Management and Access controls.

Appendix: Quick links and references

- IAM console: https://console.internal.redwood-inference.com/iam
- Procurement portal: https://procure.internal.redwood-inference.com
- Change Request board (templates): /confluence/eng-platform/change-management/templates
- Data classification rubric: /confluence/policies-and-process/data-classification-rubric-2024

Revision history

- 2025-06-12: Initial publication.
- 2025-12-04: Added vendor onboarding checklist and emergency access ledger requirements.
- 2026-02-10: Updated procurement approval limits and added canary thresholds for change management.

Contact

For questions about this document, contact the Process Owner: priya.natarajan@redwood-inference.com or raise an item in #policy-questions on Slack.
text:
Overview

This document defines Redwood Inference’s cross-functional governance lattice: a compact, operational catalog of company-wide policies and the workflow templates teams must use for access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement and expenses, vendor management, and travel. It is intended for process owners, managers, and auditors who run or depend on these corporate functions.

Scope

- Applies to all full-time employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to Redwood systems and data.
- Covers the following policy families: Access & Permissions, Change Management, Data Management, Procurement & Expenses, Vendor Management, Travel, and shared Templates used by teams.

Why a governance lattice?

The lattice model privileges intersecting ownership (technical owners + business owners + legal/compliance) rather than a single central gatekeeper. This reduces single points of friction while preserving auditability and consistent controls.

Executive summary of controls

- Least privilege by default: role-based groups in IAM with quarterly attestation.
- Change management: three-tier change model (low, standard, critical) with automated gating for critical changes.
- Data classification: four tiers (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) with handling procedures and retention windows.
- Procurement & spend: delegated approval matrix with automated PO creation for purchases > $5,000.
- Vendor onboarding: security, legal, and procurement must sign off before production access.
- Travel: pre-approval, expense caps, and required receipt policy for reimbursements.

Table: Policy Owners (text table)

Role | Primary Owner | Secondary Owner | Review Cadence
- Access & Permissions | Identity & Access Team | Eng Security | Quarterly
- Change Management | Eng Platform | SRE | Monthly
- Data Management | Data Governance | Legal | Annually
- Procurement & Expenses | Finance | Procurement Ops | Quarterly
- Vendor Management | Procurement | Security | On onboarding
- Travel Policy | People Ops | Finance | Annually

Access & Permissions

Principles
- Default to least privilege.
- Use group-based roles; avoid ad-hoc user-level permissions.
- All privileged roles require MFA and hardware-backed keys for production access.

Processes
1) Requesting access:
   - Submit: Use the Access Request template (see Templates section).
   - Approval chain: team lead -> service owner -> IAM admin (automated where possible).
   - Duration: temporary access expires automatically; permanent requests require justification and attestation.
2) Quarterly attestation:
   - Managers receive a review bundle showing current access for their teams; re-certify or request revocation within 7 business days.
3) Emergency access (break-glass):
   - Time-limited (max 8 hours), logged in the Emergency Access ledger, and requires post-use review by Security and the responsible manager within 48 hours.

Technical controls and SLOs
- All console logins to production services require MFA and are logged to AuditLog service (retention: 365 days).
- Provisioning latency SLO: 95% of standard role grants executed in < 4 hours.

Change Management

Change tiers
- Low (config tweaks, documentation edits) — no formal CAB; auto-approve after CI test pass.
- Standard (non-disruptive infra changes, model rollout config updates) — requires automated test results + owner approval.
- Critical (schema migrations, runtime changes affecting >5% of traffic, kernel updates) — requires formal RFC, pre-deploy canary, and CAB approval.

Procedure for Standard and Critical changes
1) Create Change Request (CR) using the Change Request template. Include rollback plan, safety checks, and traffic impact analysis.
2) Run automated preflight tests in staging.
3) Schedule canary: 5–10% traffic for at least 30 minutes with health metrics and monitoring hooks.
4) CAB review (for Critical): CAB includes Eng Platform, SRE, Security, Product owner, and a Finance representative for high-cost rollouts.
5) Post-deploy verification and 24h watch.

SLOs and thresholds
- Canary health: error rate must remain within 2x baseline; CPU/GPU usage increases must be < 30% for the canary window.
- Rollback threshold: 3x baseline error rate or sustained latency above SLO for 10 minutes.

Data Management

Classification and handling
- Public: safe to publish. No retention beyond business need.
- Internal: operational data for employees. Access: company directory groups. Retention: default 2 years.
- Confidential: customer or internal IP. Encryption at rest and transit mandatory. Retention: per contract (minimum 1 year).
- Restricted: regulated PII/PHI or customer-sensitive secrets. Access requires documented business justification and periodic attestation.

Data lifecycle controls
- Ingest: source classification, schema validation, and provenance metadata required.
- Storage: encryption keys managed in KMS; restricted-tier data must be isolated in dedicated storage namespaces.
- Retention & deletion: automated retention jobs; deletion requests must follow Data Deletion Request template with owner approval.
- Auditing: all accesses to Restricted-tier data yield detailed audit trails retained for 3 years.

Change of classification
- Any re-classification to a higher sensitivity requires a Data Impact Assessment and Legal review.

Procurement & Expenses

Approval matrix (text)
- Individual contributor: up to $500 — manager approval.
- Manager: up to $5,000 — manager + finance notification.
- Director: up to $25,000 — director + procurement approval.
- VP+: > $25,000 — procurement + finance + VP sign-off.

Process highlights
- All vendor spend must be raised via the Procurement portal to generate a PO before service activation for amounts > $5,000.
- Corporate card use: receipts required for all transactions; monthly reconciliation due 10 business days after month end.
- Capital expense vs operating expense: Finance retains final classification authority and will flag exceptions during monthly close.

Vendor Management

Onboarding checklist (required)
1) Vendor information: legal name, tax ID, contractual contact.
2) Security assessment: complete Security Questionnaire and provide SOC2/ISO evidence where applicable.
3) Legal: NDA and Master Services Agreement signed.
4) Procurement: PO issued and terms captured.
5) Access: limiting principle — vendors granted only the minimal access necessary; all vendor access must be time-bound and logged.

Ongoing reviews
- High-risk vendors: quarterly security posture review and annual business review.
- Low-risk vendors: annual contract review.

Travel Policy (summary)

Booking & approvals
- All travel must be pre-approved via the Travel Request template. Approvers: direct manager for domestic travel; director-level approval required for international travel.
- Preferred suppliers: use corporate Travel Portal to receive negotiated rates.

Expense reimbursement
- Receipts required for all meals, transportation, and lodging. Card transactions without receipts must be explained in the Expense Note and may be denied.
- Per diem: set per region; exceptions require pre-approval.

Safety & Security
- Emergency contact: People Ops + Security on call.
- Travel to high-risk regions requires Security sign-off and mandatory pre-travel briefing.

Templates & Example Artifacts

- Access Request template (example snippet):

  {
    "requester": "<email>",
    "resource": "<service-name>",
    "role_requested": "<role>",
    "justification": "<business reason>",
    "duration": "<hours/days/perm>",
    "manager_approval": "<manager-email>"
  }

- Change Request template (example snippet):

  Title: <short title>
  Owner: <service owner name/email>
  Tier: low | standard | critical
  Description: <what and why>
  Impact: <approx traffic/users/costs>
  Rollback plan: <steps>
  Approval signatures: <list>

- Data Deletion Request (example snippet):

  Requestor: <email>
  Data scope: <table of datasets>
  Reason: <business/legal>
  Desired deletion date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
  Data owner sign-off: <name>

Standard process flow (text diagram)
- Request -> Triage -> Owner review -> Automated tests -> Preflight -> Canary -> CAB (if required) -> Deploy -> Post-deploy watch -> Close

Roles & Responsibilities

- Service owner: technical owner accountable for feature behavior, deployment, and rollback plans.
- Process owner: maintains the policy text, templates, and review cadence. Usually mapped to a central function (e.g., Finance for procurement).
- Approvers: named individuals authorized to sign on approvals in the matrix.
- IAM admin: operationally executes access changes and maintains automation.

Escalation path

1) First-line: team lead and service owner (0–1 hour)
2) Second-line: process owner and security/finance as appropriate (1–4 hours)
3) Executive escalation: VP-level (4+ hours)

Implementation & Adoption Plan

- Q1: Rollout of Access attestation automation and procurement PO enforcement for > $5,000.
- Q2: Enforce Change Request gating for critical changes and integrate canary automation into CI.
- Q3: Complete Data classification tagging across datasets and begin Restricted-tier isolation.

Audit & Compliance

- Monthly compliance snapshot shared with Execs: access compliance %, outstanding attestation items, open high-risk vendor issues.
- Annual external audit for SOC2 readiness mapped to Data Management and Access controls.

Appendix: Quick links and references

- IAM console: https://console.internal.redwood-inference.com/iam
- Procurement portal: https://procure.internal.redwood-inference.com
- Change Request board (templates): /confluence/eng-platform/change-management/templates
- Data classification rubric: /confluence/policies-and-process/data-classification-rubric-2024

Revision history

- 2025-06-12: Initial publication.
- 2025-12-04: Added vendor onboarding checklist and emergency access ledger requirements.
- 2026-02-10: Updated procurement approval limits and added canary thresholds for change management.

Contact

For questions about this document, contact the Process Owner: priya.natarajan@redwood-inference.com or raise an item in #policy-questions on Slack.

title: Incident Retrospectives, Error-Budget Governance, and Capacity Ownership Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiatives.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated clusters) and the routing/control plane that affects request admission or fallbacks.
- Excludes non-production test clusters unless an incident impacts production via shared infra.

Principles

1. Make decisions from measurable signals (SLOs, queue depth, GPU metrics, and latency histograms).
2. Prioritize fast guardrails over perfect prediction — we want safe defaults and clear escalation.
3. Keep reviews blameless, actionable, and fast: initial incident timeline within 72 hours, RCA within 7 days.

SLOs and Error Budget Policy

SLO definitions (examples):

| Service Area | Key Metric | SLO (monthly) | Alert Thresholds |
|-------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Text generation API (default route) | Request success rate (4xx+5xx) | 99.95% | Page if 5-min rolling error rate > 0.3% and absolute errors > 50/min |
| Text generation API (default route) | P99 latency (server-side) | 300 ms (99.9% of requests <= 300ms) | Alert when 5-min p99 > 350ms or p95 > 250ms |
| Embeddings service | Median latency | 50 ms (99% <= 120ms) | Warn if 10-min median > 75ms |
| Routing/control plane | Request admission latency | 50 ms (99.9%) | Page if 5-min p99 > 100ms |

Error budget burn policy (operational):

- Compute burn rate = (error_budget_consumed) / (time_window_fraction). If burn rate > 2 for 1 hour -> SRE paging and immediate mitigation call.
- If burn rate > 4 sustained over 6 hours -> declare service-level reliability incident and trigger capacity mitigation plan (see Capacity section).
- If cumulative monthly budget consumed > 60% -> schedule a reliability initiative (owner: SRE product reliability lead).

Procedures when error budget is burning rapidly:

1. Triage (first 30 minutes): identify metric(s) consuming budget (errors vs latency). Post findings in the incident channel.
2. Apply short-term mitigations: traffic shaping, autoscale policies, route weight adjustments to lower-cost/compatible model variants, enable aggressive caching.
3. If mitigations fail and burn rate remains > 2 after 60 minutes, escalate to oncall manager + product platform owners.
4. Document every mitigation and its observed effect in the incident timeline.

Capacity Planning and Ownership

Cadence and deliverables:

- Weekly: Capacity healthboard review (light, 30-min) — owners present queue depth, GPU utilization, and forecast for next 7 days.
- Monthly: Capacity planning sync (1-hour) — review monthly growth, committed Dedicated capacity, large upcoming experiments, and quota requests.
- Quarterly: Capacity roadmap (2-hours) — long-term procurement, region expansion, and reserve planning for seasonal peaks.

Signals used in planning (minimum):

- 95/99/99.9 latency percentiles per route
- Error rate by code class (4xx vs 5xx)
- Active concurrent requests and average request length (tokens)
- GPU utilization (per-device), GPU memory pressure, NVMe IO stalls
- Queue depth / request backlog (per-service)
- Cache hit ratio (prefix and KV cache)

Capacity ownership matrix (high level):

- SRE: runbook maintenance, alerting, incident review facilitation, and capacity healthboard.
- Serving Runtime: model packing, kernel tuning, quantization profiles, autoscale policies.
- Platform/Cloud Ops: cluster provisioning, GPU fleet procurement, networking and capacity quotas.
- Product Teams: forecast workload changes (feature launches, marketing events) 4 weeks in advance.

Capacity triggers and automated actions (examples):

- Queue depth > 2000 requests for 10 minutes -> autoscale add 2 nodes (if spare pool available).
- GPU utilization > 85% and Swap/Memory pressure > 70% for 5 minutes -> scale up by 1 node and alert owners.
- Warmup cold-start rate > 10% of total requests in last 15 minutes -> prewarm policy enable for model variants.

Reliability Initiatives and Prioritization

Initiatives are prioritized by business impact and technical debt cost. Examples of repeatable initiative types:

- Cost-stability: Reduce per-token variance by implementing prefix caching for high-volume prompt shapes.
- Latency-tail: Improve attention kernel selection and batching heuristics to reduce p99 by 20%.
- Observability: Add route-specific histograms and request traces to reduce triage time.

Prioritization rubric (used by SRE and Product):

1. User-facing severity (page frequency, customer SLA exposure) — 40%
2. Expected reduction in error budget burn — 30%
3. Implementation complexity and risk — 20%
4. Cross-team dependencies and visibility — 10%

Incident Review Standards (postmortem policy)

Severity definitions (high level):

- Sev0: Global outage (>30% of production traffic impacted or >10% revenue impact).
- Sev1: Major degradation (important routes > 5% traffic affected for > 15 minutes).
- Sev2: Partial degradation (localized customer impact or transient errors).
- Sev3: Minor, single-customer, or non-customer-impacting operational issue.

Timeline and artifacts:

1. Incident declared -> incident channel and page created (T+0).
2. Initial incident timeline posted in Confluence within 72 hours (T+72h). Template must include timeline, impact summary, mitigations, and owners.
3. Root Cause Analysis and action items documented within 7 calendar days (T+7d). Action items must have an owner and target completion date.
4. Blameless review meeting scheduled within 14 days. Attendance: incident oncall, SRE manager, owning service PM and engineering lead.

Postmortem template (required headings):

- Title and incident ID
- Severity and scope
- Timeline (UTC)
- Customer impact (metrics)
- Root cause
- Contributing factors
- Actions (owners & due dates)
- Follow-up initiatives (if any)
- Learning / documentation updates

Operational Tooling & Dashboards

Key dashboards (names and intent):

- SRE / Healthboard: Global SLO dashboard with burn rate, per-route SLOs, and team ownership.
- Runtime Performance: Per-model p50/p95/p99 latency trends, GPU metrics, and batch sizes.
- Queue Insights: Backlog, inflight, retry counts, and cache hit ratios.
- Alert Triage: Active alerts, correlated symptoms, and past-24h alert volume.

Alerting best practices (do):

- Alert on symptoms you can act on in < 15 minutes.
- Prefer narrow, route-scoped alerts for noisy systems.
- Set paging thresholds conservatively; use warnings for near-threshold states.

Alert examples (concrete):

- Pager: "text-gen-default:p99 > 350ms (5m)" — PAGER -> oncall SRE + runtime lead.
- Warning: "embeddings-median > 75ms (10m)" — slack-only alert to owners.

Runbook snippets and CLI commands (copyable):

- Drain a serving node (k8s):
  1) kubectl cordon <node>
  2) kubectl delete pod -l app=serving-runtime --field-selector spec.nodeName=<node> --grace-period=60
  3) Monitor queue depth and in-flight requests until < 5% are draining

- Temporarily reduce model variant weight (routing API):
  curl -X POST https://controlplane.internal/api/v1/route-weight \
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer $CP_TOKEN' \
    -d '{"route":"text-gen-default","weights":{"fast-quant":"0.5","fallback":"0.5"}}'

- Force-enable prefix caching for route (CLI):
  redwood-cli routing cache set --route text-gen-default --mode aggressive --ttl 180

SRE Runbooks (scenario-driven)

Runbook: Persistent p99 tail latency increase (p99 > 350ms for 10 minutes)

1. Scope: Apply to text-generation and chat routes. Owner: oncall SRE.
2. Triage: Check SLO dashboard -> isolate whether latency is model compute-bound, queuing, or network.
3. Immediate mitigations (in order):
   a) Reduce max concurrency per worker to favor latency over throughput (serving-runtime knob).
   b) Shift 25–50% of traffic to smaller model variant via route-weight change.
   c) Enable aggressive batching cutoff (ends long context batching) if p95 latency unaffected.
4. If p99 remains > 350ms after 30 minutes, escalate to runtime lead and open an incident review.
5. Post-incident: Create action item to adjust kernel selection or change quantization profile.

Runbook: Sudden queue/backlog growth and dropped requests

1. Scope: Any route with sustained backlog growth (queue depth increase > 500 within 5 minutes).
2. Triage: Verify autoscaler health, node churn, and any recent deploys. Look for increased average tokens per request.
3. Immediate mitigations:
   a) Increase autoscale target (if spare capacity exists).
   b) Route-weight diversion to Dedicated or lower-latency region.
   c) Temporarily set request admission control to shed low-priority traffic.
4. Escalation: If backlog not reduced within 15 minutes -> page platform operations.
5. Follow-up: Plan capacity increase in next weekly capacity healthboard if trend continues.

Runbook: Model-serving KV/cache exhaustion (cache miss spike causing CPU/GPU pressure)

1. Triage: Correlate cache hit ratio drop with latency and GPU utilization.
2. Mitigations:
   a) Increase KV cache size (where safe) for affected fleet.
   b) Enable aggressive TTL extension for newly-seen prefixes.
   c) If cache resizing requires rollout, shift traffic to cached-compatible model variants.
3. Owners: runtime + infra. Required safety checks: memory pressure < 60% before increasing local cache size.
4. Post-incident: Add synthetic test that warms common prefixes used in production.

Metrics and Reporting

- Monthly reliability report (owner: SRE lead) must include SLO attainment, top 5 incident types, trend of error budget consumption, and active initiatives.
- Quarterly executive summary with capacity commitments and any procurement needs.

Appendix: Quick checklist for a declared incident

1. Create incident page and channel (T+0).
2. Capture initial metrics and short timeline (T+15m).
3. Apply first-line mitigations and note effects (T+30m).
4. Decide whether to page escalation (T+60m) based on burn rate/impact.
5. Post initial timeline within 72h and assign RCA within 7 days.

Related pages and references:
- SLO escalation guide: /confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/slo-oncall-escalation-and-capacity-triggers-2026
- Runbook examples: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/service-degradation-and-fallbacks-runbook-2026
- Observability standards: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards

Document maintenance

- Owner: SRE (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: every 6 months or after any Sev0/Sev1 with > 4 hours time-to-resolution.
- To propose edits: open a PR in the confluence content repo and add reviewer Marcus Lee.

End of playbook.

Runbook: Persistent p99 tail latency increase (p99 > 350ms for 10 minutes)

1. Scope: Apply to text-generation and chat routes. Owner: oncall SRE.
2. Triage: Check SLO dashboard -> isolate whether latency is model compute-bound, queuing, or network.
3. Immediate mitigations (in order):
   a) Reduce max concurrency per worker to favor latency over throughput (serving-runtime knob).
   b) Shift 25–50% of traffic to smaller model variant via route-weight change.
   c) Enable aggressive batching cutoff (ends long context batching) if p95 latency unaffected.
4. If p99 remains > 350ms after 30 minutes, escalate to runtime lead and open an incident review.
5. Post-incident: Create action item to adjust kernel selection or change quantization profile.

Runbook: Sudden queue/backlog growth and dropped requests

1. Scope: Any route with sustained backlog growth (queue depth increase > 500 within 5 minutes).
2. Triage: Verify autoscaler health, node churn, and any recent deploys. Look for increased average tokens per request.
3. Immediate mitigations:
   a) Increase autoscale target (if spare capacity exists).
   b) Route-weight diversion to Dedicated or lower-latency region.
   c) Temporarily set request admission control to shed low-priority traffic.
4. Escalation: If backlog not reduced within 15 minutes -> page platform operations.
5. Follow-up: Plan capacity increase in next weekly capacity healthboard if trend continues.

Runbook: Model-serving KV/cache exhaustion (cache miss spike causing CPU/GPU pressure)

1. Triage: Correlate cache hit ratio drop with latency and GPU utilization.
2. Mitigations:
   a) Increase KV cache size (where safe) for affected fleet.
   b) Enable aggressive TTL extension for newly-seen prefixes.
   c) If cache resizing requires rollout, shift traffic to cached-compatible model variants.
3. Owners: runtime + infra. Required safety checks: memory pressure < 60% before increasing local cache size.
4. Post-incident: Add synthetic test that warms common prefixes used in production.

Metrics and Reporting

- Monthly reliability report (owner: SRE lead) must include SLO attainment, top 5 incident types, trend of error budget consumption, and active initiatives.
- Quarterly executive summary with capacity commitments and any procurement needs.

Appendix: Quick checklist for a declared incident

1. Create incident page and channel (T+0).
2. Capture initial metrics and short timeline (T+15m).
3. Apply first-line mitigations and note effects (T+30m).
4. Decide whether to page escalation (T+60m) based on burn rate/impact.
5. Post initial timeline within 72h and assign RCA within 7 days.
